Wound Care (137) -

Apply Chitosan Dressing to Control Arterial Bleeding
Critical Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. Hold the foil over-pouch so that the instructions can be read and the unsealed edges are at the top.
2. Grasp the unsealed edges.
3. Peel open the over-pouch by pulling the unsealed edges apart.
4. Use your hand and thumb to trap...
Procedure:

1. Hold the foil over-pouch so that the instructions can be read and the unsealed edges are at the top.
2. Grasp the unsealed edges.
3. Peel open the over-pouch by pulling the unsealed edges apart.
4. Use your hand and thumb to trap the dressing between the bottom foil and the green/black polyester backing.
5. Hold the dressing by the nonabsorbent green/black backing and discard the foil over-pouch.

Important Note: Do not let moisture from your hand come into contact with the dressing before you apply the bandage. The moisture could cause the sponge to become sticky.
6. Apply the light-colored sponge portion directly over the wound and apply pressure to the green/black backing with your fingers.

Important Note: Do not let the bandage come into contact with the casualty's eyes.
7. Apply manual pressure and maintain the pressure until the dressing adheres and the bleeding stops (usually 2 to 4 minutes). Do not try to reposition the bandage once it is applied.

Important Note: If the bleeding does not stop within 4 minutes, remove the chitosan dressing, apply another chitosan dressing, and apply manual pressure again. Do not apply a new chitosan dressing over an old one. The old chitosan dressing must be removed so that the sponge portion of the new chitosan dressing can come into contact with the blood and fluids from the wound.
8. Apply a field dressing or a cravat to secure the chitosan dressing and to prevent contamination.

Important Note: For smaller wounds, you may want to cut the chitosan dressing before applying it to the wound. In this way, you will have a second dressing to apply if the first dressing is not sufficient to stop the bleeding or to use on another wound (entrance and exit wound, for example).
chitosan dressing,application,arterial bleeding,field dressing,pressure dressing
Apply Compressive Bandage to Traumatic Amputation Stump
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

1. If there is a traumatic amputation (the arm or leg has been blown off), place a compressive bandage over the stump, even if there is no bleeding yet.
traumatic amputation, compressive bandage, stump care
Handle Impaled Objects in a Wound
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure for handling impaled objects in a wound:

1. Do not attempt to remove the object:
- If an object is lodged in or extending from a wound, do not try to remove it.

2. Apply a dressing around the object:
- Use a field...
Procedure for handling impaled objects in a wound:

1. Do not attempt to remove the object:
- If an object is lodged in or extending from a wound, do not try to remove it.

2. Apply a dressing around the object:
- Use a field dressing and place it directly over the wound, ensuring that the object remains in place.

3. Stabilize the object with additional materials:
- Add bulky, clean materials or improvised dressings around the object to stabilize it and prevent further injury.

4. Secure the dressing:
- Use a supporting bandage over the bulky materials to hold them in place.
- Ensure that the dressing is firm enough to stay in place but not so tight that it causes a tourniquet-like effect.
impaled object, wound care, first aid, foreign body in wound
Manage Arterial Hemorrhage with Digital Pressure and Compressive Bandage
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

1. If blood spurts from the wound with each heartbeat (arterial haemorrhage), apply digital pressure on the nearest accessible arterial pressure point immediately.
2. Apply a bulky dressing on the bleeding wound.
3. **Elevate...
Procedure:

1. If blood spurts from the wound with each heartbeat (arterial haemorrhage), apply digital pressure on the nearest accessible arterial pressure point immediately.
2. Apply a bulky dressing on the bleeding wound.
3. Elevate the injured limb.
4. Apply a compressive bandage. Maintain pressure by means of a firm elastic bandage applied in a figure-of-eight.
5. Check distal blood circulation and make a note if present or not (if necessary, loosen the bandage to avoid a tourniquet effect, but also understand that the distal blood circulation may be absent because of the injury itself, if the major artery of the limb has been cut).
arterial hemorrhage, digital pressure, compressive bandage
Stop the Bleeding and Protect the Wound - Tourniquet Application
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Use a tourniquet only for life-threatening bleeding: Apply a tourniquet only when direct pressure, elevation, or other methods fail to control severe bleeding.
2. Select an appropriate tourniquet: Use a sturdy material...
Procedure:

1. Use a tourniquet only for life-threatening bleeding: Apply a tourniquet only when direct pressure, elevation, or other methods fail to control severe bleeding.
2. Select an appropriate tourniquet: Use a sturdy material such as a bandage, belt, or commercial tourniquet.
3. Apply the tourniquet above the wound: Place it 2-4 inches above the injury site on the limb.
4. Tighten until bleeding stops: Twist the windlass or tighten the tourniquet until bleeding is controlled and the pulse in the affected limb is no longer felt.
5. Mark the time of application: Note the time the tourniquet was applied on the casualty’s body or a nearby object.
6. Monitor for complications: Check periodically to ensure that the tourniquet is not causing excessive swelling or damage to the limb.
7. Call for emergency assistance immediately: A tourniquet should be used as a last resort and only until professional medical help arrives.
tourniquet, wound care, first aid
Control Bleeding from a Wound Using Direct Pressure
Critical Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Steps to Control Bleeding:

1. Raise the injured part above the level of the heart if possible.
2. Use a clean thick cloth, or your hand if no cloth is available, and press directly on the wound.
3. **Keep pressing until bleeding...
Steps to Control Bleeding:

1. Raise the injured part above the level of the heart if possible.
2. Use a clean thick cloth, or your hand if no cloth is available, and press directly on the wound.
3. Keep pressing until bleeding stops, which may take 20 minutes to an hour or more.
4. This method can stop most wounds from bleeding, even in cases where part of the body has been cut off.

If direct pressure does not control bleeding:

• Keep applying pressure on the wound.
• Keep the wounded part as high as possible.
• Bind the wound tightly with a bandage or clean clothing to maintain pressure.
• Squeeze at pressure points on the artery that supplies blood to the injured area. Use the flat part of your fingers to push the artery against a bone, slowing or stopping blood flow.
control bleeding, direct pressure, first aid, wound care
Perform Prompt Surgical Debridement for War Wound Infections
Critical Source: Emergency War Surgery
### Prompt surgical debridement is the cornerstone of prophylaxis/treatment of war wound infections.

• Indication: All open wounds, especially those contaminated or showing signs of infection (e.g., redness, swelling, pus).

-...
### Prompt surgical debridement is the cornerstone of prophylaxis/treatment of war wound infections.

• Indication: All open wounds, especially those contaminated or showing signs of infection (e.g., redness, swelling, pus).

• Procedure:
1. Ensure a sterile field and proper surgical equipment is available.
2. Administer local anesthesia if possible; use sedation or general anesthesia in more severe cases.
3. Expose the wound completely to assess the extent of tissue damage, foreign bodies, and necrotic tissue.
4. Remove all non-viable tissue (debris, dead muscle, fat) using surgical instruments such as scalpels, forceps, and scissors.
5. Irrigate the wound thoroughly with sterile saline or an antiseptic solution to remove remaining debris and bacteria.
6. Apply hemostatic agents if needed to control bleeding.
7. Leave the wound open for drainage unless it is a clean, shallow wound that can be closed immediately.

• Post-debridement care:
- Cover the wound with a sterile dressing and change it regularly.
- Administer broad-spectrum antibiotics as indicated based on suspected pathogens.
- Monitor for signs of infection recurrence or complications (e.g., sepsis, tetanus).
- Provide tetanus prophylaxis if not up to date.
surgical debridement, war wound infections, wound care, infection control, antibiotic administration, tetanus prophylaxis
Prevent and Treat Tetanus (Lockjaw)
Critical Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

• Identify High-Risk Wounds: Be aware of wounds that are very likely to cause tetanus, such as:
- Animal bites, especially from dogs and pigs.
- Gunshot wounds.
- Puncture wounds from thorns, splinters, or nails.
-...
Procedure:

• Identify High-Risk Wounds: Be aware of wounds that are very likely to cause tetanus, such as:
- Animal bites, especially from dogs and pigs.
- Gunshot wounds.
- Puncture wounds from thorns, splinters, or nails.
- Injuries caused by barbed wire.
- Dirty needle wounds.

• Prevent Tetanus: Ensure that all individuals are up to date with tetanus vaccinations. If a high-risk wound occurs and the person has not been vaccinated recently, seek medical attention immediately for a tetanus shot.
• Clean Wounds Thoroughly: Clean any wound thoroughly with soap and water to reduce the risk of infection.
tetanus, lockjaw, prevention, wound care, vaccination, high-risk wounds
Treat High-Risk Wounds to Prevent Severe Infection
Critical Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

Identify high-risk wounds:
• Dirty wounds or those made with dirty objects.
• Puncture wounds and deep wounds that do not bleed much.
• Wounds in areas where animals are kept (e.g., corrals, pig pens).
• Large wounds with severe...
Procedure:

Identify high-risk wounds:
• Dirty wounds or those made with dirty objects.
• Puncture wounds and deep wounds that do not bleed much.
• Wounds in areas where animals are kept (e.g., corrals, pig pens).
• Large wounds with severe mashing or bruising.
• Bites from pigs, dogs, or people.
• Bullet wounds.

Treatment steps:
1. Wash the wound thoroughly with boiled water and soap. Remove all dirt, blood clots, and dead tissue using a syringe or suction bulb.
2. If the wound is deep, a bite, or may still contain dirt, administer an antibiotic such as:
- Ceftriaxone (1 g/day for 3–7 days)
- Erythromycin
- Cotrimoxazole
- Sulfa drug.
3. Do not close the wound with stitches or butterfly bandages. Leave it open to allow drainage.
4. If the person has not been vaccinated against tetanus, administer penicillin or ampicillin immediately, even for small wounds. For severe injuries, give large doses of penicillin/ampicillin for a week or more.
5. Consider administering tetanus antitoxin (see precautions on page 70 if using horse serum-derived antitoxin).
6. If the wound is from an animal bite and there is a chance of rabies, get immunization immediately.
high-risk wounds,tetanus prevention,rabies immunization,wound cleaning
Use Pressure Points to Control Severe Bleeding
Critical Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Steps for Using Pressure Points:

• Identify the artery that supplies blood to the injured part of the body.
• Use the flat part of your fingers to push the artery against a bone, which can slow or stop blood flow.
• Apply pressure for...
Steps for Using Pressure Points:

• Identify the artery that supplies blood to the injured part of the body.
• Use the flat part of your fingers to push the artery against a bone, which can slow or stop blood flow.
• Apply pressure for at least 20 minutes before checking if bleeding has stopped.

Important Notes:

• Do not give up applying pressure, even if it is hard work.
• Maintain pressure with your other hand on the wound itself if needed.
pressure points, severe bleeding, first aid, wound care
Using First Aid Items in a Survival Situation
Critical Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Procedure for Using First Aid Items in a Survival Situation:

1. Use band-aids, steristrips, adhesive tape, and non-stick pads to cover wounds and prevent infection.
2. Apply burn ointments or anti-septic solutions to treat burns and open...
Procedure for Using First Aid Items in a Survival Situation:

1. Use band-aids, steristrips, adhesive tape, and non-stick pads to cover wounds and prevent infection.
2. Apply burn ointments or anti-septic solutions to treat burns and open wounds.
3. Use muslin bandages to wrap injured limbs or apply pressure to stop bleeding.
4. Use salt and sugar for rehydration or wound care as needed.
5. Clean eyes with eye wash solution if contaminated by debris or chemicals.
6. Use alcohol prep pads to sterilize tools or clean wounds before applying dressings.
7. Use a suture kit and scalpel for more severe injuries that require stitches.
8. Apply yarrow from the vile as an herbal remedy to aid in wound healing.
first aid, survival, wound care
Apply Compressive Bandage to Control Bleeding
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

1. While maintaining the pressure point, apply a bulky dressing on the bleeding wound.
2. **If you are alone, release the pressure point and hold the dressing in place with a firm elastic bandage applied in a...
Procedure:

1. While maintaining the pressure point, apply a bulky dressing on the bleeding wound.
2. If you are alone, release the pressure point and hold the dressing in place with a firm elastic bandage applied in a figure-of-eight.
3. If you have a helper, maintain the pressure point and guide the helper in applying the compressive bandage.
4. If bleeding appears through the dressing, place another bandage firmly on top – “over-bandage” – using even more pressure.
5. DO NOT remove the original dressing. Blood may have already clotted in the wound under the dressing.
compressive bandage, apply dressing, control bleeding
Apply Emergency Bandage to Control Bleeding from a Wound
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
### Procedure: Apply Emergency Bandage to Control Bleeding from a Wound

Overview: This procedure outlines how to apply an emergency bandage to control bleeding from a wound on the arm or leg.

Materials Required:
• Emergency bandage
-...
### Procedure: Apply Emergency Bandage to Control Bleeding from a Wound

Overview: This procedure outlines how to apply an emergency bandage to control bleeding from a wound on the arm or leg.

Materials Required:
• Emergency bandage
• Elastic bandage (if not included in the emergency bandage)

### Steps:
1. Place the pad of the emergency bandage over the wound with the white side down.
2. Wrap the elastic bandage around the limb, ensuring it is snug but not too tight.
3. Insert the elastic bandage into the pressure bar of the emergency bandage.
4. Tighten the elastic bandage to apply pressure to the wound.
5. Pull back on the bandage to force the pressure bar down onto the pad.
6. Wrap the elastic bandage tightly over the pressure bar and cover all edges of the pad.
7. Secure the hooking ends of the closure bar into the elastic bandage.

### Important Notes:
• Do NOT touch the white (sterile) side of the dressing or allow it to come into contact with any surface other than the wound.
• Emergency bandages should not have a tourniquet-like effect. If the skin beyond the injury becomes cool, blue, or numb, loosen the dressing immediately.

### Warnings:
• Do NOT attempt to clean the wound or remove clothing stuck to it.
• In a chemical environment, do NOT remove protective clothing; apply dressings over the protective clothing.
emergency bandage, bleeding wound, first aid, tourniquet-like effect, pressure dressing
Apply Manual Pressure and Elevate Injured Limb to Reduce Bleeding
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure:

1. Apply firm manual pressure over the dressing for 5 to 10 minutes, when practical.

2. Elevate the injured part above the level of the heart, unless a fracture is suspected and has not been splinted.
manual pressure, elevate limb, bleeding control
Apply Sterilized Dressing to a Wound
High Priority Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure for Applying a Sterilized Dressing to a Wound:

1. Cover wounds promptly with a sterilized dressing to prevent infection.
2. A sterilized dressing is a surgical dressing that has been treated to be free from germs.
3. These...
Procedure for Applying a Sterilized Dressing to a Wound:

1. Cover wounds promptly with a sterilized dressing to prevent infection.
2. A sterilized dressing is a surgical dressing that has been treated to be free from germs.
3. These dressings can be purchased at drug stores and are available in various forms, such as the Red Cross First Aid Dressing.
4. Do not touch the surface of the dressing that will come into contact with the wound.
5. Avoid washing the wound with water or any other substance, as this may introduce germs.
6. Never handle the wound directly; let the dressing do the job.
7. If a sterilized dressing is not available, consider alternative methods such as boiling a towel for 15 minutes and using it as a dressing after squeezing out the water without touching its inner surface.
8. If no boiled towel is available, use a recently washed and unused towel or handkerchief as a temporary dressing.
9. Secure the dressing in place with a bandage.
10. Do not be afraid to leave wounds exposed to air, as germs do not float freely in the air, making this safer than using unsterilized materials.

Important Notes:
• Germs can cause infection, leading to blood poisoning or even death if left untreated.
• Even clean hands may carry germs, so it is crucial to avoid direct contact with wounds.
• Using unsterilized materials such as a towel that has not been boiled or a piece of cotton from your shirt increases the risk of harm to the injured person.
sterilized dressing,wound care,prevention of infection,first aid
Apply a Field Dressing to Control Bleeding
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. Do NOT touch the white (sterile) side of the dressing. Ensure it does not come into contact with any surface other than the wound.

2. Wrap each tail, one at a time, in opposite directions around the wound so the dressing...
Procedure:

1. Do NOT touch the white (sterile) side of the dressing. Ensure it does not come into contact with any surface other than the wound.

2. Wrap each tail, one at a time, in opposite directions around the wound so the dressing is covered and both sides are sealed.

3. Tie the tails into a nonslip knot over the outer edge of the dressing—NOT over the wound.

4. Check the dressing to make sure that it is tied firmly enough to prevent slipping without causing a tourniquet-like effect.

5. Apply manual pressure and elevate the arm or leg to reduce bleeding, if necessary:
- Apply firm manual pressure over the dressing for 5 to 10 minutes, when practical.
- Elevate the injured part above the level of the heart, unless a fracture is suspected and has not been splinted.

6. If a field dressing was applied and bleeding continues, apply a pressure dressing:
- Keep the arm or leg elevated.
- Place a wad of padding directly over the wound.
- Place an improvised dressing over the wad of padding and wrap it tightly around the limb.
- Tie the ends in a nonslip knot directly over the wound.
- Check the dressing to make sure that it does not have a tourniquet-like effect.
field dressing, pressure dressing, bleeding control, wound care, first aid
Apply and Secure Chitosan Dressing for Bleeding Control
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure:

1. Apply manual pressure to the wound with the chitosan dressing until the dressing adheres and the bleeding stops (usually 2 to 4 minutes). Do not try to reposition the bandage once it is applied.

2. If the bleeding does not...
Procedure:

1. Apply manual pressure to the wound with the chitosan dressing until the dressing adheres and the bleeding stops (usually 2 to 4 minutes). Do not try to reposition the bandage once it is applied.

2. If the bleeding does not stop within 4 minutes:
- Remove the chitosan dressing.
- Apply another chitosan dressing.
- Reapply manual pressure again.
- Do not apply a new chitosan dressing over an old one. The old chitosan dressing must be removed so that the sponge portion of the new chitosan dressing can come into contact with the blood and fluids from the wound.

3. For smaller wounds, you may want to cut the chitosan dressing before applying it to the wound. In this way, you will have a second dressing to apply if the first dressing is not sufficient to stop the bleeding or to use on another wound (entrance and exit wound, for example).
chitosan dressing, apply pressure, bleeding control, wound care
Assemble a Basic Suture Tray
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Basic Suture Tray Contents:

1. Needle driver, 5"
2. Adson forceps, 1x2 teeth
3. Sharp/blunt scissors, 5" straight
4. Iris tissue scissors, curved
5. Mosquito hemostat, curved
6. **#3 Scalpel handle & #10, #11, #15...
Basic Suture Tray Contents:

1. Needle driver, 5"
2. Adson forceps, 1x2 teeth
3. Sharp/blunt scissors, 5" straight
4. Iris tissue scissors, curved
5. Mosquito hemostat, curved
6. #3 Scalpel handle & #10, #11, #15 blades

Additional items:
• Ethilon, or Prolene, or Silk suture: 2-0, 4-0, 6-0, cuticular needles
• Vicryl or Chromic suture: 2-0, 4-0, 6-0 cuticular needles
• Skin staplers & remover
• Steri-Strips and Benzoin
• Several tubes Super-glue Gel
• Skin cleaner of choice
• Irrigation syringes & ability to purify water
• Sterile gloves appropriate sizes
• Sterile drapes, disposable or reusable
• Appropriate anaesthesia and dressing of choice
suture tray, medical tools, wound repair
Assemble a Field Wound Repair Kit
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Field Wound Repair Kit Contents:

1. Tube Super-glue Gel
2. Steri-Strips, and Benzoin adhesive, or duct tape
3. Betadine swab packet, or skin cleaner of choice
4. Irrigation syringe and ability to purify at least **2...
Field Wound Repair Kit Contents:

1. Tube Super-glue Gel
2. Steri-Strips, and Benzoin adhesive, or duct tape
3. Betadine swab packet, or skin cleaner of choice
4. Irrigation syringe and ability to purify at least 2 quarts water; tablets, etc.
5. Dressings of choice

Optional items to consider include:
• 1 Disposable laceration tray with needle driver, pick-ups, scissors, 4x4s, drape
• 1 Disposable scalpel, #10
• 1 Ethilon, or Prolene, or Silk 4-0 & 6-0
• 1 Vicryl or Chromic 4-0
• 1 Skin stapler, 15 shot
• 1 Staple remover
• 1 Sterile gloves your size
• 1 Lidocaine 1% or 2% WITHOUT epinephrine
field wound repair, first aid kit, medical supplies, wound care

Bleeding Control Procedures
High Priority Source: Ranger Handbook 2006
Bleeding Control:

1. Apply Direct Pressure: Apply direct pressure to the wound using a clean cloth or bandage.
2. Elevate the Wound: Elevate the injured area above the level of the heart if possible.
3. Use Tourniquet if Necessary:...
Bleeding Control:

1. Apply Direct Pressure: Apply direct pressure to the wound using a clean cloth or bandage.
2. Elevate the Wound: Elevate the injured area above the level of the heart if possible.
3. Use Tourniquet if Necessary: If bleeding is severe and cannot be controlled with direct pressure, apply a tourniquet proximal to the wound (above the injury site).
4. Monitor for Shock Signs: Watch for signs of shock such as pale skin, rapid pulse, or confusion.
5. Call for Help: Call for emergency medical assistance immediately if bleeding is severe or uncontrolled.
bleeding control, first aid techniques, wound management
Burn Injury Care Procedures
High Priority Source: Ranger Handbook 2006
Burn Care:

1. Cool the Burn Immediately: Cool the burn under running water for at least 10 minutes or until pain subsides.
2. Cover with a Sterile Dressing: Cover the burn with a sterile, non-adhesive dressing to protect it from...
Burn Care:

1. Cool the Burn Immediately: Cool the burn under running water for at least 10 minutes or until pain subsides.
2. Cover with a Sterile Dressing: Cover the burn with a sterile, non-adhesive dressing to protect it from infection.
3. Avoid Breaking Blisters: Do not break blisters as this can increase the risk of infection.
4. Monitor for Signs of Infection: Watch for signs such as increased redness, swelling, or pus formation.
5. Call for Help: Call for emergency medical assistance immediately if the burn is severe or covers a large area.
burn care, first aid techniques, thermal injury management
Check Distal Blood Circulation After Applying Compressive Bandage
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

1. To check distal blood circulation, look for the following signs:
- Pulse: If you know how, feel the distal pulse at the wrist or foot.
- Capillary blood refill time:
- Briefly press the nail bed of a toe or...
Procedure:

1. To check distal blood circulation, look for the following signs:
- Pulse: If you know how, feel the distal pulse at the wrist or foot.
- Capillary blood refill time:
- Briefly press the nail bed of a toe or finger of the injured limb with the bandage: it becomes white.
- Release the pressure; the normal pinkish colour should return in two seconds.
- Do the same on the opposite limb to compare with the normal.
2. If there is no pulse or the nail bed does not return to normal, then the application of the compressive bandage has acted as a tourniquet and stopped the circulation.
3. Loosen the bandage just enough to allow distal circulation, but not enough to allow bleeding to re-occur.
check circulation, distal pulse, capillary refill
Clean Noma Sore
High Priority Source: Where There is No Dentist
Gently pull away any dead skin with tweezers.

Wash the inside of the sore with hydrogen peroxide, being sure to measure it carefully (see page 8).

Apply a wet dressing:
• Soak cotton gauze in salt water. Squeeze out extra water so...
Gently pull away any dead skin with tweezers.

Wash the inside of the sore with hydrogen peroxide, being sure to measure it carefully (see page 8).

Apply a wet dressing:
• Soak cotton gauze in salt water. Squeeze out extra water so that it is damp but not wet.
• Put it into the hole and cover with a dry bandage.
• Every day, remove the bandage, wash the hole with hydrogen peroxide, and put in a new dressing.
• Continue this until the hole no longer smells and there is no more dark dead skin.

Alternative: Clean the sore with an iodine solution.
noma, wound cleaning, hydrogen peroxide, dressing, salt water
Managing Serious Bleeding
High Priority Source: Family Emergency Handbook
Procedure:

1. Ensure there is no further danger to you or the victim.
2. Remove clothing to expose the wound fully.
3. Help the victim lie down or get comfortable.
4. Cover the wound with a sterile cloth if available; otherwise, use your bare...
Procedure:

1. Ensure there is no further danger to you or the victim.
2. Remove clothing to expose the wound fully.
3. Help the victim lie down or get comfortable.
4. Cover the wound with a sterile cloth if available; otherwise, use your bare hand.
5. Apply firm pressure directly over the wound with your hand.
6. Elevate the bleeding part unless a bone is broken.
7. When bleeding stops, apply an additional dressing on top of the original and bandage firmly.
serious bleeding, first aid, wound dressing, pressure application
Perform Debridement in Soft-Tissue Wounds
High Priority Source: NATO Emergency War Surgery
Procedure:

1. Ensure the wound is clean and the patient is under appropriate anesthesia.
2. Use sterile instruments to remove all non-viable tissue, foreign bodies, and debris from the wound bed.
3. Irrigate the wound thoroughly with saline or...
Procedure:

1. Ensure the wound is clean and the patient is under appropriate anesthesia.
2. Use sterile instruments to remove all non-viable tissue, foreign bodies, and debris from the wound bed.
3. Irrigate the wound thoroughly with saline or an appropriate antiseptic solution.
4. Apply hemostatic agents if bleeding is present.
5. Cover the wound with a sterile dressing after debridement.

Warnings:
• Debridement should be performed only by trained medical personnel to avoid complications such as infection or excessive tissue damage.
• Avoid using non-sterile instruments or solutions that may introduce contaminants into the wound.

Details:
• Use a scalpel, forceps, and irrigation syringe for debridement.
• Ensure proper hand hygiene and use of personal protective equipment (PPE) to prevent cross-contamination.
• Monitor the patient for signs of infection or excessive bleeding post-procedure.
debridement, wound care, soft-tissue injury, infection prevention
Precautions for Controlling Bleeding and Preventing Shock
High Priority Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Important Precautions:

• Never use a tourniquet unless absolutely necessary (as it can lead to loss of the limb).
• If using a tourniquet, never use string or wire, as they can cut through skin.
• **Avoid using dirt, kerosene, lime, or...
Important Precautions:

• Never use a tourniquet unless absolutely necessary (as it can lead to loss of the limb).
• If using a tourniquet, never use string or wire, as they can cut through skin.
• Avoid using dirt, kerosene, lime, or coffee to stop bleeding; these are ineffective and potentially harmful.

Shock Prevention:

• When bleeding is severe, raise the feet and lower the head to prevent shock (see p. 77).
• Keep blood away from any cuts or sores on your skin to avoid infection (see p. 75).
precautions, shock prevention, tourniquet use, infection control
Prevent Infection in Wounds and Injuries
High Priority Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Objective: Keep wounds clean and free from harmful organisms to prevent infection.

Procedure:
1. Clean the wound immediately with water or a sterile solution if available.
2. Avoid using unsterile objects such as dirt, clothing, or...
Objective: Keep wounds clean and free from harmful organisms to prevent infection.

Procedure:
1. Clean the wound immediately with water or a sterile solution if available.
2. Avoid using unsterile objects such as dirt, clothing, or hands to touch the wound.
3. Apply a clean dressing over the wound to protect it from further contamination.
4. If possible, use an antiseptic (such as iodine or hydrogen peroxide) to reduce the risk of infection.
5. Monitor the wound for signs of infection such as redness, swelling, pus, or increased pain.
6. Seek medical assistance if the wound is deep, large, or shows signs of infection.

Warning: Infections can result from harmful microscopic organisms present in air, water, soil, and on skin and clothing.
infection prevention,wound cleaning,dressing application
Raise Injured Limb to Control Bleeding
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

1. If bleeding continues, raise and support the injured limb above the level of the heart.
2. Keep the injured site raised, and the head low.
raise limb, control bleeding, first aid
Remove Foreign Fragments from Bleeding Wound Safely
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

1. If there are fragments in the bleeding wound, remove them if they are not embedded.
2. Take care not to harm yourself on sharp objects.
foreign fragments, wound care, safety
Splint Injured Limb Before Elevating for Fractures
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

1. In case of a broken bone in the bleeding limb, splint the injured limb before elevating it.
splint fracture, elevate limb, first aid
Stop Bleeding from Large Blood Vessels
High Priority Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Stopping Bleeding from Large Blood Vessels

1. Apply lint or sponge: Use lint or a sponge with moderate compression or bandaging to stop the bleeding.
2. Leave in place: Do not remove the compress for two to three days.
3....
Procedure for Stopping Bleeding from Large Blood Vessels

1. Apply lint or sponge: Use lint or a sponge with moderate compression or bandaging to stop the bleeding.
2. Leave in place: Do not remove the compress for two to three days.
3. If pressure fails: Apply caustic substances such as lunar caustic, or use an actual cautery (a heated thick wire) to seal the vessel.
4. Seek professional help: If a surgeon is available, they may tie the vessel using a crooked needle and waxed thread.
stop bleeding from vessels, cauterization procedure, wound control
Stopping Bleeding and Protecting the Wound
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Evaluate the casualty for location, type, and size of the wound or injury.
2. Cut or tear his clothing and carefully expose the entire area of the wound to properly visualize the injury and avoid further contamination.
3. If...
Procedure:

1. Evaluate the casualty for location, type, and size of the wound or injury.
2. Cut or tear his clothing and carefully expose the entire area of the wound to properly visualize the injury and avoid further contamination.
3. If clothing is stuck to the wound, leave it in place to avoid further injury.
4. DO NOT touch the wound; keep it as clean as possible.

WARNING:
• DO NOT REMOVE protective clothing in a chemical environment. Apply dressings over the protective clothing.
• The casualty should be continually monitored for development of conditions which may require the performance of necessary basic lifesaving measures, such as clearing the airway and mouth-to-mouth resuscitation.
• All open (or penetrating) wounds should be checked for a point of entry and exit and first aid measures applied accordingly.
stopping bleeding, wound care, first aid, injury management
Use Field First Aid Dressing for Wounds and Bleeding Control
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Use the field first aid dressing to cover wounds, protect against further contamination, and stop bleeding (pressure dressing).
2. When administering first aid to another person, use their dressing; save your own for later use.
3....
Procedure:

1. Use the field first aid dressing to cover wounds, protect against further contamination, and stop bleeding (pressure dressing).
2. When administering first aid to another person, use their dressing; save your own for later use.
3. Check your first aid case regularly and replace any used or missing dressing.
4. Obtain replacement dressings from unit supply if needed.

Details:
• The field first aid dressing is a standard sterile compress with attached bandages.
• It should be carried at all times by the service member for immediate use.
field dressing, wound care, first aid, bleeding control
Wound Debridement in Battlefield Casualties
High Priority Source: NATO Emergency War Surgery
Objective: Remove foreign material and nonviable tissue from wounds to prevent infection.

Steps:
1. Make generous incisions along the long axis of the extremity, avoiding flexion creases at right angles.
2. Plan incisions so that they...
Objective: Remove foreign material and nonviable tissue from wounds to prevent infection.

Steps:
1. Make generous incisions along the long axis of the extremity, avoiding flexion creases at right angles.
2. Plan incisions so that they can be extended if needed in future procedures.
3. Position incisions to avoid compromising potential reconstructive surgery.
4. Expose the full extent of the wound including the deep fascia for thorough debridement.
5. Remove all foreign material, devitalized muscle, and other nonviable tissue.
6. Discard small fragments of bone without soft-tissue attachment; retain larger fragments that contribute to length and circumferential integrity or have significant soft tissue attachments.
7. Clean large, completely detached bone fragments thoroughly and replace them as close to their anatomical position as possible.
wound debridement, battlefield wound care, tissue removal
Wound Dressing and Avoidance of Wound Closure in Battlefield Casualties
High Priority Source: NATO Emergency War Surgery
Objective: Provide temporary wound coverage without attempting definitive closure.

Steps:
1. Do not close the wound at this stage.
2. Avoid any attempt to effect wound coverage, including relaxing incisions, pedicle flaps, or other...
Objective: Provide temporary wound coverage without attempting definitive closure.

Steps:
1. Do not close the wound at this stage.
2. Avoid any attempt to effect wound coverage, including relaxing incisions, pedicle flaps, or other definitive plastic techniques.
3. Dress the wound with a single layer of fine-mesh gauze, followed by bulky fluffed gauze, and wrap it securely.
4. Avoid packing the wound, as this can impede drainage and capillary flow.
wound dressing, wound closure avoidance, battlefield dressing
Wound Irrigation in Major Battlefield Injuries
High Priority Source: NATO Emergency War Surgery
Objective: Thoroughly clean wounds to reduce infection risk.

Steps:
1. Use pulsatile lavage with an antibiotic solution whenever possible for wound irrigation.
2. For major injuries, use approximately 10 liters of solution during...
Objective: Thoroughly clean wounds to reduce infection risk.

Steps:
1. Use pulsatile lavage with an antibiotic solution whenever possible for wound irrigation.
2. For major injuries, use approximately 10 liters of solution during the process.
3. Ensure that irrigation is performed copiously and thoroughly to remove all debris and contaminants.
wound irrigation, pulsatile lavage, antibiotic solution
Apply Direct Pressure to Stop Bleeding
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure:

1. Apply firm, direct pressure to the wound using a clean cloth, bandage, or any available material.
2. Maintain pressure for at least 5-10 minutes without checking the wound.
3. If bleeding continues after 10 minutes, add another...
Procedure:

1. Apply firm, direct pressure to the wound using a clean cloth, bandage, or any available material.
2. Maintain pressure for at least 5-10 minutes without checking the wound.
3. If bleeding continues after 10 minutes, add another layer of dressing on top of the first and continue applying pressure.
4. Do not remove the dressing to check the wound unless it becomes soaked with blood.
5. Elevate the injured area above the level of the heart if possible.

Note: All bleeding stops eventually, but direct pressure is the most effective method for immediate control.
direct pressure, stop bleeding, wound care
Apply Dressings and Bandages for Hemostasis and Wound Protection
High Priority Source: Emergency War Surgery
Procedure:

1. Control all bleeding before applying a dressing or bandage.
2. Assess the neurologic status and circulation of extremities before and after applying a dressing or bandage.
3. Immobilize suspected fractures to...
Procedure:

1. Control all bleeding before applying a dressing or bandage.
2. Assess the neurologic status and circulation of extremities before and after applying a dressing or bandage.
3. Immobilize suspected fractures to prevent further injury.
4. Keep dressings as clean as possible to reduce infection risk.
5. Ensure that dressings cover the entire wound, and bandages cover the entire dressing.
6. Avoid skin-to-skin contact with the dressing material.
7. Leave fingers and toes exposed for monitoring circulation.
8. Use appropriate materials such as gauze, bandages, or hemostatic agents to promote hemostasis and protect wounds from contamination.
dressing application, wound protection, hemostasis
Apply Pressure to a Wound Using Manual Pressure
High Priority Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

1. Identify the location of the wound and ensure your hands are clean.
2. Place your fingers or手掌 directly over the wound and apply firm pressure.
3. Maintain continuous pressure for at least 5 minutes unless the wound is on a limb,...
Procedure:

1. Identify the location of the wound and ensure your hands are clean.
2. Place your fingers or手掌 directly over the wound and apply firm pressure.
3. Maintain continuous pressure for at least 5 minutes unless the wound is on a limb, in which case apply pressure for 10–15 minutes.
4. If bleeding continues after 10–15 minutes, add another layer of gauze or cloth over the existing pressure and continue applying pressure.
5. Elevate the injured limb above heart level if possible to reduce blood flow to the wound.
6. Monitor the wound for continued bleeding and adjust pressure as needed.

Notes:
• Manual pressure is most effective on small wounds or when direct pressure can be applied without moving the injured person.
• This method should not replace other bleeding control techniques like tourniquets in severe cases.
manual pressure, wound care, first aid, bleeding control
Bandage Head and Ankle
High Priority Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Clean the wound with clean water or antiseptic if available.
2. Apply sterile gauze directly over the wound to absorb any bleeding.
3. Wrap a bandage around the head or ankle, ensuring it is snug but not too tight to avoid cutting...
Procedure:

1. Clean the wound with clean water or antiseptic if available.
2. Apply sterile gauze directly over the wound to absorb any bleeding.
3. Wrap a bandage around the head or ankle, ensuring it is snug but not too tight to avoid cutting off circulation.
4. Secure the ends of the bandage with tape or by tying them loosely.
5. Monitor for signs of infection or excessive swelling.

Details:
• Use pressure dressings if there is heavy bleeding.
• Ensure that the bandage does not slip or become loose.
bandaging,head injury,ankle injury,wound care
Basic Measures for First Aid: Wound Contamination and Infection Prevention
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

• All wounds are considered to be contaminated since infection-producing organisms (germs) are always present on the skin and clothing, and in the soil, water, and air.
• Any missile or instrument (such as a bullet, shrapnel, knife,...
Procedure:

• All wounds are considered to be contaminated since infection-producing organisms (germs) are always present on the skin and clothing, and in the soil, water, and air.
• Any missile or instrument (such as a bullet, shrapnel, knife, or bayonet) causing a wound pushes or carries the germs into that wound.
• Infection results as these organisms multiply.
• That a wound is contaminated does not lessen the importance of protecting it from further contamination.
• You must dress and bandage a wound as soon as possible to prevent further contamination.
wound contamination, infection prevention, first aid wound care
Clean Boiled Water as an Antiseptic
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure:

1. Boil water for at least one minute to ensure it is clean and safe.
2. Allow the boiled water to cool before using it on wounds or rashes.
3. Apply the cooled boiled water gently to the affected area using a clean cloth or cotton...
Procedure:

1. Boil water for at least one minute to ensure it is clean and safe.
2. Allow the boiled water to cool before using it on wounds or rashes.
3. Apply the cooled boiled water gently to the affected area using a clean cloth or cotton pad.
4. Repeat as needed, ensuring that the water remains clean and uncontaminated.

Note: Clean boiled water is an effective antiseptic in austere environments.
boiled water, antiseptic, wound care
Clean and Care for Wounds to Prevent Tetanus
High Priority Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
When you have a wound, especially a dirty or deep wound, clean and take care of it in the manner described on page 89. If the wound is very big, deep, or dirty, seek medical help. If you have not been vaccinated against tetanus, take...
When you have a wound, especially a dirty or deep wound, clean and take care of it in the manner described on page 89. If the wound is very big, deep, or dirty, seek medical help. If you have not been vaccinated against tetanus, take penicillin. Also consider getting an injection of an antitoxin for tetanus (see p. 388).
tetanus,wound,cleaning,antitoxin,penicillin
Creating a Sling for an Arm with a Serious Injury
High Priority Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Make a sling like this to support an arm with a gunshot wound or other serious injury.
sling creation,arm injury,gunshot wound
Debride a Wound in Austere Conditions
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure:

The objective of debridement is to remove all foreign substances and devitalised tissues from the wound while maintaining adequate blood supply to the injured area. This can be accomplished through a two-step procedure:

**Step 1:...
Procedure:

The objective of debridement is to remove all foreign substances and devitalised tissues from the wound while maintaining adequate blood supply to the injured area. This can be accomplished through a two-step procedure:

Step 1: Initial Debridement
• Perform this as soon as possible after injury.
• If delay is unavoidable, start systemic antibiotics immediately.
• Leave the wound open (with few exceptions) to allow for granulation tissue formation.

Step 2: Delayed Primary Closure
• This should occur within 4–10 days after injury.
• Ensure the wound remains clean during this time and continue antibiotic therapy as needed.
• Consider delayed primary closure only if the wound appears clean and free of infection.

Antibiotic Wound Therapy (Prior to Debridement):
1. Penicillin: 10 million units IV every 8 hours for 3 days.
2. Kanamycin: 500mg IM every 12 hours for 3 days, then review.
- *Note from Editors:* Current military recommendations suggest using Cefotetan 2gm every 12 hours instead of Kanamycin.
3. Tetanus Toxoid: Administer 0.5 ml IM once to prevent tetanus infection.

Notes:
• This procedure is adapted from the US Special Forces Medical Handbook (1982) and covers debridement in austere or wartime conditions.
debride wound,wound care,austere medicine,penicillin,kanamycin,tetanus toxoid,military wound treatment
Do Not Stitch Dirty or Old Wounds
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure:

1. Inspect the wound for signs of infection (e.g., redness, swelling, pus).
2. If the wound is more than 24 hours old or appears dirty, avoid stitching it.
3. Clean the wound thoroughly with clean boiled water and apply an antiseptic...
Procedure:

1. Inspect the wound for signs of infection (e.g., redness, swelling, pus).
2. If the wound is more than 24 hours old or appears dirty, avoid stitching it.
3. Clean the wound thoroughly with clean boiled water and apply an antiseptic if available.
4. Cover the wound with a sterile dressing to protect it from further contamination.

Note: Stitching a contaminated or old wound can increase the risk of infection.
stitch wounds, infection prevention
Elevate the Limb to Control Bleeding
High Priority Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

1. Identify the location of the wound and ensure the injured person is in a comfortable position.
2. Support the injured limb with padding or clothing to prevent further injury.
3. Elevate the limb above heart level, if possible,...
Procedure:

1. Identify the location of the wound and ensure the injured person is in a comfortable position.
2. Support the injured limb with padding or clothing to prevent further injury.
3. Elevate the limb above heart level, if possible, using a pillow, blanket, or other object.
4. Maintain elevation for at least 10–15 minutes unless bleeding continues, in which case continue elevating until professional help arrives.
5. Monitor the wound for continued bleeding and adjust elevation as needed.

Notes:
• Elevating the limb helps reduce blood flow to the wound, slowing or stopping the bleeding.
• This method should be used in conjunction with other bleeding control techniques like digital pressure or tourniquets.
elevate limb, wound care, first aid, bleeding control
Elevating and Immobilizing a Seriously Wounded Limb
High Priority Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
When the wound is serious, raise the wounded part a little higher than the heart and keep the injured person completely still. This way the wound will heal faster and is less likely to become infected. Walking on an injured leg or sitting with...
When the wound is serious, raise the wounded part a little higher than the heart and keep the injured person completely still. This way the wound will heal faster and is less likely to become infected. Walking on an injured leg or sitting with the leg hanging down will slow healing and encourage infection.
elevate limb,wound care,immobilization,seriously wounded
Inspect and Dress Wounds in Casualties
High Priority Source: TCCC 17 13 Tactical Combat Casualty Care
Procedure for Inspecting and Dressing Wounds:

1. Inspect and dress known wounds.
2. Check for additional wounds, such as scalp lacerations.
wound inspection,dressing,wound care
Manage Soft Tissue Injuries to the Face and Scalp
High Priority Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

1. Assess the injury for abrasions, contusions, lacerations, or avulsions.
2. For abrasions, clean the area with water and apply a sterile dressing if bleeding is minimal.
3. For contusions, apply ice packs to reduce...
Procedure:

1. Assess the injury for abrasions, contusions, lacerations, or avulsions.
2. For abrasions, clean the area with water and apply a sterile dressing if bleeding is minimal.
3. For contusions, apply ice packs to reduce swelling and monitor for signs of more serious injury (e.g., dizziness, confusion).
4. For lacerations or avulsions:
- Apply direct pressure with a clean cloth or gauze to control bleeding.
- Do not attempt to reattach torn tissue.
- Cover the wound with a sterile dressing and seek medical assistance immediately.
5. Monitor for signs of infection (e.g., redness, swelling, pus) and report them to medical personnel.
soft tissue injury, facial injury, scalp injury, laceration, avulsion
Managing Puncture Wounds
High Priority Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Puncture wounds may lead to infection at the base of deep, penetrating wounds. Tetanus is a risk, particularly with puncture wounds, and all expedition team members should be immunised. The skin surface should be prevented from sealing over by...
Puncture wounds may lead to infection at the base of deep, penetrating wounds. Tetanus is a risk, particularly with puncture wounds, and all expedition team members should be immunised. The skin surface should be prevented from sealing over by placing a small wick into the wound. This allows healing to occur from the bottom of a puncture wound upwards, otherwise abscess formation may occur.
puncture_wounds,tetanus_immunisation,wick_placement,abscess_prevention
Preparing Medicinal Plant Poultices and Compresses for Wound Treatment
High Priority Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Preparing Medicinal Plant Poultices and Compresses for Wound Treatment

• Tea Preparation:
- Place the medicinal plant (e.g., Coyote Willow, Yarrow) in a container. Pour two cups of boiling water over the plant. Let steep for 20 minutes...
Preparing Medicinal Plant Poultices and Compresses for Wound Treatment

• Tea Preparation:
- Place the medicinal plant (e.g., Coyote Willow, Yarrow) in a container. Pour two cups of boiling water over the plant. Let steep for 20 minutes before drinking.

• Poultice Preparation:
- Place the plant material in gauze or similar fabric and fold it to secure the plant inside. Enclose this in a larger cloth (about 6" x 8") and roll the sides inward. Fold the cloth over without losing the plant.

• Boiling Process:
- While holding the edges of the cloth, dip the bottom portion containing the plant into boiling water until it becomes saturated. Wring out excess water by twisting the cloth and returning the excess to the pot.

• Application:
- Apply the poultice to the affected area as soon as it has cooled enough to touch. The poultice should be as hot as tolerable for maximum effectiveness. Keep applied for 1 to 24 hours, as needed. When throbbing pain subsides, the poultice has completed its task and can be removed. Replace with a fresh poultice if necessary.

• Compress Preparation:
- Prepare similarly to a poultice but apply it cold to the wound. Use for cooling or reducing inflammation.

• Usage Notes:
- Poultices are used for drawing out infection and neutralizing toxins. Compresses are used for cooling or reducing swelling. Both should be applied as needed until healing is achieved.
poultice preparation, compress application, wound treatment, medicinal plants
Prevent Infection in Open Wounds
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Objective: Keep wounds clean and free of harmful organisms to prevent infection.

Procedure:
1. Ensure the area around the casualty is safe.
2. Wash your hands thoroughly with soap and water before handling the wound.
3. Clean the wound...
Objective: Keep wounds clean and free of harmful organisms to prevent infection.

Procedure:
1. Ensure the area around the casualty is safe.
2. Wash your hands thoroughly with soap and water before handling the wound.
3. Clean the wound gently with clean water or a sterile saline solution:
- Rinse away any dirt or debris from the wound.
4. Apply an antiseptic (such as iodine or hydrogen peroxide) to kill harmful organisms on the surface of the wound.
5. Cover the wound with a sterile dressing or bandage to protect it from further contamination.
6. Change the dressing regularly and monitor for signs of infection:
- Redness, swelling, warmth, or pus around the wound site.
7. If signs of infection are present, seek medical assistance immediately.

Important: Infections result from the multiplication and growth of harmful microscopic organisms that can invade a break in the skin or an open wound.
infection prevention, wound care, antiseptic application, first aid, sterile dressing
Primary Wound Closure Procedure
High Priority Source: Medical Survival Wound Closure Manual
Purpose: To approximate wound edges to facilitate rapid healing.

Procedure:
1. Clean the wound thoroughly with sterile saline or water and apply antiseptic solution if available.
2. Use appropriate suture material based on wound location,...
Purpose: To approximate wound edges to facilitate rapid healing.

Procedure:
1. Clean the wound thoroughly with sterile saline or water and apply antiseptic solution if available.
2. Use appropriate suture material based on wound location, depth, and tissue type.
3. Approximate the wound edges using a suitable needle and suture technique (e.g., simple interrupted, continuous, or subcuticular suturing).
4. Ensure even tension across the wound to promote optimal healing and minimize scarring.
5. Tie off the suture securely with square knots, ensuring that each knot is tight but not overly constricting to surrounding tissue.
6. Apply a sterile dressing over the wound after closure.

Notes: Primary closure should be performed as soon as possible after injury to reduce infection risk and promote healing.
primary wound closure, suture technique, wound approximation, first aid
Provide Basic Wound Care to Prevent Infection in Austere Environments
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure for Basic Wound Care Without Antibiotics:

1. Clean the Wound: Use copious amounts of clean water (or any available sterile or non-contaminated liquid) to thoroughly rinse out the wound.

2. Remove Debris: Gently remove any...
Procedure for Basic Wound Care Without Antibiotics:

1. Clean the Wound: Use copious amounts of clean water (or any available sterile or non-contaminated liquid) to thoroughly rinse out the wound.

2. Remove Debris: Gently remove any visible debris, dirt, or foreign objects from the wound using clean hands or a clean cloth.

3. Cover the Wound: Apply a clean dressing or bandage over the wound to keep it protected and prevent further contamination.

4. Monitor for Infection Signs: Watch for signs of infection such as increased redness, swelling, pus, fever, or worsening pain.

5. Rest and Elevate the Limb: If the injury is on a limb, rest it and elevate it above heart level for 48-72 hours to reduce swelling and promote healing.

6. Maintain Hygiene: Keep the wound clean and dry, changing the dressing regularly if possible.

Notes: While antibiotics may be necessary in some cases, basic hygiene and nursing care can significantly reduce the risk of infection and complications without requiring advanced medical interventions.
basic wound care, infection prevention, austere medicine, no antibiotics, hygiene, survival first aid
Secondary Closure Procedure
High Priority Source: Medical Survival Wound Closure Manual
Purpose: To reinforce primary wound closure and protect it from stress.

Procedure:
1. After completing the primary closure of a wound, place retention sutures approximately 2 inches away from the wound edges to provide additional...
Purpose: To reinforce primary wound closure and protect it from stress.

Procedure:
1. After completing the primary closure of a wound, place retention sutures approximately 2 inches away from the wound edges to provide additional support.
2. Use appropriate suture material based on the type of tissue being reinforced (e.g., non-absorbable for long-term reinforcement).
3. Ensure that the retention sutures are placed in such a way that they do not interfere with the primary closure or cause unnecessary tension on surrounding tissues.
4. Tie off the retention sutures securely, using square knots to ensure stability and prevent slippage.
5. Apply a sterile dressing over the wound after secondary closure is completed.

Notes: Secondary closure should be performed in conjunction with primary closure to provide added support and reduce the risk of dehiscence or infection.
secondary wound closure, retention sutures, wound reinforcement
Sewing a Tear or Episiotomy After Birth
High Priority Source: A Book for Midwives
Steps to sew a tear or episiotomy after birth:

• Most tears can be prevented if the mother is in good general health. During pregnancy she should eat well, get plenty of rest, and do squeezing exercises regularly (see page 44). It may also...
Steps to sew a tear or episiotomy after birth:

• Most tears can be prevented if the mother is in good general health. During pregnancy she should eat well, get plenty of rest, and do squeezing exercises regularly (see page 44). It may also help to slow the birth of the baby’s head during labor (see page 207).
• Small tears will usually heal on their own. Ask the woman to rest for a couple of weeks after the birth. She should keep her legs together as much as possible, although she should move them regularly. Others should do her household work for her and help her with the new baby.
• Other tears and cuts heal better if they are sewn together. It is not hard to sew them, but it is important to learn how from a skilled teacher.
sewing tears,episiotomy repair,wound care,midwife,birth
Stitching a Wound with Thread and Needle
High Priority Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure for Stitching a Wound:

1. Determine if stitches are needed: Check if the edges of the skin come together by themselves. If they do, usually no stitches are needed.

2. Prepare materials:
- Boil a sewing needle and a thin...
Procedure for Stitching a Wound:

1. Determine if stitches are needed: Check if the edges of the skin come together by themselves. If they do, usually no stitches are needed.

2. Prepare materials:
- Boil a sewing needle and a thin thread (nylon or silk is best) for 20 minutes.

3. Clean the wound: Wash the wound with cool, boiled water as described in previous sections.

4. Wash hands thoroughly: Wash your hands very well with boiled water and soap.

5. Stitching steps:
- Make the first stitch in the middle of the cut and tie it closed (1. and 2.).
- If the skin is tough, hold the needle with a pair of pliers (or needle holder) that has been boiled.
- Make enough other stitches to close the whole cut (3.).

6. Leave the stitches in place:
- For wounds on the face: 5 days.
- For wounds on the body: 10 days.
- For wounds on the hand or foot: 14 days.

7. Remove the stitches: Cut the thread on one side of the knot and pull the knot until the thread comes out.

Warnings and Contraindications:
• Only close wounds that are very clean and less than 12 hours old.
• Old, dirty, or infected wounds must be left open.
• Bites from people, dogs, pigs, or other animals should also be left open. Closing these can cause dangerous infections.

Important Note: If the wound that has been closed shows any signs of infection, remove the stitches immediately and leave the wound open (see p. 88).
stitching,wound care,needle,thread,medical emergency
Treat Wounds with Broken Skin
High Priority Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Assess the Injury: Identify if the skin has been broken (a wound) and determine its severity.
2. Prevent Infection: The primary goal is to prevent germs from reaching the injured part. This can be done by:
- Gently...
Procedure:

1. Assess the Injury: Identify if the skin has been broken (a wound) and determine its severity.
2. Prevent Infection: The primary goal is to prevent germs from reaching the injured part. This can be done by:
- Gently cleaning the wound with clean water or saline solution.
- Applying antiseptic if available.
3. Cover the Wound: Use a sterile dressing or cloth to cover the wound and protect it from further contamination.
4. Monitor for Signs of Infection: Watch for symptoms such as increased redness, swelling, pain, or discharge from the wound.

Important Principle: Any injury where the skin is unbroken is less dangerous than one with broken skin because the skin acts as a natural barrier against germs.
wound treatment,germ prevention,sterile dressing,infection monitoring
Use Adhesive Plaster and Sewing for Wounds
High Priority Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Using Adhesive Plaster and Sewing for Wounds

1. For minor wounds: Use a strip of common adhesive plaster to keep the wound edges together.
2. For deeper or clean cuts: Close the lips of the wound with one or more stitches...
Procedure for Using Adhesive Plaster and Sewing for Wounds

1. For minor wounds: Use a strip of common adhesive plaster to keep the wound edges together.
2. For deeper or clean cuts: Close the lips of the wound with one or more stitches using a moderately coarse needle and thread.
3. Tie the threads: Secure each stitch by tying the thread, leaving the ends long enough for later removal when the wound has healed.
4. Reasoning: Tying the threads prevents difficulty in removing them later if the wounded area swells, which could cause pain or further injury.
adhesive plaster use, wound suturing technique, equine wound closure
Using Alternative Suture Materials in Austere Situations
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure:

1. Clean the wound thoroughly with antiseptic solution.
2. Select an appropriate alternative suture material from available options: fishing line, sewing nylon, dental floss, or cotton.
3. In extreme cases, consider using horse hair...
Procedure:

1. Clean the wound thoroughly with antiseptic solution.
2. Select an appropriate alternative suture material from available options: fishing line, sewing nylon, dental floss, or cotton.
3. In extreme cases, consider using horse hair or homemade “gut” sutures (only if no other materials are available).
4. Ensure the chosen material is sterile and free of debris before use.
5. Thread the suture material through a needle (see alternative needles section for suitable options).
6. Suture the wound carefully, ensuring even tension and proper alignment of edges.
7. Tie secure knots at both ends to prevent slippage.
8. Monitor the wound regularly for signs of infection or irritation.

Notes:
• Organic materials (e.g., gut or horse hair) increase the risk of tissue irritation and infection.
• Use with caution, as improper suturing can worsen the injury.
• Only consider these options in absolute emergencies when commercial suture material is unavailable.
alternative suture materials,austere medicine,wound closure,improvised sutures
Wound Care and Infection Prevention
High Priority Source: USMC Winter Survival Course Handbook
Wound Care and Infection Prevention:

• Wounds are breaks in the skin caused by accidents or animals. They can lead to serious complications such as infection and blood loss.
• Proper care of wounds is essential to reduce the risk of infection...
Wound Care and Infection Prevention:

• Wounds are breaks in the skin caused by accidents or animals. They can lead to serious complications such as infection and blood loss.
• Proper care of wounds is essential to reduce the risk of infection and tissue damage.
• Steps for wound care:
- Cleanse the wound immediately with water to remove dirt, debris, and bacteria.
- Avoid suturing or closing the wound, as this can trap contaminants inside.
- Allow the wound to drain freely; this reduces the risk of infection and life-threatening complications.
• Additional considerations:
- Monitor for signs of infection (e.g., redness, swelling, pus).
- Increase water intake to help with healing and prevent dehydration.
infection prevention, wound care, open treatment, drainage
Wound Care in a Survival Situation
High Priority Source: USMC Summer Survival Course Handbook
Procedure for managing wounds:

• Cleanse the wound thoroughly with water and soap to remove dirt and debris.
• Apply an antiseptic to prevent infection.
• Cover the wound with a sterile dressing or clean cloth to protect it from...
Procedure for managing wounds:

• Cleanse the wound thoroughly with water and soap to remove dirt and debris.
• Apply an antiseptic to prevent infection.
• Cover the wound with a sterile dressing or clean cloth to protect it from further contamination.
• Monitor for signs of infection (e.g., redness, swelling, pus) and seek medical help if necessary.
• Avoid suturing or closing the wound unless absolutely necessary (this can trap bacteria inside).
wound care, infection prevention, antiseptic
Wound Cleaning and Irrigation
High Priority Source: USMC Winter Survival Course Handbook
Wound Cleaning and Irrigation:

• Early cleansing of wounds is critical to reduce the risk of infection and remove viruses like rabies.
• Irrigation process:
- Use clean water or a saline solution to flush out dirt, debris, and bacteria...
Wound Cleaning and Irrigation:

• Early cleansing of wounds is critical to reduce the risk of infection and remove viruses like rabies.
• Irrigation process:
- Use clean water or a saline solution to flush out dirt, debris, and bacteria from the wound.
- Bleeding wounds can aid in irrigation by helping dislodge contaminants.
• Aftercare:
- Apply an antiseptic to prevent infection.
- Cover the wound with a sterile dressing to protect it from further contamination.
• Note: Do not use substances like super glue or maggot treatments unless instructed by a medical professional.
wound irrigation, antiseptic use, infection prevention
Wound Inspection and Dressing in Tactical Field Care
High Priority Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Procedure:

1. Inspect the wound for signs of infection or additional injury.
2. Clean the wound with sterile saline if available, or use clean water as a last resort.
3. Apply a sterile dressing to cover the wound and protect it from...
Procedure:

1. Inspect the wound for signs of infection or additional injury.
2. Clean the wound with sterile saline if available, or use clean water as a last resort.
3. Apply a sterile dressing to cover the wound and protect it from further contamination.
4. Secure the dressing in place using adhesive tape or bandages.
5. Recheck the wound periodically for signs of worsening infection or bleeding.
wound_inspection,dressing,tactical_field_care
Wound Irrigation and Open Wound Management
High Priority Source: USMC Summer Survival Course Handbook
Procedure for wound irrigation:

• Irrigate the wound with clean water to remove debris and reduce the risk of infection.
• Bleeding wounds can help in the initial irrigation process by flushing out contaminants.

**Open wound management...
Procedure for wound irrigation:

• Irrigate the wound with clean water to remove debris and reduce the risk of infection.
• Bleeding wounds can help in the initial irrigation process by flushing out contaminants.

Open wound management (open treatment method):
• Do not attempt to suture or close the wound, as this may trap bacteria inside.
• Allow the wound to drain naturally, which reduces the risk of infection and tissue damage.
• Cover the wound with a sterile dressing to protect it from further contamination.
wound irrigation, open treatment, infection prevention
Avoiding Adverse Effects During DERMABOND Application
Standard Source: Medical Survival Wound Closure Manual
Objective: Minimize discomfort and adverse effects during the application of high viscosity DERMABOND adhesive.

Procedure:
1. Apply multiple thin layers (at least 3) to avoid large droplets, which can cause a sensation of heat or...
Objective: Minimize discomfort and adverse effects during the application of high viscosity DERMABOND adhesive.

Procedure:
1. Apply multiple thin layers (at least 3) to avoid large droplets, which can cause a sensation of heat or discomfort due to the exothermic polymerization reaction.
2. Avoid applying excessive pressure with the applicator tip against the wound edges or surrounding skin, as this may force the edges apart and allow adhesive into the wound, potentially delaying healing or causing adverse cosmetic outcomes.
3. Wait for full apposition strength (about 2.5 minutes after the final layer is applied), although the top layer may remain tacky for up to approximately 5 minutes.
4. Full polymerization occurs when the top high viscosity DERMABOND adhesive layer is no longer sticky.
adhesive application, heat sensation, wound healing, polymerization
Control Bleeding from Scalp Wounds
Standard Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Procedure for Controlling Bleeding from Scalp Wounds:

1. Apply direct pressure: Use clean gauze or cloth to apply firm, continuous pressure to the wound until bleeding stops.
2. Bring skin edges together: Options include:
- Tying the...
Procedure for Controlling Bleeding from Scalp Wounds:

1. Apply direct pressure: Use clean gauze or cloth to apply firm, continuous pressure to the wound until bleeding stops.
2. Bring skin edges together: Options include:
- Tying the hair together around the wound.
- Using surgical “superglue” (e.g., Histoacryl) to close the wound.
- Suturing the skin edges if necessary.
scalp wound, direct pressure, surgical glue, suturing
Hair Tying for Scalp Laceration Closure
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure:

1. Clean the scalp wound thoroughly with antiseptic solution.
2. Gently comb or brush hair along the edges of the wound to form bundles.
3. Tie opposite bundles of hair across the wound, pulling them together to approximate the skin...
Procedure:

1. Clean the scalp wound thoroughly with antiseptic solution.
2. Gently comb or brush hair along the edges of the wound to form bundles.
3. Tie opposite bundles of hair across the wound, pulling them together to approximate the skin edges.
4. Secure the tied hair with small clips or pins if needed for stability.
5. Monitor the wound daily and ensure it remains clean and dry.
6. After 5-7 days, carefully cut the hair from the wound edges using sterile scissors.

Notes:
• This method is not ideal but can be used in austere situations when no other materials are available.
• It is most effective for scalp wounds where hair is abundant and easily accessible.
hair tying,wound closure,austere medicine,scalp laceration
Post-Application Care for Wounds Treated with DERMABOND Adhesive
Standard Source: Medical Survival Wound Closure Manual
Objective: Ensure proper post-application care to prevent complications.

Procedure:
1. Do not apply liquid or ointment medications onto wounds closed with high viscosity DERMABOND adhesive, as this can weaken the polymerized film and...
Objective: Ensure proper post-application care to prevent complications.

Procedure:
1. Do not apply liquid or ointment medications onto wounds closed with high viscosity DERMABOND adhesive, as this can weaken the polymerized film and lead to wound dehiscence.
2. Apply a protective dry dressing (e.g., gauze) only after the high viscosity DERMABOND adhesive has completely solidified/polymerized (no longer tacky to the touch), which occurs approximately five minutes after application.
3. If a dressing, bandage, or tape is applied before complete polymerization, it may adhere to the film and disrupt it when removed, potentially causing wound dehiscence.
4. Instruct patients not to pick at the polymerized film of high viscosity DERMABOND adhesive, as this can cause disruption of adhesion and wound dehiscence. An overlying dressing may help discourage picking.
5. Apply a dry protective dressing for children or other patients who may not follow instructions properly.
post-application care, wound dehiscence, protective dressing, patient education
Preparing and Applying Bandages for Wounds
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure for Preparing and Applying Bandages:

1. Ensure bandage materials are clean: Cloth used for bandages should be washed and then dried with an iron or in the sun, in a clean, dust-free place.

2. Clean the wound first: Make sure...
Procedure for Preparing and Applying Bandages:

1. Ensure bandage materials are clean: Cloth used for bandages should be washed and then dried with an iron or in the sun, in a clean, dust-free place.

2. Clean the wound first: Make sure the wound has been cleaned as described on p. 84.

3. Use sterile gauze if available: Cover the wound with a sterile gauze pad before bandaging. These pads are often sold in sealed envelopes in pharmacies.

4. Prepare your own sterile gauze or cloth:
- Wrap it in thick paper, seal it with tape, and bake it for 20 minutes in an oven.
- Put a pan of water in the oven under the cloth to keep it from charring.

5. Apply the bandage: Ensure that the wound is covered properly with the clean or sterile material.

6. Monitor and change the bandage:
- If a bandage gets wet or dirt gets under it, take the bandage off, wash the cut again, and put on a clean bandage.
- Change the bandage every day.

Important Note: It is better to have no bandage at all than one that is dirty or wet.

Additional Tips for Children: For children, it is often better to bandage the whole hand or foot instead of one finger or toe. The bandage will not come off as easily.

CAUTION: Be careful that a bandage that goes around a limb is not so tight it cuts off the flow of blood.

Note on Small Scrapes and Cuts: Many small scrapes and cuts do not need bandages. They heal best if washed with soap and water and left open to the air. The most important thing is to keep them clean.
bandage,wound care,sterile gauze,medical emergency
Proper Removal of Skin Closure Tapes
Standard Source: Medical Survival Wound Closure Manual
Proper Removal of Skin Closure Tapes

1. Using sterile technique, remove the card from its sleeve and tear off the tab.
2. Peel off tapes as needed in a diagonal direction.
3. Apply tapes at 1/8-inch intervals as needed to complete wound...
Proper Removal of Skin Closure Tapes

1. Using sterile technique, remove the card from its sleeve and tear off the tab.
2. Peel off tapes as needed in a diagonal direction.
3. Apply tapes at 1/8-inch intervals as needed to complete wound apposition. Make sure the skin surface is dry before applying each tape.
4. When healing is judged to be adequate, remove each tape by peeling off each half from the outside toward the wound margin. Then, gently lift the tape away from the wound surface.
removal,tapes,wound closure,skin closure
Remove Splinters from the Skin
Standard Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Procedure for Removing Splinters:

1. Use fine tweezers or a sterile needle: Carefully extract the splinter from the skin.
2. Soak if necessary: For stubborn splinters, soak the affected area in water to soften the skin and make removal...
Procedure for Removing Splinters:

1. Use fine tweezers or a sterile needle: Carefully extract the splinter from the skin.
2. Soak if necessary: For stubborn splinters, soak the affected area in water to soften the skin and make removal easier.
3. Apply salicylic acid ointment: This can help soften the skin for sea urchin spines or other difficult-to-remove splinters.
4. Wait for inflammation: If a sea urchin spine is present, wait a few days until the wound becomes inflamed before attempting removal.
splinter removal, tweezers, salicylic acid, sea urchin spine
Selecting Alternative Needles for Suturing in Austere Situations
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure:

1. Identify suitable needles from available resources: sail makers’, glove makers’, or upholster’s needles.
2. Ensure the needle is sharp and free of damage or rust.
3. If no specialized needles are available, use any sewing needle...
Procedure:

1. Identify suitable needles from available resources: sail makers’, glove makers’, or upholster’s needles.
2. Ensure the needle is sharp and free of damage or rust.
3. If no specialized needles are available, use any sewing needle (curved ones are easier to handle).
4. Thread the needle with appropriate suture material (see alternative suture materials section for options).
5. Use the needle to pass the suture through tissue carefully and evenly.
6. Tie secure knots at both ends of the suture.

Notes:
• Curved needles are preferred for easier passage through tissue.
• Any sewing needle can be used in a pinch, but ensure it is clean and sterile.
alternative needles,austere medicine,suturing,wound closure
Stop Bleeding from Cuts, Scrapes, and Abrasions
Standard Source: The Complete Outdoorsmans Handbook
Procedure:

1. Make the patient sit or lie down to reduce blood flow.
2. Elevate the bleeding part (except in the case of a fractured limb).
3. Do not remove any blood clot that has already formed.
4. Remove visible foreign bodies from the wound...
Procedure:

1. Make the patient sit or lie down to reduce blood flow.
2. Elevate the bleeding part (except in the case of a fractured limb).
3. Do not remove any blood clot that has already formed.
4. Remove visible foreign bodies from the wound using a clean dressing.
5. Apply and maintain pressure until the bleeding stops.
6. Apply a dressing pad and secure it with a bandage.

Notes:
• For lacerations, apply hand pressure above the wound before applying a dressing pad.
• In the case of very severe lacerations, use a tourniquet if necessary. Loosen the tourniquet every 15 minutes to ensure blood flow to the rest of the limb.
