• If a foreign body or broken bone is present in the wound, press alongside it and not over it.
cuts, scrapes, abrasions, bleeding, first aid, wound care
Suturing Wounds in an Expedition Setting
Standard Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Steri-strips should be used where possible. If Steri-strips will not close the wound, sutures will be necessary. Only clean wounds that are less than 12 hours old are suitable for suturing. Deep wounds may need to be closed in layers by a qualified...
Steri-strips should be used where possible. If Steri-strips will not close the wound, sutures will be necessary. Only clean wounds that are less than 12 hours old are suitable for suturing. Deep wounds may need to be closed in layers by a qualified surgeon. This is outside the skill of an expedition paramedic; in this case the wound should be cleaned, packed open and redressed daily. This may allow the wound to heal from the bottom upwards. Sutures should never be applied to animal or human bites, deep wounds or contaminated wounds.
suturing,wounds,expedition,paramedic,steri-strips
Suturing a Wound
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Sewing Up a Wound (Suturing)

Proper suturing is essential to close wounds and prevent infection. Follow these steps:

1. Clean the wound: Wash the area with soap and water, then rinse thoroughly.
2. Apply antiseptic: Use an antiseptic...
Sewing Up a Wound (Suturing)

Proper suturing is essential to close wounds and prevent infection. Follow these steps:

1. Clean the wound: Wash the area with soap and water, then rinse thoroughly.
2. Apply antiseptic: Use an antiseptic solution like iodine or alcohol to kill bacteria.
3. Hold the edges together: Gently press the edges of the wound together to align them properly.
4. Use sterile materials: Ensure all tools and suture material are clean and sterile to avoid infection.
5. Insert the needle: Hold the needle with a non-dominant hand, and use the dominant hand to guide it through the skin on one side of the wound.
6. Pass the needle through the other side: Bring the needle out on the opposite side of the wound, ensuring even tension across both sides.
7. Tie the suture: Tie a square knot or surgeon’s knot securely but not too tightly to avoid cutting off blood flow.
8. Repeat for additional stitches: Continue this process along the length of the wound, spacing stitches evenly (about 1 cm apart).
9. Trim excess suture material: Cut any extra thread close to the knot to prevent irritation.
10. Apply a bandage or dressing: Cover the wound with a sterile bandage and change it regularly to keep it clean.
suturing, wound closure, first aid, infection prevention
Technique for Applying DERMABOND Adhesive
Standard Source: Medical Survival Wound Closure Manual
Procedure:

1. Follow standard surgical practice for wound preparation and achieve hemostasis.
2. Approximate the skin edges, using deep sutures if necessary to relieve tension.
3. To apply adhesive:
- For original DERMABOND:
...
Procedure:

1. Follow standard surgical practice for wound preparation and achieve hemostasis.
2. Approximate the skin edges, using deep sutures if necessary to relieve tension.
3. To apply adhesive:
- For original DERMABOND:
a. Crack the vial in an upright position, invert it, and apply pressure to saturate the tip.
b. Release pressure, then reapply pressure to express adhesive.
- For DERMABOND ProPen applicator:
a. Twist the collar to crack the vial.
b. Lightly press the button to saturate the tip and express the adhesive.
4. Apply 2 thin layers of adhesive, waiting approximately 30 seconds between layers (note: 3 layers are required for original DERMABOND adhesive).
dermabond application,adhesive technique,wound closure
Treating Chronic Sores on the Legs or Feet in Older Adults
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

1. Keep the sore clean by washing it with boiled water and mild soap.
2. Change the bandage frequently to prevent infection.
3. Monitor for signs of infection such as redness, swelling, or pus.
4. If infection is suspected, treat...
Procedure:

1. Keep the sore clean by washing it with boiled water and mild soap.
2. Change the bandage frequently to prevent infection.
3. Monitor for signs of infection such as redness, swelling, or pus.
4. If infection is suspected, treat according to guidelines on page 88.
5. Elevate the affected limb when possible (see page 176).

Notes:
• Chronic sores may be caused by poor circulation, varicose veins, or diabetes.
• Infection can rapidly worsen and lead to serious complications if not treated promptly.
sores,wound care,circulation,diabetes
Using Bandage Scissors for Safe Dressing Removal
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Purpose: To safely remove bandages without causing injury to the patient.

Procedure:
1. Use bandage scissors, which are specifically designed with rounded tips to cut away bandages near the body without puncturing skin or creating holes...
Purpose: To safely remove bandages without causing injury to the patient.

Procedure:
1. Use bandage scissors, which are specifically designed with rounded tips to cut away bandages near the body without puncturing skin or creating holes in the dressing.
2. Always cut away from the body to avoid accidental cuts.
3. Ensure that the scissors are sterilized before and after use to prevent infection.
4. Handle the patient gently during removal, especially if the wound is sensitive or inflamed.
bandage scissors, dressing removal, safe cutting
Wound Inspection and Dressing
Standard Source: TCCC 17 13 Army Tactical Combat Casualty Care
Procedure for Wound Inspection and Dressing:

1. Inspect all known wounds.
2. Dress the wounds appropriately.
3. Check for additional wounds, such as scalp lacerations.
wound inspection, dressing wounds, wound care
Aftercare for Adhesive Tapes on Wounds
Standard Source: Medical Survival Wound Closure Manual
Aftercare for Adhesive Tapes on Wounds

• Adhesive tapes should be left in place as long as possible, at least as long as sutures would be left before removal.
• Patients must be warned to keep the tapes as dry as possible and not to cover them...
Aftercare for Adhesive Tapes on Wounds

• Adhesive tapes should be left in place as long as possible, at least as long as sutures would be left before removal.
• Patients must be warned to keep the tapes as dry as possible and not to cover them with ointments.
• Showering is permitted. During the first week, many surgeons recommend that patients cover their wounds and tape with a nonadherent dressing.
aftercare,wound closure,tapes,wound care
Application of Standard Bandages and Types of Bandages
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid + Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

1. Select standard bandages made of gauze or muslin.
2. Apply over a sterile dressing to secure it in place, close its edge from dirt and germs, and create pressure on the wound to control bleeding.
3. Use bandages to support...
Procedure:

1. Select standard bandages made of gauze or muslin.
2. Apply over a sterile dressing to secure it in place, close its edge from dirt and germs, and create pressure on the wound to control bleeding.
3. Use bandages to support an injured part or secure a splint.
4. Tailed bandages may be attached to the dressing as indicated on the field first aid dressing (Figure A-1C).
5. Ensure that standard bandages are available under CTA 8-100 for use in Army vehicles, aircraft, and boats.

Types and Uses of Bandages:
• Standard bandages are made of gauze or muslin and are used over a sterile dressing to:
- Secure the dressing in place
- Close off its edge from dirt and germs
- Create pressure on the wound and control bleeding
• A bandage can also support an injured part or secure a splint.
bandages, first aid, wound care, sterile dressing, splints, gauze, muslin
Apply Bandages Correctly
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

Follow the illustrated method for applying bandages as shown in the accompanying diagram.

• Ensure the wound is clean and any debris is removed before applying a bandage.
• Use sterile gauze or cloth to cover the wound.
• Apply...
Procedure:

Follow the illustrated method for applying bandages as shown in the accompanying diagram.

• Ensure the wound is clean and any debris is removed before applying a bandage.
• Use sterile gauze or cloth to cover the wound.
• Apply pressure if there is bleeding to help control it.
• Wrap the bandage snugly but not too tight to avoid restricting blood flow.
• Secure the ends of the bandage with adhesive tape or a safety pin.
• Check periodically to ensure the bandage remains in place and does not cause discomfort.
bandage application,wound care,first aid
Apply Bandages to Wounds
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. If the wound allows for a roller or bandage to be used, apply it to support the sides of the wound and reduce pain from stitches.
2. Ensure the bandage is not too tight; it should only provide enough support to keep the divided...
Procedure:

1. If the wound allows for a roller or bandage to be used, apply it to support the sides of the wound and reduce pain from stitches.
2. Ensure the bandage is not too tight; it should only provide enough support to keep the divided parts near each other in their natural position.
3. Avoid using stimulant applications such as spirits, brandy, Friar's balsam, spirit of wine and camphor, turpentine, or any other irritating substances, as they may cause inflammation, mortification, or a bad sore.
4. This method helps wounds heal more quickly, especially in the young and healthy, and reduces the chance of blemishes.

Warnings:
• Do not use irritating applications on fresh clean wounds.
• Avoid over-tightening bandages to prevent further injury.
bandage,wound care,medical treatment,first aid
Apply Ointment to Wounds
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. After a few days of proper wound care and poultice application, when the wound begins to show a healthy appearance (clean and reddish in color), apply an ointment.
2. The ointment should be made from tallow, linseed oil, beeswax,...
Procedure:

1. After a few days of proper wound care and poultice application, when the wound begins to show a healthy appearance (clean and reddish in color), apply an ointment.
2. The ointment should be made from tallow, linseed oil, beeswax, and hogs' lard in proportions that make it slightly firmer than butter.
3. Spread the ointment on soft clean tow (a type of linen fabric) and apply it to the wound.
4. Secure the ointment with a bandage of proper length and breadth; avoid using a mere cord as it may be improper.
5. The bandage should be close enough to keep the ointment in place but not so tight that it causes discomfort.
6. Change the application once daily, or every two days if the wound is nearly healed and discharging very little.

Warnings:
• Do not tie the ointment too tightly on the wound.
• Ensure the bandage used is appropriate in size and material.
ointment,wound care,medical treatment,first aid
Apply Poultice to Sores and Bruises
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. For sores or bruises where there is a tendency to suppuration, apply a poultice.
2. The poultice may be made from any of the following: fine bran, bruised linseed, mashed turnips, carrots, etc.
3. A common and useful poultice can...
Procedure:

1. For sores or bruises where there is a tendency to suppuration, apply a poultice.
2. The poultice may be made from any of the following: fine bran, bruised linseed, mashed turnips, carrots, etc.
3. A common and useful poultice can be prepared as follows:
- Take 1 quart of fine bran.
- Pour on enough boiling water to make a thin paste.
- Add linseed powder until the mixture reaches a proper consistency.
4. Apply the poultice over the affected area and secure it with suitable bandages.
5. Keep the poultice on for up to 10 days or longer if necessary, changing it once or twice daily.
6. When removing the poultice, clean the wound using a soft rag or linen cloth soaked in water that is no warmer than blood warm (avoid rough sponges).
7. For deep wounds, use a syringe to inject water into the wound to thoroughly clean it from the bottom.

Warnings:
• Avoid using rough materials for cleaning wounds.
• Ensure the poultice is changed regularly and the wound remains clean.
poultice,sores,bruises,wound care,medical treatment
Apply a Dressing to a Side-of-Head or Cheek Wound
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid + Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:
1. Remove the dressing from its wrapper.
2. Grasp the tails in both hands.
3. Hold the dressing directly over the wound with the white side down, and place it directly on the wound (Figure 3-27A). **Do not touch the white...
Procedure:
1. Remove the dressing from its wrapper.
2. Grasp the tails in both hands.
3. Hold the dressing directly over the wound with the white side down, and place it directly on the wound (Figure 3-27A). Do not touch the white (sterile) side of the dressing or allow anything except the wound to come in contact with it.
4. Hold the dressing in place with one hand (the casualty may assist if able).
5. Wrap the top tail over the top of the head, down in front of the ear (on the side opposite the wound), under the chin (Figure 3-27B), and up over the dressing to a point just above the ear (on the wound side).
6. Bring the second tail under the chin, up in front of the ear (on the side opposite the wound), and over the head to meet the other tail (on the wounded side) (Figure 3-28).

Important Notes:
• When possible, avoid covering the casualty’s ear with the dressing, as this will decrease his ability to hear.
• Ensure that the dressing
dressing, head wound, cheek wound, first aid, sterile
Apply a Dressing to a Wound on Top of the Head
Standard Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Steps:

1. Remove the dressing from the wrapper.
2. Grasp the tails of the dressing in both hands.
3. Hold it (white side down) directly over the wound. DO NOT touch the white (sterile) side of the dressing or allow anything...
Steps:

1. Remove the dressing from the wrapper.
2. Grasp the tails of the dressing in both hands.
3. Hold it (white side down) directly over the wound. DO NOT touch the white (sterile) side of the dressing or allow anything except the wound to come in contact with it.
4. Place it over the wound. (See Figure 3-19 for reference.)
5. Hold it in place with one hand. If the casualty is able, he may assist.
6. Wrap one tail down under the chin, up in front of the ear, over the dressing, and in front of the other ear. (See Figure 3-20 for reference.)
dressing,application,wound,top of head,sterile,procedure
Apply a Dressing to the Head with Tails Wrapped Around the Chin and Forehead
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid + Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Step-by-step procedure:

1. Wrap the remaining tail under the chin: Take the remaining tail of the dressing and wrap it under the chin in the opposite direction.
2. Bring the tail up the side of the face: Continue wrapping the tail up...
Step-by-step procedure:

1. Wrap the remaining tail under the chin: Take the remaining tail of the dressing and wrap it under the chin in the opposite direction.
2. Bring the tail up the side of the face: Continue wrapping the tail up the side of the face to meet the first tail.
3. Cross the tails: Cross the two tails, bringing one around the forehead (above the eyebrows) and the other around the back of the head (at the base of the skull).
4. Tie with a square knot: Bring the tails together at a point just above and in front of the opposite ear and tie them using a square knot.

Important Notes:
• Ensure that the tails remain wide and close to the front of the chin to avoid choking the casualty.
• Avoid applying excessive pressure on the wound or injured area.
dressing, head injury, first aid, tail wrapping, square knot
Applying a Dressing to a Wound on the Forehead, Back of the Head, or Top of the Head
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid + Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Objective: Apply a field dressing correctly to wounds on the forehead, back of the head, or top of the head.

Steps:
1. Remove the dressing from its wrapper.
2. Grasp both tails of the dressing in both hands.
3. **Hold the dressing...
Objective: Apply a field dressing correctly to wounds on the forehead, back of the head, or top of the head.

Steps:
1. Remove the dressing from its wrapper.
2. Grasp both tails of the dressing in both hands.
3. Hold the dressing (white side down) directly over the wound.
- Do not touch the white (sterile) side or allow anything else to come into contact with it.
4. Place the dressing directly over the wound.
5. Hold the dressing in place with one hand.
- If the casualty is able, they may assist.
6. Wrap the first tail horizontally around the head, ensuring it covers the dressing (see Figure 3-16).
7. Hold the first tail in place and wrap the second tail in the opposite direction, covering the dressing (see Figure 3-17).
8. Tie a square knot and secure the tails at the side of the head.
- Ensure the tails do not cover the eyes or ears (see Figure 3-18).

Additional Step for Wounds on Top of the Head: Wrap one tail down under the chin (Figure 3-20), up in front of the ear, over the dressing, and in front of the other ear.

Notes:
• Always use the casualty’s field dressing, not your own.
• Maintain sterility by avoiding contact with the sterile side of the dressing.
dressing, wound care, head injury, first aid, sterile procedure
Assemble a First Aid Kit for Wounds and Skin Problems
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
To assemble a first aid kit for wounds and skin problems, include the following supplies:

1. Protective gloves: Use plastic or rubber gloves, or use plastic bags as an alternative to protect your hands during wound care.
2. **Sterile gauze...
To assemble a first aid kit for wounds and skin problems, include the following supplies:

1. Protective gloves: Use plastic or rubber gloves, or use plastic bags as an alternative to protect your hands during wound care.
2. Sterile gauze pads: Keep sterile gauze pads in individual sealed envelopes for hygiene and convenience.
3. Gauze bandage rolls: Include 1-, 2-, and 3-inch gauze bandage rolls for different types of wounds.
4. Clean cotton: Use clean cotton for padding or cleaning wounds.
5. Adhesive tape (adhesive plaster): A 1-inch wide roll is recommended for securing dressings.
6. Disinfectant soap: If available, use a disinfectant soap like Betadine to help prevent infection.
7. Alcohol: Use 70% alcohol for cleaning wounds and hands.
8. Hydrogen peroxide: Store hydrogen peroxide in a dark bottle to preserve its effectiveness.
9. Petroleum jelly (Vaseline): Keep petroleum jelly in a jar or tube for applying to wounds after cleaning.
10. White vinegar: Use white vinegar as an alternative disinfectant or for other purposes like treating skin conditions.
11. Sulfur: Include sulfur for treating fungal infections or skin problems.
12. Scissors: Ensure you have clean, non-rusty scissors for cutting bandages and dressings.
13. Tweezers: Use tweezers with pointed ends to remove splinters, debris, or ticks from wounds.

These supplies are essential for managing wounds and skin problems in a village health care setting.
first aid,wound care,sterile gauze,disinfectant soap,alcohol,hydrogen peroxide,petroleum jelly,tweezers
Choosing Appropriate Suture Materials for Austere Medicine
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure:

1. In ideal conditions, use synthetic absorbable sutures like Vicryl (or equivalent) in a variety of sizes with a 1/3 circle taper needle.
2. If Vicryl is unavailable or too expensive, consider using nylon and simple gut suture...
Procedure:

1. In ideal conditions, use synthetic absorbable sutures like Vicryl (or equivalent) in a variety of sizes with a 1/3 circle taper needle.
2. If Vicryl is unavailable or too expensive, consider using nylon and simple gut suture materials in various sizes.
3. Avoid silk unless absolutely necessary, as it has poor tensile strength and may cause more tissue reaction.
4. Consider investing in disposable staplers if financially feasible for quick wound closure.
5. If improvising with alternative suture materials (e.g., fishing line or dental floss), ensure they are clean, sterile, and suitable for the type of wound being treated.

Notes:
• Always assess whether suturing is appropriate based on the injury severity and available resources.
• Improvised materials increase the risk of infection and tissue irritation.
• Proper training and knowledge are essential to avoid worsening the injury.
suture materials,austere medicine,wound closure,Vicryl,nylon,gut sutures
Create Homemade Sterile Dressings from Fabric
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Materials Needed:
• White material (100% cotton is most absorbent)
• Colored material for bandage tails (optional)
• Sewing machine
• Aluminium foil
• Oven

Steps to Make Dressings:
1. Wash and dry the fabric thoroughly.
2. **Cut the...
Materials Needed:
• White material (100% cotton is most absorbent)
• Colored material for bandage tails (optional)
• Sewing machine
• Aluminium foil
• Oven

Steps to Make Dressings:
1. Wash and dry the fabric thoroughly.
2. Cut the fabric squares according to the size of dressing you need:
- For a 2”x 2” dressing: cut a 6”x 6” square.
- For a 3”x 3” dressing: cut a 9”x 9” square.
- For a 4”x 4” dressing: cut a 12”x 12” square.
- For a 6”x 6” dressing: cut an 18”x 18” square.
- For a 12”x 12” dressing: cut a 36”x 36” square.
3. Fold the fabric squares into thirds both ways to form a compact dressing:
- Fold along the lines indicated in the example provided (see picture text for visual guidance).
4. Repeat this process for all dressings you need.

Creating Combination Dressing and Bandages:
1. Cut a tail of fabric 36-48 inches long, with a width equal to the size of the dressing you are making.
2. Place the folded dressing in the middle of the tail.
3. Stitch along each edge or down the middle of the square dressing to secure it.

Making Triangle Bandages:
1. Cut a triangle 55” across the base and 36-40 inches along the sides.
2. Ensure the bandage has a narrow hem for durability (no need to sterilize this type of bandage).
homemade dressings, sterile bandages, first aid supplies, fabric bandage making, medical textiles
Debride and Suture a Wound
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure:

1. Assess the wound to determine if it is suitable for suturing, ensuring that it is not infected or contaminated.
2. Clean the wound thoroughly with sterile saline solution or clean water, removing any debris or foreign...
Procedure:

1. Assess the wound to determine if it is suitable for suturing, ensuring that it is not infected or contaminated.
2. Clean the wound thoroughly with sterile saline solution or clean water, removing any debris or foreign objects.
3. Debride dead tissue using sterilized scissors or a scalpel, taking care to avoid healthy tissue.
4. Apply an antiseptic such as iodine or hydrogen peroxide to prevent infection.
5. Use sterile surgical needles and sutures (e.g., nylon or silk) to close the wound carefully, ensuring that the edges are aligned properly.
6. Tie off the suture ends securely, avoiding excessive tension on the skin.
7. Cover the sutured area with a sterile dressing and secure it loosely with adhesive tape or gauze.
8. Monitor for signs of infection, including increased pain, redness, swelling, or pus.
suture wound, debride wound, wound closure
Evaluate and Decide on Wound Suturing in Austere Environments
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure for Evaluating and Deciding on Wound Closure in Austere Settings

1. Assess the wound: Determine whether suturing is necessary or if the wound can heal without intervention.
2. Consider the location of the wound: Some wounds...
Procedure for Evaluating and Deciding on Wound Closure in Austere Settings

1. Assess the wound: Determine whether suturing is necessary or if the wound can heal without intervention.
2. Consider the location of the wound: Some wounds (e.g., on the face) may benefit from suturing for cosmetic reasons, while others may heal well on their own.
3. Weigh the risks and benefits of suturing:
- Benefits: Faster healing, reduced risk of infection, better cosmetic outcome.
- Risks: Poor healing, infection, adverse reactions to suture material.
4. Use appropriate materials if available:
- Ideal suture materials include surgical-grade sutures and needles.
- In a pinch, fishing line or sewing thread may be used but are not ideal for long-term use.
5. Avoid using non-sterile or inappropriate materials that could increase the risk of infection or poor healing.
6. If possible, refer to detailed suture technique guides: Several books listed in the reference section provide comprehensive instruction on suturing techniques.
7. Understand the properties of suture materials: Consult manufacturer websites (e.g., Ethicon) for information on available suture types and their appropriate uses.

### Notes:
• Many wounds can heal without suturing, especially in austere environments where resources are limited.
• Suturing is primarily done to speed up healing or improve cosmetic outcomes, not always necessary for survival.
• Always prioritize infection control and proper wound care over cosmetic considerations.
wound closure, suturing techniques, suture materials, fishing line, wound care, first aid, emergency medicine
First Aid for Wounds Without Severe Bleeding
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Use the Red Cross Outfit: Follow the instructions provided in the slip contained within the Red Cross Outfit.
2. Apply pressure with a bandage: Use a bandage to apply firm pressure to the wound to stop ordinary...
Procedure:

1. Use the Red Cross Outfit: Follow the instructions provided in the slip contained within the Red Cross Outfit.
2. Apply pressure with a bandage: Use a bandage to apply firm pressure to the wound to stop ordinary bleeding.
3. Secure the bandage: Ensure the bandage is firmly bound into place to maintain pressure and control bleeding.

Important Notes:
• These procedures are suitable for wounds that do not involve severe bleeding.
• The Red Cross Outfit should be used as described in its accompanying instructions.
first aid for wounds, red cross outfit, pressure bandage, minor bleeding treatment
Hydration Management for Open Wounds
Standard Source: USMC Summer Survival Course Handbook
Procedure for managing hydration in open wounds:

• Increase water intake significantly when dealing with an open wound to maintain fluid balance and support healing.
• Monitor for signs of dehydration (e.g., dry mouth, dizziness) and...
Procedure for managing hydration in open wounds:

• Increase water intake significantly when dealing with an open wound to maintain fluid balance and support healing.
• Monitor for signs of dehydration (e.g., dry mouth, dizziness) and replenish fluids as needed.
• Avoid consuming contaminated or untreated water sources to prevent further infection.
hydration, open wound, fluid balance
Include Moleskin in Your Bug Out Bag for Blister Care
Standard Source: The Ultimate Survival Kit Checklist
Objective: Prevent and treat blisters during long-term travel or evacuation scenarios.

Steps:
1. Add Moleskin to your bug out bag.

Details:
• Blisters are almost guaranteed during a bug out situation as you’ll likely be walking for...
Objective: Prevent and treat blisters during long-term travel or evacuation scenarios.

Steps:
1. Add Moleskin to your bug out bag.

Details:
• Blisters are almost guaranteed during a bug out situation as you’ll likely be walking for extended periods. They can slow you down, lead to infection, or even immobilize you if they go untreated.
• Moleskin is essentially an extra layer of skin you apply to the area surrounding a blister to keep it from rubbing and alleviate pain.
• Make sure to read up about blister care as part of your prep.
moleskin, blister care, bug out bag first aid

+ 37 more procedures. Get the full library in the app.

Injury Management (36) -

Explain What to Do for Snake Bite
Critical Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Keep the injured person calm and still to slow the spread of venom.
2. Apply a pressure bandage above the bite, but not too tightly, to restrict blood flow without cutting off circulation.
3. Immobilize the affected limb using a...
Procedure:

1. Keep the injured person calm and still to slow the spread of venom.
2. Apply a pressure bandage above the bite, but not too tightly, to restrict blood flow without cutting off circulation.
3. Immobilize the affected limb using a splint or by keeping it in a neutral position.
4. Do not attempt to suck out the venom or apply ice directly to the wound.
5. Seek immediate medical assistance.

Details:
• This is a life-threatening situation that requires prompt action.
• Avoid moving the injured person unnecessarily.
snake bite,venom management,pressure bandage,medical emergency
Check for Fractures in a Casualty
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Check for the following signs and symptoms of a back or neck injury:
a. Pain or tenderness of the back or neck area.
b. Cuts or bruises on the back or neck area.
c. Inability of a casualty to move or decreased sensation...
Procedure:

1. Check for the following signs and symptoms of a back or neck injury:
a. Pain or tenderness of the back or neck area.
b. Cuts or bruises on the back or neck area.
c. Inability of a casualty to move or decreased sensation to extremities (paralysis or numbness).
d. Ask about ability to move (paralysis).
e. Touch the casualty’s arms and legs and ask whether he can feel your hand (numbness).
f. Unusual body or limb position.
2. If a back or neck injury is suspected:
- Immobilize the casualty by doing the following:
a. Tell the casualty not to move.
b. If a back injury is suspected, place padding (rolled or folded to conform to the shape of the arch) under the natural arch of the casualty’s back. For example, a blanket/poncho may be used as padding.
fracture check, back injury, neck injury, immobilization
Managing Impalement Injuries
Critical Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Impalement injuries involve an object protruding from a wound and should be left in place. Removing an impaled object may cause further damage and therefore should be done in a suitably equipped hospital. Large objects, such as arrows or fence...
Impalement injuries involve an object protruding from a wound and should be left in place. Removing an impaled object may cause further damage and therefore should be done in a suitably equipped hospital. Large objects, such as arrows or fence posts, may need to be stabilised and carefully cut to allow evacuation. Pain relief will be required.
impalement,injury_stabilisation,evacuation,pain_relief,hospital_treatment
Treat an Open Fracture to Prevent Infection
Critical Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Procedure:

1. Clean the wound thoroughly to remove grit and foreign material.
2. Cover the wound with sterile dressings.
3. Administer antibiotics such as Co-amoxiclav (Augmentin) or erythromycin to prevent infection.
4. Arrange...
Procedure:

1. Clean the wound thoroughly to remove grit and foreign material.
2. Cover the wound with sterile dressings.
3. Administer antibiotics such as Co-amoxiclav (Augmentin) or erythromycin to prevent infection.
4. Arrange for urgent evacuation, as open fractures are at high risk of developing osteomyelitis (infection involving the bone), which can lead to crippling deformity or even amputation.
5. Treat the wound as a contaminated soft-tissue injury due to the breach in skin.
open fracture treatment, infection prevention, wound care
Administering First Aid to the Injured
High Priority Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Assess the situation:
- Ensure the area is safe for both the injured person and yourself.
- Check if the injured person is conscious and breathing.
- If there is a life-threatening emergency, call for help immediately...
Procedure:

1. Assess the situation:
- Ensure the area is safe for both the injured person and yourself.
- Check if the injured person is conscious and breathing.
- If there is a life-threatening emergency, call for help immediately (e.g., 911 or local emergency services).

2. Provide immediate care:
- If the person is not breathing, begin CPR if trained.
- Control any bleeding by applying direct pressure with a clean cloth or bandage.
- Immobilize any suspected fractures or spinal injuries using splints or available materials.
- Apply cold packs to reduce swelling in case of bruising or inflammation.

3. Monitor the injured person:
- Keep them warm and comfortable until professional help arrives.
- Reassure them and avoid moving them unnecessarily unless there is an immediate danger (e.g., fire, falling debris).

4. Document the incident:
- Note the time of injury, symptoms observed, and any first aid administered.
- This information can be useful for medical professionals upon arrival.

Warnings:
• Do not move a person with suspected spinal injuries unless absolutely necessary.
• Avoid giving food or drink to an unconscious person.
• If in doubt about the severity of the injury, seek professional medical help immediately.
first aid, injury management, emergency care, bleeding control, CPR, spinal injury
Aiding the Injured After a Disaster
High Priority Source: FEMA Are You Ready
### Aiding the Injured After a Disaster

Steps to take when aiding an injured person after a disaster:

1. Check for injuries. Do not attempt to move seriously injured persons unless they are in immediate danger of death or further...
### Aiding the Injured After a Disaster

Steps to take when aiding an injured person after a disaster:

1. Check for injuries. Do not attempt to move seriously injured persons unless they are in immediate danger of death or further injury.
2. If you must move an unconscious person, first stabilize the neck and back, then call for help immediately.
3. If the victim is not breathing:
- Carefully position the victim for artificial respiration.
- Clear the airway.
- Commence mouth-to-mouth resuscitation.
4. Maintain body temperature with blankets. Be sure the victim does not become overheated.
5. Never try to feed liquids to an unconscious person.

### Additional Considerations:
• Monitor family health and well-being after a disaster.
• Ensure that any injured individual receives prompt medical attention if possible.
injured person, artificial respiration, body temperature, unconscious person, first aid, disaster response
Apply Limb Splints to Reduce Bleeding from Fractures and Soft Tissue Injury
High Priority Source: Emergency War Surgery
Procedure:

1. Apply limb splints to reduce bleeding associated with fractures and soft tissue injury.
2. Splints align, stabilize, and return the limb to its natural length, which helps control hemorrhage.
3. Use rigid materials such as wood or...
Procedure:

1. Apply limb splints to reduce bleeding associated with fractures and soft tissue injury.
2. Splints align, stabilize, and return the limb to its natural length, which helps control hemorrhage.
3. Use rigid materials such as wood or metal for effective stabilization.
4. Secure the splint with bandages or straps, ensuring it is not too tight to avoid compromising circulation.
limb splints, fracture management, hemorrhage control
Check for Circulation Problems Below a Fracture Site
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure:

1. Check light-skinned persons for color of skin (skin may be pale, white, or a bluish-gray color).
2. Check dark-skinned persons by depressing the toenail or fingernail beds and observing how fast the color returns.
3. A...
Procedure:

1. Check light-skinned persons for color of skin (skin may be pale, white, or a bluish-gray color).
2. Check dark-skinned persons by depressing the toenail or fingernail beds and observing how fast the color returns.
3. A slower return of color to the injured side indicates a circulation problem.
4. Feel the injured arm or leg to see if it is colder than the uninjured one.
5. Ask the casualty about the presence of numbness, tightness, or a cold sensation.
circulation check, fracture management, first aid assessment
Definitive Care Phase in Advanced Trauma Life Support (ATLS)
High Priority Source: NATO Emergency War Surgery
Procedure:

1. Begin the definitive care phase after life-threatening conditions have been managed and resuscitation has stabilized the patient.
2. Address less life-threatening injuries during this phase, including:
- Setting of fractures...
Procedure:

1. Begin the definitive care phase after life-threatening conditions have been managed and resuscitation has stabilized the patient.
2. Address less life-threatening injuries during this phase, including:
- Setting of fractures using appropriate immobilization techniques.
- Stabilizing minor injuries with splints or bandages.
- Performing wound debridement to remove non-viable tissue and prevent infection.
3. Prepare the patient for transfer to a higher level of care if necessary, ensuring all interventions are documented and communicated effectively.
definitive care phase, fracture management, wound debridement, patient transfer
Immobilize a Fracture Using Available Materials
High Priority Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Procedure:

1. Immobolise the fractured limb to prevent further damage and reduce pain.
2. Use available materials to improvise splints:
- Karrimat
- Sleeping bags
- Inflatable splints
- Trekking poles
- Skis
- Triangular...
Procedure:

1. Immobolise the fractured limb to prevent further damage and reduce pain.
2. Use available materials to improvise splints:
- Karrimat
- Sleeping bags
- Inflatable splints
- Trekking poles
- Skis
- Triangular bandages
- Canoe paddles
- Purpose-built splints (e.g., Frakstraps)
3. Pad bony prominences to protect the skin.
4. Immobilise joints above and below the fracture site to ensure proper alignment.
5. If necessary, straighten the limb (reduction) to apply a splint or relieve pressure on blood vessels.
6. Straightening is painful but rarely causes additional damage.
7. Reduction requires strong traction/counter-traction in the long axis of the limb and should be done soon after injury before severe muscle spasm occurs.
splinting fractures, immobilize limb, field splints
Manage Bleeding from a Fracture
High Priority Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Procedure:

1. Assess the severity of bleeding associated with the fracture.
2. Be aware that all fractures can cause bleeding, which may lead to shock or even death, particularly in thigh or pelvis fractures.
3. Anticipate shock...
Procedure:

1. Assess the severity of bleeding associated with the fracture.
2. Be aware that all fractures can cause bleeding, which may lead to shock or even death, particularly in thigh or pelvis fractures.
3. Anticipate shock and treat it appropriately by ensuring the casualty is kept warm, lying down, and receiving fluids if possible.
4. Monitor for signs of shock (e.g., pale skin, rapid pulse, confusion) and provide immediate care.
fracture bleeding, shock management, blood loss
Managing Crush Injuries
High Priority Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Crush injuries may cause large amounts of tissue damage and carry a high potential for infection. The crushed part should be carefully cleaned and then elevated. Swelling in the affected part may cut off the blood supply to the limb beyond the...
Crush injuries may cause large amounts of tissue damage and carry a high potential for infection. The crushed part should be carefully cleaned and then elevated. Swelling in the affected part may cut off the blood supply to the limb beyond the injury. If the injury is severe there may be a risk of losing the limb and it is important to evacuate the casualty for medical assessment.
crush_injuries,tissue_damage,infection_prevention,elevation,evacuation
Recognize and Manage Common Injuries in Mountain Environments
High Priority Source: FM 3 97 61 Military Mountaineering
Objective: Identify and manage common injuries that occur in mountain environments.

Common Injuries:
• Sprains and strains: Often caused by uneven terrain or overexertion.
• Fractures: May result from falls or improper...
Objective: Identify and manage common injuries that occur in mountain environments.

Common Injuries:
• Sprains and strains: Often caused by uneven terrain or overexertion.
• Fractures: May result from falls or improper acclimatization.
• Abrasions and contusions: Common due to rough surfaces and exposure.
• Frostbite and hypothermia: Risks increase in cold weather conditions.
• Trench foot: A condition caused by prolonged exposure to wet, cold environments.

Management Steps:
1. Assess the injury for severity and provide immediate first aid as needed.
2. Immobilize fractures or suspected fractures using splints or available materials.
3. Apply pressure to control bleeding from abrasions or lacerations.
4. Treat frostbite by gradually warming affected areas (avoid direct heat) and seeking medical attention immediately.
5. Manage hypothermia by removing wet clothing, covering the casualty with dry blankets, and providing warm fluids if possible.
6. Monitor for signs of trench foot, such as numbness or tingling in the feet, and take preventive measures (e.g., keep feet dry).
7. Document all injuries observed and first aid administered.
sprains, strains, fractures, frostbite, hypothermia, trench foot, injury management, first aid
Remove Body Armor from an Injured Service Member
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Carefully remove body armor from the injured service member to complete the evaluation or administer first aid:

1. Begin by removing the outermost hard or soft body armor components.
2. **Open, unfasten, or cut closures, fasteners, or...
Carefully remove body armor from the injured service member to complete the evaluation or administer first aid:

1. Begin by removing the outermost hard or soft body armor components.
2. Open, unfasten, or cut closures, fasteners, or straps as needed.
3. Continue removing any successive layers in the same manner.
4. Be sure to follow other notes, cautions, and warnings regarding procedures in:
- Contaminated situations
- When a broken back or neck is suspected
5. Continue to evaluate the casualty after removal of body armor.
body armor removal, injured service member, contaminated situations, broken back, neck injury
Transport a Casualty with a Fracture Safely
High Priority Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Procedure:

1. Immobilise the fracture and address any other injuries before evacuation.
2. Consider the possibility of spinal injury, especially if there is an injury above the collarbone level.
3. Casualties with fractures in the...
Procedure:

1. Immobilise the fracture and address any other injuries before evacuation.
2. Consider the possibility of spinal injury, especially if there is an injury above the collarbone level.
3. Casualties with fractures in the upper limbs or ribs may be able to walk, but must be monitored closely.
4. Casualties with head, back, neck, or lower limb injuries must be carried by stretcher to avoid further harm.
5. Ensure that movement, circulation, and sensation are checked before and after any manipulation of the injured limb.
transport fracture casualty, evacuation procedures, injury transport
Carry a Person Down a Ladder
High Priority Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Ensure the ladder is stable and secure.
2. Position yourself at the bottom of the ladder with your arms extended to support the person being carried.
3. Carefully lift the person, keeping their body close to yours for balance.
4....
Procedure:

1. Ensure the ladder is stable and secure.
2. Position yourself at the bottom of the ladder with your arms extended to support the person being carried.
3. Carefully lift the person, keeping their body close to yours for balance.
4. Descend the ladder slowly and steadily, maintaining control throughout the process.
5. Ensure that the person is safely placed on the ground at the bottom of the ladder.

Details:
• This procedure requires strength and coordination.
• Always check the stability of the ladder before beginning.
carrying,ladder,emergency transport,injury management
Diagnose a Fracture in the Field
High Priority Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Procedure:

1. Look for signs that may indicate a fracture:
- Pain and tenderness at the injury site.
- Swelling, bruising, or discoloration around the area.
- Deformity of the limb or affected body part.
- **Grating...
Procedure:

1. Look for signs that may indicate a fracture:
- Pain and tenderness at the injury site.
- Swelling, bruising, or discoloration around the area.
- Deformity of the limb or affected body part.
- Grating (crepitus), which is a characteristic sound or feeling when bones rub together. This usually confirms a fracture.

2. Note that similar symptoms can occur with sprains and soft-tissue injuries.
3. If there is loss of limb function, this strongly suggests a fracture.
4. In an expedition setting where X-ray facilities are not available, treat the injury as a fracture if uncertain.
5. Evacuation may be delayed until further assessment can be made.
diagnose fracture, field diagnosis, injury signs
Elevate Lower Extremities to Reduce Swelling and Bleeding
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Steps to Elevate Lower Extremities:

1. Assess the injury: Check for swelling, bruising, or bleeding in the lower extremities.
2. Position the casualty: Lay the casualty down on a flat surface if possible.
3. **Elevate the affected...
Steps to Elevate Lower Extremities:

1. Assess the injury: Check for swelling, bruising, or bleeding in the lower extremities.
2. Position the casualty: Lay the casualty down on a flat surface if possible.
3. Elevate the affected limb: Raise the injured leg above the level of the heart to reduce blood flow and minimize swelling.
4. Support the limb: Use pillows or clothing to support the elevated limb and prevent it from slipping.
5. Monitor for signs of shock: Watch for symptoms such as pale skin, rapid pulse, and confusion.

Important Notes:
• Elevation should not be used if there is a suspected fracture or dislocation.
• If the casualty is in pain or discomfort, adjust the elevation to a more comfortable position.
• Continue monitoring until professional medical help arrives.
elevate legs,reduce swelling,first aid,injury management
Gather Splinting Materials for a Suspected Fracture
High Priority Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure to gather splinting materials:

1. If standard splinting materials (splints, padding, and cravats) are available, use them.
2. If standard materials are not available, gather improvised materials such as sticks, boards, or...
Procedure to gather splinting materials:

1. If standard splinting materials (splints, padding, and cravats) are available, use them.
2. If standard materials are not available, gather improvised materials such as sticks, boards, or other rigid objects that can be used to immobilize the injured extremity.
3. In cases where splinting material is completely unavailable and the suspected fracture cannot be splinted, use swathes or a combination of swathes and slings to immobilize the extremity as best as possible.
splinting materials, first aid, improvised splints, injury management
Immobilize an Injured Area if It Hurts
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure:

1. If a body part is painful or swollen, avoid moving it unnecessarily.
2. Immobilize the injured area using a splint, sling, or any rigid object available to prevent further injury.
3. Apply padding around the splint to protect the...
Procedure:

1. If a body part is painful or swollen, avoid moving it unnecessarily.
2. Immobilize the injured area using a splint, sling, or any rigid object available to prevent further injury.
3. Apply padding around the splint to protect the skin and reduce discomfort.
4. Secure the splint in place with bandages or tape, ensuring it is not too tight.

Note: Immobilization helps prevent additional damage and promotes healing.
immobilize injury, pain management
Manage Bone and Joint Injuries (Fractures, Dislocations, Sprains)
High Priority Source: Medical Emergencies
Identify the Injury:
You could face bone and joint injuries that include fractures, dislocations, and sprains.

Immediate Actions for Injuries:
• Immobilize the affected area to prevent further damage.
• Apply ice or cold compresses to...
Identify the Injury:
You could face bone and joint injuries that include fractures, dislocations, and sprains.

Immediate Actions for Injuries:
• Immobilize the affected area to prevent further damage.
• Apply ice or cold compresses to reduce swelling and pain.
• Avoid moving the injured limb unless absolutely necessary.
• If a fracture is suspected, do not attempt to realign the bone yourself.
• Use splints or improvised materials (e.g., sticks, boards) to immobilize the affected area.
• Elevate the injured part above heart level if possible and safe.
• Monitor for signs of shock and treat accordingly if necessary.
• Seek professional medical help as soon as possible.
bone injury,joint injury,fracture management,splinting,immobilization,pain relief
Managing an Injured Person - General Directions
High Priority Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Keep cool. There is no cause for excitement or hurry.

Action Steps:
1. Do not waste time in cases of severe bleeding or poisoning.
2. If possible, always send for a doctor, unless the injury is trivial.
3. Do not wait for the doctor...
Keep cool. There is no cause for excitement or hurry.

Action Steps:
1. Do not waste time in cases of severe bleeding or poisoning.
2. If possible, always send for a doctor, unless the injury is trivial.
3. Do not wait for the doctor to arrive before providing care.
4. Keep crowds back and loosen tight clothing.
5. If the patient's face is pale, place him on his back with his head low.
6. If the patient's face is flushed, fold your coat and put it under his head to raise it slightly.
7. In case of vomiting, place the patient on his side.
8. Do not give an unconscious person a stimulant, as it may cause choking.
9. If the injury is covered by clothing, remove it by cutting or tearing, but only remove what is necessary to avoid making the patient feel cold.
10. In severe injuries about the feet, cut shoes and boots.
injured person, first aid, general directions, trauma care
Pad Splints for a Suspected Fracture
High Priority Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure to pad splints:

1. Pad the splints where they will touch any bony part of the body, such as the elbow, wrist, knee, ankle, crotch, or armpit areas.
2. Padding is essential to prevent excessive pressure on these areas, which could...
Procedure to pad splints:

1. Pad the splints where they will touch any bony part of the body, such as the elbow, wrist, knee, ankle, crotch, or armpit areas.
2. Padding is essential to prevent excessive pressure on these areas, which could lead to circulation problems.
3. Use available padding materials (e.g., cloth, bandages) to ensure comfort and avoid further injury.
splint padding, first aid, injury management, pressure prevention
Recognize and Manage Fractures
High Priority Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Fracture is a broken bone. To manage fractures:

1. Immobilize the affected area using a splint or sling to prevent further injury.
2. Apply ice to reduce swelling and pain.
3. Elevate the injured limb above heart level if possible.
4. Seek...
Fracture is a broken bone. To manage fractures:

1. Immobilize the affected area using a splint or sling to prevent further injury.
2. Apply ice to reduce swelling and pain.
3. Elevate the injured limb above heart level if possible.
4. Seek medical attention as soon as possible for proper diagnosis and treatment.
5. Avoid moving the broken bone unnecessarily to prevent additional damage.

Note: Fractures can be painful and may require surgical intervention depending on their severity.
fracture, bone injury, first aid
Administer Pain Relief for Fractures
Standard Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Procedure:

1. Provide pain relief to the casualty with fractures.
2. Administer painkillers before attempting reduction of the fracture or during evacuation.
3. Effective immobilisation also helps reduce pain caused by fractures.
pain management, fracture relief, medication for injury
Apply Swathes for Fracture Immobilization
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure:

Note: Apply swathes when the casualty has a splinted, suspected fracture of the elbow or leg, or when a suspected fracture cannot be splinted. (Improvise swathes from large pieces of cloth or belts.)
swathe application, fracture immobilization, first aid technique
Assemble a SAM Splint for Limb Immobilization
Standard Source: The Ultimate Survival Kit Checklist
Purpose: To immobilize injured limbs using a SAM Splint.

Materials Needed:
• SAM Splint (C-Splint)

Procedure:
1. Assess the injury to determine if a splint is needed for proper immobilization.
2. Unfold the SAM Splint and...
Purpose: To immobilize injured limbs using a SAM Splint.

Materials Needed:
• SAM Splint (C-Splint)

Procedure:
1. Assess the injury to determine if a splint is needed for proper immobilization.
2. Unfold the SAM Splint and position it along the injured limb, ensuring that it covers the area above and below the injury.
3. Secure the splint using straps or bandages, making sure it is snug but not too tight to avoid restricting blood flow.
4. Check for circulation, sensation, and movement in the affected limb periodically to ensure the splint is not causing additional harm.
5. Monitor the injury site for signs of worsening pain, swelling, or numbness, which may indicate improper immobilization.

Notes:
• SAM Splints are lightweight and easy to carry, making them ideal for emergency situations.
• Ensure that the splint is properly aligned with the limb's natural position to avoid further injury.
SAM Splint, immobilize limbs, first aid, injury management
Obtain Splinting Materials for Fracture Immobilization
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure:

1. Get splints (wooden boards, tree branches, poles, an unloaded rifle) long enough to reach beyond the joints above and below the broken part.
2. Get materials to pad the splints, such as a jacket, blanket, poncho, shelter...
Procedure:

1. Get splints (wooden boards, tree branches, poles, an unloaded rifle) long enough to reach beyond the joints above and below the broken part.
2. Get materials to pad the splints, such as a jacket, blanket, poncho, shelter half, or leafy vegetation.
3. Get tie materials, such as strips of cloth or belts, to secure the splints.

Note: If splinting materials are not available, use the chest wall to immobilize a suspected fracture of the arm and the uninjured leg to immobilize the fractured leg. Continue with steps 7 and 8.
splinting materials, fracture immobilization, first aid supplies
Pad Splints for Fracture Immobilization
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure:

1. Apply padding between the splint and the bony areas of the body.
2. Suggested sites for padding: wrist, elbow, ankle, knee, crotch, and armpit.

Note: If using a universal or “SAM” splint (coated with synthetic padding),...
Procedure:

1. Apply padding between the splint and the bony areas of the body.
2. Suggested sites for padding: wrist, elbow, ankle, knee, crotch, and armpit.

Note: If using a universal or “SAM” splint (coated with synthetic padding), additional padding is not required.
splint padding, fracture immobilization, first aid supplies
Apply a Triangular Bandage to Form a Sling
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Use a triangular bandage and fold it into a sling.
2. Place the sling under the injured arm, ensuring that the arm is supported properly.
3. Tie the ends of the sling at the side (or hollow) of the neck on the uninjured side.
4....
Procedure:

1. Use a triangular bandage and fold it into a sling.
2. Place the sling under the injured arm, ensuring that the arm is supported properly.
3. Tie the ends of the sling at the side (or hollow) of the neck on the uninjured side.
4. Twist and tuck the corner of the sling at the elbow to secure it properly.

Details:
• The triangular bandage should be folded in a way that allows for proper support of the injured arm.
• Ensure that the sling is snug but not too tight, allowing for proper circulation.
• If possible, use padding along the splint where it touches bony parts of the body for comfort and protection.

Materials Needed:
• A triangular bandage

Important Notes:
• Ensure that the sling is not too tight, as this may impair circulation.
• Monitor for signs of impaired circulation (numbness, tingling, or coldness in the extremity).
• This procedure is intended for temporary immobilization until professional medical assistance can be provided.
triangular bandage, sling, first aid, upper extremity fracture, immobilization
Classify Fractures Based on Type
Standard Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Procedure:

1. Identify the type of fracture based on the following classifications:
- Simple fractures: A single, clean break in the bone.
- Comminuted fractures: The bone is broken into more than two fragments.
- **Open or...
Procedure:

1. Identify the type of fracture based on the following classifications:
- Simple fractures: A single, clean break in the bone.
- Comminuted fractures: The bone is broken into more than two fragments.
- Open or closed fractures: Open if the skin is breached; closed if it is not.
- Complicated fractures: If other tissues (e.g., nerves, blood vessels) are involved.
fracture classification, types of fractures, bone injuries
Immobilizing and Preparing a Casualty with a Suspected Neck Fracture
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Objective: Immobilize a casualty with a suspected neck fracture to prevent further injury and safely prepare them for transportation.

Steps:
1. Do not move the casualty unless absolutely necessary, as movement may cause permanent injury...
Objective: Immobilize a casualty with a suspected neck fracture to prevent further injury and safely prepare them for transportation.

Steps:
1. Do not move the casualty unless absolutely necessary, as movement may cause permanent injury or death.
2. Leave the casualty in the position they were found in.
3. If the casualty's head and neck are in an abnormal position:
- Immobilize the neck and head immediately.
4. If the casualty is lying face up:
- Raise their shoulders slightly.
- Place a roll of cloth (with the bulk of a bath towel) under their neck, ensuring it only slightly arches their neck and leaves the back of their head on the ground.
- DO NOT bend their neck or head forward.
- DO NOT raise or twist their head.
5. To immobilize the head:
- Use heavy objects (such as rocks) or fill boots with dirt, sand, gravel, or rock.
- Pad these items and place them on each side of the casualty's head to prevent movement.
6. If the casualty is lying face down:
- DO NOT move them.
7. At least two people are required to move the casualty, as the head and trunk must be moved in unison.
8. Ensure both individuals work in close coordination to avoid bending the neck.
9. Place a wide board lengthwise beside the casualty, ensuring it extends at least 4 inches beyond their head and feet.
10. If the casualty is lying face up:
- One person steadies the casualty's head and neck between their hands.
- The second person positions one foot and knee against the board to prevent slipping.
- They then gently slide the casualty onto the board by grasping them under the shoulder and hip.
11. If the casualty is lying face down:
- One person steadies the head and neck between their hands.
- The second person gently rolls the casualty over onto the board.
12. Continue to steady the casualty's head and neck during movement.
neck fracture, immobilization, first aid, casualty preparation, transportation
Managing Bruises
Reference Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Bruises (contusions) are usually caused by a direct blow to the skin surface. Bleeding under the skin gives the bruise its characteristic appearance. Rest, ice, compression and elevation (RICE) all help to reduce swelling and pain. Compression...
Bruises (contusions) are usually caused by a direct blow to the skin surface. Bleeding under the skin gives the bruise its characteristic appearance. Rest, ice, compression and elevation (RICE) all help to reduce swelling and pain. Compression may be achieved by applying a crepe bandage firmly around the affected area. Anti-inflammatory drugs such as ibuprofen or aspirin may also help. After a day or two the affected part should be mobilised to reduce stiffness. A subungual haematoma (a blood blister beneath the finger nail) can be easily treated by melting a hole through the nail using an opened paper clip heated to red heat in a flame. This is surprisingly painless and gives immediate relief.
bruises,RICE_method,compression,anti-inflammatory_drugs,subungual_haematoma
Remove Jewelry from Injured Limb
Reference Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure:

1. Remove all jewelry from the affected limb.
2. Place the removed jewelry in the casualty’s pocket.
3. Inform the casualty that this is being done to prevent further injury if swelling occurs later.
jewelry removal, injury prevention, swelling management
Understanding and Managing Swelling
Reference Source: Where There is No Dentist
Swelling refers to an area of the skin that is abnormally large or puffed up. To manage swelling:

1. Apply a cold compress to the swollen area for 10-15 minutes at a time.
2. Elevate the affected area above the level of the heart if...
Swelling refers to an area of the skin that is abnormally large or puffed up. To manage swelling:

1. Apply a cold compress to the swollen area for 10-15 minutes at a time.
2. Elevate the affected area above the level of the heart if possible.
3. Avoid pressing on or irritating the swollen area.
4. Monitor for signs of infection, such as increased redness, warmth, or pus.
5. If swelling is severe or accompanied by pain, seek medical attention.

Note: Swelling can be a sign of injury, infection, or other underlying conditions.
swelling,cold compress,elevation,infection signs
Manage a Sucking Chest Wound
Reference Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts + The Ultimate Survival Kit Checklist
If there is a sucking chest wound

• You must cut or remove the casualty’s clothing to expose the wound.

• Use an occlusive dressing or a chest seal (e.g., North American Rescue Hyfin Chest Seal) over the wound to close off the opening. The...
If there is a sucking chest wound

• You must cut or remove the casualty’s clothing to expose the wound.

• Use an occlusive dressing or a chest seal (e.g., North American Rescue Hyfin Chest Seal) over the wound to close off the opening. The dressing/seal should be:
- large enough not to be sucked into the chest cavity;
- with three sides sealed to the skin, the fourth being left open to allow air to escape (if using an occlusive dressing);
- applied over both entry and exit wounds if using a chest seal.

• Assess the injury – Look for signs of a sucking chest wound: visible entry and exit wounds, with air being sucked in during inhalation and expelled during exhalation.

• If possible, clean the area around the wound to remove debris before applying the dressing or seal.

• If the breathing worsens after applying the dressing/seal, quickly remove it and then replace it properly.

• Monitor the patient – Watch for improvement in breathing and seek immediate medical help.

• Warnings:
- Do not apply the seal if there is no visible wound or if the patient is conscious and can breathe on their own.
- Ensure the seal is applied correctly to prevent further air leakage.
sucking chest wound, chest seal, first aid, emergency care, occlusive dressing

Airway Management (36) -

Airway Management in Trauma Patients
Critical Source: Emergency War Surgery
Manage Airway in Trauma Patients:

1. Initial Assessment:
- Check for airway patency, breathing, and circulation (ABCs).
- Look for signs of airway obstruction such as stridor, cyanosis, or altered mental status.
2. **Techniques:
-...
Manage Airway in Trauma Patients:

1. Initial Assessment:
- Check for airway patency, breathing, and circulation (ABCs).
- Look for signs of airway obstruction such as stridor, cyanosis, or altered mental status.
2. **Techniques:
- Chin-Lift/Head-Tilt: Used to open the airway in unconscious patients without suspected cervical spine injury.
- Two-Handed Jaw Thrust: Preferred for patients with potential cervical spine injury.
3. **Intubation:
- Use endotracheal intubation if airway is compromised or patient is not breathing adequately.
- Ensure proper placement using capnography and auscultation.
4. **Alternative Airway Devices:
- Oropharyngeal/Nasopharyngeal Airway: Used for unconscious patients without suspected cervical spine injury.
- Laryngeal Mask Airway (LMA): Can be used as a temporary airway device in difficult intubation scenarios.
5. **Cricothyrotomy:
- Indicated when standard intubation is impossible and patient is not breathing.
- Use a scalpel to create an opening below the cricoid cartilage, then insert a tube.
6. Post-Intubation Care:
- Secure the airway with tape or a specialized device.
- Monitor for complications such as pneumothorax or esophageal intubation.
airway management,trauma intubation,cricothyrotomy,laryngeal mask airway
Assessing and Managing Airway Obstruction
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
AIMS OF THE FIRST AIDER: On the spot, while protecting the cervical spine when necessary, you must:

• Identify airway obstruction
• Clear the obstruction rapidly
• Maintain the airway open
• Identify the airway at risk and be prepared for...
AIMS OF THE FIRST AIDER: On the spot, while protecting the cervical spine when necessary, you must:

• Identify airway obstruction
• Clear the obstruction rapidly
• Maintain the airway open
• Identify the airway at risk and be prepared for immediate action
• Assist the conscious casualty in self-management of the airway

EXAMINATION:
• If the casualty replies normally and coherently to questions, the airway is patent.
• A free airway makes no obvious sound and requires no obvious effort for air intake.
• Noisy breathing and efforts to take in air mean an obstructed airway.
• Total silence and total absence of effort indicate apnoea (breathing has stopped).

Look:
• Incident type, situation, and possible mechanism of injury
• Signs of unconsciousness and respiratory distress
• Head, face, and neck injuries
• Airway self-management by the conscious casualty (e.g., sitting with face down)

Listen:
• Abnormal sounds (repetitive coughs, snoring, gurgling, hoarseness) are associated with partial airway obstruction. That also means the casualty is breathing.
• Casualty complains of difficult swallowing.

Talk:
• Any inappropriate or incomprehensible response suggests a threat to the airway due to deteriorating level of consciousness.
• Absence of verbal or non-verbal response indicates unconsciousness.
airway management, airway obstruction, first aid techniques, breathing assessment
Check for and Remove Obstruction During Chest Compressions
Critical Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure: Look in the mouth for the object between compressions and breaths and if you can see it, remove it.
obstruction removal, airway clearance, first aid
Demonstrate a Surgical Airway (Cricothyroidotomy)
Critical Source: TCCC 17 13 Army Tactical Combat Casualty Care
Procedure:

1. Ensure the casualty is unconscious and not breathing.
2. Identify the cricothyroid membrane by palpating the larynx.
3. Make an incision through the cricothyroid membrane using a scalpel or other sharp instrument.
4. Insert a tube...
Procedure:

1. Ensure the casualty is unconscious and not breathing.
2. Identify the cricothyroid membrane by palpating the larynx.
3. Make an incision through the cricothyroid membrane using a scalpel or other sharp instrument.
4. Insert a tube into the airway to establish a patent passage for ventilation.
5. Secure the tube in place and monitor for signs of complications.
surgical airway, cricothyroidotomy
Demonstrate the Chin Lift/Jaw Thrust Maneuver on a Casualty
Critical Source: TCCC 17 13 Tactical Combat Casualty Care
Purpose: To open the airway of an unconscious casualty in a tactical combat casualty care (TCCC) setting.

Procedure:
1. Place one hand on the casualty's forehead and gently tilt the head back to open the airway.
2. Use the other hand to...
Purpose: To open the airway of an unconscious casualty in a tactical combat casualty care (TCCC) setting.

Procedure:
1. Place one hand on the casualty's forehead and gently tilt the head back to open the airway.
2. Use the other hand to lift the chin upward, ensuring that the jaw is thrust forward slightly to prevent the tongue from blocking the airway.
3. Avoid placing any pressure on the neck or causing additional injury.
4. Monitor for signs of breathing and ensure that the airway remains open.
chin lift, jaw thrust, airway management, TCCC
Demonstrate the Sit Up/Lean Forward Airway Position on a Casualty
Critical Source: TCCC 17 13 Tactical Combat Casualty Care
Purpose: To maintain an open airway in a casualty who is conscious and breathing but may be at risk of losing consciousness.

Procedure:
1. Assist the casualty to sit up slightly, leaning them forward with their head resting on your shoulder...
Purpose: To maintain an open airway in a casualty who is conscious and breathing but may be at risk of losing consciousness.

Procedure:
1. Assist the casualty to sit up slightly, leaning them forward with their head resting on your shoulder or lap.
2. Ensure that the casualty's chin is lifted and their jaw is thrust forward to keep the airway open.
3. Monitor for signs of breathing and ensure that the airway remains open.
4. If the casualty becomes unconscious, place them in the recovery position immediately.
sit up/lean forward, airway management, TCCC
Open the Airway and Restore Breathing
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Check for responsiveness:
- Gently shake the casualty and ask, “Are you OK?”
2. Call for help immediately.
3. Position the unconscious casualty so that he is lying on his back on a firm surface.

Note: Attend to...
Procedure:

1. Check for responsiveness:
- Gently shake the casualty and ask, “Are you OK?”
2. Call for help immediately.
3. Position the unconscious casualty so that he is lying on his back on a firm surface.

Note: Attend to any airway, breathing, or bleeding problems immediately, as these may become life threatening.
open airway, restore breathing, first aid, CPR
Opening the Airway of an Unconscious or Not Breathing Casualty
Critical Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

1. Call for help immediately. Ensure that assistance is on the way.
2. Position the casualty onto his back. Move (roll) the casualty onto his back if he is not already in this position.
3. Perform a finger sweep. If...
Procedure:

1. Call for help immediately. Ensure that assistance is on the way.
2. Position the casualty onto his back. Move (roll) the casualty onto his back if he is not already in this position.
3. Perform a finger sweep. If foreign material or vomitus is visible in the mouth, remove it carefully. Do not spend excessive time doing so.
4. Open the airway using either the jaw-thrust technique or the head-tilt/chin-lift technique.

Jaw-Thrust Technique (Recommended for Suspected Neck Injury):
1. Grasp the angles of the casualty’s lower jaw with both hands, one on each side.
2. Lift the jaw forward and upward, displacing it from the throat.
3. Ensure that the rescuer’s elbows rest on the surface where the casualty is lying.
4. If the lips close, retract the lower lip with the thumb.
5. If mouth-to-mouth breathing is necessary, close the nostrils by placing your cheek tightly against them.
6. Carefully support the head without tilting it backward or turning it from side to side.
7. If the jaw-thrust technique is unsuccessful, tilt the head back very slightly.

Head-Tilt/Chin-Lift Technique (Use with Caution):
1. Place one hand on the casualty’s forehead and apply firm, backward pressure with the palm to tilt the head back.
2. Place the fingertips of the other hand under the bony part of the lower jaw and lift, bringing the chin forward.
3. Do not use the thumb to lift the chin.
airway management,unconscious casualty,jaw-thrust technique,head-tilt/chin-lift
Relieve Tension Pneumothorax with Needle Decompression
Critical Source: The Ultimate Survival Kit Checklist
Objective: Relieve air buildup in the pleural space (tension pneumothorax) to prevent suffocation.

### Steps:
1. Assess the injury – Look for signs of tension pneumothorax: difficulty breathing, cyanosis, tracheal deviation, and decreased...
Objective: Relieve air buildup in the pleural space (tension pneumothorax) to prevent suffocation.

### Steps:
1. Assess the injury – Look for signs of tension pneumothorax: difficulty breathing, cyanosis, tracheal deviation, and decreased breath sounds on one side.
2. Prepare the device – Use a North American Rescue ARS for Needle Decompression (3.25" 14 Gauge).
3. Position the patient – Sit them upright if possible to ease breathing.
4. Insert the needle decompressor – Place it at the second intercostal space in the midclavicular line on the affected side, and insert until air is released.
5. Secure the device – Ensure the decompressor remains in place and does not become dislodged.
6. Monitor the patient – Watch for improvement in breathing and seek immediate medical help.

### Warnings:
• This procedure should only be performed by trained individuals or under supervision.
• Improper use can lead to further injury or complications.
needle decompression, tension pneumothorax, airway management
Use Endotracheal Tube for Airway Management
Critical Source: Personal Wilderness Medical Kit
Procedure:

1. Select the appropriate endotracheal tube size (e.g., 6.5 mm) based on the patient's age and anatomy.
2. Prepare the airway by clearing any secretions or obstructions in the mouth and throat.
3. **Insert the endotracheal...
Procedure:

1. Select the appropriate endotracheal tube size (e.g., 6.5 mm) based on the patient's age and anatomy.
2. Prepare the airway by clearing any secretions or obstructions in the mouth and throat.
3. Insert the endotracheal tube into the trachea, ensuring proper depth and securement with a one-way valve.
4. Confirm proper placement of the tube using clinical signs (e.g., chest rise, breath sounds) and capnography if available.
5. Secure the tube in place to prevent dislodgement during patient movement or transport.
endotracheal tube,airway management,one-way valve
Advanced Airway Management Techniques
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Use the following advanced airway devices and techniques:

• Mechanical suction to remove blood, vomit, debris, or foreign bodies from the airway using a foot or hand-operated or electric pump.
• Simple airway devices:
- Oropharyngeal...
Use the following advanced airway devices and techniques:

• Mechanical suction to remove blood, vomit, debris, or foreign bodies from the airway using a foot or hand-operated or electric pump.
• Simple airway devices:
- Oropharyngeal device (Guedel tube)
- Nasopharyngeal airway (used when Guedel tube cannot be used)
- Laryngeal mask airway
• Oesophageal-Tracheal Combitube: A double-lumen tube for emergency or difficult intubation. It can enter the oesophagus or trachea, with inflatable cuffs and side openings ensuring ventilation.
• Needle crico-thyroidotomy: Insert a needle through the skin into the larynx to allow air passage as a temporary measure.
• Endotracheal intubation: Insert a tube through the mouth or nose into the trachea. Avoid using paralyzing drugs if ventilation cannot be established.
advanced airway management,oesophageal-tracheal combitube,endotracheal intubation
Airway Management in Tactical Field Care
Critical Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Procedure:

1. Assess airway status for unconscious patients.
2. If the patient is unconscious without airway obstruction, insert a nasopharyngeal airway as the airway of first choice until CASEVAC phase.
3. If the patient is...
Procedure:

1. Assess airway status for unconscious patients.
2. If the patient is unconscious without airway obstruction, insert a nasopharyngeal airway as the airway of first choice until CASEVAC phase.
3. If the patient is unconscious with airway obstruction, perform cricothyroidotomy immediately.
4. Do not use endotracheal intubation during Tactical Field Care; it is reserved for the CASEVAC phase.
5. Consider evaluation of laryngeal mask airway and Combitube for use by SOF corpsmen and medics.
airway,management,tactical_field_care,cricothyroidotomy,nasopharyngeal_airway
Assess and Manage Consciousness in Casualties
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- Deterioration of the level of consciousness will compromise the airway. Loss of consciousness is first and foremost a problem of the airway.
• Use the Glasgow coma scale for assessment of unconsciousness.
consciousness assessment, airway management, first aid, Glasgow coma scale
Establish and Maintain Airway in Facial or Neck Injuries
Critical Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

1. Assess the casualty for signs of facial or neck injuries.
2. If a neck fracture or spinal cord injury is suspected, immobilize the injury immediately to prevent further damage.
3. Maintain airway clearance by ensuring...
Procedure:

1. Assess the casualty for signs of facial or neck injuries.
2. If a neck fracture or spinal cord injury is suspected, immobilize the injury immediately to prevent further damage.
3. Maintain airway clearance by ensuring the casualty’s head is in a neutral position and not tilted back excessively.
4. If necessary, perform basic life support measures (e.g., rescue breathing) if the casualty is unconscious or not breathing adequately.
airway management, facial injury, neck injury, spinal cord injury, immobilization
Perform the Jaw-Thrust Technique for Airway Opening (Suspected Neck Injury)
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

• Kneel above the casualty’s head with your elbows resting on the ground.
• Stabilize the casualty’s neck in a neutral in-line position.
• Grasp the angles of the lower jaw with the four fingers of each hand, with thumbs...
Procedure:

• Kneel above the casualty’s head with your elbows resting on the ground.
• Stabilize the casualty’s neck in a neutral in-line position.
• Grasp the angles of the lower jaw with the four fingers of each hand, with thumbs on the lower front teeth.
