• Lift with both hands, one on each side, moving the jaw up and forward to open the airway.

> This technique is preferred for casualties with suspected neck injury as it avoids extending the neck.
jaw-thrust technique, airway opening, neck injury
Position the Unconscious Casualty to Maintain an Open Airway
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

• If the unconscious casualty is lying on his back: Turn him over using the log-roll technique and stabilize him in the lateral recovery position.
• If the unconscious casualty is lying face down: Do not turn them onto...
Procedure:

• If the unconscious casualty is lying on his back: Turn him over using the log-roll technique and stabilize him in the lateral recovery position.
• If the unconscious casualty is lying face down: Do not turn them onto their back. Place them in the lateral recovery position, ensuring the airway remains open.
• If the unconscious casualty has face and jaw injuries: Open and clean the mouth, place the casualty head-down and face-down, and cut a hole through the stretcher to allow the face to be free.
lateral recovery position, airway management, unconscious casualty
Prevent Object from Being Forced Deeper into Airway
Critical Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Do not push any object deeper into the airway with a finger. This can cause further injury or blockage, worsening the casualty's condition.
airway, object, choking, first aid, do not push
Remove Obstructions from the Mouth of an Unconscious Casualty
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

• Look in the mouth and remove any blood, vomit, debris (broken teeth, bone fragments), or foreign bodies without pushing them further into the airway.
• Use the finger-sweep technique: Insert the index finger down along the...
Procedure:

• Look in the mouth and remove any blood, vomit, debris (broken teeth, bone fragments), or foreign bodies without pushing them further into the airway.
• Use the finger-sweep technique: Insert the index finger down along the inside of the cheek to the base of the tongue. Use a hooking motion from the side of the mouth towards the centre to dislodge any foreign body, blood, or vomit.
• If blood or vomit are present, put a clean absorbent cloth around your fingers to wipe dry.
mouth clearance, foreign body removal, airway management
Secure Airway in Casualty with Throat Injury from Shrapnel
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

• Secure the airway immediately.
• Position the casualty head down and in the lateral recovery position, to allow blood to exit.
• Monitor for signs of airway obstruction or worsening condition.
shrapnel injury, airway management, throat trauma
Airway Management Essentials
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Key considerations for airway management:

• Deterioration of consciousness can compromise the airway.
• Airway status directly affects breathing, whether spontaneous or assisted.
• Simple manual maneuvers are the primary life-saving technique for...
Key considerations for airway management:

• Deterioration of consciousness can compromise the airway.
• Airway status directly affects breathing, whether spontaneous or assisted.
• Simple manual maneuvers are the primary life-saving technique for controlling the airway on-site.
airway management,consciousness,breathing
Assess for Unconsciousness Using the Touch Method
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

1. Gently touch the casualty's face or shoulder.
2. Observe for any reaction (e.g., blinking, movement, verbal response).
3. Absence of reaction indicates unconsciousness.
4. Proceed with further assessment and airway management...
Procedure:

1. Gently touch the casualty's face or shoulder.
2. Observe for any reaction (e.g., blinking, movement, verbal response).
3. Absence of reaction indicates unconsciousness.
4. Proceed with further assessment and airway management as needed.
unconsciousness, touch method, airway assessment
Demonstrate Endotracheal Intubation (Medical Officers and Operating Room Special Operations Medics)
High Priority Source: TCCC 17 13 Army Tactical Combat Casualty Care
Procedure:

1. Ensure the casualty is unconscious and not breathing.
2. Lubricate the endotracheal tube with water-soluble gel or sterile lubricant.
3. Insert the tube into the mouth, aiming it toward the back of the throat.
4. Continue...
Procedure:

1. Ensure the casualty is unconscious and not breathing.
2. Lubricate the endotracheal tube with water-soluble gel or sterile lubricant.
3. Insert the tube into the mouth, aiming it toward the back of the throat.
4. Continue insertion until resistance is felt (approximately 10 cm from the tip).
5. Confirm proper placement using capnography or other monitoring devices.
endotracheal intubation, airway management
Demonstrate the Insertion of a Supraglottic Airway
High Priority Source: TCCC 17 13 Army Tactical Combat Casualty Care
Procedure:

1. Ensure the casualty is unconscious and not breathing.
2. Lubricate the supraglottic airway with water-soluble gel or sterile lubricant.
3. Insert the device into the mouth, aiming it toward the back of the throat.
4. Continue...
Procedure:

1. Ensure the casualty is unconscious and not breathing.
2. Lubricate the supraglottic airway with water-soluble gel or sterile lubricant.
3. Insert the device into the mouth, aiming it toward the back of the throat.
4. Continue insertion until resistance is felt (approximately 10 cm from the tip).
5. Ensure the airway is patent and monitor for signs of discomfort or bleeding.
supraglottic airway insertion, airway management
Evacuation of Unconscious Casualty with Compromised Airway
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
When evacuating an unconscious casualty without a secured airway:

• Do not transfer the casualty lying on his back.
• Ensure continuous monitoring during transport to maintain airway clearance.
• Continue cervical spine immobilization as best...
When evacuating an unconscious casualty without a secured airway:

• Do not transfer the casualty lying on his back.
• Ensure continuous monitoring during transport to maintain airway clearance.
• Continue cervical spine immobilization as best possible, but prioritize airway control over spinal stabilization.
evacuation,airway management,cervical spine immobilization
Manage Airway in Conscious Casualty with Face and Jaw Injuries
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

• Help the casualty to sit up and bend forward, allowing blood and saliva to exit.
• If necessary, help to disimpact displaced bone by pulling it forward with your gloved fingers.
• Be aware that this is a **painful...
Procedure:

• Help the casualty to sit up and bend forward, allowing blood and saliva to exit.
• If necessary, help to disimpact displaced bone by pulling it forward with your gloved fingers.
• Be aware that this is a painful manoeuvre and may require reassurance from the casualty.
face injury, jaw injury, airway management
Open the Airway Using Jaw-Thrust Method
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure:

1. Kneel above the casualty’s head (looking toward the casualty’s feet).
2. Rest your elbows on the ground or floor.
3. Place one hand on each side of the casualty’s lower jaw at the angle of the jaw, below the ears.
4....
Procedure:

1. Kneel above the casualty’s head (looking toward the casualty’s feet).
2. Rest your elbows on the ground or floor.
3. Place one hand on each side of the casualty’s lower jaw at the angle of the jaw, below the ears.
4. Stabilize the casualty’s head with your forearms.
5. Use the index fingers to push the angles of the casualty’s lower jaw forward.

Important Notes:
• Use this method if a spinal or neck injury is suspected.
• If you are unable to maintain an airway after the second attempt, use the head-tilt/chin-lift method.
• Do not tilt or rotate the casualty’s head.
jaw thrust, airway management, spinal injury
Perform the Tongue-Jaw Lift Technique for Airway Opening
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

• Open the mouth by pressing down on the tongue with your thumbs and lifting the lower jaw with your fingers.
• If unable to open the mouth, push the teeth apart by pressing your thumb or knuckle of the middle finger against the...
Procedure:

• Open the mouth by pressing down on the tongue with your thumbs and lifting the lower jaw with your fingers.
• If unable to open the mouth, push the teeth apart by pressing your thumb or knuckle of the middle finger against the cheek between upper and lower teeth (cheek pad protects your fingers if bitten).
• Pull the tongue forward. If lips close: retract the lower lip with your thumbs.
tongue-jaw lift technique, airway opening
Recognize Situations at Risk of Late Airway Obstruction
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

Be alert for the following conditions that may lead to late airway obstruction:

• Head injury: The casualty slowly loses consciousness after some time.
• Face injury: Leads to later swelling (oedema) of the tongue and...
Procedure:

Be alert for the following conditions that may lead to late airway obstruction:

• Head injury: The casualty slowly loses consciousness after some time.
• Face injury: Leads to later swelling (oedema) of the tongue and throat.
• Neck injury: Accumulation of blood in the neck pressing on the airway from the outside.
• Burn or chemical injury of the face and airway, or inhalation of smoke fumes: Oedema of the throat and windpipe (larynx and trachea) may not develop for some hours.
airway obstruction, head injury, neck trauma, face injury, burn injury
Manage Airway in Conscious Casualty Who Can Talk or Cough
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

• Do not worry, the airway is open.
• Let the casualty talk or cough freely.
• Encourage the casualty to cough up the obstructing object.
• Do not attempt to remove foreign objects manually unless they are visible and...
Procedure:

• Do not worry, the airway is open.
• Let the casualty talk or cough freely.
• Encourage the casualty to cough up the obstructing object.
• Do not attempt to remove foreign objects manually unless they are visible and easily dislodged.
airway management, conscious casualty, coughing, foreign body
Understanding Advanced Airway Management Equipment
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Advanced airway management equipment is typically used in professional medical settings and may not be necessary for most survival scenarios.

### Types of Advanced Airway Devices:
• Laryngeal masks: These are inserted into the pharynx to...
Advanced airway management equipment is typically used in professional medical settings and may not be necessary for most survival scenarios.

### Types of Advanced Airway Devices:
• Laryngeal masks: These are inserted into the pharynx to maintain an open airway.
• Combitubes: A type of supraglottic airway device that can be used in emergency situations.
• Endotracheal intubation: This is considered the gold standard for airway management. It involves inserting a plastic tube through the mouth into the trachea to allow ventilation.

### Ventilation Equipment:
• Mask - self inflating bag combination (e.g., Ambubag): Used to provide artificial ventilation after an airway has been established.

Note: In austere situations, advanced airway management is generally not recommended unless you have the proper training and equipment. Most patients who require advanced airway interventions are unlikely to survive without professional medical care.
advanced airway management, laryngeal masks, combitubes, endotracheal intubation, ambubag
Respect Casualty's Preferred Airway Position if Conscious
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

• If the casualty is conscious and prefers a certain position (e.g., sitting), respect this airway self-management.
• Ensure that the chosen position does not compromise their breathing or comfort.
conscious casualty, airway position, patient preference
Airway Check
Reference Source: Family Emergency Handbook
Procedure:

• If there is a suspected neck injury: Steady your hands on the victim’s cheek bones. Grasp the angle of the jaw and, without tilting the head, lift the jaw upward.
• If there is no suspected neck injury: Place one hand...
Procedure:

• If there is a suspected neck injury: Steady your hands on the victim’s cheek bones. Grasp the angle of the jaw and, without tilting the head, lift the jaw upward.
• If there is no suspected neck injury: Place one hand on the victim's forehead to tilt the head back. Place fingers of the other hand under the chin to lift the jaw.
• Ensure the airway is clear in both cases.
airway check, neck injury, first aid, emergency procedure
Airway Management Techniques
Reference Source: Ranger Handbook 2006 + Survival and Austere Medicine
Airway Management:

1. Clear the Airway: If the casualty is unconscious and not breathing, clear any obstructions from their airway (e.g., vomit, blood, or foreign objects).
2. Head Tilt-Chin Lift: Use the head tilt-chin lift maneuver to...
Airway Management:

1. Clear the Airway: If the casualty is unconscious and not breathing, clear any obstructions from their airway (e.g., vomit, blood, or foreign objects).
2. Head Tilt-Chin Lift: Use the head tilt-chin lift maneuver to open the airway if there is no suspected neck injury.
3. Jaw Thrust Maneuver: If a neck injury is suspected, use the jaw thrust maneuver instead of the head tilt-chin lift.
4. Use of Equipment: If available, use an oral airway or nasal airway to maintain airway patency. These devices help keep the airway open and prevent the tongue from blocking it.
5. Monitor Airway: Continuously monitor the casualty’s airway and breathing until professional help arrives.
airway management, head tilt-chin lift, jaw thrust, oral airway, nasal airway, emergency procedures
Check for Breathing After Opening Airway
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid + STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure:

After establishing an open airway, maintain that position to keep the airway open. Check for breathing by performing the following actions within 3 to 5 seconds:

1. LOOK for the chest to rise and fall.
2. LISTEN for air...
Procedure:

After establishing an open airway, maintain that position to keep the airway open. Check for breathing by performing the following actions within 3 to 5 seconds:

1. LOOK for the chest to rise and fall.
2. LISTEN for air escaping during exhalation by placing your ear near the casualty’s mouth.
3. FEEL for the flow of air on your cheek.
4. If the casualty does not resume breathing spontaneously, PERFORM rescue breathing.

Notes:
• If the casualty resumes breathing, monitor and maintain the open airway.
• Transport the casualty to an MTF as soon as practical.

Additional Steps from Version 2:
1. While maintaining the open airway position, place an ear over the casualty’s mouth and nose, looking toward the chest and stomach.
2. Count the number of respirations for 15 seconds.
3. If the casualty is unconscious; if respiratory rate is less than 2 in 15 seconds, and/or if the casualty is making snoring or gurgling sounds, insert an NPA.
4. If the casualty’s lips are still closed after the jaw has been moved forward, use your thumbs to retract the lower lip and allow air to enter the casualty’s mouth.
breathing check, airway management, rescue breathing, NPA insertion, first aid
Identifying and Managing Upper Airway Obstruction
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid + Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

1. Understand Causes of Airway Obstruction: Upper airway obstructions often occur because—
- The casualty’s tongue falls back into his throat while he is unconscious. The tongue *falls back* and *obstructs* the airway, it is...
Procedure:

1. Understand Causes of Airway Obstruction: Upper airway obstructions often occur because—
- The casualty’s tongue falls back into his throat while he is unconscious. The tongue *falls back* and *obstructs* the airway, it is not swallowed by the casualty.
2. Recognize Signs of Obstruction: Look for signs such as inability to breathe, coughing, or choking.
3. Take Immediate Action: If an obstruction is suspected, proceed with foreign-body airway obstruction maneuvers (see paragraph 2-10) if repositioning the head does not resolve the issue.
4. If the casualty is unconscious and has a blocked airway due to the tongue falling back, immediately take action to clear the airway (see paragraph 2-10 for specific maneuvers).
5. Ensure that the casualty’s head is properly repositioned if initial attempts at ventilation are unsuccessful.
6. If improper chin and head positioning is suspected as the cause of difficulty with ventilation, reposition the casualty’s head and repeat rescue breathing.
upper airway obstruction, tongue fall back, rescue breathing, foreign-body airway obstruction, head repositioning
Open and Maintain an Airway Using the Head-Tilt/Chin-Lift Technique
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid + STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure:

1. Kneel at the level of the casualty’s shoulders.
2. Place one hand on the casualty’s forehead and apply firm, backward pressure with the palm to tilt the head back.
3. Use the other hand to lift the chin, ensuring that the airway...
Procedure:

1. Kneel at the level of the casualty’s shoulders.
2. Place one hand on the casualty’s forehead and apply firm, backward pressure with the palm to tilt the head back.
3. Use the other hand to lift the chin, ensuring that the airway is open by keeping the tongue from blocking the throat. Place the fingertips under the bony part of the lower jaw and lift, bringing the chin forward.
4. Do not press deeply into the soft tissue under the chin, as this may obstruct the airway further.
5. Maintain this position while checking for breathing and during rescue breathing.

Important Notes:
• This technique should be used if there is no suspected spinal injury.
• If a spinal or neck injury is suspected, use the jaw-thrust method instead to open the airway without moving the head.
• Do NOT use the thumb to lift.
• Do NOT completely close the casualty’s mouth.
• If foreign material or vomit is in the mouth, remove it as quickly as possible.
head-tilt/chin-lift, airway management, first aid, spinal injury, jaw-thrust method

Cold Injuries (27) -

Manage Hypothermia (Mild)
Critical Source: SH 21 76 Ranger Handbook
Procedure for Managing Mild Hypothermia

1. Warm body evenly and without delay. (Heat source must be provided.)
2. Keep dry, protect from elements.
3. Warm liquids may be given to conscious casualty only.
4. Be prepared to start CPR.
5. Seek...
Procedure for Managing Mild Hypothermia

1. Warm body evenly and without delay. (Heat source must be provided.)
2. Keep dry, protect from elements.
3. Warm liquids may be given to conscious casualty only.
4. Be prepared to start CPR.
5. Seek medical treatment immediately.
hypothermia, warming techniques, medical attention
Manage Hypothermia (Severe)
Critical Source: SH 21 76 Ranger Handbook
Procedure for Managing Severe Hypothermia

1. Quickly stabilize body temperature.
2. Attempt to prevent further heat loss.
3. Handle the casualty gently.
4. Evacuate to nearest medical treatment facility as soon as possible.
hypothermia, emergency evacuation, medical attention
Provide First Aid for Deep Cold Injuries (Frostbite)
Critical Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
First aid for deep cold injuries (e.g., frostbite):

1. Immediate actions:
- Remove the casualty from the cold environment (build an improvised shelter if necessary).
- Provide first aid for other injuries simultaneously while waiting...
First aid for deep cold injuries (e.g., frostbite):

1. Immediate actions:
- Remove the casualty from the cold environment (build an improvised shelter if necessary).
- Provide first aid for other injuries simultaneously while waiting for transportation.
- Continue first aid measures during transport to a medical treatment facility (MTF).

2. Rewarming techniques:
- Rewarm the affected part using body heat:
- Apply firm, steady pressure with your hands.
- Place the affected part under your arms or against the stomach of a buddy.

3. Avoid the following actions:
- Do not rub or massage the affected area.
- Do not expose to fire, stove, or cold water.

4. Monitor for complications:
- Deep frostbite can lead to loss of fingers, toes, hands, or feet if not treated promptly.
- Seek immediate medical attention.
frostbite treatment, deep cold injury first aid, rewarming frostbite
Provide First Aid for Frostbite in Cold Weather
Critical Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
### Provide First Aid for Frostbite in Cold Weather

Steps:

1. (a) Face, ears, and nose: Cover the casualty’s affected area with his/her and/or your bare hands until sensation and color return.
2. (b) Hands: Open the casualty’s field...
### Provide First Aid for Frostbite in Cold Weather

Steps:

1. (a) Face, ears, and nose: Cover the casualty’s affected area with his/her and/or your bare hands until sensation and color return.
2. (b) Hands: Open the casualty’s field jacket and shirt. Place the affected hands under the casualty’s armpits. Close the field jacket and shirt to prevent additional exposure.
3. (c) Feet: Remove the casualty’s boots and socks if he/she does not need to walk any further to receive additional treatment. Place the affected feet under clothing and against the body of another Soldier.

CAUTION: Do NOT remove clothing in a chemical environment.

WARNING: Do NOT attempt to thaw the casualty’s feet or other seriously frozen areas if the Soldier will be required to walk or travel to a medical center to receive medical treatment. The possibility of injury from walking is less when the feet are frozen than after they have been thawed (if possible, avoid walking). Thawing in the field increases the possibility of infection, gangrene, or injury.

Note: Thawing may occur spontaneously during transportation to the aid station or hospital. This cannot be avoided since the body in general must be kept warm.

4. (4) Ensure that the casualty is kept warm and that he/she is covered (to avoid further injury).
5. (5) Seek medical treatment as soon as possible.
6. (6) Reassure the casualty, protect the affected area from further injury by covering it lightly with a blanket or any dry clothing, and seek shelter out of the wind.
7. (7) Remove or loosen constricting clothing (except in a contaminated environment) and increase insulation.
8. (8) Ensure that the casualty exercises as much as possible (avoiding trauma to the injured part) and is prepared for pain when thawing occurs.
9. (9) Protect the frostbitten part from additional injury. Do NOT—
- (a) Rub the injured part with snow or apply cold water soaks.
- (b) Warm the part by massage or exposure to open fire because the frozen part may be burned due to the lack of feeling.
- (c) Use ointments or other salves.
- (d) Manipulate the part in any way to increase circulation.
- (e) Allow the Soldier to use alcohol or tobacco.
frostbite, first aid, cold weather
Treat Superficial or Deep Frostbite
Critical Source: USMC Summer Survival Course Handbook
Treatment for Superficial or Deep Frostbite:

• Evacuate the casualty immediately and re-warm in the rear (medical facility).
• Do not rewarm frostbite in the field, unless it is an extreme emergency.
• Freeze-Thaw-Re-Freeze injury...
Treatment for Superficial or Deep Frostbite:

• Evacuate the casualty immediately and re-warm in the rear (medical facility).
• Do not rewarm frostbite in the field, unless it is an extreme emergency.
• Freeze-Thaw-Re-Freeze injury can occur if a frostbitten extremity is thawed, then refrozen before healing. This worsens the initial injury.

In an extreme emergency: It is better to walk out on a frostbitten foot than to warm it up and risk re-freezing.

• Treat frozen extremities as fractures: Carefully pad and splint them.
• Treat frozen feet as litter cases (non-ambulatory).
• Prevent further freezing injury.
• Do not forget about hypothermia: Keep the victim warm and dry.

Once in the rear, re-warm frostbitten extremities in a water bath at 101°F to 108°F.
superficial frostbite, deep frostbite, evacuation, rewarming, freeze-thaw-re-freeze injury
Administer First Aid for Cold Injuries
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Objective: Provide immediate care to individuals suffering from cold-related injuries such as frostbite or hypothermia.

Steps:
1. Recognize the signs of cold injury:
- Hypothermia: shivering, slurred speech, confusion, drowsiness,...
Objective: Provide immediate care to individuals suffering from cold-related injuries such as frostbite or hypothermia.

Steps:
1. Recognize the signs of cold injury:
- Hypothermia: shivering, slurred speech, confusion, drowsiness, and loss of coordination.
- Frostbite: numbness, tingling, and pale or waxy skin that may turn red or blister after warming.
2. Move the casualty to a warmer environment:
- If possible, move the individual to a sheltered or heated area.
3. Warm the casualty gradually:
- For hypothermia: remove wet clothing and cover with dry blankets. Use body heat (e.g., hugging) if available. Avoid direct heat sources such as fire or hot water.
- For frostbite: gently rewarm affected areas using warm (not hot) water (98.6°F to 104°F). Do not rub the affected area, as this can cause further tissue damage.
4. Monitor the casualty’s condition:
- Continuously check for signs of improvement or deterioration (e.g., worsening confusion, loss of consciousness).
5. Seek medical assistance immediately:
- Hypothermia and frostbite are serious conditions that require professional care.

Warnings:
• Avoid using direct heat sources such as fire or hot water to warm a hypothermic individual, as this can cause burns or further injury.
• Do not rub frostbitten areas, as this can damage the tissue.
hypothermia, frostbite, first aid for cold injuries, warming techniques
Avoid Harmful Actions During Frostbite Treatment
Critical Source: USMC Summer Survival Course Handbook
Do not do the following during frostbite treatment:

• Do not rub any cold injury with snow.
• Do not massage the affected area.
• Do not rewarm with a stove or fire (this may cause burn injuries).
• **Loosen constricting...
Do not do the following during frostbite treatment:

• Do not rub any cold injury with snow.
• Do not massage the affected area.
• Do not rewarm with a stove or fire (this may cause burn injuries).
• Loosen constricting clothing.
• Avoid tobacco products, as they can impair circulation.
avoid actions, frostbite treatment, no rubbing, no massage, no fire rewarming
Manage Dehydration
Critical Source: SH 21 76 Ranger Handbook
Procedure for Managing Dehydration

1. Keep warm, loosen clothes.
2. Replace lost fluids, rest, and additional medical treatment.
dehydration, fluid replacement, medical attention
Recognize and Respond to Frostbite Injuries
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Objective: Recognize signs of frostbite and provide appropriate first aid.

Signs and Symptoms:
• Loss of sensation (numbness) in any part of the body.
• Sudden blanching (whitening) of the skin, followed by a momentary tingling...
Objective: Recognize signs of frostbite and provide appropriate first aid.

Signs and Symptoms:
• Loss of sensation (numbness) in any part of the body.
• Sudden blanching (whitening) of the skin, followed by a momentary tingling sensation.
• Redness of skin in light-skinned individuals; grayish coloring in dark-skinned individuals.
• Blisters may form on affected areas.
• Swelling or tender areas may be present.
• Loss of previous pain sensation in the affected area.
• Pale, yellowish, waxy-looking skin.
• Frozen tissue that feels solid (or wooden) to the touch.

First Aid Measures:
1. For face, ears, and nose: Cover the affected area with your bare hands or the casualty’s own hands until sensation and color return.
2. For hands: Open the casualty’s field jacket and shirt (avoid removing protective gear in a chemical environment). Place the affected hands under the casualty’s armpits and close the clothing to prevent further exposure.
3. For feet: Remove boots and socks if the casualty does not need to walk further. Place the feet against another person’s body or under their clothing for rewarming.
4. DO NOT rub, massage, or apply heat or ice directly to the affected area.
5. Transport the casualty to an MTF as soon as possible for medical evaluation and treatment.

Warnings: Deep frostbite is a very serious injury that can lead to loss of body parts if not treated promptly.
frostbite,cold injury,first aid
Treat Frosting (Mild Frostbite)
Critical Source: USMC Summer Survival Course Handbook
Treatment for Frosting:

1. Use body heat rewarming: Hold the affected area skin to skin for 15 minutes.
- *Rewarm face, nose, and ears with hands.*
- *Rewarm hands in armpits, groin, or belly.*
- *Rewarm feet with a...
Treatment for Frosting:

1. Use body heat rewarming: Hold the affected area skin to skin for 15 minutes.
- *Rewarm face, nose, and ears with hands.*
- *Rewarm hands in armpits, groin, or belly.*
- *Rewarm feet with a mountain buddy’s armpits or belly.*

2. If the affected area cannot be rewarmed within 15 minutes, suspect Superficial Frostbite or Deep Frostbite.

Important: Do not attempt to further rewarm if frostbite is suspected.
frosting, rewarming, body heat, mild frostbite, treatment
Identify Signs and Symptoms of Cold Injuries
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Signs and symptoms of cold injuries include:

1. Superficial cold injury:
- Numbness or tingling sensations
- Often relieved by loosening boots/clothing and exercising to improve circulation

2. **Deep cold injury (e.g.,...
Signs and symptoms of cold injuries include:

1. Superficial cold injury:
- Numbness or tingling sensations
- Often relieved by loosening boots/clothing and exercising to improve circulation

2. Deep cold injury (e.g., frostbite):
- Affected part feels like a stump or block of wood
- Discoloration of the skin:
- Light-skinned individuals: skin reddens then becomes pale/waxy white
- Dark-skinned individuals: grayness in the skin
- Injured foot/hand feels cold to touch
- Swelling (may indicate deep injury)
- Blisters may occur after rewarming affected parts

3. General symptoms:
- Cold and general discomfort
- Numbness may prevent awareness of injury

Note: Service members should work in pairs (buddy teams) to check each other for signs of discoloration and other symptoms
cold injuries,signs,symptoms,frostbite,chilblain,hypothermia,immersion syndrome
Provide First Aid for Frostbite on Face, Ears, and Nose
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

Action Steps:
1. Cover the casualty’s affected area with his/her and/or your bare hands until sensation and color return.
2. Ensure that the individual is removed from cold exposure immediately.
3. Avoid rubbing or massaging the...
Procedure:

Action Steps:
1. Cover the casualty’s affected area with his/her and/or your bare hands until sensation and color return.
2. Ensure that the individual is removed from cold exposure immediately.
3. Avoid rubbing or massaging the affected areas to prevent further tissue damage.
4. Seek professional medical evaluation as soon as possible, as signs of tissue damage may be slow to appear.

Warnings:
• Do not use direct heat sources (e.g., fire, hot water) for rewarming frostbitten areas.
• Avoid attempting to rewarm the affected area without proper guidance.
frostbite, face, ears, nose, first aid
Provide Immediate Care for Trenchfoot/Immersion Foot
High Priority Source: USMC Winter Survival Course Handbook
Immediate Actions:
• Evacuate all cases of trenchfoot, as it cannot be treated effectively in the field.
• While awaiting evacuation, perform the following steps:
- Dry the feet thoroughly.
- Warm the feet gently (avoid using...
Immediate Actions:
• Evacuate all cases of trenchfoot, as it cannot be treated effectively in the field.
• While awaiting evacuation, perform the following steps:
- Dry the feet thoroughly.
- Warm the feet gently (avoid using direct heat or hot water, which can cause further damage).
- Elevate the feet to reduce swelling.
- Manage pain: Severe cases may require medication such as morphine due to intense pain, even if the injury appears mild.
trenchfoot treatment, immersion foot care, cold injury first aid, pain management
Recognize and Respond to Deep Frostbite
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

Signs and Symptoms of Deep Frostbite:
• Freezing extends below the skin.
• Requires immediate first aid and medical treatment to avoid or minimize loss of body parts.

Action Steps:
1. Identify signs of deep frostbite, such...
Procedure:

Signs and Symptoms of Deep Frostbite:
• Freezing extends below the skin.
• Requires immediate first aid and medical treatment to avoid or minimize loss of body parts.

Action Steps:
1. Identify signs of deep frostbite, such as freezing extending beyond the skin surface.
2. Provide immediate first aid (e.g., rewarming affected areas without rubbing or massaging them).
3. Seek professional medical treatment as soon as possible to prevent permanent damage or loss of body parts.

Warnings:
• Do not rub or massage frostbitten areas, as this can cause further tissue damage.
• Avoid using direct heat sources (e.g., fire, hot water) for rewarming.
frostbite, first aid, cold injury, rewarming, tissue damage
Rewarm Frostbitten Areas Safely
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Objective: Rewarm frostbitten areas without causing further damage.

Steps:
1. Apply firm steady pressure with your hands, or place the affected part under your arms or against the stomach of a buddy to rewarm it.
2. **DO NOT rub or...
Objective: Rewarm frostbitten areas without causing further damage.

Steps:
1. Apply firm steady pressure with your hands, or place the affected part under your arms or against the stomach of a buddy to rewarm it.
2. DO NOT rub or massage the affected area, as this can cause tissue damage.
3. If possible, use body heat from another person (e.g., placing the affected part under their armpits or against their chest).
4. Avoid using direct heat sources such as fire, hot water, or heating pads.
5. Keep the affected area dry and protected from trauma or secondary infections.
6. Elevate the injured part to relieve swelling if possible.
7. Transport the casualty to a medical treatment facility (MTF) as soon as possible for further evaluation and care.

Warnings: Signs and symptoms of tissue damage may be slow to appear, so medical personnel should evaluate the injury even if initial signs seem mild.
frostbite,rewarm,cold injury,first aid
Identify and Respond to Cold Injuries
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
### Identify the Type of Cold Injury

1. Chilblain: Look for the following signs and symptoms:
- Prolonged exposure of bare skin at temperatures of 60° F to 32° F or 20° F for acclimated, dry, unwashed skin.
- The area may be acutely...
### Identify the Type of Cold Injury

1. Chilblain: Look for the following signs and symptoms:
- Prolonged exposure of bare skin at temperatures of 60° F to 32° F or 20° F for acclimated, dry, unwashed skin.
- The area may be acutely swollen, red, tender, and hot with itchy skin.
- There may be no skin tissue loss in untreated cases but continued exposure may lead to infected, ulcerated, or bleeding lesions.

2. Frostbite: Look for the following signs and symptoms:
- Exposure to cold, usually below 32°F depending on the wind chill factor, duration of exposure, and adequacy of protection. Individuals with a history of cold injury are likely to suffer an additional cold injury.
- The body parts most easily frostbitten are the cheeks, nose, ears, chin, forehead, wrists, hands, and feet. Frostbite may involve only the skin (superficial), or it may extend to a depth below the skin (deep).
- Loss of sensation or numb feeling in any part of the body.
- Sudden blanching (whitening) of the skin of the affected part, followed by a momentary tingling feeling.
- Redness of skin in light-skinned Soldiers, grayish coloring in dark-skinned Soldiers.
- Blisters.
- Swelling or tender areas.
- Loss of previous feeling of pain in the affected area.
- Pale, yellowish, waxy-looking skin.
- Frozen tissue that feels solid (or wooden) to the touch.

CAUTION: Deep frostbite (when freezing extends to a depth below the skin) is a very serious injury and requires immediate first aid and subsequent medical treatment to avoid or minimize loss of body parts.

3. Immersion Foot/Trench Foot: Look for the following signs and symptoms:
- Fairly long exposure of feet to wet conditions at temperatures from about 50° F to 32° F.
- First stage:
- Parts of the affected foot are cold and painless.
- Pulse is weak.
- Numbness may be present.
- Second stage:
- Parts may feel hot and burning.
- Shooting pains may begin.
- Later stages:
- Skin is pale with a bluish cast.
- Pulse decreases.
- Other signs that may follow include blisters, swelling, redness, heat, bleeding, or gangrene.

4. Snow Blindness: Look for the following signs and symptoms:
- Sensation of grit in the eyes with pain in and over the eyes, made worse by moving the eyeball.
- Watery eyes.
- Redness.
- Headache.
- Increased pain with exposure to light.

5. Hypothermia: Look for the following signs and symptoms:
- Early stage:
- Shivering.

### Provide First Aid Based on Injury Type

• For all cold injuries, seek medical aid as soon as possible. Do NOT stop the first aid; but, if the situation allows, send another person to find medical aid.
• Ensure the casualty is kept warm and dry.
• If the casualty has chilblains or frostbite, do not rub the affected area with snow or apply direct heat (e.g., fire, hot water). Instead, rewarm the area gradually using body heat or a blanket.
• For immersion foot/trench foot, keep the feet dry and warm. Avoid walking on the affected feet if possible.
• For snow blindness, protect the eyes from further exposure to light by covering them with a clean cloth or eye patch. Seek medical aid immediately.
• For hypothermia, remove wet clothing and replace it with dry clothing. Use blankets or other insulating materials to keep the casualty warm. If the casualty is conscious, give them warm, non-alcoholic drinks. Do not apply direct heat (e.g., fire, hot water) to the casualty.
cold injury, frostbite, chilblain, immersion foot, trench foot, snow blindness, hypothermia, first aid
Manage Frostbite (Deep)
High Priority Source: SH 21 76 Ranger Handbook
Procedure for Managing Deep Frostbite

1. Protect the part from additional injury.
2. Seek medical treatment as fast as possible.
deep frostbite, immediate medical attention
First Aid for Frozen Extremities (Cold Injuries)
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Do not attempt to thaw the casualty’s feet or other frozen areas if they will be required to walk or travel to an MTF for additional treatment. The possibility of injury from walking is less when the feet are frozen than when...
Procedure:

1. Do not attempt to thaw the casualty’s feet or other frozen areas if they will be required to walk or travel to an MTF for additional treatment. The possibility of injury from walking is less when the feet are frozen than when they have been thawed. (However, if possible, avoid walking.) Thawing in the field increases the possibility of infection, gangrene, or injury.

2. Loosen or remove constricting clothing and remove any jewelry.

3. Increase insulation by covering with a blanket or other dry material. Ensure the casualty exercises as much as possible, avoiding trauma to the injured part.
frozen feet, first aid, cold injury, thawing, insulation
Manage Frostbite (Superficial)
Standard Source: SH 21 76 Ranger Handbook
Procedure for Managing Superficial Frostbite

1. Keep casualty warm; gently warm affected parts.
2. Decrease constricting clothing, increase exercise and insulation.
frostbite, warming techniques, medical attention
Recognize and Treat Chilblain
Standard Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Chilblain signs and symptoms:

• Caused by prolonged exposure of bare skin to temperatures between 60°F (15°C) and 32°F (0°C), or 20°F (-7°C) for acclimated, dry, unwashed skin.
• Signs include:
- Acute swelling.
- Redness.
-...
Chilblain signs and symptoms:

• Caused by prolonged exposure of bare skin to temperatures between 60°F (15°C) and 32°F (0°C), or 20°F (-7°C) for acclimated, dry, unwashed skin.
• Signs include:
- Acute swelling.
- Redness.
- Tenderness.
- Heat sensation.
- Itching.
• Untreated cases may lead to infected, ulcerated, or bleeding lesions.

First aid measures:
1. Rewarm the affected area using body heat:
- Apply firm, steady pressure with your hands.
- Place the affected part under your arms or against the stomach of a buddy.
2. Do not rub or massage the affected area.
3. Monitor for signs of worsening (e.g., infection or ulcers) and seek medical aid if necessary.
chilblain treatment, cold injury first aid, chilblain symptoms
First Aid for Frostbite
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid + Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

1. Identify the injury: Look for loss of sensation (numbness), sudden whitening of the skin, tingling, redness in light-skinned individuals, grayish coloring in dark-skinned individuals, blisters, swelling or tender areas, and...
Procedure:

1. Identify the injury: Look for loss of sensation (numbness), sudden whitening of the skin, tingling, redness in light-skinned individuals, grayish coloring in dark-skinned individuals, blisters, swelling or tender areas, and pale yellowish, waxy-looking skin that feels solid to the touch.
2. Warm the area immediately at the first sign of frostbite using firm, steady pressure from a warm part of the body (e.g., hand, underarm, or abdomen).
3. Protect sensitive areas like face, ears, and nose: Cover these areas with hands (either the casualty’s own or a buddy’s) to provide warmth.
4. Warm hands using body heat: Open a field jacket and place the casualty’s hands against his body, then close the jacket to prevent heat loss.
5. Warm feet using another person’s body heat: Remove the casualty’s boots and socks and place their feet against the body of another service member for rewarming.
6. Do not rub or massage the affected area as this can cause further tissue damage.
7. Avoid thawing if walking is required: Do not attempt to thaw the casualty’s feet or other frozen areas if they will need to walk or travel to a medical treatment facility (MTF) for additional care.
8. Seek medical aid immediately, especially if blisters, swelling, or signs of severe frostbite are present.
frostbite, first aid, cold injury, numbness, waxy skin, body heat rewarming
First Aid for Immersion Syndrome (Trench Foot/Immersion Foot)
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid + USMC Summer Survival Course Handbook + Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

1. Identify symptoms of immersion syndrome: Look for cold, numb, and painless affected parts that may later become hot with burning or shooting pains. Advanced stages include pale skin with a bluish cast, decreased pulse,...
Procedure:

1. Identify symptoms of immersion syndrome: Look for cold, numb, and painless affected parts that may later become hot with burning or shooting pains. Advanced stages include pale skin with a bluish cast, decreased pulse, blistering, swelling, heat, hemorrhaging, and gangrene.
2. Gradual rewarming: Rewarm the affected area gradually by exposing it to warm air (e.g., body heat, hot water if safe). Avoid rapid warming to prevent further damage.
3. Avoid massaging or moistening the skin: Do not massage or moisten the skin of the affected part to prevent worsening the condition.
4. Protect from trauma: Take steps to protect the affected parts from additional injury or trauma. Handle them gently and avoid any unnecessary pressure or walking on the affected area.
5. Dry feet thoroughly: Ensure that the casualty’s feet are completely dry, and avoid walking on them to prevent further complications.
6. Seek medical aid immediately: Consult a healthcare professional for proper treatment and monitoring. In field conditions, evacuate the casualty as soon as possible since immersion syndrome cannot be treated effectively in the field without proper medical care.

Notes:
• Severe pain may require administration of pain medication (e.g., morphine) while waiting for evacuation.
• This condition can lead to severe complications such as tissue damage, infection, and gangrene if not treated promptly.
immersion syndrome, trench foot, first aid, cold injury, rewarming, medical evacuation
Identify and Provide First Aid for Cold Injuries
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid + STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Conditions: You see a casualty who has signs and symptoms of a cold injury. You will need a canteen of potable water, blanket (or similar item to use for warmth), and dry clothing.

Standards: Identify the type of cold injury and give the...
Conditions: You see a casualty who has signs and symptoms of a cold injury. You will need a canteen of potable water, blanket (or similar item to use for warmth), and dry clothing.

Standards: Identify the type of cold injury and give the correct first aid.

Performance Steps

_Note:_ When performing first aid on a casualty, seek medical aid as soon as possible. Do NOT stop the first aid; but, if the situation allows, send another person to find medical aid.

1. Identify the type of cold injury.
- a. Chilblain. If the signs and symptoms are as follows, go to step 2a.
- (1) Prolonged exposure of bare skin at temperatures of 60° F to 32° F or 20° F for acclimated, dry, unwashed skin.
- (2) The area may be acutely swollen, red, tender, and hot with itchy skin.
- (3) There may be no skin tissue loss in untreated cases but continued exposure may lead to infected, ulcerated, or bleeding lesions.
- b. Frostbite. If the signs and symptoms are as follows, go to step 2b.

2. Provide first aid based on the type of cold injury:

### Superficial Cold Injury (e.g., Chilblain):

1. Rewarm the affected part using body heat (e.g., covering cheeks with hands, putting fingertips in armpits, placing feet under a buddy’s clothing near their belly)
2. Do NOT:
- Massage the injured part
- Expose to fire or stove
- Rub with snow
- Slap or chafe the area
- Soak in cold water
3. Avoid walking on injured feet

### Deep Cold Injury (Frostbite):

1. First priority: Remove the casualty from the cold environment (e.g., build an improvised shelter)
2. Provide first aid for other injuries simultaneously while waiting for transportation or evacuation
3. If transported in a nonmedical vehicle, continue first aid measures en route to the MTF (Medical Treatment Facility)
4. Do NOT:
- Massage the injured part
- Expose to fire or stove
- Rub with snow
- Slap or chafe the area
- Soak in cold water
cold injury, frostbite, chilblain, first aid, rewarm, body heat
Manage Immersion (Trench) Foot
Standard Source: SH 21 76 Ranger Handbook
Procedure for Managing Immersion (Trench) Foot

1. Gradual warming by exposure to warm air.
2. DO NOT massage or moisten skin.
3. Protect affected parts from trauma.
4. Dry feet thoroughly: avoid walking.
5. Seek medical treatment.
trench foot, warming techniques, medical attention
Provide First Aid for Chilblain in Cold Weather
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
### Provide First Aid for Chilblain in Cold Weather

Steps:

1. (1) Rewarm the affected part by applying firm steady pressure with your hands or placing the affected part under your arms or against the stomach of a buddy.
2. (2) Do NOT...
### Provide First Aid for Chilblain in Cold Weather

Steps:

1. (1) Rewarm the affected part by applying firm steady pressure with your hands or placing the affected part under your arms or against the stomach of a buddy.
2. (2) Do NOT rub or massage affected areas.
3. (3) Have medical personnel evaluate the injury because signs and symptoms of tissue damage may be slow to appear.
chilblain, first aid, cold weather
Manage Chilblains
Reference Source: SH 21 76 Ranger Handbook
Procedure for Managing Chilblains

1. Area usually responds to locally applied warming (body heat).
2. Do Not rub or massage area.
3. Seek medical treatment.
chilblains, warming techniques, medical attention
First Aid for Frozen Body Parts
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Warning: Do not attempt to thaw the casualty’s feet or other frozen areas if he will be required to walk or travel to an MTF for additional medical treatment. The possibility of additional injury from walking is less when the feet are frozen...
Warning: Do not attempt to thaw the casualty’s feet or other frozen areas if he will be required to walk or travel to an MTF for additional medical treatment. The possibility of additional injury from walking is less when the feet are frozen than when they are thawed. (However, if possible avoid walking.) Thawing in the field increases the possibilities of infection, gangrene, or other injury.

Note: Thawing may occur spontaneously during transportation to the MTF; this cannot be avoided since the body in general must be kept warm. In all of the above areas, ensure that the casualty is kept warm and that he is covered (to avoid further injury). Seek medical treatment as soon as possible.

Procedure:
1. Reassure the casualty.
2. Protect the affected area from further injury by covering it lightly with a blanket or any dry clothing.
3. Seek shelter out of the wind.
4. Remove or loosen constricting clothing (except in a contaminated environment) and increase insulation.
5. Loosen or remove constricting clothing and remove any jewelry.
6. Increase insulation by covering with a blanket or other dry material. Ensure the casualty exercises as much as possible, avoiding trauma to the injured part.
frozen body parts, first aid, cold injury, thawing, infection, gangrene

Dental Injuries (26) -

Handle a Knocked-Out Permanent Tooth
High Priority Source: Where There is No Dentist
Procedure for Handling a Knocked-Out Permanent Tooth

When a permanent tooth is knocked out:

1. Ask how long ago it happened. If it was less than 12 hours ago, you can attempt to reimplant the tooth.
2. The sooner the tooth is replaced,...
Procedure for Handling a Knocked-Out Permanent Tooth

When a permanent tooth is knocked out:

1. Ask how long ago it happened. If it was less than 12 hours ago, you can attempt to reimplant the tooth.
2. The sooner the tooth is replaced, the better. If you replace the tooth in the first hour, it has a much better chance of joining with the gum and bone.
3. Wash the tooth gently with saline, milk, or clean water. Ensure there are no bits of dirt on the root.
4. Keep the tooth damp with wet cotton gauze until you can reimplant it.

Do not scrape away any skin from the root or inside the socket.

To reimplant the tooth:

1. If anesthetic is not available, inform the patient that it will hurt somewhat.
2. Gently push the tooth up into the socket, using a slight turning movement back and forth.
3. Ensure the biting edge of the loose tooth is at the same level as the surrounding teeth.
4. Hold the tooth in place with your fingers for about 5 minutes.

Stabilizing the Tooth:

1. Soften some beeswax and form it into two thin rolls.
2. Place one roll near the gums on the front side of five teeth: the loose tooth and the two teeth on each side.
3. Press the wax firmly but carefully against these teeth.
4. Do the same with the second roll of wax on the back side of the same teeth, again near the gums.
5. Ensure the wax on the back side is touching the wax on the front side to help hold the teeth more firmly.
6. Use cotton tweezers to push the wax between the teeth if needed.
7. Keep the wax in place for at least 3 weeks.
permanent tooth, knocked out, reimplantation, dental first aid
Manage Segment/Jaw Fracture with Multiple Teeth Involved
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
- A fracture of the bony structure of the jaw involving two or more teeth.
• The affected teeth move independently of each other.
• Replace and splint as in subluxation cases.
• Keep the tooth splinted for 6 weeks to allow healing.
jaw fracture, segment fracture, splinting, dental first aid
Manage Tooth Loss (Avulsion)
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
- Do not touch the root or scrub the tooth or socket.
• Rinse both the tooth and socket with saline until clean.
• Replace the tooth into the socket as soon as possible and splint it in place.
• If immediate replacement is not possible,...
- Do not touch the root or scrub the tooth or socket.
• Rinse both the tooth and socket with saline until clean.
• Replace the tooth into the socket as soon as possible and splint it in place.
• If immediate replacement is not possible, store the tooth in saline, milk, or saliva.
• Implantation after 24 hours has little chance of success.
• Follow dental first aid measures.
tooth loss, avulsion, splinting, dental first aid
Handle a Loose Tooth with a Broken Root
High Priority Source: Where There is No Dentist
Procedure for Handling a Loose Tooth with a Broken Root

If the tooth is loose because its root has been broken:

1. Take out both parts of the tooth.
2. If you have trouble taking out the broken root, leave it and try again a week later.
broken root, loose tooth, dental extraction
Manage Extrusion of a Tooth
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
- The tooth is partially pulled down out of the socket.
• Gently replace the tooth into its socket and splint if needed.
• Have the patient bite down gently to ensure proper repositioning.
• Follow dental first aid measures.
• Consider...
- The tooth is partially pulled down out of the socket.
• Gently replace the tooth into its socket and splint if needed.
• Have the patient bite down gently to ensure proper repositioning.
• Follow dental first aid measures.
• Consider tooth extraction if pain is not manageable or infection develops.
extrusion, repositioning, splinting, dental first aid
Manage Intrusion of a Tooth
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
- The tooth is driven deeper into the socket.
• Use dental first aid measures to manage the injury.
• Long-term survival of the tooth is generally poor.
• Consider tooth extraction if pain is not manageable or infection develops.
intrusion, deep socket, dental first aid, tooth extraction
Manage Lateral Luxation of a Tooth
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
- The tooth is intact but the root has been displaced, often indicated by a bulge in the gum tissue.
• There may be a high metallic ring on percussion.
• Reposition the tooth into its normal position by **pushing the root back into the...
- The tooth is intact but the root has been displaced, often indicated by a bulge in the gum tissue.
• There may be a high metallic ring on percussion.
• Reposition the tooth into its normal position by pushing the root back into the socket while pulling out on the distal portion using a teeter-totter motion.
• If needed, splint the tooth in place.
• Consider tooth extraction if pain is not manageable or infection develops.
lateral luxation, root displacement, teeter-totter motion, splinting
Manage Soft Tissue Injuries in the Oral Cavity
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
- Clean and thoroughly irrigate the wound with saline.
• Close wounds as soon as possible, preferably using dissolving sutures.
• If unable to close within 12 hours, wait until 5 days when bacterial counts are lower before...
- Clean and thoroughly irrigate the wound with saline.
• Close wounds as soon as possible, preferably using dissolving sutures.
• If unable to close within 12 hours, wait until 5 days when bacterial counts are lower before closing.
• Carefully repair the vermilion border of the lip.
• For full-thickness cheek wounds:
- Close the mucus membrane from the inside first.
- Then close the muscle and skin from the outside in standard fashion.
• Use two sets of instruments to minimize contamination with oral flora.
• Follow standard dental first aid measures.
soft tissue injury, oral cavity, wound closure, dissolving sutures
Manage a Complicated Crown +/- Root Fracture
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
- Remove any fragments or pieces from the tooth.
• Thoroughly flush the area with saline.
• If the pulp has been exposed for more than 24 hours, remove about 2 mm of the pulp tissue.
• Seal the exposed pulp using Dycal, IRM, **Glass...
- Remove any fragments or pieces from the tooth.
• Thoroughly flush the area with saline.
• If the pulp has been exposed for more than 24 hours, remove about 2 mm of the pulp tissue.
• Seal the exposed pulp using Dycal, IRM, Glass Ionomer, or wax.
• Construct a smooth surface to avoid irritation and food trapping.
• Consider tooth extraction if pain is not manageable or infection develops.
complicated crown fracture, pulp exposure, dycal, irm, tooth extraction
Manage a Root Fracture Below the Gum Line
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
- Remove all fragments from the affected area.
• If the entire crown is broken away, do not attempt to extract the roots and apex.
• Reposition the tooth and stabilize it using splinting techniques, such as wire & brace bar or adhesive...
- Remove all fragments from the affected area.
• If the entire crown is broken away, do not attempt to extract the roots and apex.
• Reposition the tooth and stabilize it using splinting techniques, such as wire & brace bar or adhesive ribbon.
• Allow a minimum of three months for bone healing.
• Seal any exposed pulp tissue as described above.
• Consider tooth extraction if pain is not manageable or infection develops.
root fracture, splinting, bone healing, pulp sealing
Managing Bleeding from a Tooth Socket
High Priority Source: Where There is No Dentist
Procedure:
1. Apply direct pressure to the bleeding area using gauze or a clean cloth.
2. If bleeding persists, use a sterile suture to close the socket (refer to proper suturing techniques).
3. Monitor for signs of excessive bleeding or...
Procedure:
1. Apply direct pressure to the bleeding area using gauze or a clean cloth.
2. If bleeding persists, use a sterile suture to close the socket (refer to proper suturing techniques).
3. Monitor for signs of excessive bleeding or infection (e.g., increased swelling, fever).
4. Administer antibiotics if prescribed by a healthcare provider to prevent infection.
5. Keep the area clean and avoid disturbing the socket until bleeding stops.
bleeding,socket,suture,infection,antibiotics
Stopping Bleeding from a Tooth Socket
High Priority Source: Where There is No Dentist
Steps to stop the bleeding:

1. Remove the clot with cotton tweezers.
2. Ask the person to bite on a piece of cotton.

See page 118 for further instructions.
tooth socket bleeding,clot removal,dental first aid
Treating a Broken Tooth
High Priority Source: Where There is No Dentist
Procedure:
1. Assess the severity of the break (e.g., enamel only vs. pulp exposure).
2. Clean the broken area gently with water or antiseptic.
3. Apply a temporary filling material if possible to protect exposed pulp.
4. Administer analgesics...
Procedure:
1. Assess the severity of the break (e.g., enamel only vs. pulp exposure).
2. Clean the broken area gently with water or antiseptic.
3. Apply a temporary filling material if possible to protect exposed pulp.
4. Administer analgesics like acetaminophen or aspirin for pain relief, following correct dosages:
- Acetaminophen: 15-20 mg/kg every 4-6 hours as needed.
- Aspirin: 10-15 mg/kg every 4-6 hours as needed (do not hold against teeth).
5. Seek professional dental care as soon as possible for permanent restoration.
broken tooth,pain relief,temporary filling,dental care
Treating a Knocked-Out Baby Tooth
High Priority Source: Where There is No Dentist
Procedure:
1. Locate the knocked-out baby tooth and assess for damage.
2. Do not attempt to reinsert the tooth unless it is a permanent tooth.
3. Clean the area gently with water or antiseptic.
4. Monitor for signs of infection (e.g., swelling,...
Procedure:
1. Locate the knocked-out baby tooth and assess for damage.
2. Do not attempt to reinsert the tooth unless it is a permanent tooth.
3. Clean the area gently with water or antiseptic.
4. Monitor for signs of infection (e.g., swelling, fever).
5. Administer analgesics like acetaminophen or aspirin for pain relief, following correct dosages:
- Acetaminophen: 15-20 mg/kg every 4-6 hours as needed.
- Aspirin: 10-15 mg/kg every 4-6 hours as needed (do not hold against teeth).
6. Seek professional dental care if there are concerns about the development of permanent teeth.
knocked-out tooth,baby teeth,pain relief,dental care
Use Wax to Replace a Knocked-Out Tooth
High Priority Source: Where There is No Dentist
Wax can be used temporarily to replace a knocked-out tooth until professional dental care is available.

Steps:

1. Clean the area: Gently clean the mouth with water or antiseptic solution.
2. Apply wax: Use medical-grade wax or a...
Wax can be used temporarily to replace a knocked-out tooth until professional dental care is available.

Steps:

1. Clean the area: Gently clean the mouth with water or antiseptic solution.
2. Apply wax: Use medical-grade wax or a suitable alternative to cover the exposed socket and provide comfort.
3. Avoid pressure: Ensure that the wax does not cause additional discomfort or interfere with speech.

Note: This is only a temporary measure and should be followed by professional dental care as soon as possible.
wax, knocked-out tooth, first aid, dental injury
Use Wire Ligature for Dental Procedures
High Priority Source: Where There is No Dentist
Wire ligature can be used in certain dental procedures to secure or stabilize a tooth.

Steps:

1. Prepare the wire: Cut a small length of wire and bend it into a loop or suitable shape.
2. Apply with care: Use the wire to gently secure...
Wire ligature can be used in certain dental procedures to secure or stabilize a tooth.

Steps:

1. Prepare the wire: Cut a small length of wire and bend it into a loop or suitable shape.
2. Apply with care: Use the wire to gently secure or support a loose or damaged tooth, ensuring not to cause additional harm.
3. Monitor for discomfort: Check regularly for signs of irritation or pain.

Note: This is only a temporary measure and should be followed by professional dental care as soon as possible.
wire ligature, dental procedure, first aid, dental injury
Handle a Loose Tooth with a Cracked Bone Around the Root
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Procedure for Handling a Loose Tooth with a Cracked Bone Around the Root

If the tooth is loose because the bone around its root is cracked (the bone moves when you push against the tooth):

1. Do not take out the tooth, as doing so may...
Procedure for Handling a Loose Tooth with a Cracked Bone Around the Root

If the tooth is loose because the bone around its root is cracked (the bone moves when you push against the tooth):

1. Do not take out the tooth, as doing so may cause the bone to come out with it.
2. Instead, hold the tooth with wires (see page 110).
cracked bone, loose tooth, dental stabilization
Manage Injuries to Primary (Baby) Teeth
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
- These are generally not repaired unless needed for the patient’s comfort.
• Follow dental first aid measures.
• Consider tooth extraction if pain is not manageable or infection develops.
baby teeth, primary teeth, dental first aid, tooth extraction
Manage Periapical Inflammation (Root Apex Inflammation)
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Periapical inflammation is inflammation at the root base of a tooth without infection. The affected tooth may protrude slightly and cause pain with chewing. There is usually no external swelling.

### Management:
• Follow **standard dental first...
Periapical inflammation is inflammation at the root base of a tooth without infection. The affected tooth may protrude slightly and cause pain with chewing. There is usually no external swelling.

### Management:
• Follow standard dental first aid procedures, including:
- Oral anti-inflammatories and pain medications
- Cold packs
- Saline gargles
- Soft diet
periapical inflammation,dental root inflammation,standard dental first aid
Manage Pulpitis (Toothache) with Standard Dental First Aid
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Pulpitis is inflammation of the dental pulp, causing toothache that may radiate to other teeth. The affected tooth is usually not sensitive to percussion or palpation but may be sensitive to heat, cold, or sweets.

### Management:
1. Administer...
Pulpitis is inflammation of the dental pulp, causing toothache that may radiate to other teeth. The affected tooth is usually not sensitive to percussion or palpation but may be sensitive to heat, cold, or sweets.

### Management:
1. Administer oral anti-inflammatories (e.g., ibuprofen) and pain medications (e.g., acetaminophen).
2. Apply local nerve blocks if available and appropriate.
3. Use cold packs on the affected area to reduce inflammation and pain.
4. Rinse with saline gargles to soothe the mouth.
5. Maintain a soft diet to avoid further irritation of the tooth.

This is referred to as standard dental first aid and applies to multiple conditions.
pulpitis,toothache,dental pain,standard dental first aid
Manage Subluxation & Concussion of a Tooth
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
- In subluxation, the tooth is abnormally loose due to damage to the periodontal ligament and gingiva.
• In concussion, the tooth is tender but not loose.
• Avoid chewing on the affected tooth.
• If the tooth is excessively loose, consider...
- In subluxation, the tooth is abnormally loose due to damage to the periodontal ligament and gingiva.
• In concussion, the tooth is tender but not loose.
• Avoid chewing on the affected tooth.
• If the tooth is excessively loose, consider splinting it in place.
• Follow dental first aid measures.
subluxation, concussion, splinting, periodontal ligament
Removing Blood from the Mouth
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Use wet cotton gauze to wipe away the old blood from inside the mouth. Then you can see where it is coming from. Treat the cause of the bleeding.
blood in mouth,oral bleeding,dental first aid
Assess and Manage a Broken Tooth
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Signs of a broken tooth:

• Pain when breathing air or drinking water.
• Blood from the gums around the tooth.
• Tooth moves when you touch it.

When to take out the broken tooth:

• If its nerve is not covered, and no one can give...
Signs of a broken tooth:

• Pain when breathing air or drinking water.
• Blood from the gums around the tooth.
• Tooth moves when you touch it.

When to take out the broken tooth:

• If its nerve is not covered, and no one can give special root canal treatment. Germs from the saliva have already gone inside the tooth and started a small infection.
• If its root is broken: To see if it is broken, push gently against the tooth as you feel the bone around its roots. The tooth’s root probably is broken if the tooth moves but the bone does not. The root probably is not broken if both the tooth and bone move. However, the bone around the roots may be broken (page 109).

When to save a broken tooth:

• If the nerve is still covered and the root is not broken.

How to save a broken tooth:

• Use a file on the sharp edges around the break. This makes them smooth so they do not cut the tongue.
• Later, an experienced dental worker who has the equipment can cover the broken part with a cap or a filling.
• Until this is possible, tell the person how to protect the tooth:
- Give the tooth a rest. Use other teeth to eat.
- Do not drink things that are very hot or cold, and do not eat spicy food.
- Watch the tooth. See if it changes color (gets darker). Also watch the gums near the root. See if a sore (gum bubble) develops.
• A dark tooth and gum bubble are signs that the tooth is dying. Take it out, unless you can give special nerve treatment.
broken tooth, root canal, nerve treatment, dental injury, tooth extraction
Handle a Loose Tooth Caused by a New Permanent Tooth Growing Under It
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Procedure for Handling a Loose Tooth Due to a New Permanent Tooth

If the tooth is loose because a new permanent tooth is growing under it:

1. Tell the mother and child what is happening.
2. If the loose baby tooth is hurting the child,...
Procedure for Handling a Loose Tooth Due to a New Permanent Tooth

If the tooth is loose because a new permanent tooth is growing under it:

1. Tell the mother and child what is happening.
2. If the loose baby tooth is hurting the child, pull out the loose baby tooth.
loose tooth, baby tooth, permanent tooth growth
Manage Aphthous Ulcers (Oral Mucosal Lesions)
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Aphthous ulcers are painful lesions on oral mucosa that last 7–15 days and may be triggered by trauma or stress. There are often multiple ulcers.

### Management:
• Follow standard dental first aid, including:
- Oral...
Aphthous ulcers are painful lesions on oral mucosa that last 7–15 days and may be triggered by trauma or stress. There are often multiple ulcers.

### Management:
• Follow standard dental first aid, including:
- Oral anti-inflammatories and pain medications
- Soft diet
• Apply topical steroids (e.g., hydrocortisone cream) to the affected area to shorten healing time.
aphthous ulcers,oral ulcers,topical steroids,dental first aid
Manage Muscle Pain & Spasm in the Jaw (Chewing Muscles)
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Muscle pain and spasm can occur due to teeth grinding, jaw clenching, or heavy chewing.

### Management:
1. Provide muscle rest by avoiding hard or chewy foods.
2. Maintain a soft diet.
3. Administer oral anti-inflammatories (e.g.,...
Muscle pain and spasm can occur due to teeth grinding, jaw clenching, or heavy chewing.

### Management:
1. Provide muscle rest by avoiding hard or chewy foods.
2. Maintain a soft diet.
3. Administer oral anti-inflammatories (e.g., ibuprofen) to reduce pain and inflammation.
jaw muscle pain,muscle spasm,dental first aid

Burn Treatment (25) -

Treatment for Deep Burns (Third Degree) and Large Burn Areas
Critical Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

1. Take the person to a health center immediately if they have deep burns that destroy the skin and expose raw or charred flesh, or if the burn covers large areas of the body.
2. In the meantime:
- Wrap the burned part with a...
Procedure:

1. Take the person to a health center immediately if they have deep burns that destroy the skin and expose raw or charred flesh, or if the burn covers large areas of the body.
2. In the meantime:
- Wrap the burned part with a very clean cloth or towel moistened with clean water.
3. If it is impossible to get medical help, treat the burn as described above.
4. If no Vaseline is available, leave the burn in the open air and cover it only with a loose cotton cloth or sheet to protect it from dust and flies.
5. Keep the cloth very clean and change it each time it gets dirty with liquid or blood from the burn.
6. Give an antibiotic as needed.

Notes:
• Deep burns are always serious and require immediate medical attention.
• Never apply grease, fat, hides, coffee, herbs, or feces on a burn.
deep burns, emergency transport, wound covering
Cool and Cover Burns Immediately for Burn Victims
Critical Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Action: If you or someone else is a burn victim:

• Call 9-1-1 immediately.
• Cool the burns by running them under cool (not cold) water for at least 10 minutes or until medical help arrives.
• Cover the burns with a sterile,...
Action: If you or someone else is a burn victim:

• Call 9-1-1 immediately.
• Cool the burns by running them under cool (not cold) water for at least 10 minutes or until medical help arrives.
• Cover the burns with a sterile, non-adhesive bandage or clean cloth to reduce the risk of infection and further injury.
burn treatment, cool burns, first aid for burns, emergency burn care
Extinguishing a Fire on Clothing
Critical Source: the ten bushcraft books
Procedure:

1. If clothing catches fire, do not panic or run. Running can cause the flames to spread due to air feeding the fire.
2. Beat out the flames with your hands or roll in the dirt. This helps smother the fire and reduce...
Procedure:

1. If clothing catches fire, do not panic or run. Running can cause the flames to spread due to air feeding the fire.
2. Beat out the flames with your hands or roll in the dirt. This helps smother the fire and reduce burns.
3. Better still, grab a blanket or rug and roll in it. This method is more effective at extinguishing the fire and minimizing injury.
4. If possible, remove the burning clothing immediately.

Warning: Severe burns can occur if you run, so staying calm and using proper techniques to put out the flames is critical.
clothing fire, burn treatment, rolling in dirt, blanket
Putting Out Burning Clothing
Critical Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. If Your Own Clothing Catches Fire:
- Do Not Run for Help: Running will fan the flames and make the situation worse.
- Lie Down and Roll: Immediately lie down and roll over as tightly as possible in an overcoat,...
Procedure:

1. If Your Own Clothing Catches Fire:
- Do Not Run for Help: Running will fan the flames and make the situation worse.
- Lie Down and Roll: Immediately lie down and roll over as tightly as possible in an overcoat, blanket, or rug to smother the fire.

2. If No Covering Is Available:
- Roll Over Slowly: Lie down and roll over slowly while using your hands to beat out the flames.

3. If Someone Else's Clothing Catches Fire:
- Throw Them to the Ground: Help the person lie on the ground immediately.
- Smother the Fire: Use a coat, blanket, or rug to smother the flames and prevent further burning.
burning clothing, fire safety, first aid, burn treatment
Seek Medical Assistance for Burn Injuries
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Notify medical personnel immediately.
2. Provide a detailed account of the burn type (chemical, electrical, WP, laser), severity, and any first aid administered.
medical assistance, burn treatment, first aid
Assess and Treat Burns on an Expedition
High Priority Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Procedure:

1. Assessment:
- Identify the type of burn:
- *Superficial burns:* Redness, swelling, tenderness (e.g., mild sunburn or scald from hot water).
- *Partial-thickness burns:* Painful, red, raw skin with blisters.
-...
Procedure:

1. Assessment:
- Identify the type of burn:
- *Superficial burns:* Redness, swelling, tenderness (e.g., mild sunburn or scald from hot water).
- *Partial-thickness burns:* Painful, red, raw skin with blisters.
- *Full-thickness burns:* Pale, waxy, or charred skin with loss of sensation.
2. Immediate Treatment:
- Halt the burning process by dousing the burn with copious amounts of cold water.
- Lie the casualty down and clean the burn carefully without removing adherent burnt clothing.
- Drain large blisters using a sterile needle inserted at the edge of the blister, but do not remove the skin.
3. Apply Antiseptic:
- Use Flamazine cream (silver sulphadiazine) or Bactroban cream to reduce infection risk.
4. Dress the Burn:
- Cover with a protective layer such as plastic kitchen clingfilm or a polythene glove for hand burns.
5. Monitor and Evacuate:
- Change dressings every one or two days, being aware that each change increases infection risk.
- Arrange urgent removal to hospital if the burn is extensive (use the rule of nines to estimate surface area affected) or full-thickness.

Notes:
• Full-thickness burns require skin grafting and must be evacuated for specialist care.
• The rule of nines can help estimate the percentage of body surface burned, with a patient’s palm representing approximately 1% of the body surface area.
burn treatment,burn classification,expedition burns,rule of nines
Cleaning and Dressing Burn Wounds
High Priority Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Procedure:

1. Gently wash the burn with tepid water and mild soap to remove debris.
2. Pat dry the area carefully.
3. Remove the skin from blisters that have popped open, but do not intentionally pop blisters unless necessary for...
Procedure:

1. Gently wash the burn with tepid water and mild soap to remove debris.
2. Pat dry the area carefully.
3. Remove the skin from blisters that have popped open, but do not intentionally pop blisters unless necessary for function of hands or feet.
4. Apply a thin layer of antibiotic ointment to the burn site.
5. Cover the burn with Second Skin (if small enough), or use a thin layer of gauze or clean, dry clothing.
6. Burns on the face, neck, and hands may be left open after applying silvadene ointment.

Important Notes:
• Do not use an occlusive dressing to cover burns.
• Covering the wound reduces pain and evaporative losses.
clean burn wounds, antibiotic ointment, dressing burns
Initial Care for Burn Injuries
High Priority Source: Emergency War Surgery
Procedure:

1. Stop the burning process immediately:
- Extinguish and remove any burning clothing.
- Remove the patient from a burning vehicle or building if necessary.
- For electrical injuries, ensure the power source is turned...
Procedure:

1. Stop the burning process immediately:
- Extinguish and remove any burning clothing.
- Remove the patient from a burning vehicle or building if necessary.
- For electrical injuries, ensure the power source is turned off before attempting to move the patient, taking care to avoid injury to the rescuer.
- Wash chemical agents from the skin with copious water lavage.
2. Ensure airway patency, control any hemorrhage, and splint fractures if present.
3. Remove all constricting items such as rings, bracelets, wristwatches, belts, and boots to prevent complications from swelling.
- Do not remove clothing unless the injury was caused by a chemical agent; in that case, remove all contaminated clothing.
4. Cover the patient with a clean sheet and blanket if appropriate to maintain body temperature and reduce contamination during transport.
5. Establish intravenous access through unburned skin if possible; if not, use burned skin or consider intraosseous access as an alternative.
6. Begin resuscitation with lactated Ringer’s solution (LR) or a similar fluid replacement solution and continue this during evacuation.
7. Dress white phosphorus-injured patients with saline-soaked dressings to prevent reignition of the phosphorus by contact with air.
burn injuries,first aid,resuscitation,lactated Ringer’s solution,white phosphorus
Apply a Dressing to a Burn
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
### Notes:
• If the burn is caused by white phosphorus, the dressing must be wet.

### Warnings:
• Do not place the dressing over the face or genital area.
• Do not break the blisters.
• **Do not apply grease or ointments to the...
### Notes:
• If the burn is caused by white phosphorus, the dressing must be wet.

### Warnings:
• Do not place the dressing over the face or genital area.
