Objective: Apply an occlusive dressing to an open chest wound to prevent air from entering the chest cavity and to stabilize the casualty.

### Steps to Follow:
1. Evaluate the Casualty:
- Check for signs of a chest injury, such as difficulty breathing, pain in the chest or shoulder area, and a sucking sound when breathing.
- Be prepared to perform first aid measures including clearing the airway, rescue breathing, treatment for shock, and/or bleeding control.

2. Expose the Wound:
- If appropriate, cut or remove clothing around the wound to expose it.
- DO NOT remove clothing that is stuck to the wound as this may cause additional injury.
- DO NOT attempt to clean the wound.

3. Check for Entry and Exit Wounds:
- Examine the casualty for both entry and exit wounds.
- If there are two wounds, treat them in order of severity (heavier bleeding or larger wound first).
- Use strips of cloth, such as a torn T-shirt, to improvise dressings for additional wounds if needed.

4. Listen for Sucking Sounds:
- Listen for sucking sounds that may indicate the chest wall is punctured.

5. Handle Impaled Objects Carefully:
- If there is an object impaled in the wound, DO NOT remove it.
- Apply a dressing around the object and use additional improvised bulky materials (use the cleanest available) to build up the area around the object.
- Use a supporting bandage over the bulky materials to hold them in place.

6. Avoid Removing Protective Clothing in Chemical Environments:
- If the casualty is in a chemical environment, DO NOT remove protective clothing.
- Apply dressings over the protective clothing.

7. Prepare the Plastic Wrapper for an Occlusive Dressing:
- In cases of sucking chest wounds, use the plastic wrapper with the field dressing to create an occlusive dressing.
- If a plastic wrapper is not available, use cellophane, foil, the casualty’s poncho, or similar material that is wide enough to extend at least 2 inches beyond the edges of the wound in all directions.

8. Open the Plastic Wrapper:
- Tear open one end of the plastic wrapper covering the field dressing carefully without destroying it.
- DO NOT touch the inside of the wrapper.

9. Remove the Inner Packet (Field Dressing):
- Remove the inner packet containing the field dressing from the wrapper.

10. Complete Tearing Open the Wrapper:
- Complete tearing open the empty plastic wrapper to create a flat surface for use as an occlusive dressing.

11. Place the Plastic Wrapper Over the Wound:
- Place the inside surface of the plastic wrapper directly over the wound when the casualty exhales, and hold it in place.
- The casualty may help hold the plastic wrapper in place if they are able to do so.

12. Apply the Field Dressing to the Wound:
- Use your free hand to shake open the field dressing (Figure 3-3).
- Place the white side of the dressing on top of the plastic wrapper covering the wound (Figure 3-4).

13. Secure the Plastic Wrapper and Dressing:
- Have the casualty breathe normally.
- While maintaining pressure on the dressing, grasp one tail of the field dressing with your other hand and wrap it around the casualty’s back.
- If tape is available, use it to secure three sides of the plastic wrapper to the chest wall. Leave one side untaped for emergency air escape in case air builds up in the chest.
- If tape is not available, secure the wrapper on three sides with field dressing and leave the fourth side as a flap.

14. Wrap the Other Tail:
- Wrap the other tail of the field dressing in the opposite direction, bringing both tails over the dressing (Figure 3-5).

### Important Notes:
• Use the casualty’s field dressing, not your own.
• Ensure that the occlusive dressing is wide enough to extend at least 2 inches beyond the wound on all sides.
• If there are two wounds, treat the more serious one first.
• Never remove an object impaled in a wound unless it is a medical professional doing so.
open chest wound, occlusive dressing, first aid, field dressing, sucking chest wound, impaled object, chemical environment, protective clothing
Identify and Assess Chest Wounds
Critical Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Purpose: Recognize signs of chest injuries to provide timely first aid.

Signs and Symptoms:
• Pain in the chest or shoulder area
• Difficulty breathing
• Abnormal chest movement (not rising normally)
• Coughing up blood
• Rapid or weak...
Purpose: Recognize signs of chest injuries to provide timely first aid.

Signs and Symptoms:
• Pain in the chest or shoulder area
• Difficulty breathing
• Abnormal chest movement (not rising normally)
• Coughing up blood
• Rapid or weak heartbeat
• Sucking sound when breathing (indicative of a punctured chest wall)

Actions:
1. Observe the casualty for any signs of injury.
2. Check if there is an entry and exit wound; treat both wounds if present, starting with the more serious one.
3. Listen for sucking sounds to determine if the chest wall is punctured.
4. Do not remove clothing that is stuck to the wound or attempt to clean it.
5. If in a chemical environment, do not remove protective clothing; apply dressings over it.
chest wound, sucking chest wound, punctured lung, first aid, trauma assessment
Manage Chest Wounds from Trauma or Injury
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Assess the casualty for chest injuries caused by blunt trauma, bullet wounds, stab wounds, or falls.
2. These injuries can be life-threatening and may cause rapid death if not treated promptly.
3. Monitor for signs of internal...
Procedure:

1. Assess the casualty for chest injuries caused by blunt trauma, bullet wounds, stab wounds, or falls.
2. These injuries can be life-threatening and may cause rapid death if not treated promptly.
3. Monitor for signs of internal bleeding or compromised breathing (e.g., labored respiration, cyanosis).
4. Provide immediate first aid to stabilize the casualty and prevent further injury.
5. Seek medical assistance immediately.
chest wounds, trauma, first aid
Position Casualty After Applying Dressing for Chest Wound
Critical Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. Position the casualty on the injured side or in a sitting position, whichever makes breathing easier.
positioning, chest wound, first aid
Position Casualty with Chest Injury
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Steps to Position a Casualty with a Chest Injury:

1. Place the casualty on his injured side or in a sitting position, whichever makes breathing easier (Figure 3-7). This helps reduce pressure on the injured area and facilitates easier...
Steps to Position a Casualty with a Chest Injury:

1. Place the casualty on his injured side or in a sitting position, whichever makes breathing easier (Figure 3-7). This helps reduce pressure on the injured area and facilitates easier breathing.
2. Ensure that the casualty is comfortable and stable in this position.

Additional Notes:
• If the casualty’s condition worsens after placing the dressing (e.g., difficulty breathing, shortness of breath, restlessness, or blueness/grayness of the skin), quickly lift or remove, and then replace the occlusive dressing.
positioning,casualty,chest injury,breathing
Seal Chest Wall Holes
Critical Source: NATO Emergency War Surgery
Objective: Prevent air from entering the pleural space through open chest wounds.

Procedure:
1. Assess the wound for size and depth.
2. Apply an occlusive dressing (e.g., plastic wrap, sterile gauze) over the wound to seal it.
3....
Objective: Prevent air from entering the pleural space through open chest wounds.

Procedure:
1. Assess the wound for size and depth.
2. Apply an occlusive dressing (e.g., plastic wrap, sterile gauze) over the wound to seal it.
3. Secure the dressing with tape or a bandage to prevent air from entering the pleural cavity.
4. Monitor the casualty for signs of tension pneumothorax (e.g., decreased breath sounds, tracheal deviation).

Warnings:
• Do not apply pressure directly over the wound if there is suspicion of underlying lung injury.
• If a chest tube is required later, ensure that the dressing does not interfere with its placement.
chest wound, occlusive dressing, tension pneumothorax, pleural space
Stabilize a Flail Chest Injury
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
If a chest segment is moving paradoxically when the casualty breathes (a flail chest)

• Stabilize the injured segment by laying the casualty on the injured side.

• Or, strap the chest by placing a large adhesive bandage over the injured...
If a chest segment is moving paradoxically when the casualty breathes (a flail chest)

• Stabilize the injured segment by laying the casualty on the injured side.

• Or, strap the chest by placing a large adhesive bandage over the injured ribs.

• The bandage should cover the injured area well behind and in front, as well as the ribs above and below, to stabilize the segment.

• The strapping should not be too tight, to avoid restricting the inspiration movement.
flail chest, stabilization, adhesive bandage
Examine for Signs of Chest Injury
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

• Look:

- Conscious casualty sitting. Breathing is rapid, shallow and uneven, or difficult and painful.

- Casualty is restless and “fighting for air”.

- Bluish colour of lips, nail beds and skin.

- Visible wound on the...
Procedure:

• Look:

- Conscious casualty sitting. Breathing is rapid, shallow and uneven, or difficult and painful.

- Casualty is restless and “fighting for air”.

- Bluish colour of lips, nail beds and skin.

- Visible wound on the chest (front and/or back); swelling or bruising.

- Paradoxical movement of a chest segment during breathing. Two or more ribs are fractured in two places creating a floating segment. The segment can be seen to move opposite to the rest of the chest wall. This is called a flail chest.

- Spitting or coughing up frothy, bright red blood.
chest injury signs, flail chest, breathing difficulty
Palpation of the Chest for Injuries
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:
• Put one hand on the middle of the upper part of the casualty’s chest and press gently.
• Ask the casualty to cough.
• Put one hand on each side of the casualty’s chest and gently compress.

Later during examination:
• Turn the...
Procedure:
• Put one hand on the middle of the upper part of the casualty’s chest and press gently.
• Ask the casualty to cough.
• Put one hand on each side of the casualty’s chest and gently compress.

Later during examination:
• Turn the casualty over to look for injuries on the sides or back of the chest.
chest palpation, injury assessment, rib fracture detection
Position the Casualty for Breathing Comfort
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure for Positioning the Casualty for Breathing Comfort:

1. Position the casualty on the injured side or in a sitting position, whichever makes breathing easier.

Important Notes:
• This positioning helps reduce pain and...
Procedure for Positioning the Casualty for Breathing Comfort:

1. Position the casualty on the injured side or in a sitting position, whichever makes breathing easier.

Important Notes:
• This positioning helps reduce pain and facilitates easier breathing.
• Monitor the casualty's respiratory rate and effort to ensure adequate oxygenation.
casualty positioning, breathing comfort, first aid
Positioning Casualty with Chest Injuries for Comfort and Breathing
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- Help the casualty to sit, leaning towards the injured side.
• Or in the lateral position, whichever makes breathing easier and less painful.
• Always put an unconscious person in the lateral recovery position, lying on the injured side.
positioning for chest injury, lateral recovery position
Strapping a Fractured Chest to Stabilize and Reduce Pain
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- Strap the chest using a wide adhesive tape to fully cover the injured ribs and the ribs above and below.
• Do not tape too tightly, to avoid limiting the inspiration movement.
• Strap the injured hemi-thorax only.
rib fracture strapping, chest stabilization
Managing a Broken Rib Injury
High Priority Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

1. Administer pain relief as needed:
- For adults: Take aspirin or acetaminophen (avoid aspirin for children).
2. Encourage deep breathing exercises: Take 4 to 5 deep breaths in a row every 2 hours until normal breathing...
Procedure:

1. Administer pain relief as needed:
- For adults: Take aspirin or acetaminophen (avoid aspirin for children).
2. Encourage deep breathing exercises: Take 4 to 5 deep breaths in a row every 2 hours until normal breathing is restored.
3. Avoid splinting or binding the chest, as this may restrict breathing and slow healing.
4. Monitor for complications:
- If a rib breaks through the skin, coughs blood, or develops breathing difficulties (other than pain), seek immediate medical help.
5. Use antibiotics if necessary: In cases of suspected infection or open fractures, use an antibiotic such as dicloxacillin, clindamycin, or ciprofloxacin.
6. Rest and allow time for healing—it may take months before the pain is completely gone.
broken rib,pain management,breathing exercises,chest injury

Burns (21) -

Act When Clothes Take Fire
Critical Source: The Household Cyclopedia
How to Act when the Clothes take Fire:

Three persons out of 4 would rush right up to the burning individual, and begin to paw with their hands without any definite aim. It is useless to tell the victim to do this or that, or call for water. In...
How to Act when the Clothes take Fire:

Three persons out of 4 would rush right up to the burning individual, and begin to paw with their hands without any definite aim. It is useless to tell the victim to do this or that, or call for water. In fact, it is generally best to say not a word, but seize a blanket from a bed, or a cloak, or any woollen fabric -- if none is at hand, take any woollen material -- hold the corners as far apart as you can, stretch them out higher than your head, and, running boldly to the person, make a motion of clasping in the arms, most about the shoulders. This instantly smothers the fire and saves the face. The next instant throw the unfortunate person on the floor. This is an additional safety to the face and breath, and any remnant of flame can be put out more leisurely. The next instant, immerse the burnt part in cold water and all pain will cease with the rapidity of lightning. Next, get some common flour, remove from the water, and cover the burnt parts with an inch thickness of flour, if possible; put the patient to bed, and do all that is possible to soothe until the physician arrives. Let the flour remain until it falls off itself, when a beautiful new skin will be found. Unless the burns are deep, no other application is needed. The dry flour for burns is the most admirable remedy ever proposed, and the information ought to be imparted to all. The principle of its action is that, like the water, it causes instant and perfect relief from pain, by totally excluding the air from the injured parts. Spanish whiting and cold water, of a mushy consistency, are preferred by some. Dredge on the flour until no more will stick, and cover with cotton batting.
clothes on fire, burns first aid, flour treatment, fire emergency
First Aid for Heat Burns (Including Napalm and Metal Incendiaries)
Critical Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure for First Aid for Heat Burns:

1. Cool the burn immediately with large amounts of cool, clean water for at least 10–20 minutes.
2. Cover the burn loosely with a sterile dressing or clean cloth to protect it from infection.
3. Do not...
Procedure for First Aid for Heat Burns:

1. Cool the burn immediately with large amounts of cool, clean water for at least 10–20 minutes.
2. Cover the burn loosely with a sterile dressing or clean cloth to protect it from infection.
3. Do not apply ice directly to the burn (can cause further tissue damage).
4. Do not break blisters or remove dead skin.
5. Avoid using greasy ointments, butter, or other substances on the burn.
6. Administer pain relief if available and appropriate (e.g., ibuprofen or acetaminophen).
7. Seek medical assistance as soon as possible, especially for severe burns or if signs of infection develop (e.g., increased redness, swelling, pus).
heat burn,treatment,first aid,napalm
Handle Phosphorus Burns with Special Precautions
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Safely manage burns caused by phosphorus to prevent further injury.

Procedure:
1. Avoid Contamination:
- Avoid direct contact with phosphorus particles or burning wounds.
2. Extinguish Flames:
- Extinguish any...
Objective: Safely manage burns caused by phosphorus to prevent further injury.

Procedure:
1. Avoid Contamination:
- Avoid direct contact with phosphorus particles or burning wounds.
2. Extinguish Flames:
- Extinguish any flames from the wound and cover it with water or a saline solution.
3. Remove Large Fragments (if possible):
- Use tools such as pliers to remove large, non-adherent phosphorus fragments and place them in a dish filled with water.
4. Apply Moist Dressings:
- Keep the burn area covered with moist dressings and ensure they remain wet at all times.

Notes: Phosphorus burns are extremely dangerous due to spontaneous combustion; avoid drying out the wound.
phosphorus burn care, first aid burns
Remove Casualty from Electrical Burn Source
Critical Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure for Removing a Casualty from an Electrical Burn Source

Step 1: Turn off the electrical current if possible. DO NOT attempt to turn off the electricity if the source is not close by.

Step 2: If the electricity cannot be...
Procedure for Removing a Casualty from an Electrical Burn Source

Step 1: Turn off the electrical current if possible. DO NOT attempt to turn off the electricity if the source is not close by.

Step 2: If the electricity cannot be turned off, wrap any _nonconductive_ material (dry rope, clothing, wood, and so forth) around the casualty’s back and shoulders and drag the casualty away from the electrical source. DO NOT make body-to-body contact with the casualty or touch any wires because you could also become an electrical burn casualty.

Important Notes:
• Synthetic materials such as nylon may melt and cause further injury.
• Speed is critical; do not waste unnecessary time.
electrical burn, remove casualty, nonconductive material, first aid
Take Precautions and Seek Medical Assistance for Burns
Critical Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure for Taking Precautions and Seeking Medical Assistance for Burns

Step 1: Take the following precautions:
• DO NOT place the dressing over the face or genital area.
• DO NOT break the blisters.
• DO NOT apply grease or...
Procedure for Taking Precautions and Seeking Medical Assistance for Burns

Step 1: Take the following precautions:
• DO NOT place the dressing over the face or genital area.
• DO NOT break the blisters.
• DO NOT apply grease or ointments to the burns.

Additional Precautions for Specific Burn Types:
• For electrical burns, check for both an entry and exit burn from the passage of electricity through the body. Exit burns may appear on any area of the body despite the location of the entry burn.
• For burns caused by wet or dry chemicals, flush the burns with large amounts of water and cover with a dry dressing.
• For burns caused by WP (White Phosphorus), flush the area with water, then cover with a wet material, dressing, or mud to exclude air and keep the WP particles from burning.
• For laser burns, apply a field dressing.

Step 2: If the casualty is conscious and not nauseated, give him small amounts of water.

Step 3: Notify medical personnel.
medical assistance, burn precautions, first aid
Assess Burn Severity Using DALO Criteria
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Determine the severity of a burn based on depth, area, location, and origin.

Procedure:
1. Depth (D): Assess how deep the burn is (first degree, second degree, or third degree).
2. Area (A): Estimate the surface area...
Objective: Determine the severity of a burn based on depth, area, location, and origin.

Procedure:
1. Depth (D): Assess how deep the burn is (first degree, second degree, or third degree).
2. Area (A): Estimate the surface area affected using the rule of 9s or by comparing to the casualty’s palm (approximately 1% of body surface area).
3. Location (L): Check if the burn is on critical areas such as the face, neck, over joints, circumferential burns on limbs or torso, or genitals.
4. Origin (O): Identify the source of the burn—flame, chemicals, electricity, or phosphorus.

Notes: Burns are more severe in children and elderly individuals.
burn severity, DALO criteria, burn assessment, first aid burns
Assess Burn Severity and Area Using the Rule of 9's
High Priority Source: Aids to Survival
Procedure:
1. Identify the burn severity:
- 1st degree burns: Superficial burns such as sunburn.
- 2nd degree burns: Partial thickness burns where blisters are present.
- 3rd degree burns: Full thickness with charred skin or...
Procedure:
1. Identify the burn severity:
- 1st degree burns: Superficial burns such as sunburn.
- 2nd degree burns: Partial thickness burns where blisters are present.
- 3rd degree burns: Full thickness with charred skin or white skin with a red edge.
2. Use the Rule of 9's to assess the percentage of body surface area affected:
- Arms: 9% x 2 = 18%
- Lower legs: 9% x 2 = 18%
- Upper legs: 9% x 2 = 18%
- Stomach: 9% x 1 = 9%
- Buttocks: 9% x 1 = 9%
- Chest: 9% x 1 = 9%
- Back: 9% x 1 = 9%
- Head: 9% x 1 = 9%
- Groin: 1% x 1 = 1%
3. Add the percentages to determine total burn area.

Note: This is important for determining the severity of burns and whether hospitalisation or intravenous fluid is required.
burn, rule of 9's, burn severity, body surface area
Clean and Protect a Burned Area
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Clean and protect the burned area to prevent infection.

Procedure:
1. Clean the Burn:
- Wash the burn gently with plenty of clean water (preferably running cold water if available).
2. Protect the Burn:
- Cover the...
Objective: Clean and protect the burned area to prevent infection.

Procedure:
1. Clean the Burn:
- Wash the burn gently with plenty of clean water (preferably running cold water if available).
2. Protect the Burn:
- Cover the burn with a clean dressing (sterile compress or Vaseline gauze, if available). Alternatively, use local treatments such as banana leaves.
3. Apply a Bandage:
- Use a bandage to keep the dressing in place and secure it.

Notes: Be gentle when handling burns; they can be very painful.
clean burn, protect burn, first aid burns
Examine Burned Areas for Critical Signs
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Identify signs that may indicate severe or life-threatening burns.

Procedure:
1. Look:
- Examine the surface and depth of the burn.
- Check for black traces of singeing around the nostrils.
- Look for circumferential...
Objective: Identify signs that may indicate severe or life-threatening burns.

Procedure:
1. Look:
- Examine the surface and depth of the burn.
- Check for black traces of singeing around the nostrils.
- Look for circumferential burns on limbs or torso, which can cause constriction.
- Observe if there are signs of difficulty in breathing.
2. Listen:
- Listen for signs of respiratory distress (e.g., wheezing, labored breathing).
3. Talk:
- Ask the casualty what happened, when, and how the burn occurred.

Notes: Burns on the face or neck may affect airway function; circumferential burns can compromise circulation.
burn examination, critical signs, first aid burns
Handle Burns on Hands or Feet with Special Care
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Provide appropriate care for burns on hands or feet to prevent complications.

Procedure:
1. Clean the Burn:
- Wash the burn gently with clean water.
2. Protect the Burn:
- Place the hand or foot in a clean plastic...
Objective: Provide appropriate care for burns on hands or feet to prevent complications.

Procedure:
1. Clean the Burn:
- Wash the burn gently with clean water.
2. Protect the Burn:
- Place the hand or foot in a clean plastic bag (use as a glove or sock).
3. Secure the Bag:
- Fix the bag loosely around the wrist or ankle.
4. Encourage Movement:
- Encourage the casualty to move their fingers or toes to prevent stiffness and improve circulation.

Notes: This technique helps reduce swelling and maintain mobility.
hand burn care, foot burn care, first aid burns
Hydrate and Keep the Casualty Warm After a Burn
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Prevent dehydration and maintain body temperature in a burn casualty.

Procedure:
1. Hydrate the Casualty:
- Give plenty of fluids to drink (e.g., water or oral rehydration solutions).
2. Keep Warm:
- Wrap the...
Objective: Prevent dehydration and maintain body temperature in a burn casualty.

Procedure:
1. Hydrate the Casualty:
- Give plenty of fluids to drink (e.g., water or oral rehydration solutions).
2. Keep Warm:
- Wrap the casualty in blankets or sheets to prevent hypothermia.

Notes: Burns can lead to significant fluid loss; hydration is critical.
hydrate burn casualty, keep warm, first aid burns
Manage Circumferential Burns to Prevent Constriction
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Handle circumferential burns to prevent constriction and complications.

Procedure:
1. Avoid Bandage Rolling:
- Do not roll the bandage around a limb if it is circumferential, as this may increase constriction.
2. **Monitor...
Objective: Handle circumferential burns to prevent constriction and complications.

Procedure:
1. Avoid Bandage Rolling:
- Do not roll the bandage around a limb if it is circumferential, as this may increase constriction.
2. Monitor for Constriction:
- Watch for signs of compromised circulation or breathing (e.g., swelling, pain, difficulty moving limbs).

Notes: Circumferential burns on the chest can compromise breathing; those on limbs can cut off blood flow.
circumferential burn care, first aid burns
Provide First Aid for Flash Burns in Armed Conflicts
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

1. Assess the burn severity:
- Determine if it is a first-degree (superficial), second-degree (partial thickness), or third-degree (full thickness) burn.
- Look for signs of shock, such as pale skin, rapid pulse, and...
Procedure:

1. Assess the burn severity:
- Determine if it is a first-degree (superficial), second-degree (partial thickness), or third-degree (full thickness) burn.
- Look for signs of shock, such as pale skin, rapid pulse, and confusion.

2. Cool the burn immediately:
- Run cool (not cold) water over the affected area for at least 10–20 minutes to reduce pain and swelling.
- Do not use ice or ice water, as this can cause further tissue damage.

3. Protect the wound:
- Cover the burn with a sterile dressing or clean cloth to prevent infection.
- Avoid applying any ointments, butter, or greasy substances to the burn.

4. Manage pain and shock:
- Administer pain relief if available (e.g., paracetamol or ibuprofen), unless contraindicated.
- Keep the casualty warm and lying down with their legs elevated if they are in shock.

5. Seek medical assistance immediately:
- Burns caused by napalm, phosphorus bombs, or magnesium flares require specialized treatment.
- If the burn covers more than 10% of the body surface area or involves the face, hands, feet, genitals, or major joints, seek professional help as soon as possible.
flash burns, first aid, napalm, phosphorus bombs, magnesium flares, burn treatment
Recognize Degrees of Burn Depth
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Identify the depth of a burn (first, second, or third degree).

Procedure:
1. First Degree Burns:
- Appearance: Redness only.
- Symptoms: Pain and no blisters.
2. Second Degree Burns:
- Appearance:...
Objective: Identify the depth of a burn (first, second, or third degree).

Procedure:
1. First Degree Burns:
- Appearance: Redness only.
- Symptoms: Pain and no blisters.
2. Second Degree Burns:
- Appearance: Redness with blisters and a moist surface.
- Symptoms: Severe pain.
3. Third Degree Burns:
- Appearance: Dry, leathery or dark, dirty-white skin.
- Symptoms: No pain due to nerve damage (insensitive).

Notes: Third-degree burns require immediate medical attention.
burn depth, first aid burns, degrees of burn
Treat Burns with Cold Water and Prevent Infection
High Priority Source: Aids to Survival
Procedure:
1. Apply cold water to the burn for any thickness of burn.
2. Do not use butter, as it introduces bacteria and keeps blisters intact.
3. Apply an antibiotic cream or Betadine ointment to prevent infection.
4. Monitor the burn site...
Procedure:
1. Apply cold water to the burn for any thickness of burn.
2. Do not use butter, as it introduces bacteria and keeps blisters intact.
3. Apply an antibiotic cream or Betadine ointment to prevent infection.
4. Monitor the burn site for signs of infection, which can convert a partial thickness burn into a full thickness burn.

Note: Infection is a major concern in burns and must be prevented at all costs.
burn treatment, cold water, infection prevention, antibiotic cream
Estimate Burn Surface Area Using the Rule of 9s
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Estimate the surface area of a burn using the rule of 9s.

Procedure:
1. Use the following percentages to estimate body surface area affected by burns:
- Head and neck: 9%
- Each arm (front and back): 9% each
- Trunk...
Objective: Estimate the surface area of a burn using the rule of 9s.

Procedure:
1. Use the following percentages to estimate body surface area affected by burns:
- Head and neck: 9%
- Each arm (front and back): 9% each
- Trunk (front and back): 18% each
- Each leg (front and back): 9% each
- Genitals: 1%
2. For smaller burns, use the casualty’s palm as a reference (approximately 1% of body surface area).

Notes: This method is useful for quick estimation in field conditions.
rule of 9s, burn surface area, first aid burns
Maintain Clean Dressings for Burns in Delayed Evacuation Situations
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Keep burn dressings clean and change them regularly if evacuation is delayed.

Procedure:
1. Keep the Dressing Clean:
- Change the dressing every two days to prevent infection.
2. Soak Before Removal:
- Soak the...
Objective: Keep burn dressings clean and change them regularly if evacuation is delayed.

Procedure:
1. Keep the Dressing Clean:
- Change the dressing every two days to prevent infection.
2. Soak Before Removal:
- Soak the dressing with clean water or normal saline before removing it, as it may stick to the burn.
3. Use Local Treatments if Available:
- In some regions, local treatments such as honey or banana leaves may be used for burns.

Notes: Be cautious when changing dressings; they can be very painful.
clean burn dressing, first aid burns
Treat Burns
High Priority Source: The Complete Outdoorsmans Handbook
Procedure:

1. Avoid handling the affected area more than necessary.
2. Ensure your hands are clean before treating the burn.
3. Do not apply lotions or remove burnt clothing.
4. Cover the burn with a dry, sterile dressing from your first-aid...
Procedure:

1. Avoid handling the affected area more than necessary.
2. Ensure your hands are clean before treating the burn.
3. Do not apply lotions or remove burnt clothing.
4. Cover the burn with a dry, sterile dressing from your first-aid kit.
5. Bandage the burned area firmly while seeking medical help, but if blisters are present, bandage only lightly.

Notes:
• Do not break any blisters that may form on the burn.
burn treatment, first aid, burns
Cure for Burnt Finger from Lighting a Split Match
Reference Source: the ten bushcraft books
Quick relief for a burnt fingertip:
1. Grab the lobe of your ear with the burnt finger.
2. The natural oil on your ear will seal off the small burn from the air.
burnt finger,ear remedy,burn treatment
Check for Burns and Provide First Aid
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid + STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure:

1. Look carefully for reddened, blistered, or charred skin.
2. Check for singed clothing.
3. If burns are found, stop the evaluation and begin first aid procedures immediately.
4. Refer to paragraph 3-9 for...
Procedure:

1. Look carefully for reddened, blistered, or charred skin.
2. Check for singed clothing.
3. If burns are found, stop the evaluation and begin first aid procedures immediately.
4. Refer to paragraph 3-9 for information on giving first aid for burns.

Note: Burns to the upper torso and face may cause respiratory complications. Look for:
• Singed nose hair.
• Soot around the nostrils.
• Listen for abnormal breath sounds or difficulty breathing.

Steps for Providing First Aid for Burns:
1. Eliminate the source of the burn.
- Thermal burns: Remove the casualty from the source of the burn. If the casualty's clothing is on fire, cover the casualty with a field jacket or any large piece of nonsynthetic material and roll him/her on the ground to put out the flames.
- Caution: Synthetic materials, such as nylon, may melt and cause further injury.
- Electrical burns: If the casualty is in contact with an electrical source, turn the electricity off, if the switch is nearby. If the electricity cannot be turned off, use any nonconductive material (rope, clothing, or dry wood) to drag the casualty away from the source.
- Warning: Do not touch the casualty or the electrical source with your bare hands. You will be injured too!
- Warning: High voltage electrical burns from an electrical source or lightning may cause temporary uncons
burns, first aid, thermal burns, electrical burns, respiratory complications
First Aid for Napalm Burns
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure for Providing First Aid for Napalm Burns

1. Immediate Action:
- Remove the thickened fuel mixture from clothing and skin as soon as possible.
2. Recognize that napalm burns are severe and prolonged due to the thickened fuel...
Procedure for Providing First Aid for Napalm Burns

1. Immediate Action:
- Remove the thickened fuel mixture from clothing and skin as soon as possible.
2. Recognize that napalm burns are severe and prolonged due to the thickened fuel mixture clinging to clothing and body surfaces.
3. Treat napalm burns as other heat burns, following standard first aid procedures for thermal injuries:
- Cool the burn under running water for at least 10–15 minutes.
- Cover the burn with a sterile, non-adhesive dressing.
- Avoid breaking blisters or applying ointments.
4. Monitor for signs of inhalation injury (e.g., difficulty breathing, coughing, or burning smell in the air) caused by heat and irritating gases from napalm.
5. Medical Attention:
- Seek immediate medical assistance, as these burns may require specialized treatment and monitoring for lung damage.

Important Notes:
• Napalm can cause prolonged exposure and severe burns.
• Inhalation of fumes may lead to serious respiratory injuries requiring professional treatment.
napalm burns, first aid, thermal injury, inhalation injury, fuel mixture

Chemical Exposure (19) -

First Aid for Cyanogen Chloride Exposure
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. If you experience irritation of the eyes, nose, or throat, put on your protective mask immediately.
2. If a service member is unable to put on their own mask due to symptoms, **another service member should assist...
Procedure:

1. If you experience irritation of the eyes, nose, or throat, put on your protective mask immediately.
2. If a service member is unable to put on their own mask due to symptoms, another service member should assist immediately (buddy aid).
3. Ensure that the mask is properly sealed and worn at all times during exposure or suspected exposure.

Notes:
• Cyanogen chloride can cause immediate irritation and may lead to respiratory distress if not addressed quickly.
• Buddy aid is essential in ensuring timely protection.
cyanogen chloride, first aid, chemical exposure, buddy aid
First Aid for Hydrogen Cyanide Exposure
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. If you experience sudden stimulation of breathing or detect an odor like bitter almonds, put on your mask immediately.
2. Stop breathing if possible until the mask is securely in place, as hydrogen cyanide acts...
Procedure:

1. If you experience sudden stimulation of breathing or detect an odor like bitter almonds, put on your mask immediately.
2. Stop breathing if possible until the mask is securely in place, as hydrogen cyanide acts rapidly and can make it difficult to breathe or put on a mask.
3. If you are unable to put on your own mask due to the effects of the agent, another service member should assist immediately (buddy aid).

Notes:
• Hydrogen cyanide is extremely fast-acting; time is critical in response.
• Do not attempt to remove or adjust the mask once it is on.
hydrogen cyanide, first aid, chemical exposure, buddy aid
First Aid for Toxin Exposure (Yellow Rain)
Critical Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure for First Aid in Case of Toxin Exposure (e.g., Yellow Rain):

1. Upon recognition of an attack employing toxins, take the following actions immediately:
- Stop breathing and put on your protective mask with hood.
- Resume...
Procedure for First Aid in Case of Toxin Exposure (e.g., Yellow Rain):

1. Upon recognition of an attack employing toxins, take the following actions immediately:
- Stop breathing and put on your protective mask with hood.
- Resume breathing after securing the mask.
- Put on protective clothing.
2. If severe itching on the face becomes unbearable:
- Loosen the cap on your canteen.
- Take a deep breath, lift your mask, and close your eyes.
- Flush your face with generous amounts of water (do not let water run onto clothing or protective overgarment).
3. Do not rub or scratch your eyes.
4. Put your protective mask back on, seat it properly, clear it, and check for a seal; then resume breathing.
5. Decontaminate your skin by bathing with soap and water as soon as the mission permits.
6. Change clothing and decontaminate your protective mask using soap and water. Replace filters if directed.
7. If vomiting occurs:
- Lift the mask momentarily, drain it while eyes are closed and breath is held.
- Replace, clear, and seal the mask.
8. Seek medical assistance immediately if you suspect exposure to toxins.

Signs and Symptoms of Toxin Exposure:
• Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, cramps, or stomach irritation and burning sensation
• Neurological symptoms (e.g., visual disturbances, inability to swallow, speech difficulty, lack of muscle coordination)
• Hemorrhagic symptoms: dizziness, severe itching or tingling, formation of small blisters, coughing up blood, shock (which could result in death)
toxins,yellow rain,decontamination,first aid
Administer Nerve Agent Antidote (MARK I) to Self
Critical Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

Preparation Steps:

1. Retrieve one set of MARK I from your protective mask carrier, the pocket of the MOPP suit, or another location as specified by your unit standing operating procedure (SOP).
2. Hold the set of injectors by...
Procedure:

Preparation Steps:

1. Retrieve one set of MARK I from your protective mask carrier, the pocket of the MOPP suit, or another location as specified by your unit standing operating procedure (SOP).
2. Hold the set of injectors by the plastic clip at eye level with the big injector on top.
3. Check the injection site to avoid buttons and objects in pockets where injecting.

Administration Steps:

4. Grasp the small injector without covering or holding the needle (green) end, and pull it out of the clip with a smooth motion.
5. Form a fist around the autoinjector with the needle (green) end extending beyond the little finger end of the fist. Be careful not to inject yourself in the hand.
6. If the injection is accidentally given in the hand, obtain another small injector and administer it in the proper site.
7. Place the needle end of the injector against the outer thigh muscle or buttock.
- For injections into the thigh: Grasp the trouser cargo pocket and pull forward, clearing possible obstructions from the site.
- The injection can be given in any part of the lateral thigh muscle from about a hand's width above the knee to a hand's width below the hip joint.
- Very thin Soldiers should give the injection in the upper, outer part of the buttock.
MARK I, nerve agent antidote, self-aid
First Aid Measures for Blister Agent Exposure
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Use your M291 Skin Decontaminating Kit to decontaminate your skin and use water to flush contaminated eyes.
- Decontamination must be done immediately (within 1 minute is best).
2. If blisters form, cover them loosely...
Procedure:

1. Use your M291 Skin Decontaminating Kit to decontaminate your skin and use water to flush contaminated eyes.
- Decontamination must be done immediately (within 1 minute is best).
2. If blisters form, cover them loosely with a field dressing and secure the dressing.
3. CAUTION: Blisters are actually burns. DO NOT attempt to decontaminate the skin where blisters have formed, as the agent has already been absorbed.
4. If you receive blisters over a wide area of the body, you are considered seriously burned. Seek medical assistance immediately.
5. If vomiting occurs:
a. Lift the mask momentarily and drain—while the eyes are closed and the breath is held.
b. Replace, clear, and seal the mask.
6. If vomiting or diarrhea occurs after having been exposed to blister agents, seek medical assistance immediately.
blister agents, first aid, decontamination, burns, M291 kit
Protective Measures Against Choking Agents (Lung-Damaging Agents)
Critical Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

1. Your protective mask provides adequate protection against choking agents such as phosgene (CG), diphosgene (DP), chlorine (Cl), and chloropicrin (PS).
2. Be aware that CG is the most dangerous of these agents and may be used...
Procedure:

1. Your protective mask provides adequate protection against choking agents such as phosgene (CG), diphosgene (DP), chlorine (Cl), and chloropicrin (PS).
2. Be aware that CG is the most dangerous of these agents and may be used in future conflicts.
3. If you detect an odor like newly mown hay, this could indicate a CG attack, but do not rely on odor as the sole indicator of chemical exposure.
choking agents, protective mask, phosgene, chemical exposure
Protective Measures Against Cyanogen (Blood) Agents
Critical Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

1. Your protective mask with a fresh filter provides adequate protection against field concentrations of cyanogen agents such as hydrogen cyanide (AC) and cyanogen chloride (CK).
2. In the case of exposure to liquid AC, wear...
Procedure:

1. Your protective mask with a fresh filter provides adequate protection against field concentrations of cyanogen agents such as hydrogen cyanide (AC) and cyanogen chloride (CK).
2. In the case of exposure to liquid AC, wear protective overgarments in addition to your mask.
cyanogen agents, hydrogen cyanide, protective gear
Provide First Aid for Phosphine Poisoning
Critical Source: US Army Storage And Sanitation
Purpose: Administer immediate first aid to someone exposed to phosphine gas.

Procedure:
1. Remove the affected person immediately into the open air to reduce exposure to the toxic gas.
2. Call for medical assistance as soon as...
Purpose: Administer immediate first aid to someone exposed to phosphine gas.

Procedure:
1. Remove the affected person immediately into the open air to reduce exposure to the toxic gas.
2. Call for medical assistance as soon as possible.
3. Treat for shock by:
- Placing the individual in a recumbent (lying down) position
- Keeping them quiet and warm
4. If available, apply oxygen breathing equipment to assist with respiration.
5. Do not give any antidote, especially substances composed of fats and oils such as:
- Castor oil
- Butter
- Milk
phosphine first aid, chemical exposure treatment, fumigation emergency care
Recognizing and Responding to Toxin Exposure Symptoms
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure for Recognizing and Responding to Toxin Exposure Symptoms

1. Watch for signs of toxin exposure, which may appear within minutes to several hours after contact:
- Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or stomach cramps.
- Irritation or...
Procedure for Recognizing and Responding to Toxin Exposure Symptoms

1. Watch for signs of toxin exposure, which may appear within minutes to several hours after contact:
- Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or stomach cramps.
- Irritation or burning sensation on the skin.
- Severe itching or tingling of the skin.
2. In severe cases, look for neurological symptoms, such as:
- Visual disturbances (e.g., blurred vision).
- Difficulty swallowing or speaking.
- Lack of muscle coordination.
- Sensory abnormalities (numbness in mouth, throat, or extremities).
3. In extreme cases, look for hemorrhagic symptoms, such as:
- Coughing up blood.
- Dizziness or shock (which could result in death).
4. If any of these symptoms are present, immediately take the following actions:
- Stop breathing and put on your protective mask with hood, then resume breathing.
- Put on protective clothing.
5. Seek immediate medical assistance, as toxin exposure can cause severe health complications that require professional treatment.
toxin symptoms,chemical exposure signs,yellow rain effects
Responding to Chemical Exposure in a Person
Critical Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Procedure:

1. Find any containers of the substance that are readily available to provide information to emergency services.
2. Call emergency medical services immediately.
3. **Follow the first aid instructions given by the operator or...
Procedure:

1. Find any containers of the substance that are readily available to provide information to emergency services.
2. Call emergency medical services immediately.
3. Follow the first aid instructions given by the operator or dispatcher, as advice on chemical containers may be outdated or inappropriate.
4. Do not give anything by mouth unless advised by a medical professional.
chemical exposure, first aid, emergency response
Self-Aid for Choking Agent Exposure
Critical Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

1. Put on your protective mask immediately if you experience any of the symptoms listed above or detect an odor like newly mown hay.
2. If some CG is inhaled, continue normal combat duties unless you experience:
- Difficulty...
Procedure:

1. Put on your protective mask immediately if you experience any of the symptoms listed above or detect an odor like newly mown hay.
2. If some CG is inhaled, continue normal combat duties unless you experience:
- Difficulty breathing
- Nausea or vomiting
- More than usual shortness of breath during exertion
3. If any of these symptoms occur and the mission permits, remain at quiet rest until medical evacuation can be accomplished.
self-aid, choking agents, mask use, medical evacuation
Signs and Symptoms of Blister Agent Poisoning
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Immediate and intense pain upon contact with Lewisite (L), Lewisite-H mustard mixture (LH), or phosgene oxime (CX). No initial pain upon contact with mustard agents.
2. Inflammation and blisters (burns) resulting in tissue...
Procedure:

1. Immediate and intense pain upon contact with Lewisite (L), Lewisite-H mustard mixture (LH), or phosgene oxime (CX). No initial pain upon contact with mustard agents.
2. Inflammation and blisters (burns) resulting in tissue destruction. The severity of a chemical burn is directly related to the concentration of the agent and the duration of contact with the skin. The longer the agent is in contact with the tissue, the more serious the injury will be.
3. Vomiting and diarrhea may occur from exposure to high concentrations of vesicants.
4. Death may occur from prolonged exposure to high concentrations of vapor or from extensive liquid contamination over wide areas of the skin, particularly when decontamination is neglected or delayed.
blister agents, symptoms, chemical exposure, first aid, burns
Apply Protective Mask to Casualty
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure for Applying Protective Mask to a Casualty

Steps:
1. Place the mask on the casualty.
2. If the casualty can follow directions, have him/her clear the mask.
3. Check for a complete mask seal by covering the inlet valves of the...
Procedure for Applying Protective Mask to a Casualty

Steps:
1. Place the mask on the casualty.
2. If the casualty can follow directions, have him/her clear the mask.
3. Check for a complete mask seal by covering the inlet valves of the mask.
4. Pull the protective hood over the head, neck, and shoulders of the casualty.
5. Position the casualty on the right side, similar to a swimmer's position, with the head slanted down so that the casualty will not roll back over.

Important Notes:
• Do NOT kneel at any time while providing aid to the casualty. Contact with the ground could force the chemical into or through the protective clothing.
• Reposition the casualty on his/her back, if necessary, to mask the individual.
protective mask,casualty aid,chemical exposure,first aid
Responding to a Chemical Spill at Home
High Priority Source: FEMA Are You Ready
Procedure:

1. Clean up any chemical spill immediately using rags.
2. Wear gloves and eye protection during cleanup to avoid exposure.
3. Allow the fumes from the rags to evaporate outdoors, then dispose of them by wrapping in...
Procedure:

1. Clean up any chemical spill immediately using rags.
2. Wear gloves and eye protection during cleanup to avoid exposure.
3. Allow the fumes from the rags to evaporate outdoors, then dispose of them by wrapping in newspaper and placing in a sealed plastic bag in your trash can.
chemical spill, cleanup, first aid, safety
First Aid for Tear Gas Exposure
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

### If You See Tear Gas Coming or Receive a Warning:

1. Move away from the source or position yourself upwind.
2. Use protective gear, if available, to minimize exposure to skin and face.
3. Wear a gas mask that is...
Procedure:

### If You See Tear Gas Coming or Receive a Warning:

1. Move away from the source or position yourself upwind.
2. Use protective gear, if available, to minimize exposure to skin and face.
3. Wear a gas mask that is properly fitted and sealed for optimal respiratory protection.
4. If no gas mask is available, use a bandanna soaked in water and tie it tightly around the nose and mouth.

### After Exposure:

1. Stay calm, breathe slowly, and remember that the effects are temporary.
2. Blow your nose, rinse your mouth, cough, and spit to remove irritants; avoid swallowing.
3. Do not rub your skin or eyes.
4. Avoid touching your face, eyes, or other people/equipment to prevent further contamination.

### If a Casualty is Seriously Contaminated:

1. Remove contaminated clothing using protective gloves (e.g., disposable gloves or plastic bags).
2. Wash the skin thoroughly with soap and clean water.
3. Shower with cool water, if possible.
4. Irrigate the eyes with clean water, from the inner corner of the eye to the outer corner, while tilting the casualty’s head back slightly toward the side being rinsed.
5. Advise less seriously affected casualties to apply these measures themselves.

### If You Are Contaminated:

1. Apply the same measures as above.
2. Wait until fully recovered before returning to work or engaging in activities that may expose others.

Important Notes: Effects of tear gas usually occur within seconds and subside within 10–60 minutes after exposure stops, though some individuals may experience prolonged symptoms. Contaminated clothing should be washed separately from other laundry.
tear gas, lachrymatory agents, first aid, chemical exposure, decontamination
Use of M291 Skin Decontaminating Kit
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Apply the XE-555 decontaminant resin packets from the M291 Skin Decontaminating Kit to the affected skin area.
2. Rub the powder into the contaminated skin for at least 3 minutes, ensuring full coverage of the affected...
Procedure:

1. Apply the XE-555 decontaminant resin packets from the M291 Skin Decontaminating Kit to the affected skin area.
2. Rub the powder into the contaminated skin for at least 3 minutes, ensuring full coverage of the affected area.
3. Wash the treated area thoroughly with water after application.

Important Notes:
• The M291 kit is for external use only and may be slightly irritating to the eyes.
• Keep decontaminating powder out of the eyes; if contact occurs, use water to wash toxic agent out of the eyes immediately.
• This procedure is used in environments where exposure to chemical agents is anticipated.
M291 kit, decontamination, chemical exposure, first aid
Use of Nerve Agent Pyridostigmine Pretreatment (NAPP)
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Take one NAPP tablet every 8 hours, as directed by your commander, prior to exposure to nerve agents.
2. Ensure the tablets are taken mission permitting and before exposure to allow sufficient time for blood levels to...
Procedure:

1. Take one NAPP tablet every 8 hours, as directed by your commander, prior to exposure to nerve agents.
2. Ensure the tablets are taken mission permitting and before exposure to allow sufficient time for blood levels to develop.
3. Do not use NAPP as an antidote; it is a pretreatment that improves the effectiveness of nerve agent antidotes.

Important Notes:
• Commanders must follow investigational new drug protocols when issuing NAPP.
• This procedure is only applicable in environments where exposure to nerve agents is anticipated.
NAPP, nerve agent pretreatment, first aid, chemical exposure
Buddy Aid for Chemical Agent Casualties
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid + Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Objective: Provide immediate care to a casualty exposed to chemical agents when they are unable to assist themselves.

Procedure:
1. Administer the proper chemical agent antidote as per protocol or training.
2. **Decontaminate the...
Objective: Provide immediate care to a casualty exposed to chemical agents when they are unable to assist themselves.

Procedure:
1. Administer the proper chemical agent antidote as per protocol or training.
2. Decontaminate the exposed skin of the casualty using appropriate decontamination procedures and materials.
3. Ensure that the casualty’s protective ensemble remains correctly emplaced (e.g., gas mask, suit) to prevent further exposure.
4. Maintain respiration for the casualty, ensuring they are breathing properly and not in distress; clear airways and provide oxygen if available.
5. Control bleeding if present, applying pressure or using appropriate dressings as needed.
6. Provide other standard first aid measures, such as treating wounds, stabilizing fractures, or managing shock.
7. Transport the casualty out of the contaminated area to a safe location for further treatment and decontamination.

Considerations:
• If the casualty is physically unable to perform these actions themselves, the buddy must assume full responsibility for their care.
• Buddy aid includes all necessary steps to prevent additional injury from chemical hazards.
chemical agent, buddy aid, decontamination, first aid, antidote, respiration, bleeding control
Handling Chemical Exposure During Splinting
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid + Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:
1. In a chemical environment, do not remove any protective clothing.
2. Apply dressings and splints over the garments to avoid exposing the casualty to chemicals.
3. Ensure that all materials used are compatible with the chemical...
Procedure:
1. In a chemical environment, do not remove any protective clothing.
2. Apply dressings and splints over the garments to avoid exposing the casualty to chemicals.
3. Ensure that all materials used are compatible with the chemical environment.

Notes:
• This is critical in preventing further contamination or injury.
chemical exposure, splinting, protective clothing, first aid

Wound Management (19) -

Apply Combat Gauze to Control Bleeding
Critical Source: TCCC 17 13 Army Tactical Combat Casualty Care
Step 1. Place the end of the combat gauze over the dominant hand, index finger. Using this finger, place the combat gauze into the wound, directly at the pulsating artery. Begin to pack the wound until it is full of the combat gauze. Place any...
Step 1. Place the end of the combat gauze over the dominant hand, index finger. Using this finger, place the combat gauze into the wound, directly at the pulsating artery. Begin to pack the wound until it is full of the combat gauze. Place any excess combat gauze directly over the wound and apply direct pressure for 3 minutes.

Note: More than one combat gauze may be required to completely fill the wound. If bleeding continues after 3 minutes of pressure, first remove the combat gauze and repeat step 1.

Note: Attempt to visualize the bleeding during dressing transition. Look for flowing blood from veins or spurting blood from arteries. Attempt to place the second combat gauze on the source of the bleeding.

Step 2. Once bleeding is controlled, apply an outer bandage (Ace wrap or emergency dressing) to secure the dressing to the wound.
combat,gauze,bleeding,wound,control,dressing,application
Apply First Aid Dressing to Severe Bleeding Wounds
Critical Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure for Applying a First Aid Dressing

1. Use alternative methods if tourniquet is not available:
- If the wound is below the knee or elbow, place a pad in the bend of the joint and fold the limb back over it, holding the pad...
Procedure for Applying a First Aid Dressing

1. Use alternative methods if tourniquet is not available:
- If the wound is below the knee or elbow, place a pad in the bend of the joint and fold the limb back over it, holding the pad tightly.
- Tie the arm or leg in this position to maintain pressure on the wound.

2. Apply direct pressure if necessary:
- If bleeding continues despite these methods, insert a pad into the wound and press on it firmly.
- In an emergency with no dressing available, use your fingers to apply pressure directly to the wound. However, this should only be done as a last resort due to the risk of infection.

3. Handle venous bleeding:
- Blood from veins is dark in color and flows steadily toward the heart.
- Apply a pad firmly over the wound and bandage it tightly to stop the bleeding.
- In cases of severe venous bleeding in the neck, apply direct pressure with your hand immediately, even if it risks infection, as rapid blood loss can be life-threatening.

Additional Notes:
• Keep the injured person calm at all times; even if bleeding is controlled, it may restart.
• Do not give stimulants until the bleeding has been stopped unless the patient is very weak.
• If needed, administer aromatic spirits of ammonia (one teaspoonful in half a glass of water) as a safe stimulant.
first aid dressing, venous bleeding, pressure application, wound management
Apply Pressure to Arterial Bleeding in the Arm or Leg
Critical Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure for Stopping Severe Arterial Bleeding

1. Identify the artery:
- In the arm, locate the large artery on the inner side of the upper arm, about the line of the coat seam.
- In the leg, find the artery running from the...
Procedure for Stopping Severe Arterial Bleeding

1. Identify the artery:
- In the arm, locate the large artery on the inner side of the upper arm, about the line of the coat seam.
- In the leg, find the artery running from the center of a line between the hip and the middle of the crotch, approximately in line with the inseam of trousers. Apply pressure about three inches below the crotch.

2. Apply direct pressure:
- Use your fingers to press on the artery against the bone. You may feel the pulse under your fingers.
- Continue applying pressure until the bleeding stops below the point of compression.

3. Prepare a tourniquet:
- While maintaining pressure, have another scout prepare a tourniquet using a handkerchief tied loosely around the limb.
- Insert a smooth stone or cork just above your fingers on the artery within the handkerchief.
- Place a stick about one foot long under the handkerchief at the outer side of the limb and twist it until the stone applies pressure to the artery in the same way as your fingers did.

4. Secure the tourniquet:
- Tie the stick in place so that it will not untwist.

Important Notes:
• Do not keep a tourniquet on for more than one hour, as cutting off circulation for too long is dangerous.
• Loosen the tourniquet if needed but be ready to tighten it again quickly if bleeding resumes.
• Avoid using your fingers indefinitely due to fatigue and cramping.
arterial bleeding, pressure application, tourniquet, first aid, wound management
Identify and Control Bleeding
Critical Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure: Check for bleeding by removing only the minimum amount of clothing to expose and treat injuries. Protect the casualty from the environment (heat and cold). Look for blood-soaked clothes. Look for entry and exit wounds. Place your...
Procedure: Check for bleeding by removing only the minimum amount of clothing to expose and treat injuries. Protect the casualty from the environment (heat and cold). Look for blood-soaked clothes. Look for entry and exit wounds. Place your hands behind the casualty's neck and pass them upward toward the top of the head. Note whether there is blood or brain tissue on your hands from the casualty's wounds. Place your hands behind the casualty's shoulders and pass them downward behind the back, the thighs, and the legs. Note whether there is blood on your hands from the casualty's wounds. If life-threatening bleeding is present, stop the evaluation and control the bleeding. Apply a tourniquet, chitosan dressing, emergency bandage, or field dressing, as appropriate. Treat for shock and establish a saline lock/ intravenous infusion, as appropriate. If a tourniquet was previously applied, consider converting it to a pressure dressing. Converting the tourniquet to a pressure dressing may save the casualty's limb if the tourniquet has not been in place for 6 hours. Dress all wounds, including exit wounds.
bleeding control, tourniquet application, wound dressing
Insertion of Chest Tubes and Urinary Catheters in Trauma Patients
Critical Source: NATO Emergency War Surgery
Procedure:

1. Insert Chest Tubes When Indicated: Insert chest tubes and connect them to closed drainage systems for patients with suspected or confirmed pneumothorax, hemothorax, or other thoracic injuries.
2. **Insert Indwelling Urinary...
Procedure:

1. Insert Chest Tubes When Indicated: Insert chest tubes and connect them to closed drainage systems for patients with suspected or confirmed pneumothorax, hemothorax, or other thoracic injuries.
2. Insert Indwelling Urinary Catheter: Place an indwelling urinary catheter to measure urinary output and detect hematuria (blood in urine), which may indicate internal injury.
3. Monitor Output and Hematuria: Continuously monitor the patient’s urinary output and assess for signs of hematuria, which can signal significant internal bleeding or trauma.
chest tube insertion,urinary catheter,hematuria detection
Managing Neck Wounds
Critical Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Procedure:

1. Clean the wound carefully but never probe it.
2. Assess medically unless the wound is clearly superficial.
3. Avoid placing bandages around the neck, as subsequent swelling may compromise the airway.

Notes:
-...
Procedure:

1. Clean the wound carefully but never probe it.
2. Assess medically unless the wound is clearly superficial.
3. Avoid placing bandages around the neck, as subsequent swelling may compromise the airway.

Notes:
• Injuries to the neck may involve damage to blood vessels, nerves, and the airway.
• Medical assessment is critical for non-superficial wounds.
neck wound management, airway compromise, avoid bandages, medical assessment
Apply an Improvised Dressing to Control Bleeding
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Objective: Apply an improvised dressing to control bleeding from a wound.

Materials Needed:
• Strips of cloth (e.g., T-shirts, socks, or other garments)
• Cravat (if available)

Procedure:
1. Prepare the Wound Site: Ensure the area...
Objective: Apply an improvised dressing to control bleeding from a wound.

Materials Needed:
• Strips of cloth (e.g., T-shirts, socks, or other garments)
• Cravat (if available)

Procedure:
1. Prepare the Wound Site: Ensure the area around the wound is clean and free from debris to prevent infection.
2. Place an Improvised Dressing Over Padding: Place a wad of padding over the wound, then cover it with an improvised dressing or cravat (Figure 2-28).
3. Wrap the Ends Around the Limb: Wrap the ends of the improvised dressing tightly around the injured limb, covering the previously placed field dressing (Figure 2-29). Ensure that the dressing is snug but not so tight that it restricts circulation.
4. Tie in a Nonslip Knot: Tie the ends together directly over the wound site using a nonslip knot (Figure 2-30). Do not tie so tightly that it has a tourniquet-like effect.
5. Monitor for Continued Bleeding: If bleeding continues and all other measures have failed, or if the limb is severed, then apply a tourniquet as a last resort.
6. Check for Shock: When the bleeding stops, check for signs of shock (e.g., pale skin, rapid pulse, confusion) and administer first aid for shock as necessary.

Notes:
• The distal end of wounded extremities (fingers and toes) should be checked periodically for adequate circulation. Loosen the dressing if the extremity becomes cool, blue, or numb.
• If bleeding continues despite these measures, apply digital pressure (see paragraph 2-19).
improvised dressing, wound management, bleeding control, first aid
Assess and Manage Wounds in Armed Conflicts
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Assess and manage wounds to minimize infection risk and ensure proper stabilization.

### Steps:
1. Examine the wound:
- Look for breaks in the skin (abrasion, incision, laceration, puncture, or ragged projectile wound).
-...
Objective: Assess and manage wounds to minimize infection risk and ensure proper stabilization.

### Steps:
1. Examine the wound:
- Look for breaks in the skin (abrasion, incision, laceration, puncture, or ragged projectile wound).
- Listen for localized pain reported by the casualty.
- Ask the casualty about the injury: what happened, when, and how.
- Touch around the wound (do not insert fingers inside the wound).
- Suspect underlying injuries and risk of infection, especially in cases involving small fragments from bombs or shells that may cause tiny external wounds with significant internal damage.

2. Stabilization techniques:
- Explain to the casualty what you are going to do.
- Make the casualty comfortable in a sitting or lying position.
- Work in front of the casualty and from the injured side where possible.

3. Clean the wound:
- Wash the wound gently with plenty of clean water, without rubbing.
- For large wounds, wash from the inner part towards the outer part.
- Dry the wound before dressing or covering it.

4. Protect the wound:
- Cover the wound with a clean dressing (sterile compress if available). The dressing should be bulky to absorb bleeding.
- Apply a bandage to keep the dressing in place.
- If the casualty is lying down, pass bandages under natural body hollows: ankles, knees, small of the back, and neck.
- Apply bandages to limbs in a figure-of-eight fashion – not in a circle, which could have a tourniquet effect.

5. Monitor and maintain:
- If evacuation is long or delayed, change the dressing and wash out the wound every two or three days.
- If the wound becomes infected (red, swollen, hot, painful, with pus), organize rapid evacuation.
- Clean the wound thoroughly with plenty of clean water, removing as much debris and pus as possible.
- If evacuation is delayed, change the dressing and wash out the wound daily.

### Notes:
• Wounds caused by weapons are dirty and contaminated and at high risk of infection.
• Injuries may be multiple and associated with foreign bodies (bullets, metal fragments, etc.).
• A small skin wound may cover large underlying damage.
• Always practice basic hygiene and take antiseptic measures.
wound management, infection prevention, first aid, cleaning wounds, dressing wounds, burn care
Assessing Deep Wounds for Underlying Damage
High Priority Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Procedure:

1. Check movement: Ask the patient to move the affected part through the full normal range.
2. Check circulation:
- Feel for pulses in the affected area.
- Perform a capillary refill test: Press firmly over a fingernail...
Procedure:

1. Check movement: Ask the patient to move the affected part through the full normal range.
2. Check circulation:
- Feel for pulses in the affected area.
- Perform a capillary refill test: Press firmly over a fingernail or bony prominence for 5 seconds to produce blanching. When pressure is released, the color should return quickly (in less than 2 seconds). If it does not, this may indicate shock, cold extremities, or interrupted blood supply.
3. Check sensation: Assess beyond the level of the injury for any loss of feeling.

Notes:
• If the limb is pulseless, pale, and cold, surgical treatment is required within a few hours to salvage the limb.
• Deep wounds are prone to infection and should be cleaned carefully and packed open so that the wound can heal from the bottom upwards.
• Dressings should be changed daily until the wound can be dealt with surgically.
deep wounds, circulation check, capillary refill, movement assessment, sensation check, infection prevention
Managing Hand and Foot Wounds
High Priority Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Procedure:

1. Clean the wound thoroughly.
2. Assess movement, circulation, and sensation in the affected limb.
3. Rest the injured limb in the position of function:
- For the hand: Bandage with a sock or crepe bandage initially,...
Procedure:

1. Clean the wound thoroughly.
2. Assess movement, circulation, and sensation in the affected limb.
3. Rest the injured limb in the position of function:
- For the hand: Bandage with a sock or crepe bandage initially, followed by gentle mobilisation.
- Avoid splinting the hand with fingers straight to prevent stiffness after healing.
4. Monitor for infection, and start antibiotics (e.g., flucloxacillin or erythromycin) if suspected.

Notes:
• Wounds in the hands or feet can lead to crippling deformity if not treated properly.
• Significant foot wounds require rest, as continued walking may prevent healing.
hand wound management, foot wound rest, position of function, infection monitoring
Apply Elevation to Control Bleeding
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Steps to Apply Elevation:

1. Identify the injured limb: Locate the extremity that is bleeding or showing signs of swelling.
2. Elevate above heart level: Raise the affected area as high above the heart as possible, using a splint or...
Steps to Apply Elevation:

1. Identify the injured limb: Locate the extremity that is bleeding or showing signs of swelling.
2. Elevate above heart level: Raise the affected area as high above the heart as possible, using a splint or support if needed.
3. Maintain elevation: Keep the limb elevated for at least 10–15 minutes to reduce blood flow and promote clotting.
4. Monitor for improvement: Check for signs of reduced bleeding or swelling after elevation is applied.

Warnings:
• Elevation should not be used on fractures or dislocations without proper immobilization.
• Avoid elevating the limb if it causes increased pain or numbness.
elevation,blood control,emergency first aid
Cleaning Contaminated Wounds
High Priority Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Procedure:

1. Clean the wound carefully to remove any foreign material that may cause infection.
2. Administer pain relief (e.g., painkillers) half an hour before cleaning, or use a local anaesthetic if available.
3. **Flush debris out...
Procedure:

1. Clean the wound carefully to remove any foreign material that may cause infection.
2. Administer pain relief (e.g., painkillers) half an hour before cleaning, or use a local anaesthetic if available.
3. Flush debris out of the wound using sterile saline.
4. Do not suture contaminated wounds; instead, pack them open and change dressings daily to allow healing from the bottom upwards.
5. Consider oral antibiotics if the wound is very deep or contaminated, especially if signs of infection are present (e.g., redness, swelling, pus).

Notes:
• Contaminated wounds are common in environments like jungles.
• Proper cleaning and packing reduce the risk of infection and scarring.
contaminated wound cleaning, foreign material removal, sterile saline, antibiotic use, infection prevention
Management of Vesicles and Bullae in Cold Injury
High Priority Source: NATO Emergency War Surgery
Objective: Properly manage vesicles and bullae to prevent infection and promote healing.

Procedure Steps:
1. Protect intact vesicles: Keep large vesicles or bullae intact if possible to avoid further damage.
2. **Debride ruptured...
Objective: Properly manage vesicles and bullae to prevent infection and promote healing.

Procedure Steps:
1. Protect intact vesicles: Keep large vesicles or bullae intact if possible to avoid further damage.
2. Debride ruptured vesicles: If vesicles have ruptured, debride the affected area carefully.
3. Avoid ointment dressings: Do not use ointment dressings in the management of cold injury.
4. Use protective dry dressings: Apply protective dry dressings during transportation to prevent further trauma.
5. Sterile cotton between toes: Use sterile cotton between the toes to prevent maceration and reduce risk of infection.
vesicle management, bullae treatment, cold injury wound care, first aid for frostbite
Managing Flap Wounds
High Priority Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Procedure:

1. Turn the skin flap back and clean underneath.
2. Snip away small pieces of dead tissue with sterile scissors.
3. Apply a non-stick dressing around the edges of the wound, under the flap to allow exudate to drain.
4....
Procedure:

1. Turn the skin flap back and clean underneath.
2. Snip away small pieces of dead tissue with sterile scissors.
3. Apply a non-stick dressing around the edges of the wound, under the flap to allow exudate to drain.
4. Redress daily, applying slightly less non-stick dressing each day to allow healing from base to tip.

Notes:
• Proximal flaps (attached near the trunk) have better blood supply and often heal well without infection.
• Distal flaps (attached further away from the trunk) are more likely to become infected or die, requiring surgical treatment such as skin grafting.
• Flap wounds should not be closed with sutures.
flap wound management, non-stick dressing, proximal flap, distal flap, surgical treatment
Managing Moderate Bleeding from Limbs
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

• Apply direct pressure to the wound using a clean dressing or cloth.
• If conscious, ask the casualty to help apply and maintain pressure.
• Use compression techniques for bleeding from arms or legs (peripheral...
Procedure:

• Apply direct pressure to the wound using a clean dressing or cloth.
• If conscious, ask the casualty to help apply and maintain pressure.
• Use compression techniques for bleeding from arms or legs (peripheral haemorrhage).
• Avoid compression in cases of chest or abdominal wounds (internal haemorrhage).

Notes:
• Monitor the effectiveness of the dressing and reapply if necessary.
• If bleeding persists, seek advanced medical assistance immediately.
moderate bleeding, compression technique, first aid
Protecting Yourself and Managing Visible Haemorrhage
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

• Always use disposable gloves (preferably vinyl to avoid latex allergies) and absorbent material when handling blood.
• Control visible bleeding by applying direct pressure with a clean dressing or cloth.
• **Expose...
Procedure:

• Always use disposable gloves (preferably vinyl to avoid latex allergies) and absorbent material when handling blood.
• Control visible bleeding by applying direct pressure with a clean dressing or cloth.
• Expose wounds by removing or cutting clothing to assess the injury.
• Monitor the casualty’s condition, including signs of shock (e.g., rapid weak pulse, cold extremities).

Notes:
• Explore the back and sides for penetrating wounds.
• Prevent or minimize shock by maintaining body temperature and ensuring adequate blood flow.
visible hemorrhage, first aid, wound management, shock prevention
Drain an Abscess on an Expedition
Standard Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Procedure:

1. Apply ice to numb the skin over the abscess.
2. Make a swift crescent-shaped cut in the thinned skin to allow pus to drain.
3. Insert a small piece of gauze soaked in saline into the incision to act as a wick.
4. The wick should...
Procedure:

1. Apply ice to numb the skin over the abscess.
2. Make a swift crescent-shaped cut in the thinned skin to allow pus to drain.
3. Insert a small piece of gauze soaked in saline into the incision to act as a wick.
4. The wick should be changed daily until the abscess has healed.
5. Ensure that the abscess cavity heals from the bottom upwards by keeping the wick in place.

Notes:
• This procedure is suitable for minor abscesses, such as whitlows around fingernails or toenails.
• Local heat and oral antibiotics (e.g., flucloxacillin) may help before pus forms, but draining is often quicker and kinder once pus is present.
abscess,drain abscess,expedition first aid,wound care
Manage Ear Injuries and Cover with Dressing
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure for Managing Ear Injuries

1. For lacerated (cut) or avulsed (torn) ear tissue, cover the ear lightly with a dressing.
2. DO NOT attempt to stop the flow from the inner ear canal nor put anything into the ear canal to block it.
3....
Procedure for Managing Ear Injuries

1. For lacerated (cut) or avulsed (torn) ear tissue, cover the ear lightly with a dressing.
2. DO NOT attempt to stop the flow from the inner ear canal nor put anything into the ear canal to block it.
3. If bleeding or drainage of fluids from the ear canal is present, consider the possibility of a head injury such as a skull fracture and seek professional medical assistance immediately.

Important Notes:
• This procedure applies only to minor cuts or wounds to the external ear.
ear injury, dressing application, first aid, head injury, medical emergency
Manage Large Cavity Wounds with Packing and Compressive Bandage
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

1. Apply digital pressure on the nearest accessible arterial pressure point.
