2. Pack the wound with sterile gauze, if available, or clean compress or cloth.
3. Raise the limb.
4. Apply a compressive bandage.
5....
Procedure:

1. Apply digital pressure on the nearest accessible arterial pressure point.
2. Pack the wound with sterile gauze, if available, or clean compress or cloth.
3. Raise the limb.
4. Apply a compressive bandage.
5. Check distal blood circulation.

*Note: For casualties with a major wound of the limb, compressive bandage should always be applied in order to maintain control of the bleeding during transport.*
compressive bandage, major wound, digital pressure, packing, wound management, limb injury

Dental Care (19) -

General Treatment for Gum Infection
High Priority Source: Where There is No Dentist
Steps to treat gum infection:

1. Keep the area clean to control the infection and help the skin heal.
- Use any of the methods described in the “General Treatment” box on pages 178 and 179.
2. Gently remove tartar around the teeth,...
Steps to treat gum infection:

1. Keep the area clean to control the infection and help the skin heal.
- Use any of the methods described in the “General Treatment” box on pages 178 and 179.
2. Gently remove tartar around the teeth, being especially careful not to cause damage to the gums (see “Scaling Teeth” on pages 127 to 133).
gum infection,tartar removal,clean area,scaling teeth
Remove Loose Teeth After Infection Control
High Priority Source: Where There is No Dentist
Once the area is clean and infection is controlled:

• Take out any teeth that are very loose (see pages 157 to 161 for instructions on tooth extraction).
loose teeth,extraction,infection control,clean area
Tooth Extraction
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Tooth Extraction

Extraction is necessary when a tooth cannot be saved. Follow these steps:

1. Assess the patient: Check for signs of infection or complications before proceeding.
2. Prepare the area: Clean the mouth and surrounding...
Tooth Extraction

Extraction is necessary when a tooth cannot be saved. Follow these steps:

1. Assess the patient: Check for signs of infection or complications before proceeding.
2. Prepare the area: Clean the mouth and surrounding area with antiseptic to reduce the risk of infection.
3. Administer local anesthesia: Use a syringe to inject anesthetic near the tooth’s nerve to numb the area.
4. Use appropriate tools: Use an extraction spoon or forceps to gently remove the tooth.
5. Apply pressure if needed: If the tooth is difficult to remove, apply gentle pressure with gauze or a cotton pad to control bleeding.
6. Remove any fragments: Ensure that all parts of the tooth are removed, including roots and debris.
7. Rinse the mouth: Clean the extraction site thoroughly with water or saline solution to prevent infection.
8. Apply a dressing: Place a sterile gauze pad over the extraction site and instruct the patient to bite down gently for 30 minutes to control bleeding.
9. Advise on post-extraction care: Tell the patient to avoid hard, spicy foods, and maintain good oral hygiene after the procedure.
tooth extraction, dental procedures, post-extraction care
Use Zinc Oxide for Dental and General Applications
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Zinc oxide has various uses in both dental care and general applications:

• As a topical antiseptic to treat infections or sores in the mouth.
• To provide pain relief and reduce inflammation in affected areas.

Steps:

1. **Apply...
Zinc oxide has various uses in both dental care and general applications:

• As a topical antiseptic to treat infections or sores in the mouth.
• To provide pain relief and reduce inflammation in affected areas.

Steps:

1. Apply directly: Use a small amount of zinc oxide cream or powder on the affected area.
2. Monitor for improvement: Check regularly for signs of healing or worsening symptoms.
3. Repeat as needed: Continue application until symptoms subside, unless otherwise advised by a healthcare professional.
zinc oxide, antiseptic, dental care, first aid
Care for a Groove on the Neck of a Tooth
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Steps to care for a groove on the neck of a tooth:

1. Apply fluoride toothpaste to the groove once each week until the inside part of the groove is stronger and the tooth hurts less.
2. **Paint the inside of the groove with oil of cloves...
Steps to care for a groove on the neck of a tooth:

1. Apply fluoride toothpaste to the groove once each week until the inside part of the groove is stronger and the tooth hurts less.
2. Paint the inside of the groove with oil of cloves (eugenol) to reduce pain.

Avoid the following to prevent worsening the problem:
• Do not use a hard toothbrush.
• Do not brush back and forth along the gums.
• Do not chew tobacco or betel nut, and do not hold them against the teeth.
tooth groove care, fluoride for teeth, oil of cloves treatment
Cleaning Teeth for Adults and Pregnant Women
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Steps to Clean Teeth:

1. Use a soft-bristled toothbrush that is gentle on the gums.
2. Brush all surfaces of each tooth, including the front, back, and chewing surfaces.
3. Clean between teeth using dental floss or waxed floss to...
Steps to Clean Teeth:

1. Use a soft-bristled toothbrush that is gentle on the gums.
2. Brush all surfaces of each tooth, including the front, back, and chewing surfaces.
3. Clean between teeth using dental floss or waxed floss to remove plaque from hard-to-reach areas.
4. Rinse thoroughly with water after brushing to remove any remaining debris.
5. Use a fluoride toothpaste to help prevent cavities and strengthen enamel.
6. Make it part of your daily routine, ideally twice a day, in the morning and before bedtime.

Additional Tips for Pregnant Women:
• Take extra care with oral hygiene due to hormonal changes that can increase gum sensitivity.
• Avoid using harsh or abrasive toothpaste that may irritate the gums.
• Ensure that brushing is done gently but thoroughly to prevent gum recession.
• If possible, consult a dental worker or health professional for guidance on maintaining good oral health during pregnancy.
cleaning teeth, adults, pregnant women, dental hygiene, fluoride toothpaste
Create a Dental First Aid Kit for Emergency Use
Standard Source: The Ultimate Survival Kit Checklist
Purpose: To prepare a dental first aid kit to manage dental emergencies when professional care is unavailable.

Materials Needed:
• Filling repair kit (including temporary filling material)
• Cotton rolls or gauze
• Dental wax
• Pain...
Purpose: To prepare a dental first aid kit to manage dental emergencies when professional care is unavailable.

Materials Needed:
• Filling repair kit (including temporary filling material)
• Cotton rolls or gauze
• Dental wax
• Pain relievers (e.g., ibuprofen, acetaminophen)
• Antiseptic mouthwash
• Tweezers and small scissors

Procedure:
1. Gather the necessary items to create a functional dental first aid kit.
2. Include a filling repair kit, which is essential for managing lost or damaged fillings during an emergency.
3. Store all items in a waterproof container with clear labeling so they can be easily accessed when needed.
4. In the event of a dental injury, clean the affected area with antiseptic mouthwash and apply temporary filling material if necessary.
5. Use pain relievers to manage discomfort until professional care is available.

Notes:
• A dental first aid kit can help alleviate severe pain caused by damaged fillings or other dental injuries.
• It is important to keep the kit easily accessible in your bug out bag for quick response during emergencies.
dental first aid kit, filling repair, emergency dental care
Fill Cavities in Baby Teeth with Cement
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Procedure:

1. Use cement to fill cavities in baby teeth.
2. This prevents food and air from entering the cavity, reducing the risk of further damage or infection.
3. Ensure that the cement is applied properly and thoroughly to seal the...
Procedure:

1. Use cement to fill cavities in baby teeth.
2. This prevents food and air from entering the cavity, reducing the risk of further damage or infection.
3. Ensure that the cement is applied properly and thoroughly to seal the cavity.
4. Monitor the child for any signs of discomfort or worsening symptoms after the procedure.
filling cavities, baby teeth, dental care, cement application
Identify and Treat Cold Sores or Fever Blisters
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Signs of cold sores or fever blisters:

• Painful red blisters on the gums that have burst open and become small, open sores.

Information about cold sores:

• Caused by the herpes virus, which is carried for life after infection.
• Most...
Signs of cold sores or fever blisters:

• Painful red blisters on the gums that have burst open and become small, open sores.

Information about cold sores:

• Caused by the herpes virus, which is carried for life after infection.
• Most people are infected as children.
• Sores can come and go over time.
• For persons with HIV, sores occur more frequently and last longer.
• For more information on herpes, see page 104.

Treatment:

• The sores usually heal after 1 or 2 weeks.
cold sores,fever blisters,herpes virus,dental sores,hiv
Making Waxed Floss for Dental Hygiene
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Steps to Make Waxed Floss:

1. Cut a length of regular floss (about 30-40 cm) and thread it through a small piece of wax, such as beeswax or paraffin.
2. Melt the wax slightly, either by holding it between your fingers or using a small...
Steps to Make Waxed Floss:

1. Cut a length of regular floss (about 30-40 cm) and thread it through a small piece of wax, such as beeswax or paraffin.
2. Melt the wax slightly, either by holding it between your fingers or using a small flame from a candle or fire.
3. Coat the floss evenly with the melted wax to create a smooth surface that glides easily through the teeth.
4. Allow the waxed floss to cool and solidify before use, ensuring it is not sticky or gummy.
5. Use the waxed floss daily, sliding it gently between each tooth to remove plaque and food particles.
6. Store the waxed floss in a clean container to keep it dry and prevent contamination.

Additional Tips:
• Waxed floss is particularly useful for people with tightly spaced teeth or those who find regular floss difficult to use.
• It can be made using natural materials such as beeswax, which is safe and non-toxic.
• Ensure that the wax used is of high quality to avoid any adverse effects on oral health.
waxed floss, dental hygiene, homemade tools, plaque removal
Manage Pain in the Jaw Joint Due to Malocclusion (Teeth Not Fitting Properly)
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Procedure:

1. Identify malocclusion by checking if a line drawn between the two middle upper and lower teeth shifts when the mouth is opened.
2. Warn the person not to open their mouth wide, suggesting they take small bites of food.
3....
Procedure:

1. Identify malocclusion by checking if a line drawn between the two middle upper and lower teeth shifts when the mouth is opened.
2. Warn the person not to open their mouth wide, suggesting they take small bites of food.
3. Advise the person on possible solutions: A dentist can grind the teeth in a special way to correct the alignment and relieve joint pain.
4. If possible, replace missing back teeth with dentures (page 107 of the source text) to improve occlusion.
malocclusion, teeth misalignment, jaw joint pain, denture replacement
Manage a New Molar Growing In
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Procedure:

1. If the swelling is due to a new molar growing in and there is no severe pain or inability to open the mouth, follow these steps:
- Explain the situation to the person.
- Encourage them to take action themselves to reduce...
Procedure:

1. If the swelling is due to a new molar growing in and there is no severe pain or inability to open the mouth, follow these steps:
- Explain the situation to the person.
- Encourage them to take action themselves to reduce infection and strengthen the gums.

2. The best home remedy is to rinse with warm salt water over the sore area.
- Continue rinsing until the tooth grows fully into the mouth.

3. If symptoms are severe (e.g., severe pain, swelling, or inability to open the mouth), refer to page 94 for further treatment.
warm salt water rinse, molar growth management, dental home remedy
Prevent Cavities in Baby Teeth Through Proper Feeding and Oral Hygiene
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Procedure:

1. Never feed a child juice or sweet tea from a bottle; instead, encourage breastfeeding.
2. Start adding soft foods like mashed banana or papaya to the baby’s diet at 6 months of age.
3. Wipe the baby’s teeth with a clean cloth...
Procedure:

1. Never feed a child juice or sweet tea from a bottle; instead, encourage breastfeeding.
2. Start adding soft foods like mashed banana or papaya to the baby’s diet at 6 months of age.
3. Wipe the baby’s teeth with a clean cloth after each feeding to maintain oral hygiene and introduce the concept of cleaning teeth.
4. At around 1 year of age, begin using water on a soft brush or brushstick (not toothpaste) to clean the child's teeth.
5. Scrub the sides and tops of each baby tooth as thoroughly as possible.
6. Encourage the child to participate in cleaning their own teeth, but continue assisting them daily until they are old enough for school.
baby teeth hygiene, feeding practices, oral care for infants, tooth brushing techniques
Scale Teeth to Remove Tartar and Improve Gum Health
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Purpose of scaling:
• To remove tartar (hardened plaque) from teeth.
• To improve gum health by reducing infection and soreness.

When to scale:
• When there is visible tartar buildup on the teeth.
• If a foreign object like a fish bone or...
Purpose of scaling:
• To remove tartar (hardened plaque) from teeth.
• To improve gum health by reducing infection and soreness.

When to scale:
• When there is visible tartar buildup on the teeth.
• If a foreign object like a fish bone or mango string is trapped under the gums.
• For mild gum problems (e.g., bleeding gums), wait 1 week after improving oral hygiene and rinsing with warm salt water before scaling.

Important considerations:
• Gums remain healthy only when teeth are kept clean after scaling.
• Scaling should be done only if the person is willing to maintain good oral hygiene.
• Use a mirror to show the person their gum condition and progress over time.
• If the person refuses to learn proper cleaning techniques, do not waste time scaling their teeth.
scaling teeth, tartar removal, gum health, dental care, oral hygiene
Temporary Filling for a Tooth Cavity (No Abscess)
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Steps to apply a temporary filling:

1. Try to remove any loose piece of filling with a probe.
2. Fill the hole with cement. If you have no cement, put some cotton into the hole to keep food out.
3. **Look for cavities or broken fillings...
Steps to apply a temporary filling:

1. Try to remove any loose piece of filling with a probe.
2. Fill the hole with cement. If you have no cement, put some cotton into the hole to keep food out.
3. Look for cavities or broken fillings in the other teeth. Fill each one with cement before it gets worse and starts to hurt.

Note: This is a temporary solution. Arrange for someone to replace the temporary filling with a permanent one within a few months.
temporary filling, tooth cavity treatment, dental first aid
Care of Teeth and Gums
Reference Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
### Importance of Dental Hygiene
• Healthy teeth are essential for chewing and digestion.
• Good tooth care prevents cavities and sore gums.
• **Poor dental hygiene can lead to serious infections that may affect other parts of the...
### Importance of Dental Hygiene
• Healthy teeth are essential for chewing and digestion.
• Good tooth care prevents cavities and sore gums.
• Poor dental hygiene can lead to serious infections that may affect other parts of the body.

### How to Keep Teeth and Gums Healthy
1. Avoid sweets:
- Eating a lot of sweets (sugar cane, candy, pastry, tea or coffee with sugar, soft or fizzy drinks like colas) rots teeth quickly.
- Do not accustom children to sweets or soft drinks if you want them to have good teeth.

2. Brush Teeth Well Every Day:
- Brush immediately after eating anything sweet.
- Start brushing your children’s teeth as the teeth appear.
- Teach children to brush their own teeth and ensure they do it correctly.
- Brush from top to bottom, not just side to side.
- Brush all surfaces of each tooth: front, back, top, and bottom.

3. Use Fluoride Where Needed:
- In areas where there is not enough natural fluoride in water or food, adding fluoride to drinking water or directly on teeth helps prevent cavities.
- Some health programs apply fluoride to children’s teeth once or twice a year.
- Most foods from the sea contain a large amount of fluoride.

4. Avoid Bottle Feeding Older Babies:
- Continual sucking on a bottle bathes the baby’s teeth in sweet liquid and causes early decay.
- It is best not to bottle feed at all (see p. 271).

### Fluoride Safety:
• Fluoride is poisonous if more than a small amount is swallowed.
• Use with care and keep it out of the reach of children.
• Before adding fluoride to drinking water, try to get the water tested to see how much fluoride is needed.
care of teeth and gums, dental hygiene, brushing teeth, fluoride use, avoiding sweets for children
Proper Teeth Cleaning Technique
Reference Source: Where There is No Dentist
Cleaning your teeth carefully every day is another important way to take care of both teeth and gums.

### Steps for Proper Brushing:
1. Use a soft-bristled brush, either purchased from the store or made yourself (see page 4 for instructions...
Cleaning your teeth carefully every day is another important way to take care of both teeth and gums.

### Steps for Proper Brushing:
1. Use a soft-bristled brush, either purchased from the store or made yourself (see page 4 for instructions on making one).
2. Brush all surfaces of each tooth: scrub the inside, outside, and top of each tooth.
3. Clean between teeth: push the hairs of your brush between two teeth and sweep food away.
4. Rinse thoroughly: wash your mouth with water to remove any loose bits of food.

### Special Attention:
• Pay special attention to back teeth, especially those with grooves, as they are harder to reach and more prone to cavities.
• Children need help cleaning their teeth carefully. Older children can assist younger siblings at home.
• Look at the pictures on the cover and page 18 for visual guidance on how to clean teeth effectively.
teeth cleaning, brushing technique, dental hygiene, children's oral care
Scaling Teeth
Reference Source: Where There is No Dentist
Scaling Teeth

Scaling is a procedure to remove plaque and tartar from teeth. Follow these steps:

1. Prepare the patient: Ensure they are comfortable and explain the process.
2. Use appropriate instruments: Use scalers (e.g., Gracey...
Scaling Teeth

Scaling is a procedure to remove plaque and tartar from teeth. Follow these steps:

1. Prepare the patient: Ensure they are comfortable and explain the process.
2. Use appropriate instruments: Use scalers (e.g., Gracey curettes) for scaling.
3. Access all areas: Scale both upper and lower teeth, including hard-to-reach areas like molars and along the gumline.
4. Remove plaque and tartar: Gently scrape away plaque and calculus from the tooth surfaces and below the gumline.
5. Rinse frequently: Rinse the mouth with water or a saline solution to keep it clean during the procedure.
6. Check for remaining deposits: Use a mirror or explorer to ensure all plaque and tartar are removed.
7. Instruct on post-scaling care: Advise the patient to maintain good oral hygiene, avoid hard foods, and schedule regular check-ups.
8. Only scale teeth of someone who will keep them clean: Scaling is only effective if the person maintains proper oral hygiene afterward.
scaling teeth, dental cleaning, plaque removal, oral hygiene
Select and Prepare Instruments for Scaling Teeth
Reference Source: Where There is No Dentist
Instruments needed:
• Scalers (2 double-ended or 4 single-ended)
- *Ivory C-1 scaler* – with two pointed tips for removing tartar near the gum.
- *G-11 and 12 curette* – with two blunt, rounded ends for removing tartar under the gum.
-...
Instruments needed:
• Scalers (2 double-ended or 4 single-ended)
- *Ivory C-1 scaler* – with two pointed tips for removing tartar near the gum.
- *G-11 and 12 curette* – with two blunt, rounded ends for removing tartar under the gum.
• Sharpening stone (Arkansas stone) – to keep scalers sharp.
• Mirror – to view the mouth during scaling.
• Probe (explorer) – to detect tartar and plaque buildup.
• Tweezers (cotton pliers) – for removing small objects or debris.

Important tips:
• Keep scalers sharp; a dull blade is less effective at removing tartar.
• Store all instruments in a dedicated Scaling Kit to maintain organization and hygiene.
• When ordering instruments, use both the common and proper names (e.g., *Ivory C-1 scaler*) for clarity.
scaling instruments, dental tools, scalers, curette, oral hygiene equipment

Infection Control (18) -

Demonstrate Treatment for Bite of Finger by Mad Dog
Critical Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Wash the wound thoroughly with soap and water.
2. Apply a disinfectant such as iodine or alcohol to the area.
3. Apply a sterile bandage over the wound to protect it from further contamination.
4. Administer tetanus antitoxin if...
Procedure:

1. Wash the wound thoroughly with soap and water.
2. Apply a disinfectant such as iodine or alcohol to the area.
3. Apply a sterile bandage over the wound to protect it from further contamination.
4. Administer tetanus antitoxin if available, following proper guidelines.
5. Seek immediate medical attention for rabies prophylaxis.

Details:
• This is a life-threatening situation that requires prompt action.
• Do not attempt to treat the wound without professional assistance in cases of suspected rabies.
dog bite,rabies,tetanus antitoxin,wound care
Proper Way to Put on Sterile Gloves
Critical Source: A Book for Midwives
Follow these steps carefully to ensure sterile gloves remain uncontaminated:

1. Open the package without touching the outside of the glove. Ensure that no part of your hands or fingers come into contact with the exterior surface of the...
Follow these steps carefully to ensure sterile gloves remain uncontaminated:

1. Open the package without touching the outside of the glove. Ensure that no part of your hands or fingers come into contact with the exterior surface of the gloves.
2. Carefully wash and dry your hands. Wash them thoroughly with soap and water, then let them air dry completely.
3. Pick up one glove by the cuff. Insert your hand into the glove, making sure that the inside of the glove is in contact with your skin and not the outside.
4. Wiggle your fingers to ensure a proper fit. Do not touch anything non-sterile once the glove is on.
5. Repeat for the second glove. Use your gloved hand to pick up the second glove by its cuff, then slide it onto your other hand.
6. Avoid touching any non-sterile surfaces after gloves are on. Once on, the gloves must not come into contact with anything that is not sterile, or they will no longer be sterile.

Note: Practice this process repeatedly until you can do it confidently and without error.
glove use, infection control, sterile gloves, PPE
Sterilizing Syringes and Needles for Injections
Critical Source: Where There is No Dentist
Be sure your syringe and needles are clean and sterile (see pages 86 to 89). Do not pass an infection from one person to another by using dirty needles.

For glass syringes: Boil the syringe and needle in water (page 88) for at least **30...
Be sure your syringe and needles are clean and sterile (see pages 86 to 89). Do not pass an infection from one person to another by using dirty needles.

For glass syringes: Boil the syringe and needle in water (page 88) for at least 30 minutes in a covered pot. It is also a good practice to boil your metal syringe.

For metal syringes:
• Use a new cartridge for each person who needs an injection. Do not use local anesthetic from a cartridge that you have used on another person.
• Use each disposable needle only 1 time and then throw it away in a box like the one on pages 199 to 200. If you must reuse a needle, replace the cap very carefully and put the needle in a safe place (such as a pan of bleach solution) until you are ready to clean and sterilize it (see pages 87–88).
sterilization, syringe cleaning, needle reuse, infection prevention
Dispose of or Sterilize Used Gloves
High Priority Source: A Book for Midwives
Used gloves must be disposed of or sterilized to prevent the spread of germs.

1. After using a pair of gloves once, dispose of them immediately. Do not reuse them unless they have been properly sterilized.
2. If you need to reuse gloves,...
Used gloves must be disposed of or sterilized to prevent the spread of germs.

1. After using a pair of gloves once, dispose of them immediately. Do not reuse them unless they have been properly sterilized.
2. If you need to reuse gloves, sterilize them before use again. Refer to page 66 for specific sterilization methods.
3. Never touch non-sterile surfaces with used gloves, as this can transfer germs from one area to another (e.g., from the anus to the vagina).

Note: Reusing gloves without proper sterilization increases the risk of infection and should be avoided.
glove disposal, infection control, PPE reuse
Healthcare Management for Patients with Suspected Avian Influenza
High Priority Source: LDS Preparedness Manual
Procedure:

1. Respiratory Hygiene and Cough Etiquette:
- All patients presenting to a healthcare setting with fever and respiratory symptoms should be managed according to recommendations for Respiratory Hygiene and Cough Etiquette.

2....
Procedure:

1. Respiratory Hygiene and Cough Etiquette:
- All patients presenting to a healthcare setting with fever and respiratory symptoms should be managed according to recommendations for Respiratory Hygiene and Cough Etiquette.

2. Travel History Assessment:
- Question all patients regarding their recent travel history, especially within 10 days to countries with avian influenza activity.

3. Isolation Precautions for Severe Cases:
- Patients with a history of travel to areas with avian influenza activity who are hospitalized with severe febrile respiratory illness or under evaluation for avian influenza should be managed using isolation precautions identical to those recommended for patients with known Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome (SARS).
avian influenza management, isolation precautions, SARS-like protocols, respiratory hygiene, travel history assessment
Hygiene and Preventive Measures for First Aid Providers
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

Environmental precautions:
1. Dispose of single-use materials: Place used gloves in disposable solid containers, then burn or bury them properly.
2. Clean reusable items: Wash and dry other materials, storing them in a...
Procedure:

Environmental precautions:
1. Dispose of single-use materials: Place used gloves in disposable solid containers, then burn or bury them properly.
2. Clean reusable items: Wash and dry other materials, storing them in a clean and protected location.
3. Wash contaminated clothing: Use detergent and hot water (at least 70°C / 158°F) for at least 25 minutes. If using cooler water, use cold-water suitable detergent.

Personal precautions:
4. Wash hands with soap and clean water: Do this immediately after intervening and before if possible.
5. Avoid direct contact with body fluids: Wear disposable gloves or plastic bags as protection.
6. Be cautious of sharp objects: Take care to avoid injury from sharp items on the casualty or tools being used.

After exposure to body fluids:
7. Wash contaminated areas thoroughly: Use soap and clean water immediately.
8. Apply disinfectant: Wait 10–15 minutes before rinsing with clean water.
9. Seek medical advice: Get confidential testing, counselling, and follow-up care if exposed to body fluids.
10. Protect wounds: Cover cuts or skin breaks with dry, clean dressings.
11. Avoid contamination of wounds: Prevent dirt or debris from entering the wound.
12. Use appropriate disinfectants:
- Sodium hypochlorite (household bleach 5% active chlorine): Mix 100 ml bleach + 9.9 litres clean water.
- Sodium dichloroisocyanurate (NaDCC): Use 1 tablet per litre of clean water.
hygiene, infection control, first aid, body fluids, disinfectant
Sterilize Instruments to Kill Germs
High Priority Source: Where There is No Dentist
Why it's important: Sterilizing instruments is essential to kill germs that can cause infections in patients.

Steps to follow:
1. Sterilization means killing all harmful germs, especially those that cause hepatitis, tetanus, and mouth...
Why it's important: Sterilizing instruments is essential to kill germs that can cause infections in patients.

Steps to follow:
1. Sterilization means killing all harmful germs, especially those that cause hepatitis, tetanus, and mouth infections.
2. The best method of sterilization is with heat—especially wet heat (steam), which is more effective than dry heat from an oven.
3. Use a simple rule to decide when to sterilize: Always sterilize any instrument that has come into contact with blood or bodily fluids before using it on another person.

Important note: ‘Clean looking’ is not necessarily ‘clean’. Truly clean means free of germs, which can only be achieved through proper sterilization.
instrument sterilization,wet heat,germ killing,hepatitis,tetanus
Wear Gloves to Prevent Infection Spread
High Priority Source: A Book for Midwives
Latex or plastic gloves are essential for preventing the spread of germs during medical procedures.

1. Wear clean gloves whenever you touch a mother’s genitals, blood, or any body fluids.
2. For invasive procedures, use **sterile...
Latex or plastic gloves are essential for preventing the spread of germs during medical procedures.

1. Wear clean gloves whenever you touch a mother’s genitals, blood, or any body fluids.
2. For invasive procedures, use sterile gloves to prevent infection.
3. If gloves are not available, use washed plastic bags as an alternative (washed in disinfectant soap).
4. Plastic bags are less ideal than gloves but better than no protection at all.

Note: Always ensure that any substitute (like plastic bags) is clean and disinfected before use.
gloves, infection control, personal protective equipment, PPE
Administer Antibiotic Treatment for Infection Prevention
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Prevent and treat infection.

Procedure:
1. Administer appropriate antibiotic treatment based on the casualty’s condition and available medications.
2. Ensure the correct dosage is given according to medical guidelines or...
Objective: Prevent and treat infection.

Procedure:
1. Administer appropriate antibiotic treatment based on the casualty’s condition and available medications.
2. Ensure the correct dosage is given according to medical guidelines or instructions from a supervising physician.
3. Monitor for any adverse reactions, such as allergic responses or gastrointestinal side effects.
4. Continue treatment until the full course of antibiotics is completed unless otherwise directed by a medical professional.

Notes: Antibiotics should be used only when indicated and in accordance with established protocols to avoid resistance.
antibiotic treatment,infection prevention
Maintain Cleanliness to Prevent Infections
High Priority Source: Where There is No Dentist
Why it's important: You must keep yourself and your environment clean to prevent the spread of germs and infections.

Steps to follow:
1. Wash your hands thoroughly before examining or treating anyone. Do this in front of the person, in the...
Why it's important: You must keep yourself and your environment clean to prevent the spread of germs and infections.

Steps to follow:
1. Wash your hands thoroughly before examining or treating anyone. Do this in front of the person, in the same room, to demonstrate the importance of cleanliness.
2. Wear gloves (latex or plastic) whenever you touch someone’s mouth or any blood. This protects both the patient and yourself from infections.
3. If you are filling or removing a tooth, or if you are touching any instruments that have been sterilized, use sterile gloves.
4. If gloves are not available, use plastic bags that have been washed in disinfectant soap as an alternative.

Important note: Germs can hide inside bits of old food, cement, or blood on an instrument and continue to live even in boiling water. Always scrub the working end of each instrument carefully with soap and water before use.
handwashing,gloves,instrument cleaning,germ prevention,disinfectant soap
Understand the Difference Between Clean and Sterile
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure:

1. Define clean.
- *Clean* means the absence of dirt.
2. Define sterile.
- *Sterile* means completely free of microorganisms (bacteria, viruses, fungal spores).
3. Recognize that an item can be dirty yet sterile.
...
Procedure:

1. Define clean.
- *Clean* means the absence of dirt.
2. Define sterile.
- *Sterile* means completely free of microorganisms (bacteria, viruses, fungal spores).
3. Recognize that an item can be dirty yet sterile.
- For example, if the dirt is itself sterile, it does not compromise sterility.
4. Understand when sterility is necessary.
- Sterility is required for procedures involving body cavities or deep tissues.
5. Know that clean items are often sufficient for minor wounds.
- For superficial wounds, irrigation with tap water can be as effective as sterile saline in many cases (based on municipal tap water studies).
clean vs. sterile, infection control, wound care, irrigation, tap water, sterile saline
Understand the Difference Between Disinfection and Sterilization
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure:

1. Define sterilization.
- An item is *sterile* when it is completely free of measurable levels of microorganisms (bacteria, viruses, fungal spores) through a chemical or physical process.
2. Define disinfection.
-...
Procedure:

1. Define sterilization.
- An item is *sterile* when it is completely free of measurable levels of microorganisms (bacteria, viruses, fungal spores) through a chemical or physical process.
2. Define disinfection.
- Disinfection destroys microorganisms or inhibits their growth but is not as absolute as sterilization.
3. Recognize that all sterile items are disinfected, but not all disinfected items are sterile.
- Sterilization removes all microbes; disinfection may leave some behind (e.g., fungal spores).
4. Understand the temporary nature of sterility.
- Once a sterile item is exposed to air or the environment, it can quickly become contaminated.
disinfection, sterilization, infection control, microorganisms, sterility
Use of Protective Equipment in First Aid
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

1. Learn how to use protective equipment: Familiarize yourself with gloves, facemasks, and eyeglasses.
2. Wear appropriate gear if available:
- Use vinyl, latex, rubber, or medical-grade gloves.
- Wear a facemask and...
Procedure:

1. Learn how to use protective equipment: Familiarize yourself with gloves, facemasks, and eyeglasses.
2. Wear appropriate gear if available:
- Use vinyl, latex, rubber, or medical-grade gloves.
- Wear a facemask and eyeglasses for additional protection.
3. Use alternative barriers if necessary: If gloves are unavailable, use clean plastics or cloth as protective barriers.
4. Practice artificial respiration safely:
- Use a pocket mask, face shield, handkerchief, or clean cloth to prevent direct contact with body fluids.
protective equipment, infection control, first aid, personal protection
Administer Antibiotics for Cellulitis on an Expedition
Standard Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Procedure:

1. Identify signs of cellulitis: redness, heat, pain, and swelling without an obvious source of infection.
2. Administer antibiotics appropriate for streptococci or staphylococci infections (e.g., amoxicillin plus high-dose...
Procedure:

1. Identify signs of cellulitis: redness, heat, pain, and swelling without an obvious source of infection.
2. Administer antibiotics appropriate for streptococci or staphylococci infections (e.g., amoxicillin plus high-dose flucloxacillin, or erythromycin).

Notes:
• Cellulitis is a skin infection that requires prompt treatment with antibiotics.
• Monitor the patient for improvement and signs of worsening infection.
cellulitis,antibiotics,skin infection,expedition medicine
Hygiene and Infection Control Measures
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Prevent cross-infection between the casualty and responder.

Steps:
1. Hand Hygiene: Wash hands thoroughly with soap and water or use an alcohol-based hand sanitizer before and after any contact with the casualty.
2....
Objective: Prevent cross-infection between the casualty and responder.

Steps:
1. Hand Hygiene: Wash hands thoroughly with soap and water or use an alcohol-based hand sanitizer before and after any contact with the casualty.
2. Protective Equipment: Wear gloves, masks, and eye protection if available to prevent exposure to bodily fluids.
3. Wound Care: Clean wounds with sterile saline solution or clean water and apply antiseptic dressings.
4. Avoid Contamination: Ensure all medical instruments are sterilized before use and dispose of used materials properly.
5. Environmental Hygiene: Keep the casualty’s surroundings clean to reduce the risk of infection.

Notes: Basic hygiene practices should be followed at all times, even in resource-limited environments.
hygiene, infection control, hand washing, protective equipment, wound care
Implement Home Isolation for Pandemic Influenza Patients
Standard Source: HHSPandemicInfluenzaPlan
Procedure:

1. Physically separate the patient with pandemic influenza from non-ill persons in the household as much as possible.

2. The patient should not leave the home during the period when they are most likely to be infectious...
Procedure:

1. Physically separate the patient with pandemic influenza from non-ill persons in the household as much as possible.

2. The patient should not leave the home during the period when they are most likely to be infectious (i.e., 5 days after onset of symptoms).

3. If movement outside the home is necessary (e.g., for medical care), the patient must:
- Follow respiratory hygiene/cough etiquette, such as covering the mouth and nose when coughing or sneezing.
- Wear a mask to reduce transmission risk.
home isolation, pandemic flu, respiratory hygiene, mask use
Limit Household Exposure to Pandemic Influenza Patients
Standard Source: HHSPandemicInfluenzaPlan
Procedure:

1. Persons who have not been exposed to pandemic influenza and are not essential for patient care or support should not enter the home while the patient is still having a fever due to pandemic influenza.

2. If unexposed...
Procedure:

1. Persons who have not been exposed to pandemic influenza and are not essential for patient care or support should not enter the home while the patient is still having a fever due to pandemic influenza.

2. If unexposed persons must enter the home, they should:
- Avoid close contact with the patient.

3. Household members living with the patient should:
- Limit contact with the patient as much as possible.
- Consider designating one person as the primary care provider to minimize exposure for others.

4. All household members should be vigilant for the development of influenza symptoms and consult healthcare providers about:
- Whether a pandemic influenza vaccine, if available, should be considered.
- Whether antiviral prophylaxis is appropriate.
household exposure, pandemic flu, infection control, prophylaxis
Use Antibiotics to Fight Bacterial Infections
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Title: Use Antibiotics to Fight Bacterial Infections

Content:
Antibiotics are medicines used to fight infections caused by bacteria. Broad-spectrum antibiotics such as tetracycline kill many kinds of bacteria, while narrow-spectrum...
Title: Use Antibiotics to Fight Bacterial Infections

Content:
Antibiotics are medicines used to fight infections caused by bacteria. Broad-spectrum antibiotics such as tetracycline kill many kinds of bacteria, while narrow-spectrum antibiotics like penicillin kill only a few kinds.

Usage Guidelines:
1. Take the full course of antibiotics as prescribed, even if symptoms improve before completion.
2. Do not share antibiotics with others or use them for viral infections (e.g., colds, flu).
3. Be aware of potential side effects such as diarrhea, nausea, and allergic reactions.
4. If symptoms worsen or if there are signs of an allergic reaction, seek medical attention immediately.

Warning: Overuse of antibiotics can lead to the development of antibiotic-resistant bacteria.
antibiotic,bacterial infection,tetracycline,penicillin,resistance

Pain Management (18) -

Administer Pain Medications and Antibiotics to Combat-Wounded Soldiers
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure: Administer pain medications and antibiotics (the casualty's combat pill pack) to any Soldier wounded in combat. Each Soldier will be issued a combat pill pack before deploying on tactical missions.
combat pill pack, pain medication, antibiotics
Administer Strong Painkillers for Severe Pain
High Priority Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Overview:

Strong painkillers are reserved for severe pain that is not controlled by simple or moderate-strength medications.

Available drugs:
• Nalbuphine, Tramadol (stronger formulations), and Morphine: These should be used only when...
Overview:

Strong painkillers are reserved for severe pain that is not controlled by simple or moderate-strength medications.

Available drugs:
• Nalbuphine, Tramadol (stronger formulations), and Morphine: These should be used only when necessary and under supervision.

Administration principles:
• Administer these medications regularly, following the recommended dosage.
• The dose may need to be increased gradually until pain is controlled.

Important notes:
• Strong painkillers are not typically carried by expedition groups due to their potency and potential for misuse or side effects.
• These drugs should only be used when absolutely necessary, and under the guidance of a trained medical professional.
strong painkillers, morphine, nalbuphine, severe pain
Analgesia Administration in Tactical Field Care
High Priority Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Procedure:

1. Administer morphine (5mg) IV for pain management as necessary.
2. Wait 5 minutes after administration before repeating the dose if needed.
3. Monitor the patient for adverse effects such as respiratory depression or...
Procedure:

1. Administer morphine (5mg) IV for pain management as necessary.
2. Wait 5 minutes after administration before repeating the dose if needed.
3. Monitor the patient for adverse effects such as respiratory depression or hypotension.
4. Do not administer morphine IM; it must be given IV.
morphine_administration,pain_management,tactical_field_care
Administer Moderate-Strength Painkillers for Moderate Pain
Standard Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Overview:

Moderate-strength painkillers are used when simple painkillers are insufficient to manage moderate pain.

Available drugs:
• Dihydrocodeine (DF118): Take one tablet (30mg) three to four times a day. Side effects may...
Overview:

Moderate-strength painkillers are used when simple painkillers are insufficient to manage moderate pain.

Available drugs:
• Dihydrocodeine (DF118): Take one tablet (30mg) three to four times a day. Side effects may include constipation, nausea, and drowsiness.
• Tramadol (Zydol): Used for moderate-to-severe pain. Take one to two tablets (50–100mg) every 4–6 hours, not exceeding eight tablets per day. It can also be administered as an injection (50–100mg) every 4–6 hours.

Important warnings:
• Tramadol should not be taken with alcohol or given after head injuries or to people with epilepsy, as it may trigger seizures.
• Side effects include nausea, vomiting, dry mouth, drowsiness, and rash.
dihydrocodeine, tramadol, moderate painkillers, side effects
Administer Nalbuphine (Nubain) for Pain Relief
Standard Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Indications: For severe pain relief in expedition settings where legal restrictions on other opioids may apply.

Administration Method: Subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous injection.

Dosage:
• 10–20mg every 3–6 hours for a...
Indications: For severe pain relief in expedition settings where legal restrictions on other opioids may apply.

Administration Method: Subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous injection.

Dosage:
• 10–20mg every 3–6 hours for a 70-kg patient.

Additional Considerations:
• Similar side effects to morphine, including sedation and potential respiratory depression.
• Monitor for nausea or vomiting; consider administering an antisickness drug if needed.
• Avoid prolonged use due to risk of dependence.

Documentation: Record the dose, time administered, and any patient response. Provide this information when the patient is evacuated.
nalbuphine,pain relief,expedition medicine,first aid
Managing Chest Pain from Flu-Related Coughing
Standard Source: Flu Home Treatment
Procedure:

1. Suppress the cough using dextromethorphan (DM) or other appropriate methods to allow injured tissues to heal.
2. Administer a full dose of acetaminophen and/or ibuprofen every six hours for pain relief.
3. Apply an **ice...
Procedure:

1. Suppress the cough using dextromethorphan (DM) or other appropriate methods to allow injured tissues to heal.
2. Administer a full dose of acetaminophen and/or ibuprofen every six hours for pain relief.
3. Apply an ice pack, heating pad, or hot water bottle to the chest wall if muscle spasm is suspected.

Notes:
• Chest pain during flu is often due to the effect of coughing on muscles, ribs, and cartilages surrounding the lungs.
• Ensure that the patient avoids activities that exacerbate the pain.
chest pain,cough suppression,pain relief,ice pack
Managing Fever and Pain from Breast Infection
Standard Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure for Managing Fever and Pain from Breast Infection:

• Administer 500 to 1000 mg paracetamol by mouth every 4 hours until the pain subsides.

Important Notes:
• Paracetamol is used to manage fever and discomfort associated with...
Procedure for Managing Fever and Pain from Breast Infection:

• Administer 500 to 1000 mg paracetamol by mouth every 4 hours until the pain subsides.

Important Notes:
• Paracetamol is used to manage fever and discomfort associated with mastitis.
• Do not exceed the recommended dosage or frequency.
• If symptoms persist, seek medical attention.
fever,pain management,paracetamol,mastitis
Pain Control Techniques in First Aid
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

• Administer oral analgesics if the casualty is conscious and able to swallow.
• Use intercostal nerve blocks for localized pain relief.
• Administer tramadol injections for moderate to severe pain.

Warnings:
• Avoid...
Procedure:

• Administer oral analgesics if the casualty is conscious and able to swallow.
• Use intercostal nerve blocks for localized pain relief.
• Administer tramadol injections for moderate to severe pain.

Warnings:
• Avoid using pethidine or morphine, as they can cause respiratory depression, especially in casualties with compromised breathing.

Notes:
• Pain control should be managed carefully and only by trained personnel if possible.
pain management, tramadol, intercostal nerve block, morphine warning
Administer Simple Painkillers for Mild-to-Moderate Pain
Standard Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Overview:

Simple painkillers are suitable for managing mild-to-moderate pain and fever.

Available drugs:
• Paracetamol: Effective for mild-to-moderate pain and fever. Take two tablets (1g) every 4–6 hours, not exceeding **eight...
Overview:

Simple painkillers are suitable for managing mild-to-moderate pain and fever.

Available drugs:
• Paracetamol: Effective for mild-to-moderate pain and fever. Take two tablets (1g) every 4–6 hours, not exceeding eight tablets in 24 hours.
• Aspirin: Good for mild-to-moderate pain and fever, but may cause stomach irritation or allergic reactions. Take one to three tablets (300–900mg) every 4–6 hours, not exceeding 4g in 24 hours.

Notes:
• Aspirin should be avoided by those with an allergy or a history of peptic ulcers.
• It is important to take these medications regularly, especially for pain caused by injury, rather than waiting until the pain returns.
paracetamol, aspirin, simple painkillers, mild-to-moderate pain
Assess and Manage Pain in a Patient
Standard Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Overview:

Pain management is essential for patient comfort. The perception of pain can be influenced by factors such as fear, anxiety, fatigue, extreme temperatures, and the responses of those nearby.

Steps to manage pain:
1. Provide...
Overview:

Pain management is essential for patient comfort. The perception of pain can be influenced by factors such as fear, anxiety, fatigue, extreme temperatures, and the responses of those nearby.

Steps to manage pain:
1. Provide reassurance to the patient.
2. Ensure the patient has shelter and is kept warm or cooled appropriately depending on environmental conditions.
3. Splint fractures if present to reduce movement and pain.
4. Relieve skin pressure by carefully turning the patient regularly, especially if they are immobile.
5. Provide adequate food and fluids to maintain energy levels and hydration.
6. Ensure the patient has rest to promote recovery and reduce fatigue.

Additional considerations:
• Severe pain may be associated with nausea. Use antisickness drugs, such as prochlorperazine or Stemetil, to manage these symptoms and improve comfort.
• Always assess for underlying causes of pain and treat them if possible.
pain management, reassurance, splinting, rest, nausea, antisickness drugs
Assess the Nature of Pain for Diagnosis and Treatment
Standard Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Overview:

To effectively manage pain, it is important to assess its characteristics and possible causes.

Steps to assess pain:
1. Determine when the pain started and whether there was an injury or other incident that may have caused...
Overview:

To effectively manage pain, it is important to assess its characteristics and possible causes.

Steps to assess pain:
1. Determine when the pain started and whether there was an injury or other incident that may have caused it.
2. Identify the main site of the pain and whether it radiates to other areas.
3. Note what makes the pain worse or better, such as movement, position, or specific activities.
4. Determine if the pain is constant or intermittent.
5. Describe the character of the pain: for example, burning, crushing, dull, sharp, etc.
6. Identify any associated symptoms, such as nausea, diarrhea, vomiting, or fever.

Purpose:
This assessment helps determine the likely cause of the pain and guides appropriate treatment.
pain assessment, pain characteristics, associated symptoms, diagnosis
Record and Communicate Painkiller Use in Injured Casualties
Standard Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Purpose: To ensure proper medical care during evacuation.

Procedure:
1. If strong painkillers are used, record the following:
- Name of the drug administered (e.g., morphine, nalbuphine, buprenorphine).
- Dose given.
- Time of...
Purpose: To ensure proper medical care during evacuation.

Procedure:
1. If strong painkillers are used, record the following:
- Name of the drug administered (e.g., morphine, nalbuphine, buprenorphine).
- Dose given.
- Time of administration.
- Patient response to the medication.
2. Provide this information to medical personnel when the patient is evacuated.
3. If strong painkillers are not carried, manage with weak painkillers and comfort measures (e.g., reassurance from a competent companion).

Additional Considerations:
• Ensure that documentation is clear and accurate for continuity of care.
documentation,painkiller use,evacuation,medical care
Treating Headache with Ibuprofen and Acetaminophen
Standard Source: Flu Home Treatment
Procedure:

1. Administer ibuprofen and/or acetaminophen to the patient for headache relief.
2. Apply an ice pack on the neck if the headache is due to fever or coughing.
3. If the temperature exceeds 102°F, consider using **tepid...
Procedure:

1. Administer ibuprofen and/or acetaminophen to the patient for headache relief.
2. Apply an ice pack on the neck if the headache is due to fever or coughing.
3. If the temperature exceeds 102°F, consider using tepid water baths to lower it.

Notes:
• This treatment helps manage headaches caused by fever, coughing, or direct viral infection.
• Ensure that the patient stays hydrated and rests.
headache,ibuprofen,acetaminophen,ice pack
Use Ibuprofen for Pain and Inflammation
Standard Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Overview:

Ibuprofen is an anti-inflammatory drug used to treat mild-to-moderate pain, especially when inflammation is involved.

Administration:
• Take 400mg every 8 hours, preferably with food to reduce stomach...
Overview:

Ibuprofen is an anti-inflammatory drug used to treat mild-to-moderate pain, especially when inflammation is involved.

Administration:
• Take 400mg every 8 hours, preferably with food to reduce stomach irritation.

Contraindications and warnings:
• Do not take ibuprofen with aspirin or in patients with an aspirin allergy or a history of peptic ulcers.
• Side effects may include indigestion, heartburn, nausea, and in some cases, worsening of asthma symptoms.

Combination use:
• Ibuprofen can be taken with paracetamol or other painkillers for enhanced relief, but always follow recommended dosing guidelines.
ibuprofen, anti-inflammatory, pain with inflammation, contraindications
Administer Aspirin for Pain Relief and Fever Reduction
Reference Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

1. Select the correct dosage:
- For adults: Use 300 mg (5 grain) aspirin tablets.
- For children under 12: Use 75 mg (1 ¼ grain) child’s aspirin tablets. Acetaminophen is safer for children under 12.

2. **Administer the...
Procedure:

1. Select the correct dosage:
- For adults: Use 300 mg (5 grain) aspirin tablets.
- For children under 12: Use 75 mg (1 ¼ grain) child’s aspirin tablets. Acetaminophen is safer for children under 12.

2. Administer the medication:
- Take one tablet every 4 to 6 hours as needed for pain or fever, but do not exceed the maximum daily dose (usually 3-4 grams per day for adults).

3. Monitor for side effects: Watch for signs of stomach irritation, bleeding, or allergic reactions.

Important Notes: Aspirin is a low-cost analgesic that helps reduce pain, fever, and inflammation. It also provides mild relief from coughing and itching. Avoid aspirin-containing medications for children under 12 unless specifically advised by a health worker.
aspirin, pain relief, fever reduction, child safety, analgesic
Manage Pain from Minor Injuries and Environmental Hazards
Reference Source: Aids to Survival
Procedure:
1. Identify the cause of pain (e.g., stings, bites, splinters, blisters).
2. Remove the source of pain if possible (e.g., remove a splinter or sting).
3. Treat the injury with appropriate first aid measures.

Note: Pain from minor...
Procedure:
1. Identify the cause of pain (e.g., stings, bites, splinters, blisters).
2. Remove the source of pain if possible (e.g., remove a splinter or sting).
3. Treat the injury with appropriate first aid measures.

Note: Pain from minor injuries is usually manageable with basic treatment and reassurance.
pain management, stings, splinters, blisters
Use Analgesics for Pain Relief
Reference Source: Where There is No Dentist
Title: Use Analgesics for Pain Relief

Content:
Analgesics are medicines used to relieve pain. Common examples include aspirin, acetaminophen (paracetamol), and codeine.

Usage Guidelines:
1. Read the label carefully and...
Title: Use Analgesics for Pain Relief

Content:
Analgesics are medicines used to relieve pain. Common examples include aspirin, acetaminophen (paracetamol), and codeine.

Usage Guidelines:
1. Read the label carefully and follow dosage instructions.
2. Use only as needed for pain relief.
3. Avoid combining analgesics unless directed by a healthcare provider.
4. Do not use aspirin in children under 16 years of age due to the risk of Reye’s syndrome.
5. If pain persists or worsens, seek medical attention.

Warning: Overuse of analgesics can lead to serious side effects and should be avoided unless necessary.
analgesic,pain relief,aspirin,acetaminophen,codeine
Use Local Anaesthetic to Numb a Wound
Reference Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure:

1. Select an appropriate local anaesthetic, such as lidocaine or benzocaine.
2. Sterilize the area around the wound using alcohol swabs or antiseptic solution.
3. Apply a small amount of anaesthetic cream to the skin...
Procedure:

1. Select an appropriate local anaesthetic, such as lidocaine or benzocaine.
2. Sterilize the area around the wound using alcohol swabs or antiseptic solution.
3. Apply a small amount of anaesthetic cream to the skin surrounding the wound and allow it to sit for 5-10 minutes.
4. Use a syringe with a fine needle to inject the anaesthetic directly into the tissue near the wound, avoiding major blood vessels or nerves.
5. Wait for the numbing effect to take place, which usually occurs within 5-10 minutes.
6. Proceed with cleaning, debriding, or suturing the wound as needed.
local anaesthetic, numb wound, pain relief

Eye Injuries (17) -

First Aid for Extruded Eyeballs
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Step-by-step procedure:

1. Cover the extruded eye: Gently cover the extruded eye with a loose moistened dressing.
2. Cover the unaffected eye: Also cover the unaffected eye to immobilize both eyes and reduce movement.
3. **Keep the...
Step-by-step procedure:

1. Cover the extruded eye: Gently cover the extruded eye with a loose moistened dressing.
2. Cover the unaffected eye: Also cover the unaffected eye to immobilize both eyes and reduce movement.
3. Keep the casualty quiet: Ensure the casualty remains still to prevent further injury.
4. Treat for shock: Administer first aid for shock as needed.
5. Evacuate immediately: Transport the casualty to a medical facility without delay.

Important Notes:
• Do not bind or exert pressure on the injured eye while applying the dressing.
• This is a severe injury requiring immediate professional medical attention.
extruded eyeball, eye trauma, first aid, emergency evacuation
Apply Protective Dressing to Eye Injuries Without Pressure
Critical Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Important: Do not apply pressure when there is a possible laceration of the eyeball. The eyeball contains fluid, and pressure applied over the eye will force the fluid out, resulting in permanent injury.

Procedure:
1. Gently cover the...
Important: Do not apply pressure when there is a possible laceration of the eyeball. The eyeball contains fluid, and pressure applied over the eye will force the fluid out, resulting in permanent injury.

Procedure:
1. Gently cover the injured eye with a loose moistened dressing.
2. Also cover the unaffected eye to prevent movement and further injury.
3. Do not bind or exert pressure on the injured eye while applying the dressing.
4. Keep the casualty quiet, place him on his back, treat for shock, and evacuate immediately.
eye injury, protective dressing, no pressure, extruded eyeball, first aid
Treat Chemical Exposure in the Eye
Critical Source: Family Emergency Handbook
Procedure:

1. Immediately wash the affected eye with large amounts of fresh cold water for at least 15 minutes.
2. Ensure that the water flows from the inner corner of the eye to the outer corner to flush out any chemicals.
3. Do not rub...
Procedure:

1. Immediately wash the affected eye with large amounts of fresh cold water for at least 15 minutes.
2. Ensure that the water flows from the inner corner of the eye to the outer corner to flush out any chemicals.
3. Do not rub the eye or attempt to remove embedded foreign objects.
4. If a foreign object is in the eye, cover both eyes lightly with bandages and seek professional help as soon as possible.
5. For puncture wounds to the eye, follow the same steps: cover both eyes lightly with bandages and seek immediate medical attention.
chemical exposure, eye injury, foreign object, first aid, puncture wound
Treat Eye Exposure to Blister Agents
Critical Source: NATO Emergency War Surgery
Eye Treatment:

• Immediately flush the contaminated eye with water.
• Apply antibiotic ointment, with or without steroid, to minimize infection.
• In more severe cases:
- Use local anesthetic drops or ointment (e.g., Tetracaine)...
Eye Treatment:

• Immediately flush the contaminated eye with water.
• Apply antibiotic ointment, with or without steroid, to minimize infection.
• In more severe cases:
- Use local anesthetic drops or ointment (e.g., Tetracaine) for extreme blepharospasm and pain.
- Irrigate the eye with sterile saline to remove crusted exudate.
eye injury, chemical exposure, antibiotic ointment, Tetracaine, decontamination
First Aid for Eye Burns
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Step-by-step procedure:

1. Identify the type of burn: Determine whether the eye has suffered a chemical, thermal (heat), or light burn.
2. Cover with a sterile dressing: Apply a loose sterile dressing to protect the injured eye from...
Step-by-step procedure:

1. Identify the type of burn: Determine whether the eye has suffered a chemical, thermal (heat), or light burn.
2. Cover with a sterile dressing: Apply a loose sterile dressing to protect the injured eye from further damage.
3. Avoid pressure on the eye: Do not apply any pressure to the eye, as it may cause additional injury.
4. Seek immediate medical attention: Transport the casualty to a medical facility for professional treatment.

Important Notes:
• Eye burns can lead to permanent vision loss if not treated promptly.
• This procedure is critical in minimizing long-term damage and preserving sight.
eye burn, first aid, chemical injury, thermal injury
First Aid for Lacerated/Torn Eyelids
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Step-by-step procedure:

1. Cover the injured eye: Use a sterile dressing to cover the injured eye.
2. Avoid pressure on the wound: Do not apply any pressure on the wound, as it may injure the eyeball.
3. Handle with care: Handle...
Step-by-step procedure:

1. Cover the injured eye: Use a sterile dressing to cover the injured eye.
2. Avoid pressure on the wound: Do not apply any pressure on the wound, as it may injure the eyeball.
3. Handle with care: Handle torn eyelids very carefully to prevent further injury.
4. Preserve detached pieces: If any parts of the eyelid are detached, place them on a clean bandage or dressing and send them with the casualty to the medical facility.

Important Notes:
• Lacerated eyelids may bleed heavily but usually stop quickly.
• Ensure that the sterile dressing is loose enough not to cause additional damage.
eyelid injury, first aid, sterile dressing, eye trauma
Provide First Aid for Eye Exposure to CN Riot Control Agent
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Immediately flush the affected eye(s) with water from a canteen or water bottle for at least 15 minutes.
2. Avoid rubbing the eyes, as this may cause mechanical injury and worsen chemical damage.
3. **Do not use any other...
Procedure:

1. Immediately flush the affected eye(s) with water from a canteen or water bottle for at least 15 minutes.
2. Avoid rubbing the eyes, as this may cause mechanical injury and worsen chemical damage.
3. Do not use any other substances (e.g., lotions, oils) on the eye unless directed by medical personnel.
4. If vision is temporarily impaired, reassure the individual that permanent blindness from vapor exposure has never been observed even at high concentrations.
5. Seek medical attention if symptoms persist or if there are signs of corneal opacity or chemical burns.

Notes:
• This procedure applies to exposure to CN (chloracetophenone), a riot control agent with higher toxicity than CS.
• Severe eye exposure may result in corrosive burns, corneal opacity, and permanent visual impairment if not treated promptly.
CN, eye exposure, first aid, chemical burns, corneal opacity
Remove Foreign Bodies from the Eye
High Priority Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Procedure for Removing Foreign Bodies from the Eye:

1. Check the eye surface: Ask the casualty to look in all directions and examine the eye carefully.
2. Use a moistened cotton bud: If visible, remove the foreign body with this...
Procedure for Removing Foreign Bodies from the Eye:

1. Check the eye surface: Ask the casualty to look in all directions and examine the eye carefully.
2. Use a moistened cotton bud: If visible, remove the foreign body with this tool.
3. Administer local anesthetic: Apply amethocaine drops if the foreign body is under the upper lid or eyelid spasm prevents clear view.
4. Evert the upper lid:
- Ask the patient to look downwards.
- Grasp the upper eyelashes firmly with one hand.
- Use a cotton bud or matchstick on the skin crease of the upper lid and push down while lifting the eyelashes upwards with the other hand.
5. Remove foreign body: Once the underside of the upper lid is visible, remove any foreign body present.
6. Seek medical attention: If the foreign body cannot be easily removed or if an abrasion remains, consult a doctor or nurse.
foreign body eye, amethocaine, evert lid, cotton bud, medical attention
Treat Eye Abrasions with Local Anesthetic and Padding
High Priority Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Procedure for Treating Eye Abrasions:

1. Administer local anesthetic: Apply a drop of amethocaine to the affected eye. This can be repeated as needed, but should not be used in excess.
2. Apply antibiotic ointment: If the patient is in...
Procedure for Treating Eye Abrasions:

1. Administer local anesthetic: Apply a drop of amethocaine to the affected eye. This can be repeated as needed, but should not be used in excess.
2. Apply antibiotic ointment: If the patient is in safe surroundings, apply a drop of tropicamide and chloramphenicol ointment to the eye.
3. Pad the eye firmly: Ensure that the lids are closed beneath the pad for protection and comfort.
4. Monitor healing time: A total removal of the corneal top layer should heal within 36 hours. If not, seek further specialised attention to exclude an infection or retained foreign body.
eye abrasion, corneal abrasion, amethocaine, chloramphenicol ointment, eye padding
Treat Snowblindness
High Priority Source: The Complete Outdoorsmans Handbook
Procedure:

1. Shield the victim's eyes from light by wrapping them with a bandage.
2. Seek medical aid immediately to treat snowblindness.
3. The patient may require an ophthalmic ointment and several weeks of care for full...
Procedure:

1. Shield the victim's eyes from light by wrapping them with a bandage.
2. Seek medical aid immediately to treat snowblindness.
3. The patient may require an ophthalmic ointment and several weeks of care for full recovery.

Notes:
• People who have previously suffered from snowblindness are more susceptible to it.
snowblindness treatment, first aid, eye protection
Provide First Aid for Snow Blindness
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. Cover the eyes with a dark cloth. If further exposure to light is not preventable, protect the eyes with dark bandages or the darkest glasses available.
2. Evacuate the casualty to the nearest medical treatment facility.
snow blindness, eye injury, first aid, vision protection
Removing a Foreign Object from the Eye
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Do not rub the eye: Rubbing may cause further injury to the delicate tissues of the eye.
2. Close the affected eye: Tears will accumulate and may help wash out the foreign body.
3. Try the lid method: Pull the upper...
Procedure:

1. Do not rub the eye: Rubbing may cause further injury to the delicate tissues of the eye.
2. Close the affected eye: Tears will accumulate and may help wash out the foreign body.
3. Try the lid method: Pull the upper eyelid over the lower one two or three times, then close the nostril on the opposite side and ask the patient to blow their nose hard.
4. Examine under the lower lid: Have the patient look up, press the lower lid down, and check for the foreign body. If seen, gently lift it out with a clean handkerchief.
5. Check under the upper lid: Seat the patient in a chair with their head bent backward. Place a match or thin pencil across the upper lid about half an inch from its edge. Turn the lid back over the object and remove the foreign body as before.
6. Soothe the eye: Apply a drop of castor oil to the eye after removing the foreign body.

Warnings:
• Avoid using fingers or unclean objects to remove the foreign body.
• If the object cannot be removed, seek medical help immediately.
foreign body, eye injury, first aid, castor oil, eyelid examination
Treat Snow Blindness with Eye Protection and Comfort Measures
Standard Source: USMC Summer Survival Course Handbook
Procedure:

1. Evacuate the individual if possible to a safer location.
2. Patch both eyes completely to prevent any further light exposure.
3. If conditions allow (not too cold), apply wet compresses to help relieve discomfort.
4....
Procedure:

1. Evacuate the individual if possible to a safer location.
2. Patch both eyes completely to prevent any further light exposure.
3. If conditions allow (not too cold), apply wet compresses to help relieve discomfort.
4. Allow time for healing:
- Mild cases may take up to two days to heal.
- Severe cases may require up to one week of rest and protection.

Notes:
• Do not rub or apply pressure to the eyes.
• Avoid exposure to bright light until symptoms subside.
snow blindness treatment, eye patching, cold weather first aid, uv damage
Use Minims Amethocaine for Pain Relief in the Eye
Standard Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Overview:

Minims Amethocaine is a local anesthetic used to relieve pain from eye injuries or conditions.

Administration:
• Apply one drop of Minims Amethocaine (0.5%) to the affected eye.
• This provides approximately **20 minutes of...
Overview:

Minims Amethocaine is a local anesthetic used to relieve pain from eye injuries or conditions.

Administration:
• Apply one drop of Minims Amethocaine (0.5%) to the affected eye.
• This provides approximately 20 minutes of pain relief, suitable for managing conditions such as:
- Ocular abrasion
- Snowblindness
- Foreign body in the eye

Available forms:
• 10ml bottle or 0.5ml Minims.

Notes:
• This medication is typically used for short-term pain relief during examination or treatment of ocular injuries.
minims amethocaine, eye pain relief, ocular abrasion, snowblindness
Recognize and Manage Snow Blindness
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid + FM 21 76 1 Survival Evasion and Recovery Multiservice Procedures + STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1 + SH 21 76 Ranger Handbook
Definition: Snow blindness is the effect that glare from an ice field or snowfield has on the eyes. It is more likely to occur in hazy, cloudy weather than when the sun is shining.

Signs and Symptoms:
• Sensation of grit in the eyes with...
Definition: Snow blindness is the effect that glare from an ice field or snowfield has on the eyes. It is more likely to occur in hazy, cloudy weather than when the sun is shining.

Signs and Symptoms:
• Sensation of grit in the eyes with pain in and over the eyes, made worse by moving the eyeball.
• Watering, redness, headache, and increased pain on exposure to light.
• Possible reduction in vision caused by sun exposure.

Prevention:
• Use improvised goggles or protective eyewear to prevent snow blindness. Avoid waiting until discomfort is felt before using protective measures, as a deep burn of the eyes may already have occurred.

First Aid Measures:
1. Blindfold or cover the eyes with a dark cloth which stops painful eye movement.
2. Complete rest is desirable.
3. If further exposure to light is not preventable, protect the eyes with dark bandages or the darkest glasses available.
4. Remove the individual from bright light sources immediately.
5. Administer pain relief if needed.
6. Avoid rubbing the eyes, as this can worsen corneal damage.
7. Once unprotected exposure to sunlight stops, the condition usually heals in a few days without permanent damage.
8. Evacuate the casualty to the nearest MTF or seek medical attention for further evaluation and treatment.

Note: Waiting until discomfort (pain) is felt before using protective eyewear is dangerous because a deep burn of the eyes may already have occurred.
snow blindness, eye injury, first aid, protective eyewear, light sensitivity
Bandaging the Eye with Impaled Objects or Foreign Bodies
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid + Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

1. If there is an impaled object or a significant-sized foreign body in the eye:
- Both eyes should be bandaged together to help secure the foreign body in the injured eye.
2. In a battlefield environment, leave the...
Procedure:

1. If there is an impaled object or a significant-sized foreign body in the eye:
- Both eyes should be bandaged together to help secure the foreign body in the injured eye.
2. In a battlefield environment, leave the uninjured eye uncovered so that the casualty can see.

Important Notes:
• This helps prevent further movement of the object and reduces the risk of additional injury.
• Avoid touching or attempting to remove the foreign body without professional help.
bandage, eye injury, impaled object, foreign body, first aid
First Aid for Lacerated Eyeball (Injury to the Globe)
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid + Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Step-by-step procedure:

1. Cover with a loose sterile dressing: Cover the injured eye with a loose sterile dressing.
2. Avoid pressure on the eyeball: Do not apply any pressure on the eyeball, as it may cause additional damage.
3....
Step-by-step procedure:

1. Cover with a loose sterile dressing: Cover the injured eye with a loose sterile dressing.
2. Avoid pressure on the eyeball: Do not apply any pressure on the eyeball, as it may cause additional damage.
3. Immobilize both eyes: Apply a bandage to both eyes to immobilize them. This helps prevent further movement and potential injury to the affected eye.
4. Consider environmental factors: In hazardous surroundings, leave the uninjured eye uncovered so the casualty can see.

Important Notes:
• The eyeball contains fluid; applying pressure may force it out and cause permanent damage.
• This procedure is critical for preventing further injury and preserving vision.
lacerated eyeball, injury to the globe, first aid, eye injury, sterile dressing

Head Injuries (17) -

Assess and Manage a Head Injury According to First Aid Principles
Critical Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
A Assessment of the scene: Ensure that you do not endanger yourself.

A Airway with neck control: An unconscious casualty’s airway is at risk as many people vomit following a head injury. The gag and cough reflexes may not function normally...
A Assessment of the scene: Ensure that you do not endanger yourself.

A Airway with neck control: An unconscious casualty’s airway is at risk as many people vomit following a head injury. The gag and cough reflexes may not function normally to clear the airway, depending on the level of unconsciousness, so it is important to place the casualty carefully in the recovery position (see Figure .). A chin lift and head tilt will normally open the airway. Remember the possibility of an associated neck injury, but always give the airway priority. Try to avoid overextending the neck and stabilise the neck in a neutral position.

B Breathing: Once the airway is secure, check that breathing is adequate and measure the breathing rate.

C Circulation with control of bleeding: Look for any obvious external haemorrhage and control bleeding with direct pressure. Measure the pulse rate.

D Disability: Assess the response level using AVPU:
- A wake and A lert
- V oice – responds to voice
- P ain – responds to pain
- U nresponsive.

Look at the pupils and check that they constrict when a light is shone into the eye. Rising pressure inside the skull may mean that one or both pupils fail to respond to light and are fixed and dilated. This is a serious sign and means evacuation should be arranged immediately.

The Glasgow Coma Scale (see Chapter ) allows a more comprehensive assessment of unconsciousness.

E Exposure with environment control: Examine the casualty carefully from head to toe by undressing but always be aware of the risk of hypothermia. Do not move the casualty unnecessarily.
head injury, first aid, airway management, breathing assessment, circulation control, disability assessment, exposure control, Glasgow Coma Scale
Apply Field Dressing to Head Wounds - Forehead or Back of the Head
Critical Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure for applying a field dressing to wounds on the forehead or back of the head:

1. Position the dressing correctly: Apply the dressing with the white side down, directly over the wound.
2. Extend tails toward sides of the head:...
Procedure for applying a field dressing to wounds on the forehead or back of the head:

1. Position the dressing correctly: Apply the dressing with the white side down, directly over the wound.
2. Extend tails toward sides of the head: Ensure the tails extend toward the sides of the head.
3. Wrap tails around the head: Wrap each tail one at a time around the head in opposite directions.
- Make sure the tails cover the dressing but do not cover the eyes and ears.
4. Secure with a nonslip knot: Tie the tails at the side of the head using a nonslip knot.

Important Warnings:
• Do NOT attempt to clean the wound or remove any protruding object.
• Do NOT apply unnecessary pressure on the wound or try to push brain matter back into the skull.
• Do NOT apply a pressure dressing.
• Do NOT give the casualty food or drink.
• Do NOT touch the white (sterile) side of the dressing. It should only come into contact with the wound.
field dressing, head injury, first aid, wound care, military medical procedures
Evacuation Criteria for Head Injuries in Remote Locations
Critical Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
When to Evacuate Immediately:

• The patient remains unconscious.
• There is an open or base-of-skull fracture (blood or fluid seeping from the ears or nose).
• The patient has had a convulsion or fit.

**Indicators for Considered...
