When to Evacuate Immediately:

• The patient remains unconscious.
• There is an open or base-of-skull fracture (blood or fluid seeping from the ears or nose).
• The patient has had a convulsion or fit.

Indicators for Considered Evacuation in Conscious Patients:

• Worsening headache
• Vomiting
• Drowsiness
• Confusion
• Dilated, unresponsive pupil(s) on one or both sides
• Convulsions
• Deep scalp lacerations
• Worsening Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score

Action: If in doubt, arrange for evacuation to a hospital. It is always better to be overcautious with head injuries.
head injury, evacuation criteria, remote location, unconscious, skull fracture, convulsion, vomiting, drowsiness, confusion, pupil dilation, Glasgow Coma Scale
Head Injury Management on Expeditions
Critical Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Key considerations for head injuries on expeditions:

• Head injuries are a significant risk on expeditions, particularly in mountaineering accidents, motor vehicle accidents and on building project sites.
• Head injuries can result in changes...
Key considerations for head injuries on expeditions:

• Head injuries are a significant risk on expeditions, particularly in mountaineering accidents, motor vehicle accidents and on building project sites.
• Head injuries can result in changes in conscious level, bleeding, infection and disability.
• It is very important to avoid injuring the neck when moving patients after head injuries as about % of individuals who receive a head injury that causes unconsciousness will have an associated neck injury. Be suspicious of a neck injury in anyone who has a significant injury above the collarbones.
• Minor head injuries may cause a transient loss of consciousness, but serious open head injuries are usually rapidly fatal.

Types of head injuries to be aware of:
• Closed head injuries
• Closed head injuries with internal bleeding
• Closed head injuries with brain swelling
• Open head injuries
• Base of skull fractures
head injury,expeditions,mountaineering,neck injury,closed head injuries,open head injuries
Perform First Aid for an Open Head Wound
Critical Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure:

1. Check the casualty's level of consciousness.

- a. Question the casualty.
- (1) Ask: "What is your name?"
- (2) Ask: "Where are you?"
- (3) Ask: "What is today's date (day, month, and...
Procedure:

1. Check the casualty's level of consciousness.

- a. Question the casualty.
- (1) Ask: "What is your name?"
- (2) Ask: "Where are you?"
- (3) Ask: "What is today's date (day, month, and year)?"

- b. Report incorrect answers, inability to answer, or changes in answers to medical personnel.

2. Position the casualty.

WARNING: Do NOT move the casualty if the casualty exhibits signs and/or symptoms—other than minor bleeding—of a neck, spine, or severe head injury.

- a. If the casualty is conscious or has a minor scalp wound:
- (1) Have the casualty sit up unless other injuries prohibit sitting up.
- (2) Raise the head slightly if the casualty is lying down and is not accumulating fluids in his/her throat.
- (3) Turn his/her head to the side or position the casualty on his/her side (opposite the wound) if the wound is bleeding into the mouth or throat.

- b. If the casualty is unconscious or has a severe head injury:
- (1) Treat the casualty as having a potential neck or spinal injury. Immobilize and do not move the casualty unless absolutely necessary.
- (2) Turn the casualty if he/she is choking and/or vomiting or bleeding into the mouth. Position the casualty on his/her side opposite the wound.

WARNING: If it is necessary to turn a casualty with a suspected neck or spinal injury, you will need assistance. Keeping the head, neck, and body aligned while providing support for the head and neck, roll the casualty gently onto his/her side.

3. Expose the wound by removing the casualty's helmet, if necessary.

WARNING: In a chemical, biological, radiological, or nuclear (CBRN) environment, you must leave the casualty with as much protection (such as protective mask, mission-oriented protective posture [MOPP] overgarments) as possible. What items of protective equipment can be removed depends on the casualty's injuries (where on the body and what type), the MOPP level, integrity of protective equipment (such as tears in the garment or mask seal), availability of chemical protective shelters, and the tactical situation.

4. Apply the casualty's dressing to the wound.

WARNING: Do NOT attempt to clean the wound or remove a protruding object. Do NOT put unnecessary pressure on the wound or attempt to push any brain matter back into the head (skull). Do NOT apply a pressure dressing. Do NOT give the casualty any food or drink.

CAUTION: Do NOT touch the white (sterile) side of the dressing. Do NOT allow it to come into contact with any surface other than the wound.

_Note: The following procedures are for applying a field dressing. If you are applying an emergency bandage, follow the general procedure for other bleeding wounds, while observing general guidelines and precautions.

• a. Forehead or back of the head.
- (1) Apply the dressing, white side down, directly over the wound with the tails extending toward the sides of the head.
- (2) Wrap the tails, one at a time, around the head in opposite directions, making sure the tails cover the dressing but not the eyes and ears.
- (3) Tie the tails at the side of the head using a nonslip knot.

• b. Top of the head.
- (1) Apply the dressing, white side down, directly over the wound.
- (2) Wrap one tail down under the chin and bring it up in front of the ear over the dressing to a point just above, and in front of, the opposite ear.
open head wound, first aid, dressing application, casualty positioning, CBRN environment
Understanding Closed Head Injuries
Critical Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Definition of closed head injuries:

• In closed head injuries, the skull remains intact and there is no communication between the brain and the outside world.
• Bleeding or brain swelling may complicate closed head injuries.

**Important...
Definition of closed head injuries:

• In closed head injuries, the skull remains intact and there is no communication between the brain and the outside world.
• Bleeding or brain swelling may complicate closed head injuries.

Important considerations for managing closed head injuries:
• Be aware that even though the skull is not broken, internal bleeding or brain swelling can still occur.
• These complications can be life-threatening and require prompt medical attention.
• If a casualty has a closed head injury with signs of altered consciousness, vomiting, seizures, or worsening symptoms, immediate evacuation should be considered.
closed head injury,brain swelling,internal bleeding,medical attention,evacuation
Control External Bleeding from Head Injury
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Apply a field first aid dressing to control visible bleeding from the head.
2. Do not apply pressure directly on the wound or attempt to push brain matter back into the skull.
3. Avoid using a pressure dressing for...
Procedure:

1. Apply a field first aid dressing to control visible bleeding from the head.
2. Do not apply pressure directly on the wound or attempt to push brain matter back into the skull.
3. Avoid using a pressure dressing for head injuries.
4. If there is an object protruding from the wound, do not remove it.
5. Use clean material to create bulky dressings around the object and apply a field dressing over it.
external bleeding, head injury, dressing application
Cover Exposed Brain, Eye or Other Organ
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

• Carefully cover the exposed part with a moistened dressing (use clean water or sterile normal saline if available).

• Cover with a bandage.
exposed organ, brain injury, eye injury, moistened dressing
Head Injury First Aid and Spinal Precautions
High Priority Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Objective: Provide first aid for head injuries while protecting the spinal column.

Steps:
1. Treat all head and neck injuries as potential spinal injuries unless otherwise confirmed.
2. Do not move the casualty unless he is in...
Objective: Provide first aid for head injuries while protecting the spinal column.

Steps:
1. Treat all head and neck injuries as potential spinal injuries unless otherwise confirmed.
2. Do not move the casualty unless he is in immediate danger (e.g., near a fire or falling object).
3. Stabilize the head and neck using available materials if necessary.
4. Monitor for signs of head injury, such as:
- Nausea and vomiting
- Convulsions or twitches
- Slurred speech
- Confusion and loss of memory
- Dizziness or drowsiness
- Blurred vision or unequal pupils
- Paralysis (partial or full)
- Complaints of headache
- Bleeding from the scalp, nose, or ears
- Deformity of the head
- Staggering while walking
5. Seek immediate medical aid if any signs are present.

Notes:
• Head injuries can be life-threatening and may involve internal bleeding.
• Spinal precautions must always be taken to prevent further injury.
head injury first aid, spinal precautions, head and neck injuries
Manage Injuries Around the Mouth or Maxilla
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

• Check inside the mouth for bleeding, broken teeth and tongue injuries.

• If you encounter any of the above problems, make sure the airway is open:

- if the casualty is conscious: turn his head to the side or position him on the...
Procedure:

• Check inside the mouth for bleeding, broken teeth and tongue injuries.

• If you encounter any of the above problems, make sure the airway is open:

- if the casualty is conscious: turn his head to the side or position him on the side so that blood can drain from the mouth;

- if the casualty is unconscious: put him in the lateral recovery position.
mouth injury, maxilla injury, airway management
Monitor Casualty with Suspected Head Injury
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Continuously monitor the casualty for signs that may require basic lifesaving measures.
2. After initiating first aid, request medical assistance and evacuation immediately.
3. If dedicated medical evacuation assets are...
Procedure:

1. Continuously monitor the casualty for signs that may require basic lifesaving measures.
2. After initiating first aid, request medical assistance and evacuation immediately.
3. If dedicated medical evacuation assets are not available, transport the casualty to an MTF as soon as possible.
4. Do not attempt to remove a protruding object from the head or give the casualty anything to eat or drink.
5. Be prepared to:
- Clear the airway
- Control external bleeding
- Administer first aid for shock
- Keep the casualty warm
- Protect the wound
monitoring, head injury, first aid, evacuation, medical assistance
Monitoring a Patient with a Head Injury Using the Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS)
High Priority Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Purpose: Assess severity of head injury and monitor for deterioration.

Assessment Components:

• Eye opening: Score based on spontaneous eye opening, response to voice, or pain.
• Verbal response: Score based on orientation,...
Purpose: Assess severity of head injury and monitor for deterioration.

Assessment Components:

• Eye opening: Score based on spontaneous eye opening, response to voice, or pain.
• Verbal response: Score based on orientation, confusion, or incomprehensible sounds.
• Motor response: Score based on obeying commands, localizing pain, or abnormal flexion/extension.

Procedure:

1. Assess the patient’s GCS score at least every hour following a significant head injury.
2. Record the score and monitor for changes over time.
3. A decrease in GCS score indicates the need for immediate evacuation to a hospital.
4. Use Figure 12.7 (page 126) as a reference for scoring.
Glasgow Coma Scale, head injury monitoring, GCS score, evacuation criteria, neurological assessment
Provide General First Aid for Head Injury
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Objective: Provide immediate care to a casualty with a head injury.

Steps:
1. Clear the airway if necessary.
2. Control external bleeding using a field first aid dressing.
3. Administer first aid measures for shock (e.g., keep the...
Objective: Provide immediate care to a casualty with a head injury.

Steps:
1. Clear the airway if necessary.
2. Control external bleeding using a field first aid dressing.
3. Administer first aid measures for shock (e.g., keep the casualty warm, protect the wound).
4. Ensure the casualty remains warm and protected from further injury.
5. Do not apply pressure to the wound or attempt to push brain matter back into the skull.
6. Do not use a pressure dressing on head wounds.
head injury, first aid, bleeding control, shock, airway clearance
Manage Heavy Nose Bleeding in a Conscious Casualty
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

• Place the conscious casualty in a sitting position, leaning slightly forward, and pinch the bleeding nostril.
nosebleed, conscious casualty, first aid
Check for Signs of Head Injury and Provide First Aid
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Look for the following signs and symptoms of a head injury:
- Unequal pupils.
- Fluid from the ear(s), nose, mouth, or injury site.
- Slurred speech.
- Confusion.
- Sleepiness.
- Loss of memory or consciousness.
...
Procedure:

1. Look for the following signs and symptoms of a head injury:
- Unequal pupils.
- Fluid from the ear(s), nose, mouth, or injury site.
- Slurred speech.
- Confusion.
- Sleepiness.
- Loss of memory or consciousness.
- Staggering in walking.
- Headache.
- Dizziness.
- Nausea or vomiting.
- Paralysis.
- Convulsions or twitches.
- Bruising around the eyes and behind the ears.

2. If a head injury is suspected, continue to watch for signs requiring rescue breathing, first aid measures for shock, or control of bleeding.
3. Seek medical aid immediately.
4. Refer to paragraph 3-10 for information on first aid measures for head injuries.
head injury, first aid, symptoms, unequal pupils, fluid from ear, slurred speech, confusion, sleepiness, loss of memory, staggering, headache, dizziness, nausea, vomiting, paralysis, convulsions, bruising
Manage Severe Head Injury with Protruding Brain Tissue
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Do not remove or disturb any foreign matter in the wound.
2. Carefully place a loose, moistened dressing (preferably sterile normal saline) over the protruding tissue to protect it from contamination.
3. Apply a **first...
Procedure:

1. Do not remove or disturb any foreign matter in the wound.
2. Carefully place a loose, moistened dressing (preferably sterile normal saline) over the protruding tissue to protect it from contamination.
3. Apply a first aid dressing over the moistened dressing for additional protection.
4. Position the casualty so their head is higher than their body.
5. Keep the casualty warm and seek medical assistance immediately.
6. If an object is protruding, do not remove it; instead, use clean material to create bulky dressings around the object for support.
7. Use the cleanest available material to create bulky dressings around the protruding object for support before applying a field dressing.
head injury, brain tissue protrusion, first aid, emergency care
Manage a Casualty with Suspected Concussion
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Objective: Provide immediate care and ensure prompt medical evacuation for suspected concussion.

Procedure:
1. If a casualty has received a heavy blow to the head or face and exhibits symptoms such as confusion, staggering, or difficulty...
Objective: Provide immediate care and ensure prompt medical evacuation for suspected concussion.

Procedure:
1. If a casualty has received a heavy blow to the head or face and exhibits symptoms such as confusion, staggering, or difficulty breathing:
- Transport the casualty to an MTF immediately.
2. Look for signs of concussion (e.g., confusion, staggering, temporary loss of function).
3. If a concussion is suspected:
- Do not attempt to treat the injury on-site.
- Transport the casualty to an MTF as soon as conditions permit.
4. Monitor for temporary loss of brain function (e.g., short-term confusion or impaired breathing) and for worsening symptoms during transport.
5. Ensure the casualty remains warm and protected from further injury.
concussion, head injury, first aid, transport, MTF, monitoring

Abdominal Injuries (16) -

Immobilize Pelvic Fractures with a Blanket Sling
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
### Procedure: Immobilize Pelvic Fractures with a Blanket Sling

Steps:
1. If there is a fracture of the pelvis, be aware of the danger of internal massive blood loss.
2. Place a sheet or blanket under the abdomen and pelvis of the...
### Procedure: Immobilize Pelvic Fractures with a Blanket Sling

Steps:
1. If there is a fracture of the pelvis, be aware of the danger of internal massive blood loss.
2. Place a sheet or blanket under the abdomen and pelvis of the casualty.
3. Wrap the blanket around the abdomen and pelvis, pulling the ends tight.
4. Tie the ends together to create a sling, which compresses and immobilizes the pelvis.

Details:
• This helps reduce internal bleeding and stabilize the pelvis.
• It is important not to move the casualty unnecessarily.
pelvic fracture, immobilize with blanket
Do Not Give Food or Water to Casualties with Abdominal Wounds
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

• DO NOT give casualties with abdominal wounds food or water (moistening the lips is allowed).
• _Seek medical assistance_ immediately.

Important Notes:
• This precaution helps prevent complications such as vomiting,...
Procedure:

• DO NOT give casualties with abdominal wounds food or water (moistening the lips is allowed).
• _Seek medical assistance_ immediately.

Important Notes:
• This precaution helps prevent complications such as vomiting, ruptured intestines, and further injury.
abdominal wound care, no food or water, medical emergency
Position Casualty with Abdominal Wound
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Steps to Position a Casualty with an Abdominal Wound:

1. Place and maintain the casualty on his back with his knees in an upright (flexed) position (Figure 3-8). This helps relieve pain, assist in treating shock, prevent further exposure of...
Steps to Position a Casualty with an Abdominal Wound:

1. Place and maintain the casualty on his back with his knees in an upright (flexed) position (Figure 3-8). This helps relieve pain, assist in treating shock, prevent further exposure of the bowel or abdominal organs, and help relieve abdominal pressure by allowing the abdominal muscles to relax.

Additional Notes:
• The knee-up position is critical for managing abdominal injuries and preventing complications such as internal bleeding or organ damage.
position,casualty,abdominal wound,knee up position
Ask the Casualty About the Incident
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
### Procedure: Ask the Casualty About the Incident

Steps:
1. Inquire about what happened, when it occurred, and how.

Details:
• This information can help determine the nature of the injury and guide further assessment.
casualty interview, incident details
Cover Abdominal Wounds with a Clean Dressing
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
### Procedure: Cover Abdominal Wounds with a Clean Dressing

Steps:
1. If there is a wound, cover it with a clean (if possible, sterile) dressing.
2. Tie the dressing firmly in place with a triangular or adhesive bandage without applying...
### Procedure: Cover Abdominal Wounds with a Clean Dressing

Steps:
1. If there is a wound, cover it with a clean (if possible, sterile) dressing.
2. Tie the dressing firmly in place with a triangular or adhesive bandage without applying pressure to the wound site or exposed internal parts.
3. Tie the triangular bandage tails loosely at the casualty’s side, not directly over the wound.

Details:
• This helps prevent infection and further injury to the abdominal area.
• Avoid applying direct pressure on the wound if internal organs are exposed.
cover abdominal wounds, apply dressing
Cover Exposed Intestines with a Large Moistened Dressing
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
### Procedure: Cover Exposed Intestines with a Large Moistened Dressing

Steps:
1. Wear disposable gloves to avoid contamination.
2. Do not touch the exposed intestines with bare hands.
3. Do not attempt to replace them in the abdomen.
4....
### Procedure: Cover Exposed Intestines with a Large Moistened Dressing

Steps:
1. Wear disposable gloves to avoid contamination.
2. Do not touch the exposed intestines with bare hands.
3. Do not attempt to replace them in the abdomen.
4. Cover the intestines with a large moistened dressing (use clean water or sterile normal saline if available).
5. Avoid using any material that is adherent or loses its substance when wet, such as toilet paper, facial tissue, paper towels, or absorbent cotton.
6. Secure the dressing with a bandage and tape.

Details:
• This helps prevent infection and further damage to exposed internal organs.
• It is critical not to attempt to replace the intestines back into the abdomen.
exposed intestines, cover with dressing
Examine Abdominal Wounds Without Removing Clothing Stuck to the Wound
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
### Procedure: Examine Abdominal Wounds Without Removing Clothing Stuck to the Wound

Steps:
1. Remove the casualty’s clothing to expose the abdomen.
2. Avoid tearing any clothing that is stuck to the wound.

Details:
• This step ensures...
### Procedure: Examine Abdominal Wounds Without Removing Clothing Stuck to the Wound

Steps:
1. Remove the casualty’s clothing to expose the abdomen.
2. Avoid tearing any clothing that is stuck to the wound.

Details:
• This step ensures that no further damage is done to the wound or surrounding tissue.
• It helps in assessing the full extent of the injury without causing additional harm.
abdominal wounds, examine abdomen, clothing removal
Listen to the Casualty's Complaints About Abdominal Pain
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
### Procedure: Listen to the Casualty's Complaints About Abdominal Pain

Steps:
1. Pay attention to any complaints from the casualty regarding pain in the abdomen.

Details:
• This helps determine the severity of the injury and whether...
### Procedure: Listen to the Casualty's Complaints About Abdominal Pain

Steps:
1. Pay attention to any complaints from the casualty regarding pain in the abdomen.

Details:
• This helps determine the severity of the injury and whether internal organs may be involved.
abdominal pain, listen to casualty
Look for Signs of Abdominal Injuries
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
### Procedure: Look for Signs of Abdominal Injuries

Steps:
1. Inspect the abdomen for any of the following:
- Superficial or penetrating wounds.
- Bruising or swelling (imprint abrasions or deformity).
- Exposed intestines or other...
### Procedure: Look for Signs of Abdominal Injuries

Steps:
1. Inspect the abdomen for any of the following:
- Superficial or penetrating wounds.
- Bruising or swelling (imprint abrasions or deformity).
- Exposed intestines or other internal organs.

Details:
• This visual inspection helps identify potential injuries that may require immediate attention.
abdominal injury signs, look for wounds
Palpate the Abdomen to Assess Tenderness and Stability
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
### Procedure: Palpate the Abdomen to Assess Tenderness and Stability

Steps:
1. Press with the flat palm of one hand on different parts of the abdomen in a clockwise direction.
2. Check whether the abdomen is soft (normal) or hard and/or if...
### Procedure: Palpate the Abdomen to Assess Tenderness and Stability

Steps:
1. Press with the flat palm of one hand on different parts of the abdomen in a clockwise direction.
2. Check whether the abdomen is soft (normal) or hard and/or if it hurts.
3. Place your hands on each side of the hip bone and push down and in to determine tenderness and stability.
4. Check the perineum and genital organs, which are part of the abdomen.
5. Respect cultural and social constraints during examination.

Details:
• This step helps identify internal injuries such as organ damage or fractures.
• It is important to be sensitive when examining the perineum and genital areas.
palpate abdomen, check for tenderness
Press Firmly on the Dressing to Prevent Abdominal Contents from Pushing Out
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
### Procedure: Press Firmly on the Dressing to Prevent Abdominal Contents from Pushing Out

Steps:
1. If the casualty coughs, press firmly on the dressing.

Details:
• This helps prevent internal organs or tissue from protruding through...
### Procedure: Press Firmly on the Dressing to Prevent Abdominal Contents from Pushing Out

Steps:
1. If the casualty coughs, press firmly on the dressing.

Details:
• This helps prevent internal organs or tissue from protruding through the wound.
press on dressing, abdominal contents
Tap the Abdomen to Check for Pain or Hardness
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
### Procedure: Tap the Abdomen to Check for Pain or Hardness

Steps:
1. Gently tap the abdomen with one finger.
2. Note if part or the whole abdomen is painful and/or hard.

Details:
• This helps identify potential internal injuries,...
### Procedure: Tap the Abdomen to Check for Pain or Hardness

Steps:
1. Gently tap the abdomen with one finger.
2. Note if part or the whole abdomen is painful and/or hard.

Details:
• This helps identify potential internal injuries, such as organ damage or bleeding.
tap abdomen, check for pain
Manage Abdominal Injuries
High Priority Source: SH 21 76 Ranger Handbook
Procedure for Managing Abdominal Injuries

1. Identify and control bleeding.
2. Treat for shock.
3. If internal organs are exposed, cover them with dry, sterile dressing. Do not place organs back in wound area. Do not handle internal organs.
4....
Procedure for Managing Abdominal Injuries

1. Identify and control bleeding.
2. Treat for shock.
3. If internal organs are exposed, cover them with dry, sterile dressing. Do not place organs back in wound area. Do not handle internal organs.
4. Place patient in position of comfort. Flex knees to relax abdomen.
5. Do not give anything by mouth to the patient.
abdominal injuries, internal organ exposure, shock treatment
Provide Fluids to the Casualty if Conscious and Not Suffering a Head Injury
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
### Procedure: Provide Fluids to the Casualty if Conscious and Not Suffering a Head Injury

Steps:
1. If the casualty is conscious and not suffering from a head injury, you can give them drinks.
2. Give sips of clean water or rehydration...
### Procedure: Provide Fluids to the Casualty if Conscious and Not Suffering a Head Injury

Steps:
1. If the casualty is conscious and not suffering from a head injury, you can give them drinks.
2. Give sips of clean water or rehydration fluids (ORS), up to a maximum of about two litres.
3. Stop if consciousness deteriorates or the casualty feels like vomiting.

Details:
• This helps maintain hydration and prevent shock.
• It is important not to give fluids if the casualty has a head injury, as it may worsen their condition.
provide fluids, conscious casualty
Expose Abdominal Wound and Handle Protruding Organs
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid + Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Steps to Expose an Abdominal Wound and Handle Protruding Organs:

1. Remove the casualty’s loose clothing to expose the wound, but DO NOT attempt to remove clothing that is stuck to the wound, as this may cause further injury.
2. In a...
Steps to Expose an Abdominal Wound and Handle Protruding Organs:

1. Remove the casualty’s loose clothing to expose the wound, but DO NOT attempt to remove clothing that is stuck to the wound, as this may cause further injury.
2. In a chemical environment, DO NOT REMOVE protective clothing; apply dressings over the protective clothing.
3. Gently pick up any organs that may be on the ground using a clean, dry dressing or the cleanest available material. Place the organs on top of the casualty’s abdomen (Figure 3-9).

Additional Notes:
• DO NOT probe, clean, or try to remove any foreign object from the abdomen.
• DO NOT touch with bare hands any exposed organs.
• DO NOT push organs back inside the body.
abdominal wound, protruding organs, first aid, expose wound, protective clothing
First Aid for Abdominal Wounds
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure for First Aid in Case of Abdominal Wounds

1. Evaluate the casualty, and be prepared to perform basic first aid measures.
2. Check for both entry and exit wounds. If there are two wounds, treat the more serious one first (e.g.,...
Procedure for First Aid in Case of Abdominal Wounds

1. Evaluate the casualty, and be prepared to perform basic first aid measures.
2. Check for both entry and exit wounds. If there are two wounds, treat the more serious one first (e.g., heavier bleeding, protruding organs, larger wound).
3. It may be necessary to improvise dressings for the second wound by using strips of cloth, a T-shirt, or the cleanest material available.
4. Position the casualty on his back with his knees in an upright (flexed) position (Figure 3-8). This helps relieve pain, assist in treating shock, prevent further exposure of internal organs, and help relieve abdominal pressure.
5. Expose the wound: Remove loose clothing to expose the wound. DO NOT attempt to remove clothing that is stuck to the wound, as this may cause further injury.
6. In a chemical environment, DO NOT REMOVE protective clothing; apply dressings over the protective clothing.
7. If any organs are on the ground, gently pick them up with a clean, dry dressing or the cleanest available material and place them on top of the casualty’s abdomen (Figure 3-
abdominal wounds, first aid, entry wound, exit wound, organ exposure, positioning, dressings

Skin Conditions (15) -

Treating Hot and Painful Skin Infections with Heat Compresses
High Priority Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
RULE #1

If the affected area is hot and painful, or oozes pus, treat it with heat. Put hot, moist cloths on it _(hot compresses)_ .

Detailed Steps for Rule #1:
• Keep the affected part still and elevate it (put it higher than the...
RULE #1

If the affected area is hot and painful, or oozes pus, treat it with heat. Put hot, moist cloths on it _(hot compresses)_ .

Detailed Steps for Rule #1:
• Keep the affected part still and elevate it (put it higher than the rest of the body).
• Apply hot, moist cloths.
• If the infection is severe or the person has a fever, give antibiotics (penicillin, a sulfonamide, or erythromycin).

Danger signs include:
• Swollen lymph nodes
• A red or dark line above the infected area
• A bad smell

If these do not get better with treatment, use an antibiotic and seek medical help quickly.
heat compress, hot skin infection, pus, elevation, antibiotics, penicillin, sulfonamide, erythromycin
Instructions for Using Hot Compresses (Hot Soaks)
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Instructions for Using Hot Compresses (Hot Soaks):
1. Boil water and allow it to cool until you can just hold your hand in it.
2. Fold a clean cloth so it is slightly larger than the area you want to treat, wet the cloth in the hot water, and...
Instructions for Using Hot Compresses (Hot Soaks):
1. Boil water and allow it to cool until you can just hold your hand in it.
2. Fold a clean cloth so it is slightly larger than the area you want to treat, wet the cloth in the hot water, and squeeze out the extra water.
3. Put the cloth over the affected skin.
4. Cover the cloth with a sheet of thin plastic or cellophane.
5. Wrap it with a towel to hold in the heat.
6. Keep the affected part raised.
7. When the cloth starts to cool, put it back in the hot water and repeat.
hot compress, hot soak, boiling water, cloth preparation, heat application, elevation
Treat Rashes by Drying or Wetting
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure:

1. If the rash is wet (e.g., oozing), gently pat it dry with a clean cloth.
2. Apply an antiseptic or drying agent to help absorb excess moisture and promote healing.
3. If the rash is dry, apply a cool, moist compress or use a...
Procedure:

1. If the rash is wet (e.g., oozing), gently pat it dry with a clean cloth.
2. Apply an antiseptic or drying agent to help absorb excess moisture and promote healing.
3. If the rash is dry, apply a cool, moist compress or use a hydrating cream to soothe the skin.
4. Avoid scratching the affected area to prevent further irritation or infection.

Note: This method helps manage rashes in austere environments.
rash treatment, skin care
Treating Itchy or Oozing Skin with Cold Compresses
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
RULE #2

If the affected area itches, stings, or oozes clear fluid, treat it with cold. Put cool, wet cloths on it _(cold compresses)._

Detailed Steps for Rule #2:
• Apply cloths soaked in cool water with white vinegar (2 tablespoons...
RULE #2

If the affected area itches, stings, or oozes clear fluid, treat it with cold. Put cool, wet cloths on it _(cold compresses)._

Detailed Steps for Rule #2:
• Apply cloths soaked in cool water with white vinegar (2 tablespoons of vinegar in 1 quart of pure boiled water).
• When the affected area feels better, no longer oozes, and has formed tender new skin, lightly spread on a mixture of talc and water (1 part talc to 1 part water).
• When healing has taken place, and the new skin begins to thicken or flake, rub on a little vegetable lard or body oil to soften it.
cold compress, itchy skin, oozing skin, vinegar solution, talc and water mixture, vegetable lard, body oil
Treating Scabies with Medicines
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

1. Identify the condition: Scabies is a skin infestation caused by mites that results in intense itching and small red bumps.
2. Select medication: Use permethrin cream (5%) or other scabicide medications as indicated on the...
Procedure:

1. Identify the condition: Scabies is a skin infestation caused by mites that results in intense itching and small red bumps.
2. Select medication: Use permethrin cream (5%) or other scabicide medications as indicated on the package or by a healthcare provider.
3. Application:
- Apply the medication to all areas of the body from the neck down, including under the nails and between the fingers.
- Ensure that the entire affected area is covered with a thin layer of cream.
4. Duration: Leave the medication on for 8-12 hours before washing it off with warm water.
5. Repeat treatment:
- If live mites are still present after one application, repeat the treatment in 7 days.
6. Wash bedding and clothing: Wash all clothes, bedding, and towels used by the patient in hot water (at least 130°F) to kill mites.
7. Monitor for side effects:
- Watch for allergic reactions or skin irritation at the application site.
8. Treat close contacts: All individuals who have had close contact with the infected person should also be treated, even if they show no symptoms.
scabies treatment, permethrin cream, scabicide use
General Rules for Treating Skin Problems
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
GENERAL RULES FOR TREATING SKIN PROBLEMS

Although many skin problems need specific treatment, there are a few general measures that often help:
general skin treatment, first aid, skin care
Apply First Aid for Eczema
Reference Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Treatment steps:

1. Apply cold compresses on the rash to reduce inflammation and discomfort.

2. If signs of infection develop (see page 88), treat as for impetigo (page 202).

3. Expose the affected area to sunlight, which may help...
Treatment steps:

1. Apply cold compresses on the rash to reduce inflammation and discomfort.

2. If signs of infection develop (see page 88), treat as for impetigo (page 202).

3. Expose the affected area to sunlight, which may help alleviate symptoms.

4. In difficult cases, use a cortisone or cortico-steroid cream (see page 370). Alternatively, coal tar may help.

5. Seek medical advice if symptoms persist or worsen.
eczema treatment, cold compresses, cortisone cream, sunlight therapy
Apply First Aid for Psoriasis
Reference Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Treatment steps:

1. Expose the affected skin to sunlight, which often helps reduce symptoms.

2. Bathing in the ocean may provide relief in some cases.

3. Seek medical advice, as treatment must be continued for a long time.
psoriasis treatment, sunlight therapy, ocean bathing
Manage Eczema (Red Patches with Blisters) in Infants and Adults
Reference Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

1. Keep the affected skin clean and avoid irritants such as harsh soaps or lotions.
2. Apply cool compresses to relieve itching and reduce inflammation.
3. Avoid covering the affected area, allowing it to be exposed to...
Procedure:

1. Keep the affected skin clean and avoid irritants such as harsh soaps or lotions.
2. Apply cool compresses to relieve itching and reduce inflammation.
3. Avoid covering the affected area, allowing it to be exposed to air and sunlight for healing.
4. Use gentle, non-irritating moisturizers to keep the skin hydrated.
5. Monitor for signs of infection such as increased redness, swelling, or pus, and seek further medical attention if needed.
eczema, skin rash, treatment, infant care, blisters
Protecting Skin from Sunlight Based on Affected Area
Reference Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
RULE #3

If the skin areas affected are on parts of the body often exposed to sunlight, protect them from the sun.

RULE #4

If the skin areas most affected are usually covered by clothing, expose them to direct sunlight for 10 to 20...
RULE #3

If the skin areas affected are on parts of the body often exposed to sunlight, protect them from the sun.

RULE #4

If the skin areas most affected are usually covered by clothing, expose them to direct sunlight for 10 to 20 minutes, 2 or 3 times a day.
sun protection, skin exposure, sunlight therapy, covered skin, sun exposure treatment
First Aid for Chilblains
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Identify the affected area: red, swollen, hot, tender, and itchy skin. Look for symptoms such as infection (ulcerated or bleeding) with continued exposure to cold.
2. Apply local rewarming using body heat (e.g., placing the...
Procedure:

1. Identify the affected area: red, swollen, hot, tender, and itchy skin. Look for symptoms such as infection (ulcerated or bleeding) with continued exposure to cold.
2. Apply local rewarming using body heat (e.g., placing the affected area against a warm part of the body or using a warm object).
3. Do not rub or massage the affected area to avoid causing further damage or infection.
4. Seek medical aid as soon as possible, especially if the skin becomes infected, ulcerated, or bleeding.

Note:
• Continued exposure may lead to more severe complications such as ulcers or infections.
chilblains, first aid, rewarming, infection prevention
Identify and Treat Corns on the Feet
Reference Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

1. Recognize corns as hard, thick areas of skin that form due to:
- Pressure from shoes or sandals.
- Toes pressing against each other.
2. Note that corns can be very painful, especially when walking or...
Procedure:

1. Recognize corns as hard, thick areas of skin that form due to:
- Pressure from shoes or sandals.
- Toes pressing against each other.
2. Note that corns can be very painful, especially when walking or standing.

Treatment:
• Avoid further pressure by wearing well-fitting footwear.
• Use moisturizing creams to soften the skin and reduce pain.
• If corns are severely painful, consult a health worker for professional removal (e.g., using a scalpel or other tools).

Note: Corns are not warts, but they can be confused with plantar warts. Distinguish based on appearance and location.
corns treatment, footwear pressure, painful corns, health worker removal
Manage Poison Ivy, Oak, Sumac Rash
Reference Source: Ranger Handbook 2006
Steps to Manage a Poison Ivy, Oak, or Sumac Rash:<br>1. Gently clean affected area. Clean areas 2-3 times daily. Wash clothing.<br>2. Apply topical anti-itch lotion or ointment as needed, and cover.<br>3. Avoid scratching or itching the...
Steps to Manage a Poison Ivy, Oak, or Sumac Rash:<br>1. Gently clean affected area. Clean areas 2-3 times daily. Wash clothing.<br>2. Apply topical anti-itch lotion or ointment as needed, and cover.<br>3. Avoid scratching or itching the area.<br>4. Observe for signs of infection (increasing redness, tenderness, warm to touch).<br>5. Seek medical attention if rash persists or signs of infection develop.
poison ivy,rash treatment,anti-itch ointment
Recognize and Treat Warts (Verrucae)
Reference Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

1. Identify warts on the skin:
- Most warts in children last 3 to 5 years and may disappear naturally.
2. Look for flat, painful wart-like spots on the soles of the feet, which are often plantar warts (could...
Procedure:

1. Identify warts on the skin:
- Most warts in children last 3 to 5 years and may disappear naturally.
2. Look for flat, painful wart-like spots on the soles of the feet, which are often plantar warts (could also be corns).

Treatment Options:
• Use magical or household cures that have been known to remove warts.
• Avoid using strong acids or poisonous plants, as they may cause burns or worse sores than the warts themselves.
• For painful plantar warts, consult a health worker for removal.

Note: Warts on the penis or vagina should be treated by referring to p. 402 of this book.
warts treatment, plantar warts, household cures, avoid acids, health worker removal
Treat Dandruff (Cradle Cap) in Infants and Adults
Reference Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

1. Wash the head daily with regular soap and water. A medicated soap may be used if available, but it is not always necessary.
2. Gently remove dandruff and crust using a soft cloth or towel. To loosen scales, wrap the head...
Procedure:

1. Wash the head daily with regular soap and water. A medicated soap may be used if available, but it is not always necessary.
2. Gently remove dandruff and crust using a soft cloth or towel. To loosen scales, wrap the head in towels soaked in lukewarm water before washing.
3. Keep the head uncovered, allowing it to be exposed to air and sunlight for healing.
4. Avoid covering the baby’s head with caps or cloths; keep it bare as much as possible.
5. Monitor for signs of infection such as increased redness, swelling, or pus, and treat accordingly (see page 202 for impetigo treatment if needed).
dandruff, cradle cap, seborrhea, infant care, skin treatment

Shock Management (14) -

Check for Shock in a Casualty
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Objective: Identify and prevent the development of shock to improve survival chances.

Procedure:
1. Assess the casualty’s condition by looking for signs of shock:
- Pale or clammy skin
- Rapid, weak pulse
- Confusion or...
Objective: Identify and prevent the development of shock to improve survival chances.

Procedure:
1. Assess the casualty’s condition by looking for signs of shock:
- Pale or clammy skin
- Rapid, weak pulse
- Confusion or dizziness
- Shallow breathing
2. If shock is suspected, take immediate action to prevent its progression:
- Lay the casualty down on their back with their legs elevated (if no spinal injury is suspected).
- Cover them with a blanket or clothing to keep warm.
- Reassure and comfort the casualty to reduce anxiety.
3. Monitor for changes in condition and provide additional first aid as needed.
4. If shock symptoms worsen or do not improve, seek immediate medical assistance.
shock prevention, first aid, circulatory emergency
Check for Signs of Shock in an Injured Casualty
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Objective: Identify signs of shock and take immediate action to prevent further deterioration.

Procedure:
1. Ensure the area around the casualty is safe.
2. Look for the following signs of shock:
- Pale or clammy skin.
- Rapid, weak...
Objective: Identify signs of shock and take immediate action to prevent further deterioration.

Procedure:
1. Ensure the area around the casualty is safe.
2. Look for the following signs of shock:
- Pale or clammy skin.
- Rapid, weak pulse.
- Shallow breathing.
- Confusion or loss of consciousness.
- Nausea or vomiting.
3. If shock is suspected, take immediate action to prevent further complications:
- Lay the casualty down on their back with their legs elevated about 12 inches above the level of the heart (unless there is a suspected spinal injury).
- Cover the casualty with a blanket or clothing to keep them warm.
- Reassure the casualty and keep them calm to reduce anxiety, which can worsen shock.
4. Monitor the casualty continuously for changes in condition and provide first aid as needed until medical help arrives.

Important: Shock may result in death even if the injury or condition causing it would not otherwise be fatal.
shock check, shock management, first aid, signs of shock, casualty evaluation
Administer First Aid for Shock
Critical Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Objective: Provide immediate first aid to a casualty in shock to stabilize their condition.

Steps:
1. Assume shock is present or will occur shortly. Do not wait for visible signs and symptoms before taking action, as this may jeopardize...
Objective: Provide immediate first aid to a casualty in shock to stabilize their condition.

Steps:
1. Assume shock is present or will occur shortly. Do not wait for visible signs and symptoms before taking action, as this may jeopardize the casualty’s life.
2. Position the casualty (do not move the casualty or his limbs if suspected fractures have not been splinted):
- Move the casualty to cover if available and the situation permits.
- Lay the casualty on his back.
3. Special considerations:
- If the casualty has a chest wound or is experiencing breathing difficulty, allow them to sit upright for easier breathing, but monitor carefully in case their condition worsens.
4. Elevate the casualty’s feet higher than the level of his heart, using a stable object (e.g., field pack or rolled-up clothing) to prevent slipping off.
5. Do not elevate legs if the casualty has an unsplinted broken leg, head injury, or abdominal injury.
6. Check for leg fractures and splint if necessary before elevating feet.
7. For a casualty with an abdominal wound, place their knees in an upright (flexed) position.
8. Loosen clothing at the neck, waist, or wherever it may be binding to ensure comfort and proper circulation.
first aid for shock, positioning casualty, elevate feet, splint fractures, loosen clothing, abdominal injury
Check for Shock and Administer First Aid Measures - Causes and Effects of Shock
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Understand the causes: Recognize that shock can be caused by severe bleeding, trauma, burns, or other injuries.
2. Recognize the effects: Be aware that shock leads to reduced blood flow to vital organs, which can result in...
Procedure:

1. Understand the causes: Recognize that shock can be caused by severe bleeding, trauma, burns, or other injuries.
2. Recognize the effects: Be aware that shock leads to reduced blood flow to vital organs, which can result in organ failure and death if not treated promptly.
3. Monitor for symptoms: Watch for signs such as pale skin, rapid pulse, confusion, and loss of consciousness.
4. Take immediate action: If shock is suspected, lay the casualty down and elevate their legs to improve blood flow.
5. Keep the casualty warm: Cover them with blankets or clothing to prevent hypothermia.
6. Call for emergency assistance: If shock is suspected, call for help immediately.
causes shock, effects shock, first aid
Manage Shock in a Patient
Critical Source: Ranger Handbook 2006
Steps to Manage Shock:
1. Open the victim's airway.
2. Restore his breathing.
3. Control the bleeding.
4. Initiate an IV or a saline lock.
5. Monitor his condition.
shock management,airway restoration,IV initiation
Managing Shock in a Casualty
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Raise the casualty’s legs above the level of the heart and keep the head down.

Keep the casualty warm by covering them with a blanket or similar item.
shock management,casualty care,warmth,leg elevation
Suspecting and Managing Shock
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

• Look for cold sweat on the forehead.
• Listen to complaints of thirst or feeling unwell.
• Talk with the casualty to assess confusion, agitation, or loss of consciousness.
• Touch to check for cold extremities and a...
Procedure:

• Look for cold sweat on the forehead.
• Listen to complaints of thirst or feeling unwell.
• Talk with the casualty to assess confusion, agitation, or loss of consciousness.
• Touch to check for cold extremities and a rapid, weak pulse.

Notes:
• Suspect shock in cases of:
- Severe visible or hidden haemorrhage (especially from chest or abdomen).
- Dehydration (e.g., large burns).
- Spinal cord injury.
- Allergic reactions (e.g., to penicillin).
- Severe infection (e.g., gangrene).
• Monitor the casualty closely and provide immediate care.
shock signs, shock management, first aid
Check for Signs and Symptoms of Shock in a Casualty
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure to Identify Shock in a Casualty

### Performance Steps

1. Check the casualty for signs and symptoms of shock, including:
- (a) Sweaty but cool skin.
- (b) Pale skin.
- (c) Restlessness or nervousness.
-...
Procedure to Identify Shock in a Casualty

### Performance Steps

1. Check the casualty for signs and symptoms of shock, including:
- (a) Sweaty but cool skin.
- (b) Pale skin.
- (c) Restlessness or nervousness.
- (d) Thirst.
- (e) Severe bleeding.
- (f) Confusion.
- (g) Rapid breathing.
- (h) Blotchy blue skin.
- (i) Nausea and/or vomiting.

2. Position the casualty:
- Move the casualty to cover, if available and the situation permits.
- Lay the casualty on his/her back unless a sitting position will allow the casualty to breathe easier.
- Elevate the casualty's feet higher than the heart using a stable object so the feet will not fall.

Note: This procedure is intended for casualties who are breathing and have no uncontrolled bleeding. Do not cause further injury to the casualty.
shock symptoms, first aid, casualty positioning, shock prevention
Prevent and Treat Shock in an Injured Person
High Priority Source: Medical Emergencies
Anticipate Shock:
Treat all injured persons as follows, regardless of what symptoms appear.

Positioning the Victim:
• If the victim is conscious, place him on a level surface with the lower extremities elevated 15 to 20 centimeters.
-...
Anticipate Shock:
Treat all injured persons as follows, regardless of what symptoms appear.

Positioning the Victim:
• If the victim is conscious, place him on a level surface with the lower extremities elevated 15 to 20 centimeters.
• If the victim is unconscious, place him on his side or abdomen with his head turned to one side to prevent choking on vomit, blood, or other fluids.
• If unsure of the best position, place the victim perfectly flat. Once in a shock position, do not move him.

Maintain Body Heat:
• Insulate the victim from the surroundings and apply external heat if necessary.
• Remove all wet clothing as soon as possible and replace with dry clothing.
• Improvise a shelter to insulate the victim from the weather.
• Use warm liquids or foods, a prewarmed sleeping bag, another person, warmed water in canteens, hot rocks wrapped in clothing, or fires on either side of the victim to provide external warmth.

Fluid Administration:
• If the victim is conscious, slowly administer small doses of a warm salt or sugar solution, if available.
• Do not give fluids by mouth if the victim is unconscious or has abdominal wounds.

Rest and Recovery:
• Have the victim rest for at least 24 hours.
• If you are a lone survivor, lie in a depression in the ground, behind a tree, or any other place out of the weather, with your head lower than your feet.
• If you are with a buddy, reassess your patient constantly.
shock treatment,positioning,victim care,body heat,warmth,fluid administration
Prevent or Control Shock in a Casualty
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Objective: Prevent or control shock in a casualty who is breathing and not experiencing uncontrolled bleeding.

Materials Needed: Field jacket, blanket, or poncho.

### Steps to Follow:
1. Check for signs and symptoms of shock:
-...
Objective: Prevent or control shock in a casualty who is breathing and not experiencing uncontrolled bleeding.

Materials Needed: Field jacket, blanket, or poncho.

### Steps to Follow:
1. Check for signs and symptoms of shock:
- Sweaty but cool skin.
- Pale skin.
- Restlessness or nervousness.
- Thirst.
- Severe bleeding.
- Confusion.
- Rapid breathing.
- Blotchy blue skin.
- Nausea and/or vomiting.

2. Position the casualty:
- Move the casualty to cover if available and safe to do so.
- Lay the casualty on their back unless a sitting position is necessary for easier breathing.
- Elevate the casualty's feet higher than the heart using a stable object, unless there is an unsplinted fractured leg, abdominal wound, or head/spinal injury (do not elevate legs in these cases).

3. Loosen clothing:
- Loosen clothing at the neck, waist, or any area where it is binding.
- Do NOT loosen clothing if in a chemical area.

4. Splint the limb if appropriate:
- Apply a splint to an injured limb if one or more bones are fractured.
- Splint the arm, forearm, thigh, or leg even if not fractured but there is a severe wound.
- Note: Immobilizing the limb reduces muscular activity and helps stop bleeding and reduce pain.

5. Prevent exposure to extreme temperatures:
- Cover the casualty with a blanket, clothing, or improvised cover to avoid loss of body heat in cold weather (place cover under as well as over the casualty).
- Place the casualty under a permanent or improvised shelter in hot weather to provide shade from direct sunlight.

6. Calm and reassure the casualty:
- Take charge and show self-confidence.
- Assure the casualty that they are being taken care of.

Important Warnings:
• Do NOT give the casualty anything to eat or drink.
• If the casualty is in hypovolemic shock from combat injuries, establish a saline lock and start an intravenous infusion if necessary (see tasks 081-831-1011 and 081-831-1012).
first aid, shock prevention, positioning casualty, splinting, temperature control, calming casualty
Check for Shock and Administer First Aid Measures
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Assess the casualty: Check for signs of shock such as pale or clammy skin, rapid pulse, shallow breathing, confusion, and weakness.
2. Ensure safety: Make sure the area is safe for both you and the casualty.
3. **Lay the...
Procedure:

1. Assess the casualty: Check for signs of shock such as pale or clammy skin, rapid pulse, shallow breathing, confusion, and weakness.
2. Ensure safety: Make sure the area is safe for both you and the casualty.
3. Lay the casualty down: Place the casualty in a horizontal position to improve blood flow to the brain.
4. Elevate the legs: Raise the casualty’s legs about 12 inches above the level of the heart if possible.
5. Keep the casualty warm: Cover them with blankets or clothing to prevent hypothermia.
6. Monitor breathing and pulse: Continuously check for signs of respiratory distress or changes in vital signs.
7. Administer fluids if possible: If the casualty is conscious and not vomiting, give small sips of water or oral rehydration solution to help maintain hydration.
8. Do not give food or drink if unconscious: Avoid giving anything by mouth to an unconscious casualty.
9. Call for emergency assistance: If shock is suspected, call for help immediately.
shock, first aid, casualty assessment, elevate legs, hypothermia prevention, emergency assistance
Evaluate the Casualty Continuously During Shock Treatment
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Purpose: Monitor the casualty’s condition until medical personnel arrive or the casualty is transported to an MTF.

Steps:
1. Continuously evaluate the casualty’s vital signs and symptoms.
2. **Monitor for changes in consciousness,...
Purpose: Monitor the casualty’s condition until medical personnel arrive or the casualty is transported to an MTF.

Steps:
1. Continuously evaluate the casualty’s vital signs and symptoms.
2. Monitor for changes in consciousness, breathing, pulse, and skin color.
3. Document observations and report them to medical personnel upon arrival.
4. Continue evaluating the casualty until medical personnel arrives or the casualty is transported to an MTF (Medical Treatment Facility).
5. Monitor for changes in condition, such as worsening symptoms or signs of deterioration.
evaluate casualty, shock treatment, monitor vital signs, medical personnel, MTF
Position a Casualty in Shock
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Purpose: Properly position a casualty in shock to improve circulation and prevent further injury while maintaining their calm and cooperation.

Steps:
1. Reassure the casualty throughout the entire procedure. Be authoritative and show...
Purpose: Properly position a casualty in shock to improve circulation and prevent further injury while maintaining their calm and cooperation.

Steps:
1. Reassure the casualty throughout the entire procedure. Be authoritative and show self-confidence to help them feel secure and less frightened. Assure the casualty that you are there to help him.
2. Do not move the casualty or his limbs if suspected fractures have not been splinted. See Chapter 4 for details on splinting.
3. Move the casualty to cover, if available and the situation permits.
4. Lay the casualty on his back, unless he has a chest wound or is having breathing difficulty, in which case allow him to sit upright but monitor carefully.
5. Elevate the casualty’s feet higher than the level of his heart using a stable object (e.g., field pack or rolled-up clothing) to prevent slipping.
6. Do not elevate legs if there is an unsplinted broken leg, head injury, or abdominal injury.
7. Check for leg fractures and splint if necessary before elevating the feet. For a casualty with an abdominal wound, place his knees in an upright (flexed) position.
8. Loosen clothing at the neck, waist, or wherever it may be binding, but do not remove protective clothing in a chemical environment.
position casualty, shock, first aid, elevate feet, chest wound, splinting, reassure casualty
Recognize Causes and Effects of Shock
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid + Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Purpose: Understand the causes and effects of shock to better identify and treat it.

Common Causes of Shock:
1. Heart failure (the heart is damaged and fails to work as a pump).
2. Blood loss (heavy bleeding) causing insufficient...
Purpose: Understand the causes and effects of shock to better identify and treat it.

Common Causes of Shock:
1. Heart failure (the heart is damaged and fails to work as a pump).
2. Blood loss (heavy bleeding) causing insufficient fluid volume in the vascular system.
3. Dilation of blood vessels (vasodilation), which can cause inadequate circulation even with normal fluid volume.
4. **Dehydration."
5. Allergic reactions to foods, drugs, insect stings, or snakebites.
6. **Significant loss of blood."
7. Reaction to traumatic scenes (e.g., sight of wounds, blood, or injuries).
8. Traumatic injuries, including:
- Burns
- Gunshot or shrapnel wounds
- Crush injuries
- Blows to the body (which can cause broken bones or internal organ damage)
- Head injuries
- Penetrating wounds (e.g., from a knife, bayonet, or missile)

Effects of Shock:
• Reduced blood flow to vital organs leading to tissue damage and potential organ failure.
• Decreased oxygen delivery to tissues, resulting in cellular dysfunction.
• Altered mental status due to reduced cerebral perfusion.
• Hypotension (low blood pressure) and tachycardia (rapid heart rate).
shock, causes of shock, effects of shock, first aid, heart failure, blood loss, vasodilation, dehydration, allergic reactions, traumatic injuries

Injury Assessment (13) -

Assess and Manage Blast Injuries in Armed Conflicts
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Blast injuries occur due to the detonation of high-energy explosives, which creates a blast wave that can travel around objects such as buildings or walls. This wave causes rapid changes in atmospheric pressure, affecting parts of the body...
Blast injuries occur due to the detonation of high-energy explosives, which creates a blast wave that can travel around objects such as buildings or walls. This wave causes rapid changes in atmospheric pressure, affecting parts of the body that contain air.

Injuries caused by blast waves include:
• Rupture of eardrums (causing deafness and blood to ooze from the ear).
• Damage to lung sacs (alveoli), causing respiratory distress.
• Injury to intestines, spilling their contents into the peritoneum.
• Damage to solid organs such as the liver, leading to internal haemorrhage.

Other blast-related injuries:
• Secondary fragments, from broken glass and debris, can cause penetrating wounds.
• A building collapse may result in crush injuries or blunt trauma.
• Some anti-tank mines send an indirect explosive force through the floor of a vehicle, causing closed fractures of the foot and leg (known as "pied de mine" or mine foot).

Key considerations:
• A single large explosion may injure many people at once. Injuries can be due to the blast wave itself, burning, fragments, blunt trauma, or crush injuries.
• Look for signs of internal bleeding, difficulty breathing, hearing loss, and fractures.
• Apply direct pressure to control external bleeding if present.
• Immobilize suspected fractures and seek immediate medical assistance.
blast injury, blast wave, internal hemorrhage, respiratory distress, mine foot
Assess and Manage Back of the Chest and Abdomen Injuries
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Identify potential injuries to the back of the chest or abdomen.

Procedure:
1. If a wound is found on the front of the casualty’s chest or abdomen, check for a corresponding entry or exit hole on the back or sides or perineum.
2....
Objective: Identify potential injuries to the back of the chest or abdomen.

Procedure:
1. If a wound is found on the front of the casualty’s chest or abdomen, check for a corresponding entry or exit hole on the back or sides or perineum.
2. Turn the casualty onto his side if he is lying down to facilitate examination of the back.
3. Examine the entire back of the casualty, looking for signs of injury such as wounds, bruising, swelling, or deformity.
4. Ensure in-line immobilization of the whole body when turning the casualty:
- Kneel on one side of the casualty and place your hands on his far side.
- Grasp the shoulder with one hand and the hip with the other.
- Roll the casualty towards you.
5. If possible, enlist the help of at least three persons to assist in turning the casualty:
- One person should stay near the casualty’s head and steady it while organizing the manoeuvre.
- Each person should kneel on one side of the casualty and place their hands on the far side.
- One person should grasp the thorax, another the pelvis, and yet another the lower limbs.
6. At your command, all should roll the casualty towards themselves to ensure safe and controlled movement.

Notes: Always be cautious when moving a casualty with suspected spinal injuries. Use proper immobilization techniques to prevent further harm.
chest injury,abdomen injury,spinal assessment
Approach to an Injured Casualty
High Priority Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Procedure:

1. Stop and assess safety: Before approaching any injured person, ensure your own safety.
2. Avoid creating additional casualties: Be cautious of the environment (e.g., unstable ground, falling rocks).
3. **Ask: 'Am I...
Procedure:

1. Stop and assess safety: Before approaching any injured person, ensure your own safety.
2. Avoid creating additional casualties: Be cautious of the environment (e.g., unstable ground, falling rocks).
3. Ask: 'Am I safe?': Only proceed if it is safe to do so.
4. Minimize movement of the casualty: Avoid moving the injured person unless absolutely necessary (e.g., imminent danger like rock fall or avalanche).
5. If movement is required:
- Move the casualty as carefully and quickly as possible.
- Exercise particular caution if a spinal, back, or neck injury is suspected.
6. Assess the casualty: Use first aid principles to evaluate their condition.
7. Follow ABCDE assessment (Airway, Breathing, Circulation, Disability, Exposure) for further evaluation.
approach to casualty, safety assessment, movement of injured, spinal injury, first aid principles
Assess Mechanism of Injury and Suspect Cervical Spine Injury
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Determine the mechanism of injury to identify potential cervical spine injuries.

Steps:
1. Determine Circumstances: Identify how the casualty was injured (e.g., road traffic crash, building collapse, bullet wound in the...
Objective: Determine the mechanism of injury to identify potential cervical spine injuries.

Steps:
1. Determine Circumstances: Identify how the casualty was injured (e.g., road traffic crash, building collapse, bullet wound in the head).
2. Assess Injury Type: Note whether the injury is blunt or penetrating. Blunt injuries increase the risk of cervical spine injury.
3. Suspect Cervical Spine Injury: Be alert to signs such as unconsciousness, difficulty breathing, pain localized in the nape of the neck or back, and neurological symptoms like pins and needles or electric jolts.

Notes:
• Blunt injuries above the collarbones are particularly concerning for cervical spine injury.
• Unconscious casualties should be treated with suspicion of spinal cord injury until proven otherwise.
mechanism of injury, cervical spine, blunt trauma, first aid
Check Circulation Below the Site of a Suspected Fracture
High Priority Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure to check circulation below the injury site:

1. Observe the color of the skin on the extremity. Pale, white, or bluish-gray color may indicate impaired circulation.
2. Check the temperature of the injured extremity by comparing...
Procedure to check circulation below the injury site:

1. Observe the color of the skin on the extremity. Pale, white, or bluish-gray color may indicate impaired circulation.
2. Check the temperature of the injured extremity by comparing it with the uninjured side using your hand. A colder temperature may suggest poor circulation.
3. Depress the fingernail bed or toe and observe how quickly the color returns to normal. A slower return on the injured side compared to the uninjured side indicates a problem with circulation.
4. Ask the casualty about any numbness, tingling, coldness, or tightness in the affected area.

Important: If impaired circulation is suspected (e.g., numbness, pale skin), the casualty should be evacuated by medical personnel immediately to prevent possible loss of the limb.
circulation check, first aid, injury assessment, circulation impairment
Check for Fractures in Arms and Legs
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Check the casualty’s arms and legs for open or closed fractures.
2. For open fractures, look for:
- Bleeding.
- Bones sticking through the skin.
- Check for pulse.
3. For closed fractures, look for:
-...
Procedure:

1. Check the casualty’s arms and legs for open or closed fractures.
2. For open fractures, look for:
- Bleeding.
- Bones sticking through the skin.
- Check for pulse.
3. For closed fractures, look for:
- Swelling.
- Discoloration.
- Deformity.
- Unusual body position.
- Check for pulse.

4. If a fracture is suspected, stop the evaluation and begin first aid measures (refer to Chapter 4 for splinting procedures).
fracture check, arm fracture, leg fracture, first aid
Check for Fractures in Other Body Areas
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Check for signs/symptoms of fractures in other body areas (e.g., shoulder or hip).
2. Provide first aid as necessary.
3. If a fracture is suspected, stop the evaluation and begin first aid measures.
shoulder fracture, hip fracture, first aid, injury assessment
Perform a Head-to-Toe Palpation Examination
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

1. Begin with the head and scalp, including:
- Ears, face (nose, mouth, jaw, eyes)
2. Move to the neck.
3. Examine the chest.
4. Palpate the abdomen, pelvis, and perineum.
5. Check the **shoulders, arms, and...
Procedure:

1. Begin with the head and scalp, including:
- Ears, face (nose, mouth, jaw, eyes)
2. Move to the neck.
3. Examine the chest.
4. Palpate the abdomen, pelvis, and perineum.
5. Check the shoulders, arms, and hands.
6. Inspect the legs and feet.
7. Finally, palpate the back of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis.

Details:
• Palpation should be systematic, starting from the head and moving down to the toes, front and back and sides.
• Examine both sides of the body for any abnormalities such as deformities or restricted movement.
• Use the opposite side of the body as a mirror image for comparison.
• Look at the casualty’s reaction during palpation.
• Localize precisely sites of skin wounds and any fractures, noting tenderness, deformity, or open skin.
• Check for crepitus, which is the crackling sound and/or feeling caused by broken bones rubbing together or air bubbles under the skin.
• Estimate the casualty’s body temperature.
• Inspect your gloved hands for any blood.

Notes:
• This examination helps detect injuries that may not be obvious, especially in casualties of bombing, shelling, or grenade explosions where small wounds may cause significant internal damage.
• Avoid undue manipulation or movement during palpation.
palpation,head-to-toe examination,injury detection,casualty assessment,crepitus
Conduct a Visual Inspection of the Casualty
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

1. Look at all areas on all sides of the body, especially:
- Search for any abnormalities such as deformities and restricted movement.
- Use the opposite side of the body as a mirror image for comparison.
2. Observe any...
Procedure:

1. Look at all areas on all sides of the body, especially:
- Search for any abnormalities such as deformities and restricted movement.
- Use the opposite side of the body as a mirror image for comparison.
2. Observe any reactions from the casualty during palpation.

Details:
• This step is critical in identifying injuries that may not be immediately visible, especially after explosions or gunshot wounds.
• Visual inspection should be thorough and systematic.
visual inspection,casualty assessment,body abnormalities
Gather Information from the Casualty and Witnesses
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

1. Talk to the casualty, relatives, or bystanders to gather information about:
- How and when the injury occurred.
- The casualty’s health background.
2. Mobilize nearby persons to assist in the management of the...
Procedure:

1. Talk to the casualty, relatives, or bystanders to gather information about:
- How and when the injury occurred.
- The casualty’s health background.
2. Mobilize nearby persons to assist in the management of the casualty.

Details:
• This step is crucial for understanding the context of the injury and any pre-existing conditions that may affect treatment.
• Engaging bystanders can help provide additional support during the assessment and stabilization process.
casualty interview,witness information,health background
Listen to the Casualty’s Complaints and Symptoms
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

1. Listen carefully to the casualty's complaints, including:
- Pain
- Numbness in limbs
- Cold sensations
2. Pay attention to any other symptoms that may indicate internal injuries or trauma.

Details:
• Listening is...
Procedure:

1. Listen carefully to the casualty's complaints, including:
- Pain
- Numbness in limbs
- Cold sensations
2. Pay attention to any other symptoms that may indicate internal injuries or trauma.

Details:
• Listening is an essential part of the assessment process and can reveal critical information about the casualty’s condition.
• This step should be conducted alongside visual inspection and palpation.
casualty complaints,pain assessment,limb numbness
Prepare for Palpation Examination
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

1. Respect local rules, customs, and beliefs, especially in unfamiliar environments.
2. Protect your hands with gloves or similar protection (e.g., a plastic bag).
3. Kneel at one side of the casualty to perform the...
Procedure:

1. Respect local rules, customs, and beliefs, especially in unfamiliar environments.
2. Protect your hands with gloves or similar protection (e.g., a plastic bag).
3. Kneel at one side of the casualty to perform the examination.
4. Explain the examination process to the casualty and try to enlist their cooperation:
- Ask them not to move during palpation unless instructed otherwise (e.g., to move fingers for neurological assessment).
- Encourage them to inform you if the palpation is painful.

Details:
• This preparatory phase ensures that the examination is conducted safely and respectfully, minimizing discomfort or resistance from the casualty.
palpation preparation,gloves,casualty cooperation
Assess for Suspected Fractures
Standard Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Step-by-step procedure to assess a casualty for suspected fractures:

1. Look for an unnatural position of the extremity. This is often a sign of a fracture.
2. Check for any bone sticking out (protruding). A visible bone fragment...
Step-by-step procedure to assess a casualty for suspected fractures:

1. Look for an unnatural position of the extremity. This is often a sign of a fracture.
2. Check for any bone sticking out (protruding). A visible bone fragment indicates a possible open fracture.
3. Do not make the casualty move the affected extremity, as this could worsen the injury or cause further damage.

Important: If there is an obvious deformity, do not attempt to realign the limb.
fracture assessment, injury detection, first aid, extremity evaluation

Newborn Care (12) -

Assisting a Baby with Breathing Difficulties
Critical Source: A Book for Midwives
**Babies should start to breathe normally within 1 or 2 minutes after birth. Drying the baby with a towel after birth is often enough to help the baby start breathing. Babies who cry after birth are usually breathing well. But many babies breathe...
Babies should start to breathe normally within 1 or 2 minutes after birth. Drying the baby with a towel after birth is often enough to help the baby start breathing. Babies who cry after birth are usually breathing well. But many babies breathe well and do not cry at all.

### Signs of Breathing Problems:
• Unh...unh... unh...unh... (gurgling or grunting sounds)
• Baby’s nostrils open wide as she tries to breathe.
• Skin between the baby’s ribs sucks in as she tries to breathe.
• Baby breathes very fast — more than 60 breaths a minute.
• Baby breathes very slow — fewer than 30 breaths a minute.
• Baby grunts or makes noise when she breathes.

### Steps to Take:
1. Leave the baby on her mother’s belly.
2. Rub your hand firmly up and down her back.
3. Never hit or hurt a baby or hold her upside down to make her cry.
4. If you have it, give oxygen to a baby who continues to have breathing problems.
5. Watch the baby closely — if these problems do not improve, she may need medical help.
baby breathing difficulties, newborn resuscitation, oxygen for baby
Immediate Care for a Newborn Baby
High Priority Source: A Book for Midwives
When the baby is born, even before you cut the cord:

• Dry him thoroughly.
• Put him on his mother’s belly.
• Cover him with a blanket.

The mother’s body will keep the baby warm, and the smell of the mother’s milk will encourage...
When the baby is born, even before you cut the cord:

• Dry him thoroughly.
• Put him on his mother’s belly.
• Cover him with a blanket.

The mother’s body will keep the baby warm, and the smell of the mother’s milk will encourage him to suck. Be gentle with a new baby.

### Notes:
• In many medical centers, doctors or nurses take the baby away from the mother to check his health. This is easier for the doctors and nurses, but it is not best for the baby. The baby should not be taken from the mother unless there is an emergency.
newborn care, skin to skin contact, immediate post-birth care
Checking the Baby’s Health After Birth
High Priority Source: A Book for Midwives
To see how healthy the baby is, watch her:

• Breathing
• Heartbeat
• Muscle tone
• Reflexes
• Color

All of these things can be checked while the baby is breastfeeding.

### Notes:
• Some babies are alert and strong when...
To see how healthy the baby is, watch her:

• Breathing
• Heartbeat
• Muscle tone
• Reflexes
• Color

All of these things can be checked while the baby is breastfeeding.

### Notes:
• Some babies are alert and strong when they are born. Other babies start slow, but as the first few minutes pass, they breathe and move better, get stronger, and become less blue.
baby health check, newborn assessment, post-birth monitoring
Keeping the Baby Warm and Dry
High Priority Source: A Book for Midwives
As you move the baby to the mother’s belly:

• Dry his whole body with a clean cloth or towel.
• Cover the baby with a clean, dry cloth.
• Be sure to cover his head and keep him away from drafts.

### Additional Notes:
• Babies...
As you move the baby to the mother’s belly:

• Dry his whole body with a clean cloth or towel.
• Cover the baby with a clean, dry cloth.
• Be sure to cover his head and keep him away from drafts.

### Additional Notes:
• Babies become cold easily and this can make them weak or sick. This is why skin-to-skin contact between the baby and mother is so important.
• If the weather is hot, do not wrap the baby in heavy blankets or cloths. Too much heat can cause the baby to get dehydrated. A baby needs only one more layer of clothes than an adult does.
• Wait a day before bathing a newborn baby so he does not get cold. Being cold can lead to illness.
baby warmth, drying baby, skin to skin contact, newborn care
Maintain Newborn Body Temperature and Warmth
High Priority Source: A Book for Midwives
When to use: To ensure the baby stays warm and healthy.

Procedure:
