• The easiest way to keep a baby warm is skin-to-skin contact with the mother, as she provides the ideal temperature for the baby.
• Cover both the mother and baby with...
When to use: To ensure the baby stays warm and healthy.

Procedure:
• The easiest way to keep a baby warm is skin-to-skin contact with the mother, as she provides the ideal temperature for the baby.
• Cover both the mother and baby with blankets, ensuring the baby’s head is covered.
• If the mother cannot hold the baby (e.g., to urinate), someone else may do so after washing their hands thoroughly.
• Do not leave the baby unattended without proper warmth.
hypothermia in newborns, skin-to-skin care, baby warmth
Assess General Appearance of a Newborn Baby
Standard Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

Step 1: Observe the baby’s general appearance and listen to her cry.

Key Observations:
• Size: Is the baby small or large? Fat or thin?
• Proportions: Are arms, legs, feet, hands, body, and head appropriately sized?
-...
Procedure:

Step 1: Observe the baby’s general appearance and listen to her cry.

Key Observations:
• Size: Is the baby small or large? Fat or thin?
• Proportions: Are arms, legs, feet, hands, body, and head appropriately sized?
• Tension/Relaxation: Is the baby tense or relaxed? Active or still?
• Cry: Listen to the cry. A strange, high-pitched cry may indicate illness.

Note: These observations can help identify potential health issues early.
newborn appearance assessment, cry analysis
Cleanliness for Newborn Baby Care
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

• Follow the Guidelines of Cleanliness as discussed in Chapter 12.

Special care considerations:

1. Change the baby’s diapers (nappy) or bedding each time he wets or dirties them. If the skin gets red, change the diaper more...
Procedure:

• Follow the Guidelines of Cleanliness as discussed in Chapter 12.

Special care considerations:

1. Change the baby’s diapers (nappy) or bedding each time he wets or dirties them. If the skin gets red, change the diaper more often—or better, leave it off! (See p. 215.)
2. After the cord drops off, bathe the baby daily with mild soap and warm water.
3. If there are flies or mosquitos, cover the baby’s crib with mosquito netting or a thin cloth.
4. Persons with open sores, colds, sore throat, tuberculosis, or other infectious illnesses should not touch or go near the newborn baby or the woman while she is giving birth.
5. Keep the baby in a clean place away from smoke and dust.
cleanliness,newborn,baby_hygiene,diapers,bathing
Initial Post-Birth Examination of a Newborn Baby
Standard Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

Step 1: After the mother and baby are stable (usually about an hour after birth), examine the baby from head to toe.

Step 2: Wash your hands and wear clean gloves before examining the baby to prevent infection.

Step 3:...
Procedure:

Step 1: After the mother and baby are stable (usually about an hour after birth), examine the baby from head to toe.

Step 2: Wash your hands and wear clean gloves before examining the baby to prevent infection.

Step 3: Keep the baby warm by covering her head and unexamined body parts. If possible, warm up the room.

Step 4: Be gentle with the baby during examination, as she is sensitive and needs reassurance.

Note: Bathing the baby immediately after birth is not necessary and may make her cold. You can safely wait a few hours or even days before bathing.
newborn examination, post-birth care, infection prevention
Keeping the Newborn Baby Warm but Not Too Warm
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

• Protect the baby from cold, but also from too much heat.
• Dress him as warmly as you feel like dressing yourself.
• In cold weather, wrap the baby well. In hot weather (or when the baby has a fever), leave him naked.
-...
Procedure:

• Protect the baby from cold, but also from too much heat.
• Dress him as warmly as you feel like dressing yourself.
• In cold weather, wrap the baby well. In hot weather (or when the baby has a fever), leave him naked.
• Keep him close to his mother’s body. This is especially important for a baby that is born early or very small.
• See ‘Special Care for Small, Early, and Underweight Babies’, p. 405.
baby_temperature,newborn,warmth,cold_weather,heat_protection
Care of the Newborn Baby’s Umbilical Cord
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

• To prevent the freshly cut cord from becoming infected, it should be kept clean and dry.
• The drier it is, the sooner it will fall off and the navel will heal.
• For this reason, it is better not to use a belly band,...
Procedure:

• To prevent the freshly cut cord from becoming infected, it should be kept clean and dry.
• The drier it is, the sooner it will fall off and the navel will heal.
• For this reason, it is better not to use a belly band, or if one is used, to keep it very loose (see p. 184 and 263).
umbilical_cord,newborn,clean,dry,belly_band
Document Findings During Newborn Examination
Standard Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

Step 1: Create a chart to record the baby’s physical signs and general appearance over time.

Example Chart Format:

||Mother’s name<br>Baby’s name|Mother’s name<br>Baby’s name|Mother’s name<br>Baby’s name|Mother’s...
Procedure:

Step 1: Create a chart to record the baby’s physical signs and general appearance over time.

Example Chart Format:

||Mother’s name<br>Baby’s name|Mother’s name<br>Baby’s name|Mother’s name<br>Baby’s name|Mother’s name<br>Baby’s name|Mother’s name<br>Baby’s name|Mother’s name<br>Baby’s name|
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
||physical signs<br>hour 1<br>hour 3<br>hour 4<br>hour 5<br>hour 6<br>hour 2||||||
||general<br>appearance||||||
||breathing||||||
||heartbeat||||||
||temperature||||||
||weight and length<br>legs and feet<br>hips<br>genitals<br>belly<br>shoulders, arms, and hands<br>chest<br>neck<br>nose and mouth<br>eyes<br>ears<br>head (skull)<br>back<br>skin||||||

Step 2: Record observations such as general appearance, breathing, heartbeat, temperature, weight, length, and physical signs at different time intervals.

Note: This helps track the baby’s health and identify any changes over time.
newborn examination chart, post-birth documentation
Protection of Newborn Baby’s Eyes from Conjunctivitis
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

• To protect a newborn baby’s eyes from dangerous conjunctivitis, put a line of 1% tetracycline or erythromycin 0.5% to 1% ointment in each eye within the first 2 hours (p. 221 and 378).
• This is especially important if either...
Procedure:

• To protect a newborn baby’s eyes from dangerous conjunctivitis, put a line of 1% tetracycline or erythromycin 0.5% to 1% ointment in each eye within the first 2 hours (p. 221 and 378).
• This is especially important if either parent has ever had signs of gonorrhea or chlamydia (p. 236).
conjunctivitis,newborn,eye_protection,tetracycline,erythromycin

Mental Health (12) -

Provide Care for Service Member with Continued Stress Reaction
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. If stress reaction signs continue:
- Get the service member to a safer place.
- Do not leave the service member alone; keep someone he knows with him.
- Notify a senior noncommissioned officer (NCO) or officer.
- Have...
Procedure:

1. If stress reaction signs continue:
- Get the service member to a safer place.
- Do not leave the service member alone; keep someone he knows with him.
- Notify a senior noncommissioned officer (NCO) or officer.
- Have the service member examined by medical personnel.

Details:
• This ensures that the service member receives appropriate care and support.
• Early intervention is critical to prevent worsening of symptoms.
continued stress reaction, mental health, first aid
Secure Weapons and Restrain if Necessary
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. If concerned about the service member’s reliability:
- Unload his weapon.
- Take the weapon if seriously concerned.
- Physically restrain him only when necessary for safety or transportation.

Details:
• This is to...
Procedure:

1. If concerned about the service member’s reliability:
- Unload his weapon.
- Take the weapon if seriously concerned.
- Physically restrain him only when necessary for safety or transportation.

Details:
• This is to prevent harm to self, others, or mission objectives.
• Ensure that restraint is done safely and with minimal force.
weapon safety, restraint, mental health, first aid
Calmly Engage a Distressed Service Member
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. If the service member is upset, calmly talk him into cooperating.

Details:
• Use a calm and reassuring tone to deescalate the situation.
• Avoid confrontation or aggressive behavior.
deescalation, mental health, communication, first aid
Recognize Signs of More Serious Stress Reaction
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

Identify the following signs in a service member that may indicate a more serious stress reaction:

Physical Signs:
1. Constantly moving around.
2. Flinching or ducking at sudden sounds.
3. Shaking, trembling (whole body or...
Procedure:

Identify the following signs in a service member that may indicate a more serious stress reaction:

Physical Signs:
1. Constantly moving around.
2. Flinching or ducking at sudden sounds.
3. Shaking, trembling (whole body or arms).
4. Cannot use part of the body with no physical reason (hand, arm, legs).
5. Cannot see, hear, or feel (partial or complete loss).
6. Physical exhaustion, crying.
7. Freezing under fire or total immobility.
8. Vacant stares, staggering, swaying when standing.
9. Panic running under fire.

Emotional Signs:
1. Rapid and/or inappropriate talking.
2. Argumentative, reckless movements/actions.
3. Inattentive to personal hygiene.
4. Indifferent to danger.
5. Memory loss.
6. Severe stuttering, mumbling, or cannot speak at all.
7. Insomnia, nightmares.
8. Seeing or hearing things that do not exist.
9. Rapid emotional shifts.
10. Social withdrawal.
11. Apathetic.
12. Hysterical outbursts.
13. Frantic or strange behavior.

Details:
• These signs are more severe than mild stress reactions and may require intervention.
stress reaction, mental health, signs, first aid
Assign Easy Tasks to Service Member with Stress Reaction
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Give the service member easy tasks to do when not sleeping, eating, or resting.

Details:
• This helps maintain a sense of purpose and reduces feelings of helplessness.
• Ensure that the tasks are simple and non-stressful.
task assignment, mental health, first aid
Manage Hysteria and Anxiety-Induced Behaviours in Emergencies
Standard Source: Aids to Survival
Procedure:
1. Reassure the individual to reduce fear or anxiety.
2. Secure the patient from any immediate danger (e.g., immobilise them if they are near a cliff edge or unstable surface).
3. Remove the person from the triggering environment and...
Procedure:
1. Reassure the individual to reduce fear or anxiety.
2. Secure the patient from any immediate danger (e.g., immobilise them if they are near a cliff edge or unstable surface).
3. Remove the person from the triggering environment and relocate them to a safe, secure position.

Note: Hysteria can be triggered by environmental factors such as claustrophobia in caves or fear of heights during climbing.
hysteria management, anxiety, reassurance, safety
Reassure Service Members About Stress Reactions
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Reassure everyone that the signs are probably just stress reaction and will quickly improve.

Details:
• This helps reduce fear and stigma associated with mental health issues.
• Encourage a supportive environment for recovery.
reassurance, mental health, first aid
Return Service Member to Normal Duties When Ready
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Assure the service member he will return to full duty in 24 hours.
2. Return him to normal duties as soon as he is ready.

Details:
• This helps restore confidence and a sense of normalcy.
• Monitor his progress closely before...
Procedure:

1. Assure the service member he will return to full duty in 24 hours.
2. Return him to normal duties as soon as he is ready.

Details:
• This helps restore confidence and a sense of normalcy.
• Monitor his progress closely before returning to full duties.
return to duty, mental health, first aid
Calm the Casualty During First Aid
Standard Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Objective: Reassure and keep the casualty calm during first aid.

Steps:
1. Reassure the casualty that you are there to help him.
2. Be authoritative and self-confident to instill trust.
3. Maintain a calm demeanor throughout the...
Objective: Reassure and keep the casualty calm during first aid.

Steps:
1. Reassure the casualty that you are there to help him.
2. Be authoritative and self-confident to instill trust.
3. Maintain a calm demeanor throughout the entire procedure.

Notes:
• Keeping the casualty calm is essential for effective first aid and reducing panic.
• This step should be performed continuously until medical aid arrives.
calm casualty, reassurance, first aid, mental health
Support Service Member Emotionally During Stress
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Let the service member talk about his feelings.
2. Do not dismiss or belittle his feelings of grief or worry.
3. Provide practical advice and help put emotions into perspective.

Details:
• Encourage open communication to...
Procedure:

1. Let the service member talk about his feelings.
2. Do not dismiss or belittle his feelings of grief or worry.
3. Provide practical advice and help put emotions into perspective.

Details:
• Encourage open communication to reduce emotional distress.
• Avoid judgment and offer reassurance that it is normal to feel this way.
emotional support, mental health, stress reaction, service member
Ensure Service Member Eats, Drinks, and Sleeps
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Ensure the service member eats, drinks, and sleeps as soon as possible.

Details:
• Provide nourishing food and clean water to maintain energy levels.
• Allow for adequate rest to prevent fatigue and support recovery.
nutrition, hydration, sleep, service member, mental health
Maintain Personal Hygiene for Service Members
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Ensure the service member maintains good personal hygiene.

Details:
• This includes regular bathing, oral care, and proper clothing maintenance.
• Good hygiene helps prevent illness and supports overall well-being.
hygiene, service member, personal care, mental health

Resuscitation (11) -

Administer Mouth-to-Mouth Breathing
Critical Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Mouth-to-mouth breathing is a method of helping a person who has stopped breathing to start breathing again. Follow these steps:

1. Check for responsiveness: Gently tap the person’s shoulder and shout, “Are you okay?” If there is no...
Mouth-to-mouth breathing is a method of helping a person who has stopped breathing to start breathing again. Follow these steps:

1. Check for responsiveness: Gently tap the person’s shoulder and shout, “Are you okay?” If there is no response, proceed.
2. Call for help: If you are alone, call 911 or ask someone else to do so. If you are with others, assign someone to call for emergency services immediately.
3. Open the airway: Tilt the person’s head back slightly by lifting their chin. This helps open the airway.
4. Check for breathing: Look, listen, and feel for no more than 10 seconds. If there is no normal breathing, begin mouth-to-mouth breathing.
5. Pinch the nose shut: Use your fingers to pinch the person’s nostrils closed.
6. Take a deep breath: Inhale deeply through your mouth.
7. Blow into the person’s mouth: Place your mouth completely over theirs and blow gently until you see their chest rise. This should take about 1 second per breath.
8. Repeat: Give two slow, steady breaths, then check again for breathing. If there is still no breathing, continue giving breaths every 5 seconds until help arrives or the person starts breathing on their own.

Important notes:
• Do not give breaths if the person is choking with a visible object in their mouth.
• Avoid over-inflation of the lungs; this can cause injury.
• If you are unsure about how to perform mouth-to-mouth breathing, it is better to wait for professional help.
mouth-to-mouth breathing, artificial respiration, CPR, emergency breathing, first aid
Artificial Respiration for an Unconscious Victim
Critical Source: Family Emergency Handbook
Procedure:

• Ensure the airway is clear using the appropriate method (chin-lift or head tilt).
• If the person is not breathing, start artificial respiration immediately.
• Pinch the nostrils shut.
• Place your mouth over the victim's...
Procedure:

• Ensure the airway is clear using the appropriate method (chin-lift or head tilt).
• If the person is not breathing, start artificial respiration immediately.
• Pinch the nostrils shut.
• Place your mouth over the victim's mouth.
• Give two full breaths.
• Continue with one breath every five seconds until the victim begins to breathe normally or help arrives.
artificial respiration,breathing,first aid
Basic Resuscitation (CPR)
Critical Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Procedure:

1. Understand the purpose: Basic life support maintains breathing and circulation without equipment.
2. Use simple tools if available: Airway devices or shields can be used to protect from infection during resuscitation.
3....
Procedure:

1. Understand the purpose: Basic life support maintains breathing and circulation without equipment.
2. Use simple tools if available: Airway devices or shields can be used to protect from infection during resuscitation.
3. Perform CPR:
- Combine mouth-to-mouth respiration with chest compressions.
4. Training is essential: Attend a first aid course for proper training (see Chapter 4).
5. Follow the ABCDE method:
- A: Airway – Ensure airway is clear and maintain neck control.
- B: Breathing – Check for breathing and provide assistance if needed.
- C: Circulation – Check for pulse and manage bleeding.
- D: Disability – Assess neurological status.
- E: Exposure – Expose the casualty to check for injuries and manage environmental factors.
basic resuscitation, CPR, ABCDE method, airway management, breathing support
CPR for an Unconscious Victim with No Pulse
Critical Source: Family Emergency Handbook
Procedure:

• Feel the pulse in the neck for 10 seconds.
• If no pulse is felt, start CPR immediately.
• Use head tilt/chin-lift to open the airway.
• Place the heel of one hand on the breastbone, above its low tip between the nipples.
• Place...
Procedure:

• Feel the pulse in the neck for 10 seconds.
• If no pulse is felt, start CPR immediately.
• Use head tilt/chin-lift to open the airway.
• Place the heel of one hand on the breastbone, above its low tip between the nipples.
• Place the heel of the other hand on top of the first hand.
• Press straight down to compress the chest 1.5 to 2 inches (3.8 cm to 5 cm) at a rate of 100 times per minute.
• After every 15 compressions, give two breaths.
• Continue this action until help arrives.
cpr,breathing,chest compressions
Clearing an Obstructed Airway During Artificial Respiration
Critical Source: Family Emergency Handbook
Procedure:

• If air does not enter the victim's chest during artificial respiration:
- Reposition the head and attempt again.
- Place the heel of one hand on top of the back of the other hand just above the navel but well below the tip of...
Procedure:

• If air does not enter the victim's chest during artificial respiration:
- Reposition the head and attempt again.
- Place the heel of one hand on top of the back of the other hand just above the navel but well below the tip of the breastbone.
- Press the upper abdomen with six to ten quick thrusts.
- Grasp the lower jaw between the thumb and finger and lift the chin.
- Insert the index finger of the other hand deep into the mouth and use it to sweep, dislodge, and remove any foreign object.
- Attempt to give a breath again.
- If unsuccessful, repeat the sequence of abdominal thrusts, finger sweeps, and attempts to ventilate until the obstruction is cleared.
airway obstruction,cpr,abdominal thrusts
Immediate Life-Saving Measures (Resuscitation)
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Stabilize the casualty and prevent further deterioration.

Steps:
1. Airway Management: Clear any obstructions from the airway using head tilt-chin lift or jaw thrust if necessary.
2. Breathing Support: If not breathing,...
Objective: Stabilize the casualty and prevent further deterioration.

Steps:
1. Airway Management: Clear any obstructions from the airway using head tilt-chin lift or jaw thrust if necessary.
2. Breathing Support: If not breathing, provide rescue breaths at a rate of 10–12 per minute.
3. Circulation Control: Apply direct pressure to control external bleeding. Use tourniquets only if bleeding is severe and cannot be controlled by direct pressure.
4. Spinal Protection: Avoid moving the casualty unless absolutely necessary. If spinal injury is suspected, immobilize the head, neck, and spine using a cervical collar or improvised materials.
5. Wound Dressing: Apply sterile dressings to major limb wounds and keep them clean and dry.

Notes: These actions should be performed immediately after the initial examination.
resuscitation, airway management, breathing support, circulation control, spinal protection, wound dressing
Perform CPR for Asphyxia and Unconsciousness Due to Lack of Air
Critical Source: Aids to Survival
Procedure:
1. Begin cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) immediately if the person is unresponsive and not breathing.
2. Continue performing CPR until medical help arrives, which may take 1 or more hours.
3. Monitor for signs of spontaneous...
Procedure:
1. Begin cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) immediately if the person is unresponsive and not breathing.
2. Continue performing CPR until medical help arrives, which may take 1 or more hours.
3. Monitor for signs of spontaneous breathing in cases involving marine stings, cone shell, or blue ringed octopus bites, as they may begin breathing again after a prolonged period of unconsciousness.

Note: Asphyxia can result from drowning, smoke inhalation, choking, or respiratory paralysis. CPR is critical to survival.
CPR, asphyxia, resuscitation, unconsciousness
Performing Artificial Respiration Using the Schaefer or Prone Method
Critical Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure for Performing Artificial Respiration Using the Schaefer or Prone Method:

1. Remove the person from water immediately and lay them face down on a firm surface, such as the ground.
2. **Position the patient's arms above their...
Procedure for Performing Artificial Respiration Using the Schaefer or Prone Method:

1. Remove the person from water immediately and lay them face down on a firm surface, such as the ground.
2. Position the patient's arms above their head to allow free passage of air, and slightly tilt their face to one side.
3. Kneel astride or beside the prone figure, placing your hands in the spaces between the short ribs (approximately at the level of the lower part of the ribcage).
4. Apply pressure by letting the weight of your upper body fall onto your hands resting on the patient's chest, forcing air out of their lungs.
5. Relax the pressure, allowing the chest cavity to expand and draw in air.
6. Repeat this process (pressing and relaxing) at a rate of 12 to 15 times per minute. Use a watch or count initially to maintain the correct rhythm.
7. Continue this procedure for as long as necessary, even if it takes an hour or more, until signs of recovery are observed (e.g., a flicker of an eyelid or a gasp from the patient).
8. Once breathing begins, gradually reduce the frequency and intensity of artificial respiration until natural breathing resumes.
9. Promote circulation by rubbing the legs and body toward the heart to improve blood flow.
10. Do not attempt to stimulate the patient by touching the throat or inducing vomiting until they are able to swallow.
11. Administer a teaspoonful of aromatic spirits of ammonia in half a glass of water, if available, to help revive the patient.

Important Notes:
• Laying the patient face downward helps fluids in the air passages drain and prevents the tongue from blocking the airway.
• This method requires only one operator and minimizes time wasted on preliminary steps.
artificial respiration, Schaefer method, prone method, drowning rescue, first aid for drowning
Resuscitation Procedure for Unresponsive Casualty
Critical Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
### Resuscitation Procedure for Unresponsive Casualty

Steps to follow at the scene of an incident where there appears to be an unresponsive patient:

1. Stop and think.
2. **Do NOT put yourself in danger – ask the question: “Am I...
### Resuscitation Procedure for Unresponsive Casualty

Steps to follow at the scene of an incident where there appears to be an unresponsive patient:

1. Stop and think.
2. Do NOT put yourself in danger – ask the question: “Am I safe?”
3. Approach the casualty and assess the situation.
4. Assess the casualty’s response; say loudly: “Are you OK?” Gently shake the shoulders.

• If the casualty responds:
- Assess and treat any injuries or medical conditions (see Chapter ).
- Consider placing the casualty in the recovery position (Figure .), but always remember that a spinal injury may be present.

• If there is no response:
- Shout for help.
- Open the airway by lifting the jaw upwards (chin lift), but avoid extending the neck more than necessary (head tilt).
- Remove any obvious obstructions in the mouth, but do not poke fingers blindly into the mouth.
- Look at the chest, listen and feel if the casualty is breathing out against your cheek for  seconds.

• If there is no breathing:
- Give two breaths of expired air resuscitation:
- Pinch the casualty’s nostrils.
- Take a breath, place lips over the casualty’s lips, and breathe out steadily into the casualty’s chest (this should take about  seconds).
- Watch to ensure that the chest rises.
- Use a protective shield if available.

• After two breaths, check the carotid pulse (if trained to do so) in the neck for  seconds and look for other signs of circulation: choking, coughing, return of colour.

• If there is no pulse or sign of circulation, commence chest compressions.
- First identify the site for chest compressions: run fingers along the rib margin to the breast bone.
- Place your index and middle fingers together at this point then slide the heel of the other hand to touch above your fingers. Ensure that only the heel of the hand is in contact with the casualty.
- Interlock the fingers and lean well over the casualty with your arms straight, press down vertically at a rate of approximately  compressions per minute.
- In an adult, the compressions should be about –cm in depth. Compression and release phases should be equal in time.

• After  compressions, give two breaths of expired air resuscitation and repeat. Do not stop to check for a pulse – if resuscitation is successful the casualty will start to cough, swallow or choke.
resuscitation,casualty,unresponsive,breathing,chest compressions,airway,head tilt,chin lift
Dangers of Resuscitation and Infection Control During CPR
High Priority Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Key points to consider regarding the dangers of resuscitation:

• There is understandable concern about the transmission of blood-borne diseases during resuscitation – particularly HIV and hepatitis.
• Although viruses can be isolated from the...
Key points to consider regarding the dangers of resuscitation:

• There is understandable concern about the transmission of blood-borne diseases during resuscitation – particularly HIV and hepatitis.
• Although viruses can be isolated from the saliva of infected persons, transmission is rare, with only fifteen documented cases of CPR-related infection in the literature. Three cases of HIV have been reported and were acquired during resuscitation of infected patients – on two occasions from a needle-stick injury and in the third after heavy contamination of broken skin.

To minimize the risk of acquiring infection, rescuers should:
• Wear gloves.
• Use barriers whenever possible.
• Great care must be taken with sharp objects.
infection control,CPR,HIV,hepatitis,gloves,blood-borne diseases,sharp objects
Demonstrate Sylvester and Schaefer Methods of Resuscitation
High Priority Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Sylvester Method: Apply firm pressure to the chest in a rhythmic manner, similar to artificial respiration, with the aim of stimulating the heart.
2. Schaefer Method: Use a combination of chest compressions and artificial...
Procedure:

1. Sylvester Method: Apply firm pressure to the chest in a rhythmic manner, similar to artificial respiration, with the aim of stimulating the heart.
2. Schaefer Method: Use a combination of chest compressions and artificial respiration techniques, ensuring that air is forced into the lungs while simultaneously applying pressure to the chest.

Details:
• These methods were standard first aid procedures in 1911 for resuscitation.
• Proper technique requires practice and knowledge of anatomy.
resuscitation,Sylvester method,Schaefer method,chest compressions,artificial respiration

Bandaging Techniques (11) -

Apply a Head Bandage to Stabilize the Jaw
High Priority Source: Where There is No Dentist
Steps to Apply a Head Bandage:

1. Gently close the person's jaw so that their teeth come together.
2. Support this position with a head-and-chin bandage.
3. Tie the bandage in a way that supports the jaw, not pulls it.
4. Ensure the...
Steps to Apply a Head Bandage:

1. Gently close the person's jaw so that their teeth come together.
2. Support this position with a head-and-chin bandage.
3. Tie the bandage in a way that supports the jaw, not pulls it.
4. Ensure the bandage is not too tight, allowing the mouth to remain slightly open with the teeth apart if needed.
5. Be careful to avoid choking the person with the bandage.
head bandage, jaw stabilization, first aid, dental injury
Apply Cravat Bandage to Jaw Injury
Standard Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure for Applying a Cravat Bandage to a Jaw Injury

1. Apply bandages attached to field first aid dressing to the jaw after dressing the wound using the same technique illustrated in Figure 3-32A—C.
2. **Apply a cravat bandage to the...
Procedure for Applying a Cravat Bandage to a Jaw Injury

1. Apply bandages attached to field first aid dressing to the jaw after dressing the wound using the same technique illustrated in Figure 3-32A—C.
2. Apply a cravat bandage to the jaw:
(a) Place the bandage under the chin and pull its ends upward. Adjust the bandage to make one end longer than the other (Figure 3-32A).
(b) Take the longer end over the top of the head to meet the short end at the temple and cross the ends over (Figure 3-32B).
(c) Take the ends in opposite directions to the other side of the head and tie them over the part of the bandage that was applied first (Figure 3-32C).

Important Notes:
• Before applying a bandage, remove all loose or free-floating foreign material from the casualty’s mouth.
• If the casualty is unconscious, check for obstructions in the airway and remove if possible.
• If there is profuse bleeding in the oral cavity, pack it loosely with soft bandaging material (e.g., Kerlix™ gauze) prior to applying a bandage.
• Allow the jaw enough freedom to permit passage of air and drainage from the mouth.
• The dressing and bandaging procedure serves two purposes: stopping the bleeding and protecting the wound, as well as immobilizing a fractured jaw.
cravat bandage, jaw injury, first aid, dressing, wound care
Apply Bandage to Head Wound Using Square Knot Technique
Standard Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure for Applying a Bandage to a Head Wound Using the Square Knot Technique

1. Bring the second tail under the chin, up in front of the ear (on the side opposite the wound), and over the head to meet the other tail (on the wounded...
Procedure for Applying a Bandage to a Head Wound Using the Square Knot Technique

1. Bring the second tail under the chin, up in front of the ear (on the side opposite the wound), and over the head to meet the other tail (on the wounded side) (Figure 3-28).
2. Cross the two tails on the wound side (Figure 3-29) and bring one end across the forehead (above the eyebrows) to a point just in front of the opposite ear (on the uninjured side).
3. Wrap the other tail around the back of the head (at the base of the skull), and tie the two ends just in front of the ear on the uninjured side with a square knot (Figure 3-30).

Important Notes:
• Avoid covering the casualty’s ear with the dressing, as this will decrease his ability to hear.
head wound, square knot bandage, first aid, dressing technique
Apply Cravat Bandage to Elbow or Knee
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

For the Elbow:
1. Bend the arm at the elbow and place the middle of the cravat at the point of the elbow, bringing the ends upward (Figure 3-35A).
2. Bring the ends across, extending both downward (Figure 3-35B).
3. Take both...
Procedure:

For the Elbow:
1. Bend the arm at the elbow and place the middle of the cravat at the point of the elbow, bringing the ends upward (Figure 3-35A).
2. Bring the ends across, extending both downward (Figure 3-35B).
3. Take both ends around the arm and tie them with a square knot at the front of the elbow (Figure 3-35C).

For the Knee Using Elbow Technique:
1. Use the same technique applied in bandaging the elbow as illustrated in Figure 3-39.
2. Ensure that the bandage is not too tight to avoid restricting circulation.
3. Use additional padding if needed for comfort or to prevent pressure on sensitive areas.

Notes:
• If an elbow fracture is suspected, DO NOT bend the elbow; bandage it in the position found.
cravat bandage, elbow, knee, first aid, bandaging technique
Apply Cravat Bandage to Head Wound
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Step-by-step procedure:

1. Position the bandage: Place the middle of the cravat bandage over the dressing on the head.
2. Cross the ends around the head: Cross the two ends of the bandage in opposite directions completely around the...
Step-by-step procedure:

1. Position the bandage: Place the middle of the cravat bandage over the dressing on the head.
2. Cross the ends around the head: Cross the two ends of the bandage in opposite directions completely around the head.
3. Tie securely: Tie the ends of the bandage over the dressing to secure it in place.

Additional steps for specific injuries:
4. Bring the second tail under the chin (Figure 3-28).
5. Cross the two tails (on the wound side) (Figure 3-29) and bring one end across the forehead (above the eyebrows) to a point just in front of the opposite ear (on the uninjured side).
6. Wrap the other tail around the back of the head (at the base of the skull), and tie the two ends just in front of the ear on the uninjured side with a square knot (Figure 3-30).

Important Notes:
• Ensure that the bandage is applied snugly but not too tight to avoid restricting blood flow.
• This method provides protection and support for head injuries without causing additional harm.
• This technique is used to immobilize a fractured jaw or maintain a sterile dressing that does not have tail bandages attached.
• Ensure that the bandage is applied securely but without causing pressure on the wound or restricting airway passage.
cravat bandage, head injury, first aid, bandaging technique, jaw immobilization
Apply Cravat Bandage to Palm of the Hand
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Lay the middle of the cravat over the palm of the hand with the ends hanging down on each side (Figure 3-37A).
2. Take the end of the cravat at the little finger across the back of the hand, extending it upward over the base of...
Procedure:

1. Lay the middle of the cravat over the palm of the hand with the ends hanging down on each side (Figure 3-37A).
2. Take the end of the cravat at the little finger across the back of the hand, extending it upward over the base of the thumb; then bring it downward across the palm (Figure 3-37B).
3. Take the thumb end across the back of the hand, over the palm, and through the hollow between the thumb and palm (Figure 3-37C).
4. Take the ends to the back of the hand and cross them; then bring them up over the wrist and cross them again (Figure 3-37D).
5. Bring both ends down and tie them with a square knot on top of the wrist (Figure 3-37E—F).

Notes:
• Ensure that the bandage is not too tight to avoid restricting circulation.
• Use absorbent material between fingers if available.
hand bandage, cravat bandage, palm bandage, first aid procedure
Apply Cravat Bandage to Leg (Upper and Lower)
Reference Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

1. Place the center of the cravat over the dressing (Figure 3-38A).
2. Take one end around and up the leg in a spiral motion and the other end around and down the leg in a spiral motion, overlapping part of each preceding turn...
Procedure:

1. Place the center of the cravat over the dressing (Figure 3-38A).
2. Take one end around and up the leg in a spiral motion and the other end around and down the leg in a spiral motion, overlapping part of each preceding turn (Figure 3-38B).
3. Bring both ends together and tie them with a square knot on top of the leg (Figure 3-38C).

Notes:
• Ensure that the bandage is not too tight to prevent circulation issues.
• Apply sufficient pressure over the dressing without restricting blood flow.
leg bandage, cravat, first aid
Apply Cravat Bandage to Shoulder or Armpit
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Make an extended cravat bandage by using two triangular bandages (Figure 3-34A); place the end of the first triangular bandage along the base of the second one (Figure 3-34B).
2. Fold the two bandages into a single extended...
Procedure:

1. Make an extended cravat bandage by using two triangular bandages (Figure 3-34A); place the end of the first triangular bandage along the base of the second one (Figure 3-34B).
2. Fold the two bandages into a single extended bandage (Figure 3-34C).
3. Fold the extended bandage into a single cravat bandage (Figure 3-34D). After folding, secure the thicker part (overlap) with two or more safety pins (Figure 3-34E).
4. Place the middle of the cravat bandage under the armpit so that the front end is longer than the back end and safety pins are on the outside (Figure 3-34F).
5. Cross the ends on top of the shoulder (Figure 3-34G).
6. Take one of the bandage ends across the back and under the arm on the opposite side and the other end across the chest. Tie the ends (Figure 3-34H).

Notes:
• Be sure to place sufficient wadding in the armpit.
• DO NOT tie the cravat bandage too tightly. Avoid compressing the major blood vessels in the armpit.
cravat bandage, shoulder armpit, safety pins, wadding
Apply Shoulder Bandage Using Field First Aid Dressing
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Take one bandage across the chest and the other across the back and under the arm opposite the injured shoulder.
2. Tie the ends with a square knot (Figure 3-33).

Notes:
• Ensure that the bandages are properly secured to...
Procedure:

1. Take one bandage across the chest and the other across the back and under the arm opposite the injured shoulder.
2. Tie the ends with a square knot (Figure 3-33).

Notes:
• Ensure that the bandages are properly secured to immobilize the shoulder without restricting blood flow.
• Use additional padding if needed for comfort or to prevent pressure on sensitive areas.
shoulder bandage, field first aid dressing, square knot, immobilization
Apply a Triangular Bandage to the Head
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Step-by-step procedure:

1. Position the bandage: Turn the base (longest side) of the triangular bandage up and center its base on the center of the forehead, letting the point (apex) fall on the back of the neck.
2. **Cross the ends behind...
Step-by-step procedure:

1. Position the bandage: Turn the base (longest side) of the triangular bandage up and center its base on the center of the forehead, letting the point (apex) fall on the back of the neck.
2. Cross the ends behind the head: Take the ends of the bandage behind the head and cross them over the apex.
3. Tie the ends over the forehead: Bring the crossed ends over the forehead and tie them securely.
4. Secure the apex: Tuck the apex (point) of the bandage behind the crossed part of the bandage or secure it with a safety pin, if available.

Notes:
• Ensure that the bandage is applied snugly but not too tight to avoid restricting blood flow.
• This method provides protection and support for head injuries without causing additional harm.
triangular bandage, head injury, first aid, dressing application
How to Make a Cravat Bandage
Reference Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Materials Required:
• Any pliable and durable material (e.g., T-shirts, shirts, bed linens, trouser legs, scarfs)

Steps:
1. Select an item made of pliable and durable material that can be folded, torn, or cut to the desired size.
2. Cut or...
Materials Required:
• Any pliable and durable material (e.g., T-shirts, shirts, bed linens, trouser legs, scarfs)

Steps:
1. Select an item made of pliable and durable material that can be folded, torn, or cut to the desired size.
2. Cut or tear the material into a long strip (approximately 4-5 inches wide).
3. If needed, fold the strip as necessary for application.

Notes:
• Cravat bandages are useful for securing dressings, splints, or other medical devices.
• They can be used to stabilize injuries or provide support during transport.
cravat bandage, first aid, improvisation, medical emergency, wound care

Heat Injuries (11) -

Identify Symptoms of Heatstroke
Critical Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Steps to Identify Heatstroke:

1. Look for the following symptoms:
- Red (flushed), hot, dry skin
- Weakness
- Dizziness
- Confusion
- Headache
- Seizures
- Nausea
- **Stomach pains or...
Steps to Identify Heatstroke:

1. Look for the following symptoms:
- Red (flushed), hot, dry skin
- Weakness
- Dizziness
- Confusion
- Headache
- Seizures
- Nausea
- Stomach pains or cramps
- Respiration and pulse may be rapid and weak
- Unconsciousness and collapse may occur suddenly
heatstroke, symptoms, identification, first aid, heat injury
Manage Heatstroke (Sunstroke)
Critical Source: SH 21 76 Ranger Handbook
Procedure for Managing Heatstroke

1. Move casualty to a cool, shaded area, loosen clothing, and remove outer clothing if the situation permits.
2. Immerse in cool water. If cool bath is not available, massage arms and legs with cool water. Fan...
Procedure for Managing Heatstroke

1. Move casualty to a cool, shaded area, loosen clothing, and remove outer clothing if the situation permits.
2. Immerse in cool water. If cool bath is not available, massage arms and legs with cool water. Fan casualty to increase the cooling effect of evaporation.
3. If conscious, slowly consume one quart of water.
4. SEEK MEDICAL AID AND EVACUATE AS SOON AS POSSIBLE. Perform any lifesaving measures.
heatstroke, immediate evacuation, cooling techniques
Provide First Aid for Heatstroke
Critical Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Steps to Provide First Aid for Heatstroke:

WARNING: Heatstroke is a medical emergency that may result in death if treatment is delayed. Start cooling measures immediately and continue while waiting for transportation and during...
Steps to Provide First Aid for Heatstroke:

WARNING: Heatstroke is a medical emergency that may result in death if treatment is delayed. Start cooling measures immediately and continue while waiting for transportation and during evacuation.

1. Monitor the casualty’s mental status by asking simple questions.
2. Move the casualty to a cool, shady area or improvise shade if none is available.
3. Loosen or remove the casualty’s clothing and boots, unless in a chemical environment.
4. Spray or pour water on the casualty and fan him/her. (Apply iced sheets if available.)
5. Massage the casualty’s arms and legs.
6. Elevate the casualty’s legs.
7. If the casualty is conscious, have him/her slowly drink at least one canteen of cool water.
first aid, heatstroke, treatment, medical emergency
Provide Immediate Cooling for Heatstroke
Critical Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. Monitor the casualty’s mental status by asking simple questions.
2. Move the casualty to a cool, shady area or improvise shade if none is available.
3. Loosen or remove the casualty’s clothing and boots, unless in a...
Procedure:

1. Monitor the casualty’s mental status by asking simple questions.
2. Move the casualty to a cool, shady area or improvise shade if none is available.
3. Loosen or remove the casualty’s clothing and boots, unless in a chemical environment.
4. Spray or pour water on the casualty and fan him/her. Apply iced sheets if available.
5. Massage the casualty’s arms and legs.
6. Elevate the casualty’s legs.
7. If the casualty is conscious, have him/her slowly drink at least one canteen of cool water.
8. Watch the casualty closely for life-threatening conditions, check for other injuries, and seek medical aid.

Warning: Heatstroke is a medical emergency that may result in death if treatment is delayed. Start cooling measures immediately and continue while waiting for transportation and during evacuation.
heatstroke, cooling measures, first aid, emergency treatment, survival
Recognize and Respond to Heatstroke
Critical Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Objective: Identify the signs of heatstroke and initiate immediate emergency response.

Signs and Symptoms:
• High body temperature (typically above 104°F/40°C)
• Altered mental state or confusion
• Lack of sweating despite high...
Objective: Identify the signs of heatstroke and initiate immediate emergency response.

Signs and Symptoms:
• High body temperature (typically above 104°F/40°C)
• Altered mental state or confusion
• Lack of sweating despite high temperatures
• Rapid, weak pulse
• Dry, hot, and red skin
• Seizures
• Loss of consciousness

First Aid Measures:
1. Immediately move the casualty to a cool, shady area.
2. Call for emergency medical assistance (911 or local equivalent) immediately.
3. Begin cooling the casualty by immersing him in water if possible, or using wet towels and fanning to lower body temperature.
4. Do not give any fluids to an unconscious casualty.
5. Monitor the casualty’s breathing and pulse until help arrives.

Important Notes:
• Heatstroke is a life-threatening emergency that can lead to organ failure or death if not treated promptly.
• Do not use salt solutions in first aid procedures for heat injuries.
heatstroke, emergency response, cooling techniques, life-threatening
Perform First Aid for Heat Injuries
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure for Performing First Aid for Heat Injuries

Conditions: You see a casualty who has signs and symptoms of a heat injury. The casualty has a full canteen of cool water.

Standards: Recognize the type of heat injury and give...
Procedure for Performing First Aid for Heat Injuries

Conditions: You see a casualty who has signs and symptoms of a heat injury. The casualty has a full canteen of cool water.

Standards: Recognize the type of heat injury and give appropriate first aid.

Steps:
1. Recognize the type of heat injury (e.g., heat exhaustion, heat stroke).
2. Provide appropriate first aid based on the specific type of heat injury.
3. Ensure that the casualty has access to cool water for rehydration.
4. Monitor the casualty closely and seek medical assistance if necessary.

Notes:
• This procedure is essential for preventing complications from heat-related injuries.
heat injury treatment,first aid,heat exhaustion,heat stroke
Prevent Heat Injuries Through Proper Hydration and Monitoring
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Ensure service members stay hydrated by drinking water regularly, even if not thirsty.
2. Encourage consumption of cool water, as it is absorbed more quickly than warm or cold water.
3. Monitor for signs of heat injuries...
Procedure:

1. Ensure service members stay hydrated by drinking water regularly, even if not thirsty.
2. Encourage consumption of cool water, as it is absorbed more quickly than warm or cold water.
3. Monitor for signs of heat injuries (cramps, exhaustion, and heatstroke) in all individuals exposed to hot environments.
4. If a casualty shows symptoms of any heat injury, immediately provide first aid and seek medical assistance if needed.
5. Avoid the use of salt solutions during first aid procedures for heat-related conditions.
6. Ensure that all service members are trained in recognizing and responding to heat injuries.
hydration,cool water,salt solutions,training,monitoring
Manage Heat Exhaustion
Standard Source: SH 21 76 Ranger Handbook
Procedure for Managing Heat Exhaustion

1. Move the casualty to a cool, shaded area and loosen clothing.
2. Pour water on casualty and fan to increase cooling effect of evaporation.
3. Provide at least one quart of water to replace lost...
Procedure for Managing Heat Exhaustion

1. Move the casualty to a cool, shaded area and loosen clothing.
2. Pour water on casualty and fan to increase cooling effect of evaporation.
3. Provide at least one quart of water to replace lost fluids.
4. Elevate legs.
5. Seek medical aid if symptoms continue.
heat exhaustion, cooling techniques, fluid replacement
Prevent Heat Injuries Through Proper Clothing and Environment Management
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Ensure service members are equipped with specialized clothing and equipment (e.g., cold weather gear) appropriate for the environment.
2. Use this gear properly to mitigate environmental risks.
3. Avoid exposure to extreme...
Procedure:

1. Ensure service members are equipped with specialized clothing and equipment (e.g., cold weather gear) appropriate for the environment.
2. Use this gear properly to mitigate environmental risks.
3. Avoid exposure to extreme heat, such as from direct sunlight or high temperatures.
4. If in MOPP gear, body armor, or inside closed spaces like armored vehicles, take extra precautions to prevent overheating.
5. Monitor for signs of heat injuries and ensure all service members are trained to recognize and provide first aid for these conditions.
heat injuries,clothing,environmental risks,MOPP gear,body armor,first aid training
Manage Heat Cramps
Standard Source: SH 21 76 Ranger Handbook
Procedure for Managing Heat Cramps

1. Move the casualty to a shaded area and loosen clothing.
2. Allow casualty to drink 1 quart of cool water slowly per hour.
3. Monitor casualty and provide water as needed.
4. Seek medical attention if cramps...
Procedure for Managing Heat Cramps

1. Move the casualty to a shaded area and loosen clothing.
2. Allow casualty to drink 1 quart of cool water slowly per hour.
3. Monitor casualty and provide water as needed.
4. Seek medical attention if cramps persist.
heat cramps, hydration, medical attention
Provide First Aid for Heat Cramps
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Steps to Provide First Aid for Heat Cramps:

1. Monitor the mental status by asking simple questions, such as the date or the president’s name.
2. Move the casualty to a cool, shady area or improvise shade if none is available.
3....
Steps to Provide First Aid for Heat Cramps:

1. Monitor the mental status by asking simple questions, such as the date or the president’s name.
2. Move the casualty to a cool, shady area or improvise shade if none is available.
3. Loosen the casualty’s clothing, unless in a chemical environment.
4. Have the casualty slowly drink at least one canteen of cool water.
5. Seek medical aid if the cramps continue.
6. Monitor the casualty for signs and symptoms of heat exhaustion.

_Note:_ If possible, the casualty should not participate in strenuous activity for the rest of the day.
first aid, heat cramps, treatment, medical care

Training Procedures (11) -

Train Litter Bearers in Mountain Evacuation Techniques
High Priority Source: FM 3 97 61 Military Mountaineering
Objective: Ensure litter bearers are trained to safely evacuate casualties from mountainous terrain.

Training Requirements:
• Evacuation techniques: Train litter bearers on how to properly carry and transport injured soldiers over rough...
Objective: Ensure litter bearers are trained to safely evacuate casualties from mountainous terrain.

Training Requirements:
• Evacuation techniques: Train litter bearers on how to properly carry and transport injured soldiers over rough terrain using litters or stretchers.
• First aid skills: Reinforce basic first aid training for all litter bearers, including airway management, breathing support, and circulatory care.
• Weather protection: Teach methods to protect casualties from weather conditions during transportation (e.g., covering them with blankets, keeping them dry).
• Shock management: Train on how to manage shock in injured soldiers by maintaining body temperature and providing comfort.
• Communication protocols: Establish clear communication procedures between litter bearers and medical units for efficient coordination during evacuations.
• Regular sustainment training: Conduct periodic training sessions to maintain readiness and reinforce skills.
litter bearers, evacuation training, first aid skills, shock management, communication protocols
Brief Soldier for CPR Evaluation
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure for Briefing a Soldier During CPR Evaluation

Steps:
1. Tell the Soldier to perform all necessary steps to restore breathing and pulse.
2. For step 3b, inform the Soldier that the casualty’s breathing rate is slow and have them...
Procedure for Briefing a Soldier During CPR Evaluation

Steps:
1. Tell the Soldier to perform all necessary steps to restore breathing and pulse.
2. For step 3b, inform the Soldier that the casualty’s breathing rate is slow and have them demonstrate or explain what they would do to insert an NPA on a mannequin.
3. After step 3, tell the Soldier that the casualty is not breathing.
4. When testing steps 4 and 5, vary the test by indicating whether the chest rises or not.
5. If testing steps 6 and 7, inform the Soldier that the chest rises after they remove a foreign object.
6. When testing step 8, tell the Soldier that a pulse is not found.
7. Stop the evaluation when the Soldier rechecks for the pulse in step 10.
8. Do not evaluate step 12 in the simulated mode.
CPR evaluation,briefing soldier,training procedures
Evaluation Setup for CPR Training
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure for Setting Up CPR Evaluation

Steps:
1. Use a resuscitation training mannequin (DVC 08-15) for training and testing.
2. Have a bottle of alcohol and swabs or cotton available on the table.
3. **Place the mannequin on the...
Procedure for Setting Up CPR Evaluation

Steps:
1. Use a resuscitation training mannequin (DVC 08-15) for training and testing.
2. Have a bottle of alcohol and swabs or cotton available on the table.
3. Place the mannequin on the floor and ensure that the alcohol and cotton balls are accessible.
4. Clean the mannequin’s nose and mouth before each Soldier is evaluated.
5. If an NPA-capable mannequin is available, use it to test step 3b.
CPR training setup,mannequin preparation,evaluation setup
Nasopharyngeal Airway (NPA) Training Skill Sheet
Standard Source: TCCC 17 13 Army Tactical Combat Casualty Care
Objective: To demonstrate the recommended procedure for insertion of an NPA.

References:
• *Prehospital Trauma Life Support*, Military Eighth Edition, Jones and Bartlett Learning, November 2014
• *Advanced Trauma Life Support Manual*, Ninth...
Objective: To demonstrate the recommended procedure for insertion of an NPA.

References:
• *Prehospital Trauma Life Support*, Military Eighth Edition, Jones and Bartlett Learning, November 2014
• *Advanced Trauma Life Support Manual*, Ninth Edition, American College of Surgeons, Chicago, IL, September 2012

Materials Required:
• Student checklist
• Airway simulator
• NPA (nasopharyngeal airway)
• Surgical lubricant

Instructor Guidelines:
1. Provide each instructor with a student checklist.
2. Ensure the student has all required materials (NPA, surgical lubricant, airway simulator).
3. Read the learning objective and evaluation method to the student.
4. Explain how the exercise will be graded.
5. Allow students time to extract information from the scenario provided by the instructor.

Performance Steps:
1. Assemble and test all necessary equipment (airway trainer, NPA, lubricant).
2. Assess the upper airway for visible obstruction.
3. Open the airway using a chin lift/jaw thrust maneuver.
4. Verbalize the indications for an NPA: use in unconscious patients or those unable to maintain airway patency.
5. Lubricate the NPA with surgical lubricant.
6. Insert the NPA into the nose at a 90-degree angle to the face, avoiding upward aiming toward the top of the head.
7. Advance the tube until the flange rests against the nostril.
8. Use a rotary or back-and-forth motion if resistance is encountered during insertion.
9. If unable to insert on one side, remove the NPA and attempt insertion in the other nostril.

Evaluation Criteria:
• Assessed upper airway for visible obstruction: pass/fail
• Opened airway with chin lift/jaw thrust maneuver: pass/fail
• Lubricated the NPA with surgical lubricant: pass/fail
• Inserted the NPA at a 90-degree angle, avoiding upward aiming: pass/fail
• Used rotary or back-and-forth motion to facilitate insertion: pass/fail
• Removed and reinserted NPA on the other side if unable to insert initially: pass/fail
NPA training, airway management training, medical skills training, military first aid
Performance Measures for CPR Evaluation
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure for Evaluating Performance in CPR

Steps:
1. Position the casualty correctly.
2. Open the airway using the head-tilt/chin-lift method.
3. Check for breathing by looking, listening, and feeling for signs of...
Procedure for Evaluating Performance in CPR

Steps:
1. Position the casualty correctly.
2. Open the airway using the head-tilt/chin-lift method.
3. Check for breathing by looking, listening, and feeling for signs of respiration.
4. Insert an NPA, if necessary.
5. Give breaths to ensure an open airway.
6. Reposition the casualty’s head and repeat breaths, if necessary.
7. Perform chest compressions to clear the airway, if necessary (without performing a blind finger sweep).
8. Check for a pulse.
9. Continue mouth-to-mouth or mouth-to-nose resuscitation or CPR, as required.
10. Recheck for pulse and breathing, as required.
11. Perform all necessary steps in the correct sequence.
12. Once the casualty is breathing and has a pulse, place them in the recovery position. Watch the casualty closely for life-threatening conditions, maintain an open airway, and check for other injuries, if necessary.
CPR evaluation,performance measures,training checklist
Access TCCC Guidelines and Resources
Standard Source: TCCC 17 13 Army Tactical Combat Casualty Care
Access TCCC Guidelines and Resources

• Visit the NAEMT website at http://www.naemt.org/education/TCCC/guidelines_curriculum for guidelines, slides, and additional TCCC information.
• Access resources from the Defense Health Agency at...
Access TCCC Guidelines and Resources

• Visit the NAEMT website at http://www.naemt.org/education/TCCC/guidelines_curriculum for guidelines, slides, and additional TCCC information.
• Access resources from the Defense Health Agency at http://www.health.mil/tccc.
• Use these resources to support training programs and ensure compliance with CoTCCC recommendations.
tccc, guidelines access, first aid procedures
Become a TCCC-AC Instructor
Standard Source: TCCC 17 13 Army Tactical Combat Casualty Care
Become a TCCC-AC Instructor

• Complete the Tactical Combat Casualty Care-Medical Provider (TCCC-MP) course.
• Take the online NAEMT instructor course, which takes approximately 6 hours to complete.
• Submit the NAEMT instructor application upon...
Become a TCCC-AC Instructor

• Complete the Tactical Combat Casualty Care-Medical Provider (TCCC-MP) course.
• Take the online NAEMT instructor course, which takes approximately 6 hours to complete.
• Submit the NAEMT instructor application upon completion of the course.
• An NAEMT TCCC-affiliate faculty member must monitor the first TCCC course taught by a new instructor.
• Instruction is authorized in a 10:1 ratio according to CoTCCC guidelines.
tccc, instructor training, first aid procedures
Conduct TCCC-AC Written Posttest
Standard Source: TCCC 17 13 Army Tactical Combat Casualty Care
Conduct TCCC-AC Written Posttest

• Administer the written posttest after completing all training sessions.
• Use the posttest to evaluate student understanding and mastery of TCCC-AC principles.
• Ensure that students achieve a passing score as...
Conduct TCCC-AC Written Posttest

• Administer the written posttest after completing all training sessions.
• Use the posttest to evaluate student understanding and mastery of TCCC-AC principles.
• Ensure that students achieve a passing score as per NAEMT guidelines.
tccc, written posttest, training procedures
Identify Military TCCC Instructors
Standard Source: TCCC 17 13 Army Tactical Combat Casualty Care
Identify Military TCCC Instructors

• Military TCCC instructors must be trained medical personnel such as military medics, medical technicians, corpsmen, or other medical personnel (officer or enlisted) who are trained to conduct medicine in the...
Identify Military TCCC Instructors

• Military TCCC instructors must be trained medical personnel such as military medics, medical technicians, corpsmen, or other medical personnel (officer or enlisted) who are trained to conduct medicine in the Armed Services.
• Medical simulation and training centers at various Army installations may have NAEMT TCCC-affiliate faculty designation.
• The Defense Medical Readiness and Training Institute also has NAEMT TCCC-affiliate faculty staff and offers mobile training team courses listed on the Joint Knowledge Online website.
tccc, instructor identification, first aid procedures
Review TCCC-AC Slides with Students
Standard Source: TCCC 17 13 Army Tactical Combat Casualty Care
Review TCCC-AC Slides with Students

• Present all TCCC-AC slides to students.
• Use scenarios, practical exercises, and videos included in the slide series for instruction.
• Ensure that instructor guides are used for each slide series to...
Review TCCC-AC Slides with Students

• Present all TCCC-AC slides to students.
• Use scenarios, practical exercises, and videos included in the slide series for instruction.
• Ensure that instructor guides are used for each slide series to maintain consistency and accuracy in training.
tccc, slides, training procedures
Simulate Burn Injuries for Training Purposes
Reference Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure for Simulating Burn Injuries During Training

Steps:
1. Use another Soldier to play the part of the casualty.
2. Simulate the burn(s) by marking the casualty's body with a suitable material.
3. **Provide materials...
Procedure for Simulating Burn Injuries During Training

Steps:
1. Use another Soldier to play the part of the casualty.
2. Simulate the burn(s) by marking the casualty's body with a suitable material.
3. Provide materials appropriate for the burn being simulated, such as a piece of rope, blanket, water, dressing, or clean cloth.
4. If a dressing is used, use the same one repeatedly to maintain consistency in training.
5. If an electrical burn is being simulated, have the casualty lie on a piece of wire or rope and have enough materials available to treat two simulated burns.
6. Tell the Soldier what is burning the casualty and instruct them to take appropriate action.
7. After the Soldier completes step 1, inform them that the casualty is conscious and has no injuries other than burns.
8. When testing step 2, vary the test by telling the Soldier that clothing is stuck to the burn or that a chemical environment exists.
9. Do not evaluate step 4 in the simulated mode.

Notes:
• This procedure ensures realistic training scenarios for proper burn treatment.
burn simulation,training procedures,military training,burn treatment

Pregnancy Care (10) -

Watching Mother for Infection
High Priority Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

1. Monitor the mother for signs of infection, such as fever, chills, or unusual vaginal discharge.
2. Check for redness, swelling, or tenderness in the genital area.
3. If the mother has a fever (≥100.4°F) or persistent pain, notify...
Procedure:

1. Monitor the mother for signs of infection, such as fever, chills, or unusual vaginal discharge.
2. Check for redness, swelling, or tenderness in the genital area.
3. If the mother has a fever (≥100.4°F) or persistent pain, notify a healthcare provider immediately.
4. Encourage good hygiene practices to prevent infection spread.
5. Administer antibiotics as prescribed if an infection is confirmed.

Notes:
• Infections can be serious and may require prompt treatment.
• Early detection improves outcomes for both mother and baby.
postpartum infection, maternal health, midwifery care, fever monitoring
Helping Mother to Urinate
Standard Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

1. Encourage the mother to urinate frequently and ensure she has access to a clean toilet or bedpan.
2. If the mother is unable to urinate, gently massage her lower abdomen to stimulate urination.
3. Monitor for signs of urinary...
Procedure:

1. Encourage the mother to urinate frequently and ensure she has access to a clean toilet or bedpan.
2. If the mother is unable to urinate, gently massage her lower abdomen to stimulate urination.
3. Monitor for signs of urinary retention, such as pain or swelling in the bladder area.
4. If the mother cannot urinate within 6 hours postpartum, notify a healthcare provider immediately.

Notes:
• Urinary retention can lead to infections and should be addressed promptly.
• Encouraging mobility after birth may help with urination.
urinary retention, postpartum care, midwifery, mother health
Helping Mother to Eat and Drink
Standard Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

1. Encourage the mother to consume small, frequent meals throughout the day.
2. Provide a variety of nutrient-rich foods, including fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and proteins.
3. Ensure access to clean water or electrolyte...
Procedure:

1. Encourage the mother to consume small, frequent meals throughout the day.
2. Provide a variety of nutrient-rich foods, including fruits, vegetables, whole grains, and proteins.
3. Ensure access to clean water or electrolyte solutions to prevent dehydration.
4. Monitor for signs of nausea or vomiting and adjust food choices accordingly.
5. If the mother is unable to eat or drink, notify a healthcare provider immediately.

Notes:
• Proper nutrition supports both maternal health and fetal development.
• Hydration is crucial during labor and recovery.
mother nutrition, eating during pregnancy, hydration, midwifery care
Perform Kegel Exercises During Pregnancy
Standard Source: A Book for Midwives
Why it matters: Kegel exercises strengthen pelvic and vaginal muscles, helping to prevent urinary incontinence, reduce tearing during birth, speed healing after childbirth, and increase sexual pleasure.

How to perform Kegels:
1. **Learn the...
Why it matters: Kegel exercises strengthen pelvic and vaginal muscles, helping to prevent urinary incontinence, reduce tearing during birth, speed healing after childbirth, and increase sexual pleasure.

How to perform Kegels:
1. Learn the technique while urinating: Squeeze the vaginal muscles until the urine stops flowing.
2. Once learned, practice only when not urinating.
3. Perform at least 4 times a day, doing 10 squeezes each time.
4. Continue practicing regularly, as these exercises are beneficial for all women, not just during pregnancy.
kegel_exercises,pregnancy,muscle_strength
Ensure Adequate Rest and Sleep During Pregnancy
Reference Source: A Book for Midwives
Why it matters: Sufficient rest helps women stay strong, resist illness, and avoid complications such as high blood pressure and preterm birth.

Steps to ensure adequate rest:
1. Rest for a few minutes every 1 or 2 hours, even if working...
Why it matters: Sufficient rest helps women stay strong, resist illness, and avoid complications such as high blood pressure and preterm birth.

Steps to ensure adequate rest:
1. Rest for a few minutes every 1 or 2 hours, even if working long hours.
2. Explain the importance of rest to family members so they understand why it is necessary for the pregnant woman’s health.
3. Create an environment that supports rest and sleep, such as a quiet, comfortable space with minimal disturbances.
rest,pregnancy,sleep_health
Managing Heat and Excessive Sweating During Pregnancy
Reference Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

1. Understand normal sweating: Feeling hot and sweating more than usual is common during pregnancy.

2. Stay cool and hydrated:
- Wear loose, breathable clothing to help regulate body temperature.
- Bathe frequently to...
Procedure:

1. Understand normal sweating: Feeling hot and sweating more than usual is common during pregnancy.

2. Stay cool and hydrated:
- Wear loose, breathable clothing to help regulate body temperature.
- Bathe frequently to stay clean and cool.
- Drink plenty of water and fluids to prevent dehydration.

3. Monitor for other symptoms: If sweating is accompanied by signs of infection (e.g., pain during urination), seek medical advice.
heat,pregnancy,sweating,hydration
Incorporate Physical Exercise During Pregnancy
Reference Source: A Book for Midwives
Why it matters: Regular exercise strengthens the body and prepares it for labor and birth. It also helps maintain energy levels and mental well-being.

Ways to stay active:
• Engage in daily physical tasks such as hauling water, working...
Why it matters: Regular exercise strengthens the body and prepares it for labor and birth. It also helps maintain energy levels and mental well-being.

Ways to stay active:
• Engage in daily physical tasks such as hauling water, working in fields, milling grain, or walking up hills.
• Women with sedentary jobs (e.g., office work, factory labor) should take long walks, dance, or do other forms of physical activity to stay active.
• Find enjoyable ways to move, such as dancing or doing household chores that involve movement.
exercise,pregnancy,physical_activity
Managing Joint Aches and Pains During Pregnancy
Reference Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

1. Understand the cause: Hormonal changes during pregnancy make joints more flexible to prepare for childbirth, which can lead to discomfort or aching in areas like the hips.

2. Move carefully:
- Avoid sudden movements...
Procedure:

1. Understand the cause: Hormonal changes during pregnancy make joints more flexible to prepare for childbirth, which can lead to discomfort or aching in areas like the hips.

2. Move carefully:
- Avoid sudden movements that may strain joints.
- Use caution when standing up or walking to prevent sprains.

3. Reassure the woman: Joint pain is temporary and typically improves after childbirth.
joint pain,pregnancy,hormonal changes,joint safety
Proper Positioning and Movement for Pregnant Women
Reference Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

1. Avoid lying flat on the back: The weight of the womb can press on major blood vessels when a woman lies flat on her back, reducing oxygen flow to the baby.

2. Use support when lying down: If lying on the back is...
Procedure:

1. Avoid lying flat on the back: The weight of the womb can press on major blood vessels when a woman lies flat on her back, reducing oxygen flow to the baby.

2. Use support when lying down: If lying on the back is necessary, place something behind the back for support and avoid lying completely flat.

3. Move carefully when getting up:
- Avoid sitting up abruptly or in a way that strains the abdominal muscles (as shown in illustrations).
- Instead, roll to the side and use hands to push up gradually into a seated position.

4. Encourage safe movement: Teach pregnant women how to move carefully to avoid strain on joints and muscles.
pregnancy,positing,movement,joint safety
Promote a Positive Pregnancy Experience Through Community Support
Reference Source: A Book for Midwives
Why it matters: A positive pregnancy experience is supported by community rituals and practices that provide emotional and practical support.

Ways to promote a positive pregnancy experience:
• **Encourage communities to honor pregnant...
Why it matters: A positive pregnancy experience is supported by community rituals and practices that provide emotional and practical support.

Ways to promote a positive pregnancy experience:
• Encourage communities to honor pregnant women through rituals, special foods, or gifts.
• Support pregnant women with household tasks, such as cooking, cleaning, or fetching water.
• Provide massages or other forms of care that help the woman feel supported and valued during this time.
• Foster a sense of pride and self-worth in pregnant women by recognizing their strength and contributions.
community_support,pregnancy,rituals

Fracture Management (10) -

Handle Open Fractures Safely
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Do not attempt to push the bone back under the skin if it is an open fracture and protruding from the wound.
2. Apply a field dressing over the wound: This will protect the area without disturbing the bone.
3. **Evacuate...
Procedure:

1. Do not attempt to push the bone back under the skin if it is an open fracture and protruding from the wound.
2. Apply a field dressing over the wound: This will protect the area without disturbing the bone.
3. Evacuate promptly: Casualties with fractures of the extremities may show impaired circulation, such as numbness, tingling, cold or pale to bluish skin tone. These casualties should be evacuated by medical personnel and treated as soon as possible to prevent possible loss of the limb.
open fracture, bone protrusion, field dressing, evacuation, impaired circulation
Immobilize Fractures in Armed Conflicts
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Immobilize fractures to relieve pain and prevent further damage.

### Steps:
1. Assess the fracture for signs of open wounds or exposed bone (open fracture), which increases the risk of infection.
2. Immobilize the fracture...
Objective: Immobilize fractures to relieve pain and prevent further damage.

### Steps:
1. Assess the fracture for signs of open wounds or exposed bone (open fracture), which increases the risk of infection.
2. Immobilize the fracture using a plaster-of-Paris splint or split cast to stabilize the injury.
3. Control pain with appropriate measures, if available.
4. Administer antibiotics if there is an open fracture to prevent infection.
5. Use X-rays (if available) to identify the position of fractures and bone fragments, detect foreign bodies, and assess for internal damage.
6. Avoid inappropriate immobilization, as it can increase damage to surrounding soft tissues, especially blood vessels and nerves, and worsen pain.

### Notes:
• A major fracture of the thigh may cause hidden bleeding and lead to shock.
• Reduction and proper immobilization will quickly relieve pain.
• Lack of or improper immobilization increases the risk of complications.
immobilize fractures, first aid, fracture management, pain control, open fracture
Check for Fractures
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure: Check for open fractures by looking for bleeding or a bone sticking through the skin. Check for closed fractures by looking for swelling, discoloration, deformity, or unusual body position. If a suspected fracture is present, stop the...
Procedure: Check for open fractures by looking for bleeding or a bone sticking through the skin. Check for closed fractures by looking for swelling, discoloration, deformity, or unusual body position. If a suspected fracture is present, stop the evaluation and apply a splint.
fracture assessment, splint application
Fracture Splinting and Pulse Rechecking in Tactical Field Care
High Priority Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Procedure:

1. Splint any fractures using available materials such as rigid boards, sticks, or improvised splints.
2. Ensure the splint is applied securely but not too tight to avoid compromising circulation.
3. Recheck pulses distal to...
Procedure:

1. Splint any fractures using available materials such as rigid boards, sticks, or improvised splints.
2. Ensure the splint is applied securely but not too tight to avoid compromising circulation.
3. Recheck pulses distal to the fracture site periodically to ensure adequate blood flow.
4. Monitor for signs of compartment syndrome (pain, pallor, paresthesia, paralysis, and pulselessness).
5. If complications arise, adjust or remove the splint as necessary.
splinting_fractures,pulse_rechecking,tactical_field_care
Preparation for Splinting Suspected Fractures
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid + Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:
1. Gather splinting materials (splints, padding, and cravats) or improvise if necessary. Materials may include boards, sticks, or cloth.
2. Ensure that splints are long enough to immobilize the joint above and below the suspected...
Procedure:
1. Gather splinting materials (splints, padding, and cravats) or improvise if necessary. Materials may include boards, sticks, or cloth.
2. Ensure that splints are long enough to immobilize the joint above and below the suspected fracture.
3. Use at least four ties (two above and two below the fracture) to secure the splints with square knots tied away from the body.
4. Check distal pulses of the affected extremity before and after applying the splint.

Notes:
• If no materials are available, use swathes or a combination of swathes and slings to immobilize the extremity.
splinting, fractures, first aid, immobilization, distal pulses
Prepare a Casualty for Splinting a Suspected Fracture
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid + STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks + Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

1. Reassure the casualty and tell him that you will provide first aid and medical help is on the way.
2. Loosen any tight or binding clothing around the injury site.
3. Remove all jewelry from the injured part and place it in the...
Procedure:

1. Reassure the casualty and tell him that you will provide first aid and medical help is on the way.
2. Loosen any tight or binding clothing around the injury site.
