Before administering rescue breathing, perform the following steps:

1. Establish unresponsiveness and call for help.
2. Turn or position the casualty appropriately.
3. Open the airway using either the jaw-thrust or...
Procedure:

Before administering rescue breathing, perform the following steps:

1. Establish unresponsiveness and call for help.
2. Turn or position the casualty appropriately.
3. Open the airway using either the jaw-thrust or head-tilt/chin-lift technique.
4. Check for breathing by placing your ear over the casualty’s mouth and nose, and looking toward his chest:
- Look for rise and fall of the chest.
- Listen for sounds of breathing.
- Feel for breath on the side of your face.
5. If the chest does not rise and fall and no air is exhaled, then the casualty is not breathing.
6. Perform rescue breathing if the casualty is not breathing.

Notes:
• Even if the casualty appears to be making respiratory efforts, the airway may still be obstructed.
• If the casualty resumes breathing, continue to maintain an open airway.
preliminary steps, rescue breathing, airway check, first aid, emergency procedures
Provide Rescue Breathing (Mouth-to-Nose)
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Steps to Provide Mouth-to-Nose Rescue Breathing:

1. Check for responsiveness: Gently tap the casualty's shoulder and shout, 'Are you okay?' If there is no response, proceed.
2. Open the airway: Tilt the casualty's head back slightly and...
Steps to Provide Mouth-to-Nose Rescue Breathing:

1. Check for responsiveness: Gently tap the casualty's shoulder and shout, 'Are you okay?' If there is no response, proceed.
2. Open the airway: Tilt the casualty's head back slightly and lift their chin to open the airway.
3. Check for breathing: Look, listen, and feel for signs of breathing for up to 10 seconds.
4. Begin rescue breathing: Close the casualty's mouth with your hand, take a deep breath, and place your mouth over their nose. Blow gently into their nose until you see the chest rise.
5. Repeat rescue breathing: Give one breath every 5-6 seconds (about 10-12 breaths per minute) until help arrives or the casualty begins to breathe on their own.

Important Notes:
• Rescue breathing should only be performed if the casualty is not breathing and has no pulse.
• If you are unable or unwilling to provide mouth-to-nose, use a face mask or other barrier device if available.
• Continue rescue breathing until professional medical help arrives.
rescue breathing,mouth-to-nose,artificial respiration,first aid
Opening the Airway and Performing Rescue Breathing on an Unresponsive Casualty
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Assess or evaluate the situation.
2. Call for help.
3. Position the casualty on his back, face up.
4. Open the airway by tilting the head and lifting the chin (refer to paragraph 2-7 for information on how to...
Procedure:

1. Assess or evaluate the situation.
2. Call for help.
3. Position the casualty on his back, face up.
4. Open the airway by tilting the head and lifting the chin (refer to paragraph 2-7 for information on how to perform mouth-to-mouth resuscitation).
5. Establish breathlessness by checking for breathing.
6. Attempt to perform rescue breathing:
- Tilt the casualty’s head back slightly and lift the chin to open the airway.
- Pinch the nose shut with your fingers.
- Place your mouth completely over the casualty’s mouth, making a tight seal.
- Blow into the casualty’s mouth until you see the chest rise (approximately 1 second per breath).
- Remove your mouth and allow the casualty to exhale naturally.
7. If still unable to ventilate the casualty, perform 6 to 10 manual (abdominal or chest) thrusts.
airway opening, rescue breathing, first aid, unresponsive casualty, mouth-to-mouth resuscitation
Performing Rescue Breathing with Jaw-Thrust Technique
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. If you suspect a neck injury, close the nostrils by placing your cheek tightly against them.
2. Take a deep breath and place your mouth in an airtight seal around the casualty’s mouth.
- *If the injured person is...
Procedure:

1. If you suspect a neck injury, close the nostrils by placing your cheek tightly against them.
2. Take a deep breath and place your mouth in an airtight seal around the casualty’s mouth.
- *If the injured person is small, cover both his nose and mouth with your mouth, sealing your lips against the skin of his face.*
3. Blow two full breaths into the casualty’s mouth (1 to 1 1/2 seconds per breath), taking a breath of fresh air each time before you blow.
4. Watch out of the corner of your eye for the casualty’s chest to rise.
- *If the chest rises, sufficient air is getting into the casualty’s lungs. Proceed as described in step (1).*
- *If the chest does not rise, perform the following steps:*
a. Take corrective action immediately by reestablishing the airway. Make sure that air is not leaking from around your mouth or out of the casualty’s pinched nose.
b. Reattempt to ventilate.
c. If the chest still does not rise, take the necessary action to open an obstructed airway (see paragraph 2-10).
5. After giving two slow breaths that cause the chest to rise, attempt to locate a pulse on the casualty.
6. Feel for a pulse on the side of the casualty’s neck closest to you by placing the first two fingers (index and middle fingers) of your hand on the groove beside the casualty’s Adam’s apple (carotid pulse).
- *Your thumb should not be used for pulse taking because you may confuse your pulse beat with that of the casualty.*
7. Maintain the airway by keeping your other hand on the casualty’s forehead.
8. Allow 5 to 10 seconds to determine if there is a pulse.
9. If signs of circulation are present and a pulse is found and the casualty is breathing— STOP ; allow the casualty to breathe on his own. If possible, keep him warm and comfortable.
10. If a pulse is found and the casualty is not breathing, continue rescue breathing.
11. If a pulse is not found, seek medically trained personnel for help as soon as possible.
rescue breathing,jaw-thrust technique,airway obstruction,chest rise,pulse check
Perform Rescue Breathing (Mouth-to-Mouth Method)
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Ensure the casualty is unresponsive and not breathing.
2. Place your hand on the casualty’s forehead, and pinch their nostrils together with the thumb and index finger of this hand to prevent air from escaping through the nose.
3....
Procedure:

1. Ensure the casualty is unresponsive and not breathing.
2. Place your hand on the casualty’s forehead, and pinch their nostrils together with the thumb and index finger of this hand to prevent air from escaping through the nose.
3. Use the same hand to exert pressure on the forehead to maintain a backward head tilt and open airway.
4. With your other hand, keep your fingertips on the bony part of the lower jaw near the chin and lift it (chin-lift technique) to further open the airway.
5. Take a normal breath, then seal your mouth over the casualty’s mouth, ensuring a tight seal.
6. Blow into the casualty’s mouth for about 1 second until you see the chest rise.
7. Remove your mouth and allow the casualty to exhale naturally.
8. Repeat this process at a rate of 10–12 breaths per minute (approximately one breath every 5 seconds) until help arrives or the casualty begins breathing on their own.

Notes:
• If the casualty has severe jaw fractures, mouth wounds, or tightly closed jaws due to spasms, use the mouth-to-nose method instead.
• Ensure that you are not pressing deeply in the casualty’s forehead; this could cause injury.
• Do not attempt rescue breathing if the casualty is vomiting or has a visible foreign object in the airway.
• If you are unable to provide effective breaths, continue with chest compressions instead.
rescue breathing, mouth-to-mouth, first aid, CPR, airway management

Casualty Handling (5) -

Proper Handling of Casualties During Rescue
High Priority Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Objective: Ensure safe handling and transportation of casualties to prevent further injury.

Steps:
1. Evaluate the type and extent of injuries before moving the casualty.
2. Reinforce dressings over wounds to prevent bleeding or...
Objective: Ensure safe handling and transportation of casualties to prevent further injury.

Steps:
1. Evaluate the type and extent of injuries before moving the casualty.
2. Reinforce dressings over wounds to prevent bleeding or contamination.
3. Immobilize fractured bones properly using splints or other available materials to avoid additional damage to muscles, blood vessels, or skin.
4. Select an appropriate manual carry method based on the casualty’s injuries and your knowledge of carrying techniques.
5. Communicate with a conscious casualty about how they will be transported to reduce fear and gain cooperation.
6. Provide blankets or rain gear if weather conditions are poor (e.g., rain, cold).
7. Prepare for shelter and warmth, especially in extreme environments like high altitudes or cold climates.
safe handling,casualty transportation,wound reinforcement,bone immobilization,manual carry methods
Roll a Casualty Safely
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure:

1. Kneel beside the casualty.
2. Raise the near arm and straighten it out above the head.
3. Adjust the legs so they are together and straight or nearly straight.
4. **Place one hand on the back of the casualty’s head and...
Procedure:

1. Kneel beside the casualty.
2. Raise the near arm and straighten it out above the head.
3. Adjust the legs so they are together and straight or nearly straight.
4. Place one hand on the back of the casualty’s head and neck.
5. Grasp the casualty under the arm with the free hand.
6. Pull steadily and evenly toward yourself, keeping the head and neck in line with the torso.
7. Roll the casualty as a single unit.
8. Place the casualty’s arms at his/her sides.

Important Notes:
• The casualty should be carefully rolled as a whole, so the body does not twist.
• This procedure is used to safely reposition an unconscious or injured casualty.
roll casualty, first aid, spinal injury, repositioning
Transportation of Casualties by Litter or Manual Carry
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Medical evacuation is preferred: Medical personnel trained in en route care should handle the transportation of sick and wounded casualties.
2. Only transport manually if necessary: Transport a casualty manually only when...
Procedure:

1. Medical evacuation is preferred: Medical personnel trained in en route care should handle the transportation of sick and wounded casualties.
2. Only transport manually if necessary: Transport a casualty manually only when there is no alternative, such as due to terrain, combat situations, or urgent life-saving needs.
3. Use litter transportation if possible: Transporting a casualty by litter is safer, more comfortable, and easier for the rescuer than manual carry.
4. Transfer to a litter when available: If a litter can be made available or improvised, transfer the casualty to it as soon as possible.
transport,casualty,litter,manual carry,first aid
Positioning a Casualty for Manual Carry
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Communicate with the casualty if conscious:
- Inform them of how they will be positioned and transported to reduce fear and gain cooperation.
2. Determine the required position:
- Roll the casualty onto their abdomen...
Procedure:

1. Communicate with the casualty if conscious:
- Inform them of how they will be positioned and transported to reduce fear and gain cooperation.
2. Determine the required position:
- Roll the casualty onto their abdomen or back, depending on their current position and the carry method needed.

To roll a casualty onto their abdomen:

1. Kneel at the casualty’s uninjured side.
2. Place their arms above their head; cross the ankle farther from you over the one closer to you.
3. Position your hands on the shoulder farther from you and place your other hand in the area of their hip or thigh (Figure B-1).
4. Roll them gently toward you onto their abdomen (Figure B-2).

To roll a casualty onto their back:

1. Follow the same procedure as above.
2. Gently roll the casualty onto their back instead of their abdomen.
positioning,casualty,manual carry,rolling,first aid
Carrying Techniques for Placing a Casualty on a Litter
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure for Using Carries to Place a Casualty on a Litter

The following carries may be used to place a casualty onto an improvised or standard litter:

1. One-Man Arms Carry (Figure B-6): Used when only one bearer is available and the...
Procedure for Using Carries to Place a Casualty on a Litter

The following carries may be used to place a casualty onto an improvised or standard litter:

1. One-Man Arms Carry (Figure B-6): Used when only one bearer is available and the casualty can support their own weight.
2. Two-Man Arms Carry (Figure B-17): Used by two bearers to lift the casualty and place them on a litter.
3. Two-Man Fore-and-Aft Carry (Figure B-18): Used for transporting a casualty over longer distances or when placing them onto a litter.
4. Two-Hand Seat Carry (Figure B-20): Used for short-distance transport or to place the casualty on a litter.
5. Four-Hand Seat Carry (Figure B-19): Used by two bearers to lift and carry the casualty, typically used when more support is needed.

Procedure for Carrying a Casualty onto a Litter

1. Choose an appropriate carry method: Any of the following carries may be used to place a casualty on a litter:
- The one-man arms carry (Figure B-6)
- The two-man arms carry (Figure B-17)
- The two-man fore-and-aft carry (Figure B-18)
- The two-hand seat carry (Figure B-20)
- The four-hand seat carry (Figure B-19)

2. Position the casualty carefully: Ensure that the casualty is moved in a way that minimizes further injury, especially if there are suspected spinal injuries.
3. Place the casualty on the litter: Use the selected carry method to transfer the casualty onto the litter with minimal movement and effort.
4. Secure the casualty on the litter: If possible, use available materials to secure the casualty on the litter for safe transport.

Notes:
• Always prioritize the safety of both the casualty and the rescuers.
• Use standard litters whenever possible; improvised litters are only used in emergencies.
• Choose the appropriate carry based on the casualty’s condition and available resources.
• Ensure that all bearers are coordinated to avoid injury to the casualty or themselves.
carrying, casualty, litter, first aid, rescue techniques

Bleeding Control (5) -

Apply a Tourniquet for Uncontrolled Bleeding
Critical Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
When to Apply a Tourniquet:

A tourniquet should be applied only when:
1. A pressure dressing has failed to stop the bleeding.
2. An arm or leg has been amputated.
3. Bleeding is from a major artery in the thigh, lower leg, or arm.
4. Bleeding...
When to Apply a Tourniquet:

A tourniquet should be applied only when:
1. A pressure dressing has failed to stop the bleeding.
2. An arm or leg has been amputated.
3. Bleeding is from a major artery in the thigh, lower leg, or arm.
4. Bleeding continues despite firm hand pressure on a soaked pressure dressing.

Steps to Apply a Tourniquet:
1. Prepare materials: Use a strong, pliable material (e.g., gauze, muslin bandages, clothing) and a rigid stick-like object for support.
2. Position the tourniquet: Place it 2–4 inches above the wound, but not directly over a joint.
3. Wrap the material tightly around the limb, ensuring it is at least 2 inches wide to minimize skin damage.
4. Insert the rigid object: Slide the stick or similar object under the tourniquet and twist it until bleeding stops.
5. Secure the tourniquet: Tie a knot or use another piece of material to hold the rigid object in place.
6. Mark the time: Note the time the tourniquet was applied on the casualty’s skin or clothing.
7. Monitor the casualty: Ensure they are taken to the nearest medical treatment facility (MTF) as soon as possible. Do not remove or loosen the tourniquet under any circumstances, even if it causes discomfort.

Important Warnings:
• A tourniquet can cause permanent damage to blood vessels and nerves if left in place too long.
• It may lead to loss of an arm or leg if not removed by a medical professional.
• Do not use wire, shoelaces, or other thin materials as the tourniquet band.
• The tourniquet must be easily visible for identification by responders.
tourniquet application, uncontrolled bleeding, first aid, emergency procedure
Manage Bleeding from Head Injuries
Critical Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

1. Assess the source of bleeding (external or internal).
2. For external bleeding, apply direct pressure with a sterile dressing or clean cloth to control bleeding.
3. If internal bleeding is suspected (e.g., swelling,...
Procedure:

1. Assess the source of bleeding (external or internal).
2. For external bleeding, apply direct pressure with a sterile dressing or clean cloth to control bleeding.
3. If internal bleeding is suspected (e.g., swelling, confusion), do not attempt to remove any foreign matter from the wound.
4. Do not apply pressure dressings if brain tissue is protruding from the wound.
5. Place a loose, moistened dressing over the exposed tissue and cover it with a sterile field dressing.
6. Keep the casualty warm and seek medical assistance immediately.
head injury bleeding, internal bleeding, external bleeding, pressure dressing
Apply a Tourniquet for Severe Bleeding
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid + STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks + Full First Aid Manual FM 2111 + The Ultimate Survival Kit Checklist
Steps to Apply a Tourniquet:

1. Assess the injury: Ensure there is severe bleeding from an extremity that cannot be controlled with direct pressure, elevation, or bandaging. This includes injuries such as those involving major arteries in...
Steps to Apply a Tourniquet:

1. Assess the injury: Ensure there is severe bleeding from an extremity that cannot be controlled with direct pressure, elevation, or bandaging. This includes injuries such as those involving major arteries in the thigh, lower leg, or arm, or from multiple arteries in traumatic amputation.
2. Position the tourniquet: Place it 2–4 inches above the wound, never directly on a joint. If using an improvised tourniquet, ensure it is at least 2 inches wide to minimize skin damage.
3. Apply the tourniquet: Tighten it until the bleeding stops or the pulse in the affected limb is no longer detectable. Use a windlass, rigid stick-like object, or the built-in mechanism of a Combat Application Tourniquet (C-A-T) to secure it in place.
4. Mark the time: Note the time the tourniquet was applied on the casualty's body or clothing using a pen or Sharpie (e.g., 'Tourniquet applied at 10:30 AM').
5. Monitor the casualty: Keep the casualty warm, check for signs of shock (pale skin, rapid pulse, confusion), and ensure they remain conscious.

Important Notes:
• A tourniquet should only be used as a last resort when all other methods fail to control severe arterial bleeding.
• Avoid using a tourniquet on an amputated limb unless it is still attached. If only part of a hand or foot has been severed, use a pressure dressing instead.
• Do not loosen or remove the tourniquet once applied, even if bleeding stops or pain/numbness occurs.
• Ensure that the tourniquet is marked clearly for medical personnel and remains in place until professional help arrives.
• Only use genuine North American Rescue Combat Application Tourniquets (CAT) to avoid failure during critical moments. Do not leave a tourniquet on for more than 2 hours unless in extreme circumstances.
tourniquet, severe bleeding, first aid, emergency, injury control, shock, direct pressure
Apply Digital Pressure to Control Bleeding
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid + First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Apply digital pressure to help control bleeding when other methods have failed.

Materials Needed:
• Fingers, thumbs, or hands (for applying pressure)

Procedure:
1. **If bleeding still continues, use indirect digital...
Objective: Apply digital pressure to help control bleeding when other methods have failed.

Materials Needed:
• Fingers, thumbs, or hands (for applying pressure)

Procedure:
1. If bleeding still continues, use indirect digital pressure.
2. Identify the Artery Site: Locate the main artery that supplies blood to the wounded area. This is typically near the skin surface or over bone.
3. Apply Pressure: Use fingers, thumbs, or hands to press directly on the identified artery site. Apply firm pressure to help shut off or slow down the flow of blood from the heart to the wound.
4. Combine with Other Methods: Digital pressure should be used in combination with direct pressure and elevation of the limb (if applicable).
5. Monitor Effectiveness: Continue applying digital pressure until bleeding is controlled or professional medical assistance arrives.

Notes:
• Digital pressure may help control bleeding when other methods, such as dressings, manual pressure, and elevation, have failed.
digital pressure, bleeding control, first aid, arterial pressure point
Control Bleeding
Reference Source: Ranger Handbook 2006 + SH 21 76 Ranger Handbook
Procedure for Controlling Bleeding

1. Apply a standard or pressure dressing.
2. If this does not control the bleeding, immediately apply a tourniquet.
3. Reassess dressings frequently to ensure bleeding has been controlled.
bleeding, tourniquet, dressing, first aid

Tourniquet Application (5) -

Apply Tourniquet to Upper Arm
Critical Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure for Applying a Tourniquet to the Upper Arm

1. Locate the artery:
- The large artery in the upper arm runs down the inner side of the big muscle, about the line of the coat seam.

2. Apply direct pressure:
- Use your...
Procedure for Applying a Tourniquet to the Upper Arm

1. Locate the artery:
- The large artery in the upper arm runs down the inner side of the big muscle, about the line of the coat seam.

2. Apply direct pressure:
- Use your fingers to press on the artery against the bone until bleeding stops below the point of compression.

3. Prepare a tourniquet:
- Have another scout prepare a handkerchief tied loosely around the upper arm.
- Insert a smooth stone or cork just above your fingers on the artery within the handkerchief.

4. Secure with a stick:
- Place a stick about one foot long under the handkerchief at the outer side of the limb and twist it until the stone applies pressure to the artery in the same way as your fingers did.

5. Tie the tourniquet securely:
- Tie the stick in place so that it will not untwist.

Important Notes:
• Do not keep a tourniquet on for more than one hour, as cutting off circulation for too long is dangerous.
• Loosen the tourniquet if needed but be ready to tighten it again quickly if bleeding resumes.
• Avoid using your fingers indefinitely due to fatigue and cramping.
tourniquet application, upper arm injury, first aid, wound management
Apply an Improvised Tourniquet Using a Rigid Object
Critical Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

1. Tie a half-knot (the same as the first part of tying a shoe lace).
2. Place a stick or similar rigid object on top of the half-knot.
3. Tie a full knot over the stick to secure it in place.
4. Twist the stick until the...
Procedure:

1. Tie a half-knot (the same as the first part of tying a shoe lace).
2. Place a stick or similar rigid object on top of the half-knot.
3. Tie a full knot over the stick to secure it in place.
4. Twist the stick until the tourniquet is tight around the limb and/or bright red bleeding has stopped.
5. In the case of amputation, dark oozing blood may continue for a short time; this is blood trapped between the wound and tourniquet.
6. Fasten the tourniquet to the limb by looping the free ends over the stick and bringing them around the limb to prevent the stick from loosening.
7. Tie the free ends together on the side of the limb.
8. Do not cover the tourniquet—it should remain in full view for visibility by medical personnel.
tourniquet application, rigid object use, first aid, emergency medical procedure
Do Not Adjust or Remove a Tourniquet Without Proper Medical Training
Critical Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

1. Only appropriately skilled medical personnel may adjust or remove/release the tourniquet in an appropriate setting.
2. Improper removal of a tourniquet can lead to further injury, excessive bleeding, or loss of limb.
3. Leave...
Procedure:

1. Only appropriately skilled medical personnel may adjust or remove/release the tourniquet in an appropriate setting.
2. Improper removal of a tourniquet can lead to further injury, excessive bleeding, or loss of limb.
3. Leave the tourniquet in place until professional medical help arrives.
tourniquet safety, first aid, emergency medical procedure
Mark and Monitor a Casualty with a Tourniquet
Critical Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

1. Mark the casualty’s forehead with a “T” and the time to indicate that a tourniquet has been applied.
2. If necessary, use the casualty’s blood to make this mark for visibility.
3. Apply a dressing to the stump if the limb...
Procedure:

1. Mark the casualty’s forehead with a “T” and the time to indicate that a tourniquet has been applied.
2. If necessary, use the casualty’s blood to make this mark for visibility.
3. Apply a dressing to the stump if the limb is missing (total amputation).
4. Ensure all wounds have a dressing to protect them from contamination.
5. Check and treat for shock.
6. Seek medical aid immediately.
tourniquet marking, shock treatment, first aid, emergency medical procedure
Transport and Handle Amputated Limbs or Body Parts
Critical Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

1. If possible, save and transport any severed (amputated) limbs or body parts with the casualty.
2. Ensure they are out of sight of the casualty to avoid causing additional distress.
3. Apply a dressing to the stump if...
Procedure:

1. If possible, save and transport any severed (amputated) limbs or body parts with the casualty.
2. Ensure they are out of sight of the casualty to avoid causing additional distress.
3. Apply a dressing to the stump if the limb is missing (total amputation).
4. All wounds should have a dressing to protect them from contamination.
amputation handling, first aid, emergency medical procedure

Surgical Procedures (5) -

Prepare for Basic Surgical Procedures in Emergencies
Critical Source: Life After Doomsday
Procedure:

1. Acquire a basic surgical toolkit, including items such as scalpels, forceps, sutures, antiseptics, and gauze.
2. Study medical manuals like *Emergency War Surgery* or *Medical Handbook for Unconventional Operations* to...
Procedure:

1. Acquire a basic surgical toolkit, including items such as scalpels, forceps, sutures, antiseptics, and gauze.
2. Study medical manuals like *Emergency War Surgery* or *Medical Handbook for Unconventional Operations* to learn about surgical procedures that may be necessary in survival situations.
3. Train family members on how to perform basic first aid and wound care techniques.
4. Keep a supply of sterile instruments, ensuring they are cleaned and stored properly to prevent infections.
5. Include anesthetic options, such as local anesthetics, in your medical kit for pain management during procedures.
6. Practice surgical techniques on non-living materials or with the guidance of a trained professional before attempting them in an emergency.
7. Store detailed instructions and diagrams from medical manuals to guide you during emergencies.
8. Maintain a list of potential surgical scenarios, such as treating wounds, fractures, or infections, and prepare accordingly.

Notes:
• This procedure is intended for extreme survival situations where professional medical care may not be available.
• Surgical procedures should only be performed when absolutely necessary and with the utmost caution to avoid further harm.
emergency surgery, surgical tools, survival medicine, first aid, wound care
Proper Handling and Disposal of Sutures
Standard Source: Medical Survival Wound Closure Manual
Handling instructions:

• Do not resterilize: Once opened or used, discard packages and unused sutures.
• Avoid damage: Handle sutures carefully to prevent crushing or crimping from surgical instruments (e.g., needle holders, forceps)...
Handling instructions:

• Do not resterilize: Once opened or used, discard packages and unused sutures.
• Avoid damage: Handle sutures carefully to prevent crushing or crimping from surgical instruments (e.g., needle holders, forceps) except when grasping the free end for an instrument tie.

Disposal instructions:
1. Discard opened packages immediately after use.
2. Dispose of unused suture material in accordance with biohazard protocols.
3. Ensure proper sharps disposal if needles are involved to prevent needle stick injuries.
suture handling,disposal,surgical safety
Proper Knot Placement for Sutures
Standard Source: Medical Survival Wound Closure Manual
Knot security is critical to ensure the integrity of suture closure:

• Use standard surgical technique: Flat and square ties are recommended.
• Add additional throws if indicated by surgical circumstances or operator experience.
-...
Knot security is critical to ensure the integrity of suture closure:

• Use standard surgical technique: Flat and square ties are recommended.
• Add additional throws if indicated by surgical circumstances or operator experience.
• Avoid improper knotting: Poorly tied knots may lead to suture failure, wound dehiscence, or complications.

Actionable steps:
1. Ensure the suture is properly tensioned before tying.
2. Use a square knot for most applications.
3. Add extra throws if the tissue is thick or under high tension.
4. Check the knot’s security after placement.
knot tying,suture technique,secure knots
Proper Suture Removal and Monitoring for Adverse Reactions
Standard Source: Medical Survival Wound Closure Manual
Sutures should be removed as indicated, depending on the wound location and healing progress:

• Conjunctival and vaginal mucosal sutures: May cause localized irritation if left in place too long; remove as needed.
• **Subcuticular...
Sutures should be removed as indicated, depending on the wound location and healing progress:

• Conjunctival and vaginal mucosal sutures: May cause localized irritation if left in place too long; remove as needed.
• Subcuticular sutures: Place as deeply as possible to minimize erythema and induration.
• Monitor for adverse reactions such as infection, local irritation, or calculus formation.

Actionable steps:
1. Remove sutures according to standard protocols (e.g., 7–14 days for skin closures).
2. Monitor the wound site for signs of infection or irritation after suture removal.
3. If any adverse reactions occur, seek medical attention promptly.
suture removal,wound monitoring,adverse reaction management
Understanding PDS II Suture Characteristics and Performance
Standard Source: Medical Survival Wound Closure Manual
PDS II (polydioxanone) suture is a synthetic absorbable suture with the following properties:

• Tensile strength retention: Retains approximately 70% of its original strength at two weeks post-implantation, 50% at four weeks, and 25% at six...
PDS II (polydioxanone) suture is a synthetic absorbable suture with the following properties:

• Tensile strength retention: Retains approximately 70% of its original strength at two weeks post-implantation, 50% at four weeks, and 25% at six weeks.
• Absorption rate: Minimal absorption until about day 90 post-implantation; essentially complete absorption within six months.

Indications for use:
• Ideal for wound closure where prolonged support is needed (e.g., skin closures, subcuticular sutures).
• Not recommended for neural tissue, adult cardiovascular tissue, or microsurgery.

Actionable steps:
1. Use PDS II suture in wounds requiring extended tensile strength during healing.
2. Avoid use in sensitive tissues such as neural and cardiovascular areas.
3. Ensure proper placement of subcuticular sutures to minimize erythema and induration.
pds ii suture,tensile strength,absorption rate,surgical use

Medicine Administration (5) -

Converting Teaspoons and Tablespoons to Milliliters
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Purpose: Convert common household measurements (teaspoons and tablespoons) into milliliters for accurate medicine dosing.

### Conversion Chart:
• 1 teaspoon (tsp.) = 5 ml.
• 1 tablespoon (Tbs.) = 15 ml.
• 3 teaspoons = **1...
Purpose: Convert common household measurements (teaspoons and tablespoons) into milliliters for accurate medicine dosing.

### Conversion Chart:
• 1 teaspoon (tsp.) = 5 ml.
• 1 tablespoon (Tbs.) = 15 ml.
• 3 teaspoons = 1 tablespoon

### Example:
• A prescription says: *Take 1 tsp.* of medicine.
• This means take 5 ml. of the liquid medication.

### Notes:
• Many household spoons may not hold exactly 5 ml or 15 ml, so it is important to use a proper measuring spoon or syringe if available.
• If using a regular teaspoon, ensure that it holds exactly 5 ml — no more and no less.
• Always check the label on liquid medicine for instructions on how much to take in milliliters.
teaspoon to milliliters, tablespoon to milliliters, measuring liquid medicine
Giving Medicines to Adults
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

1. Read the instructions carefully:
- Ensure that you understand the dosage and frequency of the medication.
2. Use a measuring cup or syringe:
- Measure the correct amount of liquid medicine precisely.
3. **Administer...
Procedure:

1. Read the instructions carefully:
- Ensure that you understand the dosage and frequency of the medication.
2. Use a measuring cup or syringe:
- Measure the correct amount of liquid medicine precisely.
3. Administer the medication with water:
- Encourage the person to drink water before, during, and after taking the medicine.
4. Take the medicine at the right time:
- Follow the prescribed schedule for optimal effectiveness.
5. Monitor for side effects:
- Watch for signs of adverse reactions (e.g., dizziness, nausea).
6. Keep track of medication intake:
- Record each dose taken to avoid overuse or underuse.
7. Store the medicine properly:
- Keep it in a cool, dry place away from children and pets.
8. If the person is unable to take the medicine orally, consult a health worker for alternative methods.
9. Never mix different medications unless instructed by a health worker:
- This can lead to dangerous interactions.
10. Dispose of expired or unused medication safely:
- Follow local guidelines for proper disposal.
adult medication, dosing instructions, safe medicine use
Administering Aspirin to Children Using Adult Tablets
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Purpose: Safely administer the correct dose of aspirin to children when baby aspirin is unavailable.

### Steps:
1. Check the dosage on adult and baby aspirin labels:
- One adult aspirin contains 300 mg of aspirin.
- One baby...
Purpose: Safely administer the correct dose of aspirin to children when baby aspirin is unavailable.

### Steps:
1. Check the dosage on adult and baby aspirin labels:
- One adult aspirin contains 300 mg of aspirin.
- One baby aspirin contains 75 mg of aspirin.
2. Calculate the correct portion for a child:
- Since 1 adult aspirin = 4 baby aspirins, cut an adult aspirin into four equal pieces.
3. Give one piece to the child in place of a baby aspirin:
- Each piece will contain exactly 75 mg of aspirin, which is equivalent to one baby aspirin.

### Notes:
• This method ensures accurate dosing and avoids overmedication.
• Ensure that the adult aspirin tablet is not damaged or broken before cutting it.
• Always confirm with a healthcare provider if unsure about dosage for children.
aspirin for children, cutting adult aspirin, child dosage equivalents
Measuring Liquid Medicines in Milliliters and Teaspoons
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Purpose: Accurately measure liquid medicines using milliliters (ml) or teaspoons (tsp).
liquid medicine measurement, milliliters to teaspoons, measuring syrups
Understand How to Measure and Give Medicine Using Symbols and Fractions
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Symbols:
• = means is equal to or is the same as
• + means and or plus

Examples:
1 + 1 = 2 (One plus one equals two)

Fractions:
• 1/2 tablet = half of a tablet
• 1/4 tablet = one quarter of a tablet
• 1/8 tablet = one-eighth of...
Symbols:
• = means is equal to or is the same as
• + means and or plus

Examples:
1 + 1 = 2 (One plus one equals two)

Fractions:
• 1/2 tablet = half of a tablet
• 1/4 tablet = one quarter of a tablet
• 1/8 tablet = one-eighth of a tablet
• 1 1/2 tablets = one and one-half tablets

Measuring Units:
• 1000 mg. = 1 g.
• 1 mg. = 0.001 g.
measuring medicine, fractions, symbols, grams, milligrams

Injury Treatment (5) -

Establish a Saline Lock for Hypovolemic Shock
Critical Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

Conditions: A casualty is in hypovolemic shock from a severe loss of blood or a medic or medical professional requests that you establish a saline lock. Bleeding has been controlled. Supplies not carried by you will be provided...
Procedure:

Conditions: A casualty is in hypovolemic shock from a severe loss of blood or a medic or medical professional requests that you establish a saline lock. Bleeding has been controlled. Supplies not carried by you will be provided by medical personnel.

Standards: Establish a saline lock in a prominent vein and ensure that there are no signs of infiltration. Do not cause further injury to the casualty.

Steps:
1. Gather supplies: an 18-gauge intravenous (IV) catheter/needle unit, saline lock adapter plug, constricting band, Tegaderm® dressing, alcohol or povidone-iodine pad, gloves, 3-5 milliliter (ml) syringe and needle, and an IV solution bag.
2. Prepare the site by cleaning it with alcohol or povidone-iodine.
3. Apply a constricting band above the intended insertion site to help visualize the vein.
4. Insert the IV catheter/needle unit into a prominent vein, ensuring proper placement and avoiding infiltration.
5. Attach the saline lock adapter plug to the IV catheter/needle unit.
6. Secure the catheter with Tegaderm® dressing to keep it in place and prevent movement.
7. Ensure that there are no signs of infiltration or other complications.
8. Monitor the casualty closely for any changes in condition and notify medical personnel if needed.
saline lock, hypovolemic shock, IV insertion, first aid, medical emergency
Recognize and Treat Illnesses and Injuries in Mountain Environments
High Priority Source: FM 3 97 61 Military Mountaineering
Procedure:

1. Assess the injury or illness:
- Determine the severity of the condition (e.g., minor wound, broken bone, infection).
2. Provide immediate first aid:
- Clean wounds with antiseptic and apply pressure to stop...
Procedure:

1. Assess the injury or illness:
- Determine the severity of the condition (e.g., minor wound, broken bone, infection).
2. Provide immediate first aid:
- Clean wounds with antiseptic and apply pressure to stop bleeding.
- Immobilize fractures using splints or other available materials.
3. Administer medication if necessary:
- Use over-the-counter medications for pain relief (e.g., ibuprofen) unless contraindicated.
4. Monitor the condition:
- Keep track of symptoms and changes in the patient’s state.
5. Evacuate if needed:
- If the injury or illness is severe, arrange for evacuation to a medical facility as soon as possible.
6. Prevent complications:
- Ensure that the injured person remains warm and dry to avoid further health issues.

Warnings:
• Do not attempt to treat serious injuries without proper training; seek professional help immediately.
• Avoid moving an injured person unless absolutely necessary, as this can worsen their condition.
illness treatment,mountain injury,first aid,emergency care
Using the Scout Staff to Make Splints
High Priority Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Use the scout staff as a splint for injured limbs.
2. Place the staff alongside the injured limb and secure it with cloth or rope.
3. Ensure that the splint is positioned correctly to prevent further injury and promote healing.
4....
Procedure:

1. Use the scout staff as a splint for injured limbs.
2. Place the staff alongside the injured limb and secure it with cloth or rope.
3. Ensure that the splint is positioned correctly to prevent further injury and promote healing.
4. Monitor the injured person's condition and seek medical help if necessary.
scout staff, splint making, first aid, injury treatment
Treat Mangling Injury of the Leg Without Severe Hemorrhage
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Clean the wound with clean water or antiseptic if available (or with soap and water if no antiseptic is present).
2. Apply sterile gauze directly over the wound to absorb any bleeding, then cover it with a sterile bandage to...
Procedure:

1. Clean the wound with clean water or antiseptic if available (or with soap and water if no antiseptic is present).
2. Apply sterile gauze directly over the wound to absorb any bleeding, then cover it with a sterile bandage to protect it from further contamination.
3. Immobilize the leg using a splint or by keeping it in a neutral position.
4. Elevate the injured leg slightly above heart level to reduce swelling.
5. Apply ice packs to the area to help reduce pain and inflammation if available.
6. Monitor for signs of infection or changes in circulation.

Details:
• Avoid moving the injured limb unnecessarily.
• If there is any sign of severe bleeding, apply direct pressure and consider using a tourniquet.
• Seek medical assistance if the injury is severe or does not heal within a reasonable time.
mangling injury, leg injury, first aid, clean wound, sterile gauze, elevate leg, ice pack, infection prevention
Treating Human Bites
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid + Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure for Treating Human Bites:

• Cleanse the wound thoroughly with soap.
• Flush it well with water.
• Cover it with a sterile dressing.
• Immobilize the injured arm or leg, if appropriate.
• Transport the casualty immediately to an MTF...
Procedure for Treating Human Bites:

• Cleanse the wound thoroughly with soap.
• Flush it well with water.
• Cover it with a sterile dressing.
• Immobilize the injured arm or leg, if appropriate.
• Transport the casualty immediately to an MTF (Medical Treatment Facility).

Important Notes:
• Human bites may become seriously infected due to bacteria in the mouth. Medical personnel MUST treat all human bites.
• Do not attempt to treat serious infections on your own; seek professional medical care immediately.
human bites, first aid, infection, sterile dressing, medical treatment

Insect Stings (4) -

Treat Wasp and Hornet Stings
High Priority Source: The Complete Outdoorsmans Handbook
Treatment for Wasp and Hornet Stings:

1. Do not attempt to remove a stinger, as wasps, hornets, and yellowjackets can sting multiple times.
2. Apply ice to the affected area immediately to slow the spread of venom.
3. Avoid heat and...
Treatment for Wasp and Hornet Stings:

1. Do not attempt to remove a stinger, as wasps, hornets, and yellowjackets can sting multiple times.
2. Apply ice to the affected area immediately to slow the spread of venom.
3. Avoid heat and other ineffective treatments like mud or ammonia.
4. Seek medical attention immediately if:
- You have been stung by more than one wasp, hornet, or yellowjacket.
- You suspect hypersensitivity or an allergic reaction to the sting.

Important Note:
• Wasps and hornets can attack in swarms, making their stings potentially life-threatening.
wasp_sting,treatment,hornet_sting,allergic_reaction
Removing a Tick Safely
High Priority Source: Dangerous Animals
Procedure:

1. Use fine-tipped tweezers: Grasp the tick as close to the skin's surface as possible.
2. Pull upward with steady pressure: Avoid twisting or jerking the tick, which can cause its mouthparts to break off and remain in the...
Procedure:

1. Use fine-tipped tweezers: Grasp the tick as close to the skin's surface as possible.
2. Pull upward with steady pressure: Avoid twisting or jerking the tick, which can cause its mouthparts to break off and remain in the skin.
3. Disinfect the area after removal: Clean the bite site with soap and water or an antiseptic.
4. Save the tick if necessary: Store it in a sealed container for identification purposes if symptoms develop later.
5. Monitor for symptoms of disease: Watch for signs like fever, rash, or flu-like symptoms that may indicate Lyme disease or other tick-borne illnesses.
6. Seek medical attention if needed: If you experience severe symptoms or are unsure about the type of tick, consult a healthcare professional.

Details:
• Do not use heat, alcohol, or petroleum jelly to remove ticks as this can cause them to regurgitate and increase the risk of infection.
• Prompt removal reduces the chance of disease transmission.
tick removal,tick bite,disinfection,Lyme disease
Prevent Insect Stings from Wasps, Hornets, and Bees
Standard Source: The Complete Outdoorsmans Handbook
Precautionary Measures:

1. Avoid disturbing wasps, yellowjackets, bumblebees, and honeybees or their nests.
2. Wear neutral-colored clothing, such as off-whites, tans, and greens; avoid bright colors and black, which can anger bees.
3....
Precautionary Measures:

1. Avoid disturbing wasps, yellowjackets, bumblebees, and honeybees or their nests.
2. Wear neutral-colored clothing, such as off-whites, tans, and greens; avoid bright colors and black, which can anger bees.
3. Do not use sweet or strong-smelling perfumes or after-shave lotions, as they attract insects.
4. Keep food, especially sweet items, covered at all times. After meals, store leftover food in your tent or cooler; do not leave it on picnic tables.
5. Avoid placing wildflowers on picnic tables.
6. Wear shoes, as yellowjackets live underground and can be disturbed by bare feet.
7. Do not throw rocks or sticks at insect nests, especially wasp nests, which may contain up to 10,000 insects.
wasps,hornets,bees,sting_prevention,insect_avoidance,outdoor_safety
Treating Bee Stings with Soda or Saleratus
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure for Treating a Bee Sting:

1. If stung by a bee, apply a little common soda or saleratus (baking powder) to the affected area.
2. Moisten the soda or saleratus before applying it to the skin.
3. This will neutralize the poison...
Procedure for Treating a Bee Sting:

1. If stung by a bee, apply a little common soda or saleratus (baking powder) to the affected area.
2. Moisten the soda or saleratus before applying it to the skin.
3. This will neutralize the poison injected by the sting and relieve pain quickly.

Notes:
• Soda or saleratus is always at hand for this purpose.
• This method provides immediate relief from bee stings.
treating bee stings, soda, saleratus, first aid for bees

Pain Relief (4) -

Make Yarrow Anesthetic for Wound Relief
High Priority Source: USMC Summer Survival Course Handbook
Procedure:

1. Harvest fresh Yarrow roots.
2. Scrub the roots in water to clean them thoroughly.
3. Crush the cleaned roots into a spongy mass.
4. Apply the crushed root mixture gently to the wounded area for pain relief.

Notes:
• Do not...
Procedure:

1. Harvest fresh Yarrow roots.
2. Scrub the roots in water to clean them thoroughly.
3. Crush the cleaned roots into a spongy mass.
4. Apply the crushed root mixture gently to the wounded area for pain relief.

Notes:
• Do not confuse Yarrow with Poison Hemlock.
• This method is suitable for creating a natural anesthetic for wound treatment.
yarrow, anesthetic, pain relief, first aid
Relieve Headaches and Pain from Altitude or Injuries
Standard Source: The Complete Outdoorsmans Handbook
Objective: Alleviate headaches caused by high altitude or pain from injuries.

Materials Needed:
• Aspirin
• Empirin compound

Instructions:
1. For headaches due to high altitude, take aspirin initially for relief.
2. If aspirin...
Objective: Alleviate headaches caused by high altitude or pain from injuries.

Materials Needed:
• Aspirin
• Empirin compound

Instructions:
1. For headaches due to high altitude, take aspirin initially for relief.
2. If aspirin is not sufficient, use an Empirin compound, which is more effective for headaches and can also help with pain from sunburn, poison-ivy rash, or sprains.
3. Note: The Empirin compound has no effect on internal organ-related pain.

Notes: Most individuals adjust to high altitude after the first day or two. If symptoms persist or worsen, seek medical attention as soon as possible.
relieve altitude headaches, pain relief in the wilderness, first aid for head pain
Administer Antispasmodic Medication for Gut Cramps
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Antispasmodics are medications used to relieve cramps or spasms of the gut.

### When to Use:
• For abdominal pain caused by cramping or spasms (e.g., from irritable bowel syndrome, gas, or menstrual cramps).

### Administration Steps:
1. **Read...
Antispasmodics are medications used to relieve cramps or spasms of the gut.

### When to Use:
• For abdominal pain caused by cramping or spasms (e.g., from irritable bowel syndrome, gas, or menstrual cramps).

### Administration Steps:
1. Read the label carefully and follow dosage instructions if available.
2. Take the medication orally, with a small amount of water if possible.
3. Monitor for side effects such as drowsiness, dry mouth, or blurred vision.
4. If symptoms persist or worsen after taking the medication, seek medical help.

### Important Notes:
• Antispasmodics should not be used in cases of suspected appendicitis, intestinal obstruction, or other serious abdominal conditions without a healthcare provider’s guidance.
• Avoid using antispasmodics for prolonged periods unless directed by a healthcare professional.
antispasmodic medication, gut cramps, abdominal pain relief
Administer Ergonovine to Relax Muscles and Calm Pain Before Setting Broken Bones
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
To relax muscles and calm pain, give 10 mg. by mouth 30 minutes before setting broken bones in an adult.

Note: This is a specific use for ergonovine, not for tetanus or other conditions.
ergonovine,pain relief,bone setting,muscle relaxation

Frostbite (4) -

Treat Frostbite
Critical Source: The Complete Outdoorsmans Handbook
Procedure:

1. As soon as frostbite is recognized, thaw the affected area by applying heat.
2. For frostbite on the face, use a warm hand to gradually thaw the area.
3. Ensure the area is dry and do not rub or massage it, as this can damage...
Procedure:

1. As soon as frostbite is recognized, thaw the affected area by applying heat.
2. For frostbite on the face, use a warm hand to gradually thaw the area.
3. Ensure the area is dry and do not rub or massage it, as this can damage fragile tissues.
4. Do not apply snow to the affected area, as it may worsen the condition.
5. If a hand or foot is frozen, warm it gradually while massaging adjacent areas to improve circulation.
6. Allow the patient to drink warm fluids but avoid applying heat directly to the frostbitten part.
7. After thawing, apply an antiseptic emulsion and bandage the area.
8. Monitor for swelling, redness, or blistering after thawing and seek medical aid as soon as possible.

Notes:
• Frostbite can be extensive before it is noticed; remain alert during winter activities.
frostbite treatment, first aid, cold injury
Treatment of Frostbite
Critical Source: Aids to Survival
Procedure:

1. Do not thaw the affected area if there is a risk of re-freezing.
2. Avoid rubbing the frozen parts, as this can cause further tissue damage.
3. Thaw rapidly and completely in warm water (40º to 42ºC).
4. **Protect the...
Procedure:

1. Do not thaw the affected area if there is a risk of re-freezing.
2. Avoid rubbing the frozen parts, as this can cause further tissue damage.
3. Thaw rapidly and completely in warm water (40º to 42ºC).
4. Protect the thawed regions from further cold exposure or injury.
5. Do not break any blisters that may form on the affected area.
6. Keep the entire body warm to promote circulation and prevent further heat loss.
7. Monitor for signs of infection or complications, such as swelling or pain.
frostbite, treatment, first aid, cold injury, thawing
First Aid for Frostbite (Protection of Toes/Feet/Fingers/Hands)
High Priority Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Objective: Reduce the risk of losing toes, feet, fingers, or hands due to frostbite.

Procedure:
1. Recognize signs of frostbite: Look for numbness, tingling, or a pale or waxy appearance on the skin.
2. Avoid further exposure: Move...
Objective: Reduce the risk of losing toes, feet, fingers, or hands due to frostbite.

Procedure:
1. Recognize signs of frostbite: Look for numbness, tingling, or a pale or waxy appearance on the skin.
2. Avoid further exposure: Move the casualty to a warmer environment immediately and protect them from wind and cold.
3. Do not rub the affected area with snow or any rough material, as this can cause more damage.
4. Warm the affected area gradually using body heat (e.g., placing hands or feet against the chest) or by wrapping in warm clothing or blankets.
5. Avoid direct heat sources such as fire, hot water, or heating pads, which can burn the skin and worsen the injury.
6. Keep the affected area dry: If the skin is wet from melting ice, gently pat it dry with a clean cloth.
7. Monitor for signs of worsening frostbite, such as blistering, blackened skin, or severe pain, and seek immediate medical attention.
8. Evacuate to an MTF (Medical Treatment Facility) as soon as possible for further evaluation and treatment.
frostbite, first aid, cold injury, toes, feet, fingers, hands
Recognition of Frostbite
High Priority Source: Aids to Survival
Procedure:

1. Look for pain in extremities (fingers, toes, ears, nose).
2. Check for failure of skin sensation, such as numbness or tingling.
3. Observe if the skin does not move freely over toes and knuckles due to stiffness or...
Procedure:

1. Look for pain in extremities (fingers, toes, ears, nose).
2. Check for failure of skin sensation, such as numbness or tingling.
3. Observe if the skin does not move freely over toes and knuckles due to stiffness or rigidity.
4. If any of these signs are present, suspect frostbite and initiate treatment immediately.
frostbite, recognition, symptoms, first aid

Shock (4) -

Treat Shock
Critical Source: The Complete Outdoorsmans Handbook
Procedure:

1. Lay the victim on their back with their head low and turned to one side (unless there is an injury to the head or chest).
2. Reassure the patient and loosen clothing around the neck, chest, and waist.
3. Wrap the patient in...
Procedure:

1. Lay the victim on their back with their head low and turned to one side (unless there is an injury to the head or chest).
2. Reassure the patient and loosen clothing around the neck, chest, and waist.
3. Wrap the patient in something warm to maintain body temperature.
4. If the patient complains of thirst, give them sips of water, tea, or another non-alcoholic liquid.
5. Do not apply heat or friction to the limbs.
6. Seek medical aid immediately.

Notes:
• Shock can occur with many injuries and is a life-threatening condition that requires prompt treatment.
shock treatment, first aid, emergency response
Do Not Give Food or Drink to a Casualty in Shock
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Purpose: Prevent choking and further complications by not giving food or drink to a casualty in shock.

Steps:
1. Do not give the casualty any food or drink.
2. If you must leave the casualty or if he is unconscious, **turn his head to...
Purpose: Prevent choking and further complications by not giving food or drink to a casualty in shock.

Steps:
1. Do not give the casualty any food or drink.
2. If you must leave the casualty or if he is unconscious, turn his head to the side to prevent him from choking if he vomits.
no food or drink in shock, choking prevention, first aid for shock
Maintain Body Temperature of a Casualty in Shock
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Purpose: Prevent hypothermia or hyperthermia by maintaining the casualty’s body temperature.

Steps:
1. In cold weather: Place a blanket or similar item over the casualty to keep him warm and under him to prevent chilling. If a...
Purpose: Prevent hypothermia or hyperthermia by maintaining the casualty’s body temperature.

Steps:
1. In cold weather: Place a blanket or similar item over the casualty to keep him warm and under him to prevent chilling. If a tourniquet has been applied, leave it exposed if possible.
2. In hot weather: Place the casualty in the shade and protect him from becoming chilled; avoid excessive use of blankets or coverings.
body temperature maintenance, shock first aid, hypothermia prevention
Calm a Casualty in Shock and Provide Reassurance
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Steps to calm the casualty:

• Reassure the casualty throughout the entire procedure of providing first aid.
• Be authoritative and show self-confidence to help keep the casualty calm.
• **Assure the casualty that you are there to help...
Steps to calm the casualty:

• Reassure the casualty throughout the entire procedure of providing first aid.
• Be authoritative and show self-confidence to help keep the casualty calm.
• Assure the casualty that you are there to help him.
calming casualty in shock, psychological first aid for shock

Infections (4) -

Manage Herpes Labialis (Cold Sores) with Symptom Control and Antivirals
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Herpes labialis is a viral infection causing cold sores on the lips, tongue, gingiva, or palate. It may be triggered by sunburn, stress, or trauma.

### Management:
1. Administer oral anti-inflammatories and pain medications (e.g.,...
Herpes labialis is a viral infection causing cold sores on the lips, tongue, gingiva, or palate. It may be triggered by sunburn, stress, or trauma.

### Management:
1. Administer oral anti-inflammatories and pain medications (e.g., ibuprofen, acetaminophen).
2. Maintain a soft diet to avoid irritation.
3. Apply a mouth rinse made of equal parts Maalox, Benadryl liquid, and viscous lidocaine, swish and spit every 2 hours as needed for pain relief.
4. Use antiviral medications such as:
- Acyclovir 200 mg 5x/day to shorten the duration of the outbreak.
herpes labialis,cold sores,antivirals,mouth rinse
Manage Oral Candidiasis (Thrush) with Antifungal Treatments and Hygiene
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Oral candidiasis is a fungal infection caused by overgrowth of yeast in the mouth. It appears as white patches that can be rubbed off, leaving red, sore areas.

### Management:
1. Eliminate sources of reinfection:
- Wash toothbrushes in...
Oral candidiasis is a fungal infection caused by overgrowth of yeast in the mouth. It appears as white patches that can be rubbed off, leaving red, sore areas.

### Management:
1. Eliminate sources of reinfection:
- Wash toothbrushes in boiling water and air dry them.
2. Apply topical antifungals, such as:
- Nystatin swish & spit 5x/day
- Mycelex troche 4x/day
3. Use a vaginal yeast cream (e.g., clotrimazole) rubbed into the mouth and gums 4x/day.
4. Administer oral antifungals, such as:
- Diflucan 100 mg tablets 2x/day until symptoms resolve.
oral candidiasis,thrush,topical antifungals,vaginal yeast cream
Treating Thrush in Mother and Baby
Standard Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure for Treating Thrush (Yeast Infection):

• Prepare a gentian violet solution at 0.25% strength by mixing 1 teaspoon of 1% gentian violet with 3 teaspoons of water.

• Use a clean cloth or finger to **apply the solution once daily...
Procedure for Treating Thrush (Yeast Infection):

• Prepare a gentian violet solution at 0.25% strength by mixing 1 teaspoon of 1% gentian violet with 3 teaspoons of water.

• Use a clean cloth or finger to apply the solution once daily for 5 days, painting both the mother’s nipples and the white spots in the baby’s mouth.

• The treatment may cause purple staining on clothing, the baby’s mouth, and the mother’s nipples — this is normal.

• Continue breastfeeding during treatment unless symptoms persist beyond 3 days without improvement.

• If there is no improvement after 3 days of treatment, discontinue gentian violet and seek medical advice for further management.
thrush,yeast infection,gentian violet,breastfeeding
Administer Sitz Bath for Yeast Infection Relief
Standard Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

To help the woman feel better from a yeast infection:

• Sit in a pan of clean, warm water for 15 minutes as often as possible.
• This is soothing to the genitals and will speed healing.
• The woman should not have...
Procedure:

To help the woman feel better from a yeast infection:

• Sit in a pan of clean, warm water for 15 minutes as often as possible.
• This is soothing to the genitals and will speed healing.
• The woman should not have sex until she and her partner are finished with treatment and all signs are gone.
sitz bath,yeast infection,relief,warm water

General Procedures (4) -

Monitor Casualty and Seek Medical Aid
Critical Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure for Monitoring the Casualty and Seeking Medical Aid:

1. Watch the casualty closely for life-threatening conditions, such as worsening respiratory distress, signs of shock, or changes in level of consciousness.
2. **Check for other...
Procedure for Monitoring the Casualty and Seeking Medical Aid:

1. Watch the casualty closely for life-threatening conditions, such as worsening respiratory distress, signs of shock, or changes in level of consciousness.
2. Check for other injuries if necessary to ensure a comprehensive assessment.
3. Treat for shock by positioning the casualty appropriately and providing warmth if needed.
4. Seek medical aid immediately after performing initial interventions such as needle chest decompression or dressing application.

Important Notes:
• Continuous monitoring is essential to detect any deterioration in the casualty's condition.
• Prompt medical assistance can significantly improve outcomes in critical situations.
monitoring casualty, shock treatment, medical aid, first aid
Notify Medical Personnel and Monitor for Complications
High Priority Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
k. Seek Medical Assistance. Notify medical personnel, watch closely for development of life-threatening conditions and/or impaired circulation to the injured extremity. (Refer to Chapter 1 for additional information on life-threatening...
k. Seek Medical Assistance. Notify medical personnel, watch closely for development of life-threatening conditions and/or impaired circulation to the injured extremity. (Refer to Chapter 1 for additional information on life-threatening conditions).
medical assistance,life-threatening conditions,circulation monitoring
Principles of First Aid
High Priority Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Procedure:

1. Assess the situation: Evaluate the environment and potential hazards.
2. Make the area safe: Ensure that both you and the casualty are not in immediate danger.
3. Assess the casualty:
- Start with the ABC of...
Procedure:

1. Assess the situation: Evaluate the environment and potential hazards.
2. Make the area safe: Ensure that both you and the casualty are not in immediate danger.
3. Assess the casualty:
- Start with the ABC of resuscitation (Airway, Breathing, Circulation).
- Identify any injury or illness.
4. Provide appropriate treatment: Administer care in a logical order of priority.
5. Document and report: Make a clear record of the casualty’s condition and actions taken.
6. Organize removal to secondary care if necessary: Ensure proper evacuation for further medical attention.
principles of first aid, casualty assessment, treatment priority, documentation, evacuation
Identify and Reference Field Manuals for First Aid
Standard Source: STP 21 24 Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 4
Objective: Locate and reference field manuals related to first aid.

Procedure:
1. Identify the relevant field manual: FM 4-25.11 (FM 21-11).
2. Verify the publication date: 23 December 2002.
3. Note associated references: {NTRP...
Objective: Locate and reference field manuals related to first aid.

Procedure:
1. Identify the relevant field manual: FM 4-25.11 (FM 21-11).
2. Verify the publication date: 23 December 2002.
3. Note associated references: {NTRP 4-02.1.1; AFMAN 44-163(I); MCRP 3-02G}.
4. Use this manual for first aid procedures and techniques.

Details: This manual provides guidance on providing immediate medical care in the field, including wound management, shock treatment, and casualty evacuation.
first aid, field manual, FM 4-25.11

General Guidelines (4) -

Do Nothing in Doubtful Situations
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Procedure:

1. If unsure about the best course of action in a medical situation, avoid taking any unnecessary steps that could cause harm.
2. Monitor the patient's condition and wait for symptoms to resolve or worsen.
3. Avoid administering...
Procedure:

1. If unsure about the best course of action in a medical situation, avoid taking any unnecessary steps that could cause harm.
2. Monitor the patient's condition and wait for symptoms to resolve or worsen.
3. Avoid administering medications or treatments without proper knowledge or training.
4. Seek professional help if the situation does not improve or becomes worse.

Note: Masterful inactivity can save lives by preventing further complications from incorrect interventions.
do nothing, avoid harm, medical uncertainty
Purpose of This Manual
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: The purpose of this manual is to guide First Aiders in providing life-saving care during armed conflicts and other violent situations.

Key Points:
• Armed conflicts are common and their nature is changing, making it essential...
Objective: The purpose of this manual is to guide First Aiders in providing life-saving care during armed conflicts and other violent situations.

Key Points:
• Armed conflicts are common and their nature is changing, making it essential for First Aiders to be prepared.
• First Aid is a critical activity that helps save lives and provide assistance to casualties.
• The ICRC has developed expertise in pre-hospital and surgical treatment of the wounded in conflict zones.
• National Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies work together with the ICRC to provide global First Aid support.

Impact:
• Early administration of life-saving measures can prevent death, disability, and complications.
• In many cases, simple interventions (e.g., antibiotic and painkiller) are sufficient for over 50% of civilian casualties in urban fighting scenarios.
• These interventions should be provided on-site rather than requiring hospitalization.

Community Role:
• First Aid training and daily activities help communities prepare effectively for emergencies.
• Involving local people ensures that programs meet community needs, respect cultural beliefs, and promote solidarity.
purpose, first aid manual, armed conflict, icrc, red cross, red crescent, emergency care
Understanding the Audience of This Manual
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Purpose: This section explains who this manual is intended for.

Audience:
• Well-trained Red Cross and Red Crescent First Aiders working in armed conflicts and other situations of violence.
• Military stretcher-bearers and medics in...
Purpose: This section explains who this manual is intended for.

Audience:
• Well-trained Red Cross and Red Crescent First Aiders working in armed conflicts and other situations of violence.
• Military stretcher-bearers and medics in advanced First Aid posts.
• Military and civilian health professionals in field or district hospitals with limited surgical capabilities.
• Everyone, as the manual emphasizes that anyone has the potential to save lives.

Importance: The manual aims to provide life-saving skills to a wide range of individuals who may be involved in providing first aid during conflicts.
audience, manual, first aid, armed conflict, red cross, red crescent
What is Covered in This Manual
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Scope: This manual provides guidance on the role of a First Aider during armed conflicts and other violent situations.

Key Topics:
• Understanding your responsibilities as a First Aider.
• Decision-making and actions during emergencies.
-...
Scope: This manual provides guidance on the role of a First Aider during armed conflicts and other violent situations.

Key Topics:
• Understanding your responsibilities as a First Aider.
• Decision-making and actions during emergencies.
• Knowledge of the distinctive emblems, Fundamental Principles of the International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement, and Geneva Conventions.
• Rights and duties of First Aiders in armed conflict.

Values Emphasized:
• The manual promotes solidarity, generosity, and altruism among individuals and communities.
• It reinforces the importance of community spirit and citizenship through humanitarian actions.
• While approaches may vary slightly in violent situations, most procedures are similar to those used daily by First Aiders.
manual content, first aider role, red cross principles, geneva conventions, emergency response

Emergency Response (4) -

Establish Unresponsiveness and Call for Help
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Gently tap the casualty’s shoulder and shout, “Are you okay?” to check for responsiveness.
2. If there is no response, immediately call for help (e.g., 911 or local emergency services).
3. While waiting for assistance, position...
Procedure:

1. Gently tap the casualty’s shoulder and shout, “Are you okay?” to check for responsiveness.
2. If there is no response, immediately call for help (e.g., 911 or local emergency services).
3. While waiting for assistance, position the casualty on their back on a firm, flat surface.
4. Ensure that the airway is open using either the head-tilt/chin-lift or jaw-thrust method, depending on suspected injuries.

Notes:
• This step should be performed as soon as possible to ensure timely medical intervention.
• If you are alone and need to perform rescue breathing, call for help first before beginning any procedures.
unresponsiveness, call for help, emergency response, first aid
Immediate Actions at the Rescue Scene
Critical Source: FM 3 97 61 Military Mountaineering
Overview: Immediate actions are critical to ensure the safety of both the victim and rescuers.

Steps to Take Immediately:
1. Assume command: One person, and only one person, must be in overall charge at all times.
2. **Prevent further...
Overview: Immediate actions are critical to ensure the safety of both the victim and rescuers.

Steps to Take Immediately:
1. Assume command: One person, and only one person, must be in overall charge at all times.
2. Prevent further injuries: Use reasonable care when reaching the victim to avoid causing additional harm to them or others.
3. Ensure basic life support:
- Ensure the victim has an open airway.
- Resume breathing if necessary.
- Control serious bleeding.
- Maintain moderate body warmth.
- If unconscious, continuously monitor pulse and protect from environmental hazards.
4. Do not move the victim: Unless it is necessary to prevent further injury or the victim is in a dangerous location (e.g., avalanche run-out zone, hanging glacier, possibility of falling rocks).
5. Pause and assess: Do nothing more until you have thoroughly considered the situation. Speed is less important than correct action.
rescue scene,immediate actions,victim care,first aid
Situation Management in Emergencies
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Steps to Manage an Emergency Situation:

1. Survey the Scene: Assess the environment for potential dangers (e.g., fire, unstable structures, ongoing violence) before entering.
2. Intervene Safely and Securely: Ensure your own safety...
Steps to Manage an Emergency Situation:

1. Survey the Scene: Assess the environment for potential dangers (e.g., fire, unstable structures, ongoing violence) before entering.
2. Intervene Safely and Securely: Ensure your own safety first. Use protective equipment if available and avoid unnecessary risks to yourself or others.
3. Assess, Decide, and Act:
- Quickly evaluate the condition of casualties (e.g., breathing, bleeding, level of consciousness).
- Make decisions based on the severity of injuries and available resources.
- Take immediate action to provide life-saving interventions (e.g., stop bleeding, ensure airway patency, immobilize fractures).
situation management, emergency response, first aid steps, safety protocols
Four Life Saving Steps in Emergency Situations
Reference Source: USMC Winter Survival Course Handbook + USMC Summer Survival Course Handbook
Steps to take in a medical emergency:

1. Start the breathing – Ensure the person is breathing properly and provide CPR or artificial respiration if necessary.
2. Stop the bleeding – Apply direct pressure to the wound using a clean cloth...
Steps to take in a medical emergency:

1. Start the breathing – Ensure the person is breathing properly and provide CPR or artificial respiration if necessary.
2. Stop the bleeding – Apply direct pressure to the wound using a clean cloth or bandage to control blood loss.
3. Treat the wound – Cleanse the wound thoroughly with water and apply an antiseptic to prevent infection.
4. Check for shock – Monitor the person's breathing, pulse, and level of consciousness. If signs of shock are present (e.g., pale skin, rapid heartbeat), lay them down flat and elevate their legs if possible.
CPR, artificial respiration, bleeding control, wound treatment, shock management

Training Guidelines (4) -

Disclaimer and Scope of the Manual
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Overview:
This manual contains general guidelines for First Aiders working in armed conflicts and other situations of violence. It cannot cover all possible scenarios.

Important Notes:
• The ICRC declines responsibility if the...
Overview:
This manual contains general guidelines for First Aiders working in armed conflicts and other situations of violence. It cannot cover all possible scenarios.

Important Notes:
• The ICRC declines responsibility if the recommendations do not align with the best course of action in a specific situation.
• This manual is gender-neutral, unless otherwise stated.
• Use of trade or brand names is for illustrative purposes only and does not imply endorsement by the ICRC.
• Illustrations are for indicative value and should be adapted to local requirements if necessary.
disclaimer, scope, icrc, gender-neutral, illustrations
Feedback and Updates for the First Aid Manual
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Overview:
This manual will be updated over time as new discoveries, inventions, or innovations impact first aid practices in armed conflicts and other situations of violence.

Action Steps:
1. Send comments and suggestions to the...
Overview:
This manual will be updated over time as new discoveries, inventions, or innovations impact first aid practices in armed conflicts and other situations of violence.

Action Steps:
1. Send comments and suggestions to the International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) Assistance Division – First Aid Manual at:
- Address: 19 Avenue de la Paix, CH-1202 Geneva, Switzerland
- Fax: +41 22 733 96 74
- Email: firstaidmanual.gva@icrc.org

Note: The manual is a living document and will be continuously improved based on user feedback.
feedback, updates, first aid manual, icrc, improvements
How to Use This First Aid Manual in Armed Conflicts
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Overview:
This manual is a supplement to training programs for First Aiders and health professionals. It should be used alongside:

1. Consideration of local characteristics of communities, armed conflicts, and other situations of...
Overview:
This manual is a supplement to training programs for First Aiders and health professionals. It should be used alongside:

1. Consideration of local characteristics of communities, armed conflicts, and other situations of violence.
2. Awareness and training sessions for National Society staff, volunteers, and local communities where relevant.
3. Field tests and regular rehearsals, ideally with all parties involved (local communities, army, civil defense, NGOs, etc.).

These efforts should:
• Ensure the involvement and participation of people concerned.
• Go beyond simple translation into local languages.
• Create opportunities to develop or strengthen the organizational and operational setup of a National Society as part of a national plan for conflict and disaster preparedness and response.

Note: This manual is not an end in itself but should be used in conjunction with practical training, field testing, and community engagement.
first aid manual, armed conflicts, training guidelines, field use, local communities
Instructor Guidelines for Cric-Key Training Exercise
Standard Source: TCCC 17 13 Tactical Combat Casualty Care
Objective: Provide instructors with guidelines to evaluate students on the performance of an emergency surgical cricothyroidotomy using the Cric-Key.

Materials Required:
• Student checklist
• Surgical cricothyroidotomy simulator
• All...
Objective: Provide instructors with guidelines to evaluate students on the performance of an emergency surgical cricothyroidotomy using the Cric-Key.

Materials Required:
• Student checklist
• Surgical cricothyroidotomy simulator
• All materials listed in the student procedure (see above)

Instructor Responsibilities:
1. Provide checklists: Ensure each student has a student checklist to document their performance during the exercise.
2. Verify equipment availability: Confirm that all required materials are available and functional for the training session.
3. Explain objectives and evaluation method: Clearly communicate the learning objectives, procedure steps, and evaluation criteria to students before beginning the exercise.
4. Clarify grading standards: Explain how the student's performance will be graded (pass or fail) based on their ability to perform the cricothyroidotomy correctly using the Cric-Key.
5. Observe and evaluate: During the training session, observe each student’s technique and use the checklist to assess accuracy and completeness of the procedure.
6. Provide feedback: After evaluation, provide constructive feedback to students on their performance and areas for improvement.
instructor guidelines,Cric-Key training,evaluation criteria,tactical combat casualty care,airway management training

Communication Reporting (4) -

Report Casualty Assessment and Next Steps
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

1. Assess casualties by reporting the number of injured individuals and their condition.
