1. Wash the thermometer: Wash the thermometer in soap and rinse with cool, clean water before and after each use.

2. Avoid hot water: Do not use hot water as it may cause the thermometer to break.

3. Soak in alcohol:...
Procedure:

1. Wash the thermometer: Wash the thermometer in soap and rinse with cool, clean water before and after each use.

2. Avoid hot water: Do not use hot water as it may cause the thermometer to break.

3. Soak in alcohol: After washing, soak the thermometer in alcohol (ethyl, isopropyl, or medical alcohol) for 20 minutes.

4. Rinse before reuse: Rinse the thermometer in clean water before using it again.

5. Do not reuse alcohol: The alcohol used for soaking should not be reused.
thermometer,cleaning,sterilizing,alcohol,cool water
Cleaning and Sterilizing a Mucus Bulb (Bulb Syringe)
Standard Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

1. Wash with soapy water: Fill the mucus bulb with soapy water and squeeze out the water several times to clean it.

2. Rinse thoroughly: Rinse the bulb syringe well in clean water after washing.

3. **Sterilization by...
Procedure:

1. Wash with soapy water: Fill the mucus bulb with soapy water and squeeze out the water several times to clean it.

2. Rinse thoroughly: Rinse the bulb syringe well in clean water after washing.

3. Sterilization by boiling:
- Ensure that water enters the inside of the bulb before boiling.
- After boiling, squeeze out all the water from the bulb to prevent contamination.
mucus bulb,bulb syringe,cleaning,sterilization,boiling

Infection Prevention (3) -

Steps to Take If Staying at Home During Labor
High Priority Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

• Give the mother antibiotics to prevent infection.
• Watch her closely for signs of infection.
• If you have a thermometer, take her temperature every 4 hours.
• If any signs of infection develop, **get medical help...
Procedure:

• Give the mother antibiotics to prevent infection.
• Watch her closely for signs of infection.
• If you have a thermometer, take her temperature every 4 hours.
• If any signs of infection develop, get medical help immediately.
• See page 179 for more on infection in labor and how to treat it.
antibiotics, infection signs, temperature check, home care, labor at home
Sterilize Tools and Maintain Hygiene During Birth
High Priority Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

When you arrive at the birth, ensure that all of your tools are sterilized (see page 59). This is critical for infection prevention.

### Tools That Must Be Sterile:
• All tools that go inside the vagina or cut the skin must be...
Procedure:

When you arrive at the birth, ensure that all of your tools are sterilized (see page 59). This is critical for infection prevention.

### Tools That Must Be Sterile:
• All tools that go inside the vagina or cut the skin must be sterile. These include:
- Gloves
- Razor or scissors used to cut the cord
- Scissors used for an episiotomy (cutting the birth opening)

### Hygiene Practices During Labor:
1. Wash your hands often during labor.
2. Clip your fingernails short to prevent injury and infection.
3. Wear clean clothes at all times.
4. Use a clean apron to keep blood and fluids off of you.

Note: Proper sterilization and hygiene are essential for the safety of both mother and baby.
sterilize tools, hand washing, infection prevention, birth hygiene
Preventing Infection in Wounds
Standard Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Purpose: To reduce the risk of infection following an injury.

Procedure:
1. Clean all wounds with antiseptic solution.
2. Remove any foreign material from the wound if possible.
3. Cover the wound with a non-stick dressing to...
Purpose: To reduce the risk of infection following an injury.

Procedure:
1. Clean all wounds with antiseptic solution.
2. Remove any foreign material from the wound if possible.
3. Cover the wound with a non-stick dressing to prevent further contamination.
4. Bandage securely to hold the dressing in place.
5. If foreign bodies are deeply embedded, leave them in place for removal by a surgeon.
6. Clean around the embedded object and dress carefully.
7. Inspect wounds at least daily during expeditions, replacing dressings as needed.
8. If dressings stick to the wound, soak them first before removing to avoid damaging healing tissue.
9. Ensure all expedition members are up-to-date with tetanus immunisation prior to travel.
wound infection prevention, antiseptic cleaning, foreign body removal, tetanus immunisation

Neonatal Resuscitation (3) -

Suction Baby's Mouth and Nose with a Suction Trap or Bulb Syringe
Critical Source: A Book for Midwives
If the baby needs help, you must suction his mouth and throat before he breathes. The best way to suction the baby is to use a suction trap, but you can also use a bulb syringe (see the next page). Whatever you use must be sterile...
If the baby needs help, you must suction his mouth and throat before he breathes. The best way to suction the baby is to use a suction trap, but you can also use a bulb syringe (see the next page). Whatever you use must be sterile (see page 59 for how to sterilize tools).

### To Use a Suction Trap:
1. Put the tube that goes to the bottom of the jar in the baby’s mouth. It should go no more than 10 centimeters (4 inches) down the baby’s throat.
2. Suck on the other tube while you wiggle the first tube around in the baby’s mouth. The fluid in the baby’s mouth or nose will go into the jar but not into your mouth.
3. After cleaning the baby’s mouth, clean the baby’s nose in the same way.

### To Use a Bulb Syringe:
1. Sterilize a bulb syringe before you use it.
2. Suction the mouth and throat until they are clear of mucus.
3. Then suction the nose.
4. Practice using the syringe to suck up water before you use it at a birth.

Important:
• Release the syringe as you wiggle it around. The bulb will suck up mucus.
• Do not squeeze the syringe while it is in the baby’s mouth or throat.
• Squeeze out the mucus after removing the syringe.
suction trap, bulb syringe, neonatal suctioning, first aid, medical emergency
Make a Suction Trap for Neonatal Care
High Priority Source: A Book for Midwives
Materials needed:
• A small jar
• A stopper that fits snugly into the top of the jar
• Very thin, soft tubing that can be cleaned easily

Steps to make a suction trap:
1. Sterilize the tubing before and after you use it.
2. Make 2 holes...
Materials needed:
• A small jar
• A stopper that fits snugly into the top of the jar
• Very thin, soft tubing that can be cleaned easily

Steps to make a suction trap:
1. Sterilize the tubing before and after you use it.
2. Make 2 holes in the stopper. The holes should be just big enough to push the tubing through.
3. Push one tube through the hole until it is just below the stopper.
4. Push the second tube through until it almost touches the bottom of the jar.
suction trap, make suction trap, neonatal care, first aid
Tie a Square Knot for Securing the Umbilical Cord
High Priority Source: A Book for Midwives
How to tie a square knot:
1. Make the first loop of a square knot by bringing one end over the other.
2. Make the second loop by bringing the opposite end over the first.
3. Pull both ends tight to secure the knot.

Important: A...
How to tie a square knot:
1. Make the first loop of a square knot by bringing one end over the other.
2. Make the second loop by bringing the opposite end over the first.
3. Pull both ends tight to secure the knot.

Important: A square knot is secure and tight, and should be used for tying the umbilical cord.
square knot, tie umbilical cord, first aid, neonatal care

Reproductive Health (3) -

Provide Immediate Care for a Woman with Complications After an Abortion
Critical Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
If a woman shows signs of complications after an abortion:

1. Try to control bleeding:
- Follow instructions on page 281 for bleeding after miscarriage.
- Give ergonovine (see page 390) to help reduce bleeding.

2. **Treat for...
If a woman shows signs of complications after an abortion:

1. Try to control bleeding:
- Follow instructions on page 281 for bleeding after miscarriage.
- Give ergonovine (see page 390) to help reduce bleeding.

2. Treat for shock:
- Refer to page 77 for steps on treating shock, including keeping the woman warm and lying down with her legs elevated if possible.

3. Treat signs of infection:
- If there are signs of infection (e.g., fever), give antibiotics as described for Childbirth Fever (see page 276).

Note: This care should be provided while waiting for professional medical help, which is essential in these cases.
abortion,bleeding control,shock treatment,infection treatment,ergonovine
Provide Care for a Woman After an Abortion
High Priority Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
For more information on caring for a woman after an abortion:

• Refer to _A Book for Midwives_ for detailed guidance.

Important considerations:
- Women who have had abortions, whether legal or not, should be treated with respect and...
For more information on caring for a woman after an abortion:

• Refer to _A Book for Midwives_ for detailed guidance.

Important considerations:
- Women who have had abortions, whether legal or not, should be treated with respect and without judgment by health workers and the community.
- Delayed care due to fear, shame, or stigma can lead to severe complications or death. It is crucial that women feel safe seeking help immediately if they experience any signs of problems after an abortion.
post-abortion care,midwife guidance,respectful treatment,community support
Using Condoms for Men (Rubber or Prophylactic)
Standard Source: A Book for Midwives
Purpose: To prevent pregnancy and protect against sexually transmitted infections (STIs), including HIV.

Materials Needed:
• New condom made from latex or polyurethane (not sheepskin)
• Water-based lubricant (e.g., saliva, K-Y Jelly,...
Purpose: To prevent pregnancy and protect against sexually transmitted infections (STIs), including HIV.

Materials Needed:
• New condom made from latex or polyurethane (not sheepskin)
• Water-based lubricant (e.g., saliva, K-Y Jelly, spermicide)

Steps to Use Condoms Correctly:
1. Choose the Right Condom: Select a new condom made from latex or polyurethane — not sheepskin.
2. Use a New Condom Each Time: A new condom must be used each time a couple has sex.
3. Apply Lubricant Properly:
- Use a water-based lubricant like saliva, K-Y Jelly, or spermicide.
- Do not use oil-based products (e.g., petroleum jelly, Vaseline, skin lotions, or butter), as they can cause the condom to break.
4. Apply Lubricant Inside the Tip:
- Apply a drop of lubricant inside the tip of the condom to make it more comfortable on the penis.
5. Apply Lubricant on the Outside (Optional):
- A little lubricant can also be rubbed on the outside of the condom after the man puts it on.

Important Notes:
• Condoms are most effective when used with spermicide and lubricant.
• Condoms may be less effective if the woman is on top during sex.
• Always ensure the condom is worn correctly to prevent breakage or slippage.
condom use,latex,polyurethane,spermicide,lubricant,correct condom application,stis,hiv

Cpr (3) -

CPR for Infants (Under 1 Year of Age)
Critical Source: Family Emergency Handbook
Procedure for Performing CPR on an Infant

• Maintain an open airway by gently tilting the head back slightly and lifting the chin.
• Make a tight seal over the mouth and nose with your mouth when giving rescue breaths.
• Use **small...
Procedure for Performing CPR on an Infant

• Maintain an open airway by gently tilting the head back slightly and lifting the chin.
• Make a tight seal over the mouth and nose with your mouth when giving rescue breaths.
• Use small breaths when giving rescue breaths.
• If the airway is obstructed, only remove a foreign object if it is visualized (seen in the mouth or throat).
• Place two to three fingers on the sternum, one finger's width below the nipple line.
• Compress the chest 0.5 to 1 inch (1.3 cm to 2.5 cm) at a rate of about 100 times per minute.
• Keep your fingers on the sternum during the upstroke to ensure proper compression depth and prevent injury.
• Ensure complete chest relaxation on the upstroke for full recoil.
• Allow for deflation between breaths to ensure proper chest recoil.

Important Notes:
• This procedure is specific to infants under 1 year of age.
• Always use gentle pressure and avoid using your fingers in a way that could cause injury.
• If you are unsure about any step, call emergency services immediately.
CPR, infants, first aid, emergency, chest compressions, rescue breaths
CPR for Small Children (1 to 8 Years of Age)
Critical Source: Family Emergency Handbook
Procedure for Performing CPR on a Child

• Use small breaths when giving rescue breaths.
• Keep the airway open by tilting the head back slightly and lifting the chin.
• If the airway is obstructed, only remove a foreign object if it...
Procedure for Performing CPR on a Child

• Use small breaths when giving rescue breaths.
• Keep the airway open by tilting the head back slightly and lifting the chin.
• If the airway is obstructed, only remove a foreign object if it is visualized (seen in the mouth or throat).
• Seal the mouth and nose properly when giving rescue breaths.
• Use the heel of one hand to perform chest compressions.
• Compress the chest 1 to 1.5 inches (2.5 cm to 3.8 cm) at a rate of about 100 times per minute.
• After every ten compressions, give one breath.
• Allow for deflation between breaths to ensure proper chest recoil.
• Continue until help arrives or the child shows signs of life.

Important Notes:
• This procedure is specific to children aged 1 to 8 years old.
• Always ensure that you are performing compressions correctly and not causing injury.
• If you are unsure about any step, call emergency services immediately.
CPR, children, first aid, emergency, chest compressions, rescue breaths
Provide Basic Life Support (BLS) for Collapse
Critical Source: Aids to Survival
Procedure:

Steps to follow when someone collapses:
1. Check response: Shake the person gently and shout to them.
2. If conscious, make them comfortable and observe their airway, breathing, and circulation.
3. If unconscious,...
Procedure:

Steps to follow when someone collapses:
1. Check response: Shake the person gently and shout to them.
2. If conscious, make them comfortable and observe their airway, breathing, and circulation.
3. If unconscious, turn them on their side into the recovery position.
4. Observe for signs of breathing and clear the airway if necessary (head tilt, jaw support).
5. If not breathing, place the person on their back and begin ear-to-ear artificial respiration (EAR) with 5 breaths in 10 seconds.
6. Check for a pulse; if present, continue EAR and check breathing and pulse every 2 minutes.
7. If no pulse, begin cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) immediately, following the ECC guidelines.
8. Continue CPR until help arrives or the person begins to breathe on their own.
CPR,BLS,collapse,resuscitation,emergency

Spinal Injury (3) -

Immobilize Spinal Fractures to Prevent Further Damage
Critical Source: Aids to Survival
Procedure:

Treatment of spinal fractures:
1. Assess the situation and ensure your own safety.
2. Do not move the patient unless absolutely necessary (e.g., immediate danger).
3. Immobilise the spine using improvised padding to...
Procedure:

Treatment of spinal fractures:
1. Assess the situation and ensure your own safety.
2. Do not move the patient unless absolutely necessary (e.g., immediate danger).
3. Immobilise the spine using improvised padding to maintain body position.
4. Strap the legs together if possible to prevent movement.
5. Keep the head straight and in extension to ensure an open airway and minimize spinal cord damage.
6. Avoid any twisting or bending of the neck or back during transport.
spinal injury,immobilization,airway,emergency
Perform a C-Spine Roll During Swift Water Rescue
Critical Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Objective: Safely perform a C-spine roll to extract an unconscious victim from swift water while minimizing spinal injury.

Steps:
1. Ensure the victim is in a stable position and not in immediate danger of being swept away.
2....
Objective: Safely perform a C-spine roll to extract an unconscious victim from swift water while minimizing spinal injury.

Steps:
1. Ensure the victim is in a stable position and not in immediate danger of being swept away.
2. Maintain in-line stabilization of the head, neck, and spine throughout the process.
3. Use your body to support the victim’s weight while performing the roll.
4. Gently rotate the victim onto their side, keeping their head aligned with their spine.
5. Continue to stabilize the C-spine as you move the victim toward shore or a secure location.
6. Once on stable ground, continue to provide spinal immobilization until professional medical help arrives.

Details:
• This technique is critical when dealing with an unconscious or injured victim in swift water.
• Always use proper body mechanics and avoid twisting the victim’s head or neck during the roll.
c-spine roll, spinal injury, swift water rescue
Identify Suspected Cervical Spine Injury
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

Look for the following signs of suspected cervical spine injury:

• Blunt injury above the collar bone (clavicles) with or without loss of consciousness;
• Conscious casualty complains of neck pain or difficulty feeling/moving...
Procedure:

Look for the following signs of suspected cervical spine injury:

• Blunt injury above the collar bone (clavicles) with or without loss of consciousness;
• Conscious casualty complains of neck pain or difficulty feeling/moving one or both arms;
• Penetrating neck wound.

If any of these are present, assume a cervical spine injury and take precautions to stabilize the head and neck during transport.
cervical spine injury, neck trauma, spinal cord injury

Burn Care (3) -

White Phosphorus Burn Dressing Protocol
Critical Source: Emergency War Surgery
White Phosphorus Burn Dressing Protocol

• Dress white phosphorus-injured patients with saline-soaked dressings to prevent reignition of the phosphorus by contact with air.
• Ensure that the dressing is thoroughly soaked in saline and...
White Phosphorus Burn Dressing Protocol

• Dress white phosphorus-injured patients with saline-soaked dressings to prevent reignition of the phosphorus by contact with air.
• Ensure that the dressing is thoroughly soaked in saline and applied immediately after decontamination.
• Avoid any exposure of the burn site to open air until the phosphorus has been fully neutralized and covered.
white phosphorus,burn dressing,saline-soaked,dressings,chemical burns
Burn Injury First Aid and Initial Care
High Priority Source: Emergency War Surgery
Burn Injury First Aid and Initial Care

• Stop the burning process: Extinguish flames, remove burning clothing, and take the patient away from the source of fire or heat. In electrical injuries, disconnect the power source safely to avoid...
Burn Injury First Aid and Initial Care

• Stop the burning process: Extinguish flames, remove burning clothing, and take the patient away from the source of fire or heat. In electrical injuries, disconnect the power source safely to avoid injuring the rescuer.
• Wash chemical burns with copious water lavage to remove harmful agents from the skin surface.
• Ensure airway patency, control any hemorrhage, and splint fractures if present.
• Remove all constricting articles: This includes rings, bracelets, wristwatches, belts, and boots. Do not undress the patient unless the injury was caused by a chemical agent; in that case, remove all contaminated clothing.
• Cover the patient with a clean sheet or blanket to maintain body temperature and prevent contamination during transport. Special burn dressings are not required at this stage.
• Watch for hypothermia, especially in patients with large surface area burns, as it is a common complication.
• Establish intravenous (IV) access: Use unburned skin if possible; if necessary, use burned skin or consider intraosseous access.
• Begin resuscitation with lactated Ringer’s solution (LR) or similar fluid and continue during evacuation.
burn first aid,chemical burns,airway management,hypothermia prevention,iv access,resuscitation,burn dressing
Expose a Burned Area and Remove Jewelry
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid + STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1 + STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks + Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure for Exposing a Burned Area and Removing Jewelry

1. Cut clothing covering the burned area: Use scissors or other cutting tools to remove clothing that is not stuck to the wound.
2. **Gently lift away clothing covering the burned...
Procedure for Exposing a Burned Area and Removing Jewelry

1. Cut clothing covering the burned area: Use scissors or other cutting tools to remove clothing that is not stuck to the wound.
2. Gently lift away clothing covering the burned area, without pulling clothing over the burns.
3. Leave in place any clothing that is stuck to the burn. Do not attempt to remove it, as this could cause additional harm and damage to the skin.
4. If the casualty’s hands or wrists have been burned, remove jewelry (rings, watches, etc.) if possible without causing further injury and place it in their pockets. This prevents the necessity to cut off jewelry since swelling usually occurs as a result of a burn.

Important Notes:
• DO NOT uncover the wound in a chemical environment. Exposure could cause additional harm. Apply the dressing directly over the casualty’s protective clothing.
• DO NOT attempt to decontaminate skin where blisters have formed.
burn, expose, clothing removal, jewelry removal, first aid

Burn Injuries (3) -

First Aid for Burns
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Assess the burn: Determine the severity of the burn (first-degree, second-degree, or third-degree) and its location on the body.
2. Cool the burn immediately: Run cool (not cold) water over the burned area for at least...
Procedure:

1. Assess the burn: Determine the severity of the burn (first-degree, second-degree, or third-degree) and its location on the body.
2. Cool the burn immediately: Run cool (not cold) water over the burned area for at least 10-20 minutes to reduce pain and swelling.
3. Cover the burn with a sterile dressing: Use a clean, non-porous material to cover the burn to prevent infection and further contamination.
4. Do not apply ice or butter to the burn: These can cause further damage to the tissue.
5. Avoid breaking blisters: If there are blisters present, do not attempt to pop them as this can increase the risk of infection.
6. Monitor for signs of shock: Watch for symptoms such as pale skin, rapid pulse, and confusion, and take appropriate action if they occur.
7. Call for medical assistance immediately: Severe burns can be life-threatening; seek professional medical aid as soon as possible.
first aid for burns, cool burn, cover with dressing, avoid ice or butter, monitor shock
Initial Burn Wound Care and Management
High Priority Source: Emergency War Surgery
Objective: Provide initial wound care to burn patients within the first 24 hours postburn.

Procedure:
1. Ensure adequate IV pain management using small, intermittent boluses of IV morphine or fentanyl for background pain control.
2....
Objective: Provide initial wound care to burn patients within the first 24 hours postburn.

Procedure:
1. Ensure adequate IV pain management using small, intermittent boluses of IV morphine or fentanyl for background pain control.
2. Administer ketamine (1 mg/kg IV) for painful wound care if needed.
3. Remove any foreign bodies from the burn site.
4. Perform debridement as necessary.
5. Cleanse the wound with surgical soap using only saline around the face.
6. Unroof all blisters.
7. Apply a topical antimicrobial cream (e.g., 1% silver sulfadiazine or 11.1% mafenide acetate) as a thick layer (1/16- to 1/8-in. thick), not as a lotion, twice daily after thorough cleansing with surgical detergent such as chlorhexidine gluconate (Hibiclens).
8. Apply burn creams either open or closed (wrapped in gauze):
- Extremity wounds: Wrap in a thick layer of roller gauze and change dressings twice daily.
- During active wound exudation, use bulky dressings beneath the burned parts to absorb exudate.
- Reapply burn cream as often as needed to keep open burns covered.
burn wound care,topical antimicrobial,debridement,pain management,surgical soap
Provide Initial Treatment for Burn Injuries in a Radiation Emergency
High Priority Source: Planning Guidance for Response to Nuclear Detonation May 2022 FEMA
Objective: Provide immediate care to stabilize burn casualties before advanced medical resources are available.

Steps:
1. Cool the burn:
- Apply cool (not cold) water to the affected area for 10–20 minutes to reduce pain and...
Objective: Provide immediate care to stabilize burn casualties before advanced medical resources are available.

Steps:
1. Cool the burn:
- Apply cool (not cold) water to the affected area for 10–20 minutes to reduce pain and swelling, if safe to do so.
2. Protect the wound:
- Cover with a sterile dressing or clean cloth to prevent infection.
3. Avoid breaking blisters:
- Do not pop blisters as this can increase risk of infection.
4. Administer pain relief:
- If available, provide over-the-counter pain medication such as ibuprofen or acetaminophen.
5. Monitor for signs of shock:
- Watch for symptoms like rapid pulse, confusion, or low blood pressure and treat accordingly.
6. Transport to medical facility:
- Ensure prompt transport to a hospital or medical center for further treatment.
initial burn treatment, radiation emergency first aid, wound care, pain management

Respiratory Support (3) -

Administer Oxygen Therapy for Blood Loss
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Increase oxygen in blood.

Procedure:
1. Administer oxygen therapy via a mask or nasal cannula.
2. Ensure the casualty is breathing comfortably and receiving adequate oxygen flow.
3. Monitor the casualty’s respiratory rate, oxygen...
Objective: Increase oxygen in blood.

Procedure:
1. Administer oxygen therapy via a mask or nasal cannula.
2. Ensure the casualty is breathing comfortably and receiving adequate oxygen flow.
3. Monitor the casualty’s respiratory rate, oxygen saturation levels, and overall condition.
4. Adjust the oxygen delivery as needed based on the casualty's response.

Notes: Oxygen therapy should be used in conjunction with other resuscitation techniques such as IV fluid administration and blood pressure monitoring.
oxygen therapy,blood loss,respiratory support
Administer Oxygen for Hypoxia or Respiratory Distress
High Priority Source: Emergency War Surgery
Procedure:

1. Assess the patient: Look for signs of hypoxia (e.g., cyanosis, rapid breathing, confusion) or respiratory distress.
2. Provide supplemental oxygen:
- Use a non-rebreather mask to deliver 100% oxygen at a flow rate of 15...
Procedure:

1. Assess the patient: Look for signs of hypoxia (e.g., cyanosis, rapid breathing, confusion) or respiratory distress.
2. Provide supplemental oxygen:
- Use a non-rebreather mask to deliver 100% oxygen at a flow rate of 15 L/min.
3. Monitor oxygen saturation: Ensure that the patient's SpO₂ is maintained above 94% using pulse oximetry if available.
4. Watch for complications:
- Monitor for signs of oxygen toxicity (e.g., respiratory alkalosis, confusion) and adjust as needed.
5. Transport to medical care: Oxygen therapy should be followed by definitive evaluation and treatment of the underlying cause.
oxygen administration,hypoxia
Provide Hydration and Support for Pneumonia Patients
High Priority Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

• Ensure the patient drinks plenty of liquids to stay hydrated.

• If the patient is unable or unwilling to eat, provide liquid foods or Rehydration Drink (p. 152).

• Encourage the patient to breathe in **hot water vapors...
Procedure:

• Ensure the patient drinks plenty of liquids to stay hydrated.

• If the patient is unable or unwilling to eat, provide liquid foods or Rehydration Drink (p. 152).

• Encourage the patient to breathe in hot water vapors (p. 168) to ease coughing and loosen mucus.

• Use postural drainage (p. 169) to help clear mucus from the lungs.

• If the patient is wheezing, administer an anti-asthma medicine (p. 384) as needed.
hydration, postural drainage, hot water vapor, wheezing, asthma medicine

Personal Equipment (3) -

Prepare Personal Items for Extended Missions
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
To ensure readiness and comfort during extended deployments:

1. Always carry personal identification:
- Include your National Society membership card.

2. Bring essential personal items:
- Personal hygiene products and any necessary...
To ensure readiness and comfort during extended deployments:

1. Always carry personal identification:
- Include your National Society membership card.

2. Bring essential personal items:
- Personal hygiene products and any necessary medication.
- A change of clothing and laundry soap.
- Non-perishable, ready-to-use food and water (no refrigeration required).
- A torch with a hand-crank or extra batteries and a spare bulb.

3. Bring individual accommodation items:
- Include a sleeping bag and mosquito net if needed.

4. Be aware of location-specific requirements:
- Some locations may require additional items not listed here.
personal_items, extended_mission, hygiene, food_water
Prepare and Maintain Appropriate Clothing for First Aiders
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
To ensure proper attire that supports functionality, professionalism, and cultural respect:

1. Wear appropriate clothing:
- Choose garments suitable for the climate and work environment.

2. Keep clothing clean and professional-looking:
...
To ensure proper attire that supports functionality, professionalism, and cultural respect:

1. Wear appropriate clothing:
- Choose garments suitable for the climate and work environment.

2. Keep clothing clean and professional-looking:
- Maintain a neat appearance at all times.

3. Respect cultural norms:
- Be aware of and adhere to local traditions, taboos, and dress codes.

4. Wear heavy-duty, simple work clothes:
- Avoid showing off or wearing overly casual attire.

5. Bring waterproof clothing:
- Ensure you have gear that protects against rain or other weather conditions.
clothing, professionalism, cultural respect, work attire
Select Appropriate Footwear and Accessories for First Aiders
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
To ensure comfort, safety, and practicality in field operations:

1. Wear strong trainers or light walking boots:
- Choose footwear that provides good support and protection.

2. Use a modest plastic watch:
- Avoid flashy or expensive...
To ensure comfort, safety, and practicality in field operations:

1. Wear strong trainers or light walking boots:
- Choose footwear that provides good support and protection.

2. Use a modest plastic watch:
- Avoid flashy or expensive timepieces.

3. Carry a pocket knife or equivalent:
- Useful for minor tasks, but note that such items are not allowed on commercial aircraft.

4. Bring writing materials:
- Include a notebook and pencils for documentation purposes.

5. Avoid carrying jewelry or large sums of money:
- Minimize the risk of theft or distraction.

6. Avoid items associated with espionage:
- Do not carry binoculars, cameras, or audio/video recording equipment unless absolutely necessary.
footwear, accessories, safety, security

Armed Conflicts (3) -

Initial Steps for Managing a Casualty Situation
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
### Initial Steps for Managing a Casualty Situation

• Survey the scene: Quickly assess the environment and potential hazards.
• Seek help: If necessary, issue an alert and seek assistance (see Section 5.5 – Alert).
• Emergency removal:...
### Initial Steps for Managing a Casualty Situation

• Survey the scene: Quickly assess the environment and potential hazards.
• Seek help: If necessary, issue an alert and seek assistance (see Section 5.5 – Alert).
• Emergency removal: Safely move casualties to a secure location if needed.
• Initial examination: Conduct a quick assessment of the casualty’s condition.
• Immediate life-saving measures: Administer any urgent care required to stabilize the casualty.

Casualty Management:
• Complete examination: Perform a thorough check of the casualty’s injuries and overall condition.
• Stabilization techniques: Use appropriate methods to secure the casualty in a safe position.
• Psychological support: Provide emotional reassurance and comfort to the casualty.
• Rehydration: Administer fluids if needed, especially in cases of dehydration or shock.
• Casualty in a comfortable position: Ensure the casualty is placed in a position that minimizes pain and discomfort.

Monitoring and Follow-Up:
• Checking and monitoring of:
- The casualty’s condition;
- The efficiency of measures taken.
• Evacuation to a more advanced level of care: If necessary, arrange for the casualty to be moved to a facility with better medical resources.

Debriefing and Self-Care:
• Debriefing: Reflect on the incident and any actions taken.
• Recuperation and relaxation: Allow yourself time to recover physically and mentally after providing care.
casualty management,initial examination,stabilization techniques,rehydration
Situation Management for First Aiders in Non-Mass Casualty Situations
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
### Situation Management for First Aiders in Non-Mass Casualty Situations

• Alert should be issued as soon as possible, but only when manageable, and according to circumstances.
• Determine if there is a standard alert procedure.
• Ensure...
### Situation Management for First Aiders in Non-Mass Casualty Situations

• Alert should be issued as soon as possible, but only when manageable, and according to circumstances.
• Determine if there is a standard alert procedure.
• Ensure sufficient information has been gathered.
• Consider the available means of communication.

Key considerations for managing a situation:

1. Assess any hazardous conditions quickly: Think scene security.
2. Assess the casualty situation:
- Determine if it is one casualty or mass casualties.
3. Decide: To behave safely and to be equipped with the required protective equipment.
4. Act on security:
- Protect yourself and the casualty(ies).
5. Act on support: Issue alert and seek help if necessary (see Section 5.5 – Alert).

Assess > Decide > Act Table Summary:

| Assess | Decide | Act |
|---|---|---|
| Threat to your security? | Management of your security (protection) | Take cover quickly; protect yourself continuously |
| Threat to the security of casualty(ies)? | Management of the security of casualty(ies) | Identify sheltered position and safe access; perform emergency removal(s); stay in a safe place, if possible sheltered from violence and the elements (extreme temperatures, sun, rain, wind, etc.) |
| One casualty? Mass casualties? | Prepare priorities for casualty care: sift and sort (triage) | In a sheltered position: Care for casualties requiring immediate life-saving assistance; tell walking casualties to go to the collection point or to help if capable of doing so; continue caring for other casualties in accordance with the priorities for care |
| Personnel sufficient? | Seek help, if needed | Mobilize bystanders, if possible |
| Alert? | Issue alert* | Inform team leader or dispatch or command centre; seek further help, if needed |

• An alert should be issued as soon as possible, but only when manageable, and according to circumstances. Is there a standard alert procedure? Has sufficient information been gathered? What are the available means of communication?
situation management,non-mass casualty,alert procedures,triage
Approach on the Spot in Armed Conflicts
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
### Approach on the Spot in Armed Conflicts

1. Control yourself: Think before you act.

2. Protect yourself and others:
- Act in accordance with the basic rules protecting individuals in situations of violence;
- Use the distinctive...
### Approach on the Spot in Armed Conflicts

1. Control yourself: Think before you act.

2. Protect yourself and others:
- Act in accordance with the basic rules protecting individuals in situations of violence;
- Use the distinctive emblem in an appropriate manner;
- Respect security rules.

3. Offer help according to your professional ability.

4. Be humane: Treat wounded people, not just wounds.

5. Use common sense and be professional: Use proven procedures and techniques.

6. Manage resources properly: Promote teamwork and focus on priorities.

7. Communicate: Share and learn.

8. Relax: Recharge your batteries.

Your usual First Aid practices have to be adapted and supplemented to take into account the special needs you will face in armed conflicts and other situations of violence, starting with those relating to security and protection.
approach on the spot,armed conflicts,first aid,security rules

Triage (3) -

Prioritizing Treatment in Triage: Life-Threatening Conditions
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Prioritizing Treatment in Triage: Life-Threatening Conditions

For life-threatening conditions, the following order of priority should be followed:

1. Airway problems: Address airway issues first;
2. Breathing problems: Deal with...
Prioritizing Treatment in Triage: Life-Threatening Conditions

For life-threatening conditions, the following order of priority should be followed:

1. Airway problems: Address airway issues first;
2. Breathing problems: Deal with breathing problems next;
3. Circulation problems: Address circulation problems last.

This prioritization ensures that casualties who have the best chance of survival receive immediate attention.
airway, breathing, circulation, triage priority
Triage Process in a Mass Casualty Situation
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Title: Triage Process in a Mass Casualty Situation

Procedure:
1. Ensure you are in a safe and sheltered place.
2. Politely but firmly establish authority over the situation.
3. Recruit helpers, especially those with First Aid...
Title: Triage Process in a Mass Casualty Situation

Procedure:
1. Ensure you are in a safe and sheltered place.
2. Politely but firmly establish authority over the situation.
3. Recruit helpers, especially those with First Aid training.
4. Conduct a quick tour of the area with your helpers to assess the overall situation.
5. Begin triage by briefly assessing each casualty (maximum 15-20 seconds) according to the ABCDE sequence:
- Airway
- Breathing
- Circulation
- Disability (neurological status)
- Exposure (environmental factors)
6. Assign a temporary priority category based on the assessment.
7. Spend minimal time with casualties who are conscious and mobile, as they can be directed to a collection point.
8. Instruct helpers to perform immediate life-saving tasks for those in priority category 1, which includes:
- Clearing the airway and placing unconscious but breathing casualties in the lateral recovery position.
- Stopping external haemorrhaging using manual pressure techniques, and if possible, with compressive dressings and bandages (which may be improvised).
9. Take a short break to avoid fatigue.
10. Re-evaluate the priority category of each casualty.
11. Direct the walking wounded to a collection point and encourage them to lend assistance, especially if they are trained in First Aid.
12. In mass casualty situations, be prepared to perform triage on the spot and make decisions based on training in setting priorities and decision-making.
13. Proceed with casualty No. 1 of priority category 1:
- Conduct a complete examination to confirm or modify their priority category for care.
- Provide care, stabilize their condition, and protect them from environmental exposure (extreme temperatures, sun, rain, wind, etc.).
- Check the effectiveness of the measures taken.
- Once stabilized, prepare casualty No. 1 for evacuation.
14. Repeat steps 13 for casualty No. 2, then casualty No. 3, and so on, until all priority category 1 casualties are treated.
15. After completing treatment of priority category 1 casualties, proceed with those in priority category 2 and beyond, following the same process.
16. The complete examination will finalize sorting by confirming or modifying the priority category for care of each casualty.
17. Once all casualties have been treated:
- Re-assess the condition of each casualty.
- Determine the effectiveness of the measures taken so far.
- Triage the casualties for evacuation, assigning a priority category to each one based on their needs and urgency.
18. When an evacuation is decided upon, organize it and prepare the casualties accordingly.

Notes:
• This process assumes you are in a situation of mass casualties where triage must be performed quickly and efficiently.
• Use improvised materials for dressings and bandages when necessary.
• Ensure that helpers understand their roles and responsibilities during triage.
triage process,mass casualty,first aid,emergency care,priority categories,lateral recovery position,external haemorrhaging
Triage Process: Sift and Sort
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Triage Process: Sift and Sort

The process of triage must be rapid and is based on two successive sequences: sift and sort.

### Sift
• Selecting those most severely injured;
• Identifying and removing:
- The dead
- The slightly...
Triage Process: Sift and Sort

The process of triage must be rapid and is based on two successive sequences: sift and sort.

### Sift
• Selecting those most severely injured;
• Identifying and removing:
- The dead
- The slightly injured
- The uninjured

### Sort
• Categorizing the most severely injured casualties based on matching:
- The nature of the problem with treatment available in terms of personnel and supplies.

As a consequence of triage:

• Choices are made to achieve the greatest good not for any particular individual but for the greatest number of people;
• Because of limited time and resources, some casualties do not even begin to receive treatment, or their treatment is interrupted, or their evacuation is never considered;
• Triage might be difficult to perform. The decisions involved are the most difficult in all health care.
sift and sort, triage process, first aid, emergency management

Transportation (3) -

Evacuation Vehicle Usage and Safety Protocols
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Overview: Evacuation vehicles must be used exclusively for medical purposes to ensure the safety of casualties and compliance with international humanitarian law.

Steps:
1. Use evacuation vehicles only for transporting living casualties;...
Overview: Evacuation vehicles must be used exclusively for medical purposes to ensure the safety of casualties and compliance with international humanitarian law.

Steps:
1. Use evacuation vehicles only for transporting living casualties; never use them for personal or individual needs.
2. Ensure that evacuation vehicles are clean, available, and marked with the Red Cross or Red Crescent emblem (as required by international humanitarian law).
3. Avoid using evacuation vehicles to transport dead bodies if other means of transportation are available.
4. Never transport weapons with casualties or allow anyone accompanying a casualty to carry a weapon.
5. Weapons (especially grenades and handguns) should only be handled by trained personnel, not removed by First Aiders.
6. Small arms and ammunition found in medical units do not deprive them of their protection under international humanitarian law.
evacuation vehicle, weapon transport, international humanitarian law, Red Cross emblem
Transporting Casualties in Armed Conflicts
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Overview: Moving wounded individuals during armed conflicts is challenging and must be done with caution to avoid additional trauma or danger.

Steps:
1. Ensure that casualty transport complies with local regulations, including any...
Overview: Moving wounded individuals during armed conflicts is challenging and must be done with caution to avoid additional trauma or danger.

Steps:
1. Ensure that casualty transport complies with local regulations, including any restrictions on the participation of First Aiders.
2. Confirm whether you would incur liability before engaging in this activity.
3. Only move casualties when:
- Casualties are gathered at a First Aid post or similar facility.
- Casualties have been triaged and assigned an evacuation priority.
- Reliable means of transport are available.
- Routes, time-frames, and destinations are known.
- Personnel at the destination are informed and ready to receive casualties.
- Security has been ensured.
4. Casualties found on the roadside should be taken on board only if there is adequate space and no other alternative.
5. Inform your team leader or dispatch centre of any casualty found on the roadside and request instructions before proceeding.
6. Occasionally, “opportunistic casualties” (those not in urgent need of evacuation) may be transported due to available space, but this should be done with caution and only if necessary.
casualty transport, armed conflict, local regulations, triage priority, evacuation routes
Safe Lifting and Transportation Techniques for Casualties
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Overview: Proper lifting techniques are essential to avoid injury to both the casualty and the First Aider during transportation.

Steps:
1. Ensure you are in good physical condition before assisting with any movement of casualties.
2....
Overview: Proper lifting techniques are essential to avoid injury to both the casualty and the First Aider during transportation.

Steps:
1. Ensure you are in good physical condition before assisting with any movement of casualties.
2. Learn and use proper lifting techniques:
- Lift using your leg muscles, not your back.
- Keep your back straight while lifting.
3. Be familiar with the characteristics of the means of transport you will be using (e.g., stretchers, ambulances).
4. Report all departures to supervisors in charge of managing evacuations, including:
- Departure time
- Number and condition of casualties
- Destination
- Estimated travel time and route
- Number of First Aiders aboard
lifting techniques, casualty transportation, physical condition, reporting procedures

Limb Injuries (3) -

Assess Limb Injury for Deformity, Tenderness, and Circulation
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- Check for localized tenderness, deformity, and crepitus (clicking or grating of fractured bone ends).
• Assess distal circulation by checking for pulse (radial in upper limbs, femoral in lower limbs, and foot pulse if possible).
-...
- Check for localized tenderness, deformity, and crepitus (clicking or grating of fractured bone ends).
• Assess distal circulation by checking for pulse (radial in upper limbs, femoral in lower limbs, and foot pulse if possible).
• Evaluate neurological condition: test movement and feeling by gently pinching the skin at different places. The casualty should react consistently.
• If there is a wound, suspect a complex injury involving bones, muscles, nerves, or blood vessels.
injury assessment, limb injury, circulation check, neurological evaluation, crepitus, deformity
Assess and Manage Limb Injuries
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Immobilize the injured limb and assess for fractures or dislocations.

Procedure:
1. Avoid any undue manipulation or movement of the injured limb to prevent further injury.
2. Immobilize the injured limb using splints, bandages,...
Objective: Immobilize the injured limb and assess for fractures or dislocations.

Procedure:
1. Avoid any undue manipulation or movement of the injured limb to prevent further injury.
2. Immobilize the injured limb using splints, bandages, or other available materials to stabilize it in a neutral position.
3. Assess and monitor blood circulation, mobility, and feeling of the limb below the injury site:
- Check for pulses at the distal end of the limb.
- Ask the casualty if they can feel sensations such as tingling or numbness.
- Observe for any changes in skin color or temperature.
4. Reduce and immobilize a fracture to relieve pain and prevent further damage to surrounding soft tissues, especially blood vessels and nerves.
5. Use the opposite limb as a mirror image for comparison during assessment.

Notes: If the casualty is unable to move the injured limb or reports severe pain, suspect a fracture or dislocation and seek immediate medical assistance.
limb immobilization,fracture management
Palpate Lower Limbs for Injuries
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- Gently grasp the hip with both hands and palpate down the entire leg, front and back.
• Repeat with the other leg.
• Ask the casualty to move each joint and toe slightly to assess range of motion.
• Evaluate feeling by gently pinching the skin...
- Gently grasp the hip with both hands and palpate down the entire leg, front and back.
• Repeat with the other leg.
• Ask the casualty to move each joint and toe slightly to assess range of motion.
• Evaluate feeling by gently pinching the skin at different places on the leg. The casualty should react consistently everywhere.
• Check for femoral pulse on both sides. If possible, also check the pulse in the foot.
lower limb palpation, femoral pulse check, toe movement assessment

Burn Management (3) -

Manage Chemical Burns
Critical Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Eliminate the source of the chemical burn by removing the casualty from contact with the chemical and rinsing the affected area thoroughly with water.
chemical burn treatment,first aid
Perform First Aid for Burns
Critical Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Conditions: You see a casualty who is suffering from a burn. You will need the casualty's emergency bandage or field dressing and canteen of water.

Standards: Give first aid for a burn without causing further injury to the casualty....
Conditions: You see a casualty who is suffering from a burn. You will need the casualty's emergency bandage or field dressing and canteen of water.

Standards: Give first aid for a burn without causing further injury to the casualty. Eliminate the source of the burn, if necessary. Apply a dressing lightly over the burn. Ensure that the sides of the dressing are sealed and the dressing does not slip.
burn treatment,first aid,burn injury
Manage Skin Burns in Armed Conflicts
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Cool and protect burns to minimize damage and prevent complications.

### Steps:
1. Cool the burnt area immediately with clean water or any available cool liquid (not ice or cold water, which can cause further injury).
2....
Objective: Cool and protect burns to minimize damage and prevent complications.

### Steps:
1. Cool the burnt area immediately with clean water or any available cool liquid (not ice or cold water, which can cause further injury).
2. Protect the burnt area from further trauma or contamination.
3. Maintain hydration of the casualty by providing oral fluids if possible and appropriate.
4. Keep the casualty warm, especially in cold environments.
5. Watch for inhalation burns: look for signs such as difficulty breathing, coughing, hoarseness, or facial burns.

### Notes:
• Burns are a common event in armed conflicts.
• This manual does not address burns caused by nuclear or chemical weapons.
burn management, first aid, cooling burns, hydration, inhalation burns

Team Coordination (3) -

Coordinate Your Team During Casualty Care
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Coordinate your team during casualty care

• Lead triage and set priorities for the care and evacuation of casualties.
• Check documentation, including the registration list and medical cards.
• Organize the evacuation of...
Coordinate your team during casualty care

• Lead triage and set priorities for the care and evacuation of casualties.
• Check documentation, including the registration list and medical cards.
• Organize the evacuation of casualties.
• Gather information from your team members and pass it on to the relevant level.
• Organize personnel changeovers and replenishment of materials.

> Leadership is not just an individual issue. Much depends on the collective leadership of the components of the casualty-care chain. This may include organizations other than your own. The relationships between individuals and teams will also play a role.
triage, casualty care, team coordination, evacuation, documentation, leadership
Support Your Team During Casualty Care
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Support your team during casualty care

• Encourage good initiatives and correct mistakes.
• Monitor the physical and psychological condition of your team members and ensure that they take a break when they need to.
• **Empathize with...
Support your team during casualty care

• Encourage good initiatives and correct mistakes.
• Monitor the physical and psychological condition of your team members and ensure that they take a break when they need to.
• Empathize with team members and provide all necessary support.
support, team morale, psychological health, physical well-being, leadership
After the Assignment: Team Welfare and Debriefing
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
After the assignment, think of the welfare of your team members, but do not forget yourself. You too are a member of the team!

• Hold debriefing sessions communicating positive and negative feedback in a constructive manner.
• **Remind your...
After the assignment, think of the welfare of your team members, but do not forget yourself. You too are a member of the team!

• Hold debriefing sessions communicating positive and negative feedback in a constructive manner.
• Remind your team members to rest and relax – and help them to do so.
• Rest and relax yourself.
• Help replace or replenish equipment and supplies.
• Prepare the team for the next mission.

> Foster team spirit by organizing or encouraging informal events outside work. This will strengthen personal relationships and mutual trust.
debriefing, team welfare, rest, equipment replenishment, preparation, mental health

Combat Response (3) -

Response to Being Under Fire While Driving
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
If under fire, take the following actions:

1. If shooting is from the rear: Drive as fast as possible; moving targets are harder to hit.
2. If shooting is from the front: Turn off onto a side street in towns or veer off into the...
If under fire, take the following actions:

1. If shooting is from the rear: Drive as fast as possible; moving targets are harder to hit.
2. If shooting is from the front: Turn off onto a side street in towns or veer off into the countryside for cover, using your vehicle as protection.
3. Avoid reversing or turning around: These actions slow you down and make you an easier target.
4. If immobilized: Exit the vehicle quickly and take cover behind it.
under fire, driving response, combat safety, vehicle cover
Response to Shelling in Armed Conflicts
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
If under shelling, take these actions:

1. If shells are within 50-100 meters: Stop the vehicle, exit quickly, and find cover off the road (not under the vehicle). If escape is easy (e.g., a tunnel ahead), drive on.
2. **If shells are...
If under shelling, take these actions:

1. If shells are within 50-100 meters: Stop the vehicle, exit quickly, and find cover off the road (not under the vehicle). If escape is easy (e.g., a tunnel ahead), drive on.
2. If shells are distant: Drive out of the area as quickly as possible. If the next shell appears to be closer, stop, exit, and take cover.
shelling response, combat safety, vehicle cover
Response to Warning Shots in Armed Conflicts
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
If fired upon with warning shots, follow these steps:

1. Stop the vehicle immediately.
2. Exit and take cover: Get off the road quickly, using your vehicle as a shield from the direction of fire.
3. Await instructions: Contact your...
If fired upon with warning shots, follow these steps:

1. Stop the vehicle immediately.
2. Exit and take cover: Get off the road quickly, using your vehicle as a shield from the direction of fire.
3. Await instructions: Contact your team leader and wait for further directions.
4. Reverse away if no more shooting: After 15 minutes without additional fire, consider reversing to safety.
warning shots, combat response, vehicle cover

Military First Aid (3) -

Duties of Field Medics in Emergency Medical Treatment
High Priority Source: NATO Emergency War Surgery
Emergency Medical Treatment by Field Medics:

1. Airway Management: The field medic is primarily responsible for ensuring the airway is clear and properly managed.
2. Hemorrhage Control: Apply compression dressings to control...
Emergency Medical Treatment by Field Medics:

1. Airway Management: The field medic is primarily responsible for ensuring the airway is clear and properly managed.
2. Hemorrhage Control: Apply compression dressings to control hemorrhage.
3. Fracture Stabilization: Stabilize fractures using appropriate techniques.
4. IV Fluid Administration: Initiate intravenous fluid administration as needed for resuscitation.
field medic,airway management,hemorrhage control,fracture stabilization,iv fluids
Understanding Combat Lifesaver Role and Training
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Combat Lifesaver Role and Training

A _combat lifesaver_ is a member of a nonmedical unit trained in enhanced first aid procedures beyond basic self-aid and buddy aid. The individual must be selected by the unit commander, with at least one per...
Combat Lifesaver Role and Training

A _combat lifesaver_ is a member of a nonmedical unit trained in enhanced first aid procedures beyond basic self-aid and buddy aid. The individual must be selected by the unit commander, with at least one per squad, crew, team, or equivalent-sized unit.

### Responsibilities:
1. Provide immediate enhanced first aid before the arrival of a trauma specialist (MOS 91W) or hospital corpsman (HM).
2. Administer procedures such as intravenous (IV) fluids, which require additional training beyond basic first aid.
3. The primary duty of the individual does not change; their combat lifesaver role is an added responsibility.

### Training:
• Conducted by medical personnel assigned, attached, or in direct support (DS) of the unit.
• Managed by the senior medical person designated by the commander.
• Focuses on enhanced first aid measures that can save lives before professional medical help arrives.
combat lifesaver, enhanced first aid, military training, trauma specialist, hospital corpsman
Understanding First Aid Measures and Medical Treatment
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
First Aid Measures

• Urgent and immediate lifesaving actions that can be performed by nonmedical personnel when medical help is not immediately available.
• Includes self-aid (individual performing first aid on themselves) and buddy aid (a...
First Aid Measures

• Urgent and immediate lifesaving actions that can be performed by nonmedical personnel when medical help is not immediately available.
• Includes self-aid (individual performing first aid on themselves) and buddy aid (a service member assisting a fellow soldier).

Medical Treatment

• Provided by medically trained personnel such as hospital corpsmen (HM), trauma specialists, or area of concentration (AOC) personnel.
• May include emergency medical treatment (EMT), advanced trauma management (ATM), and resuscitative/surgical interventions.
• Performed in medical treatment facilities (MTFs), including battalion aid stations, Level II facilities, dispensaries, clinics, and hospitals.
first aid measures, medical treatment, self-aid, buddy aid, trauma specialist

Casualty Evaluation (3) -

Evaluate and Administer First Aid to an Unconscious Casualty
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Objective: Evaluate the casualty for injuries or illnesses and administer appropriate first aid until medical help arrives.

Procedure:
1. Ensure the area around the casualty is safe.
2. Check if the casualty is conscious by gently tapping...
Objective: Evaluate the casualty for injuries or illnesses and administer appropriate first aid until medical help arrives.

Procedure:
1. Ensure the area around the casualty is safe.
2. Check if the casualty is conscious by gently tapping their shoulder and shouting, “Are you okay?”
3. If the casualty is unconscious:
- Check for breathing (see procedure: Check for Breathing in an Unconscious Casualty).
- Check for signs of bleeding or injury.
- Check for signs of shock (see procedure: Check for Signs of Shock in an Injured Casualty).
4. Administer first aid as needed based on the findings:
- If the casualty is not breathing, begin CPR if trained and it is safe to do so.
- If there is bleeding, apply direct pressure to control it.
- If signs of shock are present, lay the casualty down with legs elevated and keep them warm.
5. If it is suspected that a nerve agent was used:
- Administer the casualty’s own nerve agent antidote autoinjector (do not use your own).
6. Continue to monitor the casualty for changes in condition until relieved by medical personnel.

Important: One of the cardinal principles for assisting a casualty is that you must continue the evaluation and first aid measures until another individual relieves you.
casualty evaluation, first aid administration, unconscious casualty, shock management, nerve agent antidote
Evaluate a Casualty for Responsiveness
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Gently shake or tap the casualty while calmly asking, “Are you OK?”
2. Watch for a response.
3. If the casualty does not respond:
- Proceed to step (2) of the evaluation.
4. If the casualty responds:
- Ask him where he feels...
Procedure:

1. Gently shake or tap the casualty while calmly asking, “Are you OK?”
2. Watch for a response.
3. If the casualty does not respond:
- Proceed to step (2) of the evaluation.
4. If the casualty responds:
- Ask him where he feels different than usual or where it hurts.
- Ask him to identify the location of pain if he can, or to identify the area in which there is no feeling.
5. If the casualty is conscious but is choking and cannot talk:
- Stop the evaluation and begin first aid measures immediately.
- Refer to paragraphs 2-10 and 2-11 for specific information on opening the airway.
responsiveness, casualty evaluation, first aid, consciousness check
Evaluate a Casualty
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Evaluate a Casualty

Follow these steps to evaluate a casualty:

1. Check for responsiveness: Gently shake or tap the casualty while calmly asking, “Are you OK?” If there is no response, proceed to the next step.
2. Check for breathing:...
Evaluate a Casualty

Follow these steps to evaluate a casualty:

1. Check for responsiveness: Gently shake or tap the casualty while calmly asking, “Are you OK?” If there is no response, proceed to the next step.
2. Check for breathing: Look, listen, and feel for signs of breathing for no more than 10 seconds. If the casualty is not breathing or only gasping, proceed to the next step.
3. Check for a pulse: Feel for a carotid or femoral pulse for no more than 10 seconds. If there is no pulse, begin CPR immediately.
4. Check for injuries or bleeding: Look for any visible wounds, fractures, or signs of shock (pale skin, rapid breathing, confusion).
5. Assess the environment: Ensure the area is safe for both you and the casualty to prevent further harm.
6. Call for emergency medical assistance if needed.

Important Notes:
• Do not move the casualty unless there is immediate danger (e.g., fire, falling debris).
• If the casualty is unconscious but breathing, place them in a safe position (recovery position).
• If the casualty is conscious but choking and cannot talk, stop the evaluation and begin first aid measures. Refer to paragraphs 2-10 and 2-11 for specific information on opening the airway.
• If the casualty responds:
- Ask him where he feels different than usual or where it hurts.
- Ask him to identify the location of pain if he can, or to identify the area in which there is no feeling.
evaluate casualty, responsiveness check, breathing check, pulse check, injuries, bleeding, shock signs, emergency medical assistance

Chemical Burns (3) -

Recognize and Treat Mustard Agent Skin Exposure
Critical Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Treatment of Mustard Agent Effects on the Skin

1. Symptoms: The most sensitive areas are the face, armpits, genitalia, neck, skin between fingers, and nail beds. Blisters may form 4-24 hours after exposure.

2. Skin Changes:
-...
Treatment of Mustard Agent Effects on the Skin

1. Symptoms: The most sensitive areas are the face, armpits, genitalia, neck, skin between fingers, and nail beds. Blisters may form 4-24 hours after exposure.

2. Skin Changes:
- Erythema (2-48 hours post-exposure): Redness resembling scarlet fever with slight edema and intense itching. As erythema fades, areas of increased pigmentation are left.
- Blistering (for higher doses, starts 4-24 hours after exposure): Blisters may rupture easily from contact with bed linen or bandages. New blisters can appear as late as the second week post-exposure. Blister fluid is not dangerous and does not cause secondary blistering.
- Deep burning leading to full thickness skin loss: Likely to occur on the penis and scrotum.

3. Healing Time:
- Superficial lesions heal in 14-21 days.
- Deep skin lesions may take up to 60 days to heal.

4. Infection Risk: Damaged tissues are extremely susceptible to infection, and healing is very slow compared to physical or caustic damage.

5. Treatment Goals: The aim of therapy is to relieve symptoms, prevent infections, and promote healing.
mustard agent, chemical burn, skin injury, blister treatment, infection prevention
Recognize and Treat Phosgene Oxime Exposure
Critical Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Treatment of Phosgene Oxime Effects

1. Symptoms: In low concentrations, phosgene oxime severely irritates the eyes and respiratory organs. In high concentrations, it also attacks the skin.

2. Skin Effects: The action on the skin is...
Treatment of Phosgene Oxime Effects

1. Symptoms: In low concentrations, phosgene oxime severely irritates the eyes and respiratory organs. In high concentrations, it also attacks the skin.

2. Skin Effects: The action on the skin is immediate: irritation resembling that caused by a stinging nettle.

3. Medical Outcome: Very few compounds are as painful and destructive to tissues as phosgene oxime.

4. Treatment Goals: Provide immediate decontamination, manage pain, and seek medical attention for specialized care.
phosgene oxime, chemical burn, skin irritation, respiratory damage, first aid
Remove Chemicals from a Burned Casualty
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. For liquid chemicals: Flush the affected area with as much water as possible.
2. For dry chemicals: Brush off loose particles using a tool or gloved hand (DO NOT use bare hands to avoid becoming a chemical burn casualty)....
Procedure:

1. For liquid chemicals: Flush the affected area with as much water as possible.
2. For dry chemicals: Brush off loose particles using a tool or gloved hand (DO NOT use bare hands to avoid becoming a chemical burn casualty). Then, flush with large amounts of water if available.
3. If large amounts of water are not available, DO NOT apply any water because it may cause a chemical reaction.
4. For WP (white phosphorus) burns: Smother the affected area with a wet cloth or mud. Keep the area covered with a wet material to exclude air and prevent further burning.
chemical burns, WP burns, first aid, decontamination

Immobilization (3) -

Maintaining Patient Immobilization
High Priority Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Procedure:

• Maintain adequate immobilization of the entire patient, as indicated.

Details:
• Use appropriate splints, boards, or other immobilization devices.
• Ensure that the patient is secured in a stable position during transport.
-...
Procedure:

• Maintain adequate immobilization of the entire patient, as indicated.

Details:
• Use appropriate splints, boards, or other immobilization devices.
• Ensure that the patient is secured in a stable position during transport.
• Avoid any movement that could exacerbate injury.
patient immobilization, first aid
Apply a Swathe for Upper Extremity Fractures
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Materials Needed:
• Any large piece of cloth, service member’s belt, or pistol belt

Steps to Apply the Swathe:
1. Wrap around the injured arm: Wrap the swathe over the injured arm, around the casualty’s back, and under the arm on the...
Materials Needed:
• Any large piece of cloth, service member’s belt, or pistol belt

Steps to Apply the Swathe:
1. Wrap around the injured arm: Wrap the swathe over the injured arm, around the casualty’s back, and under the arm on the uninjured side.
2. Secure on the uninjured side: Tie the ends of the swathe on the uninjured side to immobilize the arm.

Important Notes:
• The swathe should not be placed directly on top of the injury; it should be positioned either above or below the fracture site.
• Ensure that the swathe is not too tight, as this may impair circulation.
• Monitor for signs of impaired circulation (numbness, tingling, or coldness in the extremity).
• This procedure is intended for temporary immobilization until professional medical assistance can be provided.
swathe,application,immobilization,first aid,upper extremity fracture
Apply an Improvised Sling for Upper Extremity Fractures
Standard Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Materials Needed:
• Any large piece of cloth, belt (service member’s or pistol)

Steps to Apply the Sling:
1. Position the sling: Bring the ends of the sling up and tie them at the side (or hollow) of the neck on the uninjured side.
2....
Materials Needed:
• Any large piece of cloth, belt (service member’s or pistol)

Steps to Apply the Sling:
1. Position the sling: Bring the ends of the sling up and tie them at the side (or hollow) of the neck on the uninjured side.
2. Secure the sling at the elbow: Twist and tuck the corner of the sling at the elbow to ensure proper immobilization.

Important Notes:
• Ensure that the sling is not too tight, as this may impair circulation.
• Monitor for signs of impaired circulation (numbness, tingling, or coldness in the extremity).
• This procedure is intended for temporary immobilization until professional medical assistance can be provided.
sling,application,immobilization,first aid,upper extremity fracture

Nerve Agent Poisoning (3) -

Administering CANA to Nerve Agent Casualty
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Step 1: Obtain buddy’s CANA.

Step 2: Check injection site for any obstructions.

Step 3: Hold CANA in a closed fist with dominant hand.

Step 4: Grasp the safety cap with non-dominant hand and remove it from the injector.

**Step...
Step 1: Obtain buddy’s CANA.

Step 2: Check injection site for any obstructions.

Step 3: Hold CANA in a closed fist with dominant hand.

Step 4: Grasp the safety cap with non-dominant hand and remove it from the injector.

Step 5: Clear hard objects from the injection site.

Step 6: Inject CANA at the injection site by applying even pressure to the injector, not a jabbing motion. Hold in place for 10 seconds.

Step 7: Bend the needle of the injector by pressing on a hard surface to form a hook.

Step 8: Attach used injectors to the blouse pocket flap of the BDO/JSLIST (if applicable) and massage injection site, if mission permitting.
CANA administration, nerve agent antidote, injection procedure
Buddy Evaluation and Aid for Nerve Agent Casualty
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Step 1: Mask the casualty and position him on his side (swimmer’s position).

Step 2: Position yourself near the casualty’s thigh.

Step 3: Obtain buddy’s three or remaining MARK I autoinjectors or ATNAA.

Step 4: Check injection...
Step 1: Mask the casualty and position him on his side (swimmer’s position).

Step 2: Position yourself near the casualty’s thigh.

Step 3: Obtain buddy’s three or remaining MARK I autoinjectors or ATNAA.

Step 4: Check injection site for any obstructions.

Step 5: Hold MARK I or ATNAA with non-dominant hand.

Step 6: Remove safety cap from injector and clear hard objects from the injection site.

Step 7: Inject atropine (MARK I) or ATNAA into the casualty’s thigh using even pressure, not a jabbing motion. Hold in place for 10 seconds.

Step 8: Bend the needle of the injector by pressing on a hard surface to form a hook.

Step 9: Attach used injectors to the blouse pocket flap of the BDO/JSLIST (if applicable) and massage injection site, if mission permitting.
buddy aid, nerve agent antidote, MARK I, ATNAA, injection procedure
Safety Precautions When Administering Nerve Agent Antidotes
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Warning: Squat, do not kneel when masking the casualty or administering antidote to prevent chemical agent from entering your protective clothing.

Caution: Do not use your own MARK I, ATNAA, or CANA on a casualty. This may leave you...
Warning: Squat, do not kneel when masking the casualty or administering antidote to prevent chemical agent from entering your protective clothing.

Caution: Do not use your own MARK I, ATNAA, or CANA on a casualty. This may leave you without antidote for self-aid if needed later.

Warning: Do not inject into areas close to the hip, knee, or thighbone.
safety precautions, nerve agent antidote administration

Transportation Of Casualties (3) -

Transporting a Casualty with Suspected Back or Neck Injury
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
WARNING: Unless there is an immediate life-threatening situation (such as fire, explosion), DO NOT move a casualty with a suspected back or neck injury. Seek medical personnel for guidance on how to transport.

• Either two or four service...
WARNING: Unless there is an immediate life-threatening situation (such as fire, explosion), DO NOT move a casualty with a suspected back or neck injury. Seek medical personnel for guidance on how to transport.

• Either two or four service members (head/foot) may be used to lift a litter.
• To lift the litter, follow the procedure below:
1. Raise the litter at the same time as the other carriers/bearers.
2. Keep the casualty as level as possible.

NOTE: Use caution when transporting on a sloping incline/hill.
casualty transport, back injury, neck injury, litter lifting, first aid procedures
Transporting Casualties Using a Litter
High Priority Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure for Transporting Casualties Using a Litter

1. Assess the situation: Ensure that medical evacuation is not immediately available and that transporting the casualty by litter is necessary.
2. Prepare the litter: Use a standard...
Procedure for Transporting Casualties Using a Litter

1. Assess the situation: Ensure that medical evacuation is not immediately available and that transporting the casualty by litter is necessary.
2. Prepare the litter: Use a standard litter (FM 8-10-6) or improvise one using available materials such as boards, blankets, or other rigid objects to create a stable platform.
3. Position the casualty: Carefully place the casualty onto the litter, ensuring that their body is aligned and supported properly. Avoid any unnecessary movement that could worsen their condition.
4. Secure the casualty: Use straps, bandages, or other materials to secure the casualty to the litter if needed, especially in rough terrain or during transport.
5. Transport safely: Move the litter with care, avoiding sudden movements or jostling. Ensure that the litter is carried at a comfortable height and that all personnel involved are coordinated in their efforts.
6. Monitor the casualty: Continuously observe the casualty for any changes in condition during transport and provide necessary first aid if required.
casualty transport,litter transportation,medical evacuation,manual transportation,first aid procedures
Performing the Saddleback Carry
Standard Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure for Performing the Saddleback Carry

1. Raise Casualty to Upright Position: Use the fireman’s carry method to raise the casualty from the ground to an upright position.
2. Support with Arm Around Waist: Support the casualty by...
Procedure for Performing the Saddleback Carry

1. Raise Casualty to Upright Position: Use the fireman’s carry method to raise the casualty from the ground to an upright position.
2. Support with Arm Around Waist: Support the casualty by placing an arm around their waist. Move to the casualty’s side.
3. Encircle Bearer's Neck: Have the casualty put their arm around your neck and move in front of them with your back to support them.
4. Encircle Arms Around Bearer’s Neck: Have the casualty encircle their arms around your neck.
5. Stoop and Raise on Back: Stoop, raise the casualty onto your back, and clasp your hands together beneath their thighs if possible.

Important Considerations:
• This carry can only be used with a conscious casualty who is able to hold onto the bearer’s neck.
saddleback carry, manual carry, casualty transportation, first aid procedures, emergency transport

Circulatory System (3) -

Checking the Femoral Pulse
High Priority Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
To check the femoral pulse:

1. Locate the middle of the casualty’s groin.
2. Place the tips of your first two fingers into this area.
3. Gently press until you feel a rhythmic pulsation.
4. Count the number of beats for 15 seconds and...
To check the femoral pulse:

1. Locate the middle of the casualty’s groin.
2. Place the tips of your first two fingers into this area.
3. Gently press until you feel a rhythmic pulsation.
4. Count the number of beats for 15 seconds and multiply by 4 to determine the pulse rate per minute.

The femoral pulse is useful in assessing circulation, especially when other pulses are not accessible or compromised.
femoral pulse,pulse check,circulation assessment
Understanding Blood Circulation and the Circulatory System
High Priority Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Blood circulation is managed by the heart and blood vessels (arteries, veins, and capillaries). The heart is divided into two halves, each acting as a pump:

• The left side pumps oxygenated blood through arteries to capillaries....
Blood circulation is managed by the heart and blood vessels (arteries, veins, and capillaries). The heart is divided into two halves, each acting as a pump:

• The left side pumps oxygenated blood through arteries to capillaries. Nutrients and oxygen pass from the blood into cells, while waste products and CO2 enter the capillaries.
• The right side of the heart receives oxygen-poor blood from veins and sends it to the lungs for oxygenation.

Blood in veins is dark red due to low oxygen content. Blood flows continuously through the circulatory system, which includes the heart, blood vessels, and lymphatics.
