blood circulation,circulatory system,heart,blood vessels
Understanding the Heartbeat and Pulse
High Priority Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
The heartbeat is the rhythmic contraction and relaxation of the heart muscle that pumps blood throughout the body. The normal heartbeat rate is between 60 to 80 beats per minute.

A pulse is the rhythmical expansion and contraction of...
The heartbeat is the rhythmic contraction and relaxation of the heart muscle that pumps blood throughout the body. The normal heartbeat rate is between 60 to 80 beats per minute.

A pulse is the rhythmical expansion and contraction of arteries caused by the heartbeat. It can be felt at several points on the body:

• Carotid pulse: Located on the side of the neck, near the Adam’s apple.
• Femoral pulse: Found in the middle of the groin.
• Radial pulse: Located on the thumb side of the wrist.
• Posterior tibial pulse: Found on the inside of the ankle.

Checking these pulses is essential for assessing circulation and detecting potential circulatory issues.
heartbeat,pulse,carotid,femoral,radial,posterior tibial

Choking Rescue (3) -

Perform Abdominal Thrusts Until Obstruction is Expelled or Casualty Becomes Unresponsive
Critical Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

1. Continue performing abdominal thrusts until the obstruction is expelled or the casualty becomes unresponsive.

Notes:
• If the casualty becomes unresponsive, call for help as you proceed with steps to open the airway and...
Procedure:

1. Continue performing abdominal thrusts until the obstruction is expelled or the casualty becomes unresponsive.

Notes:
• If the casualty becomes unresponsive, call for help as you proceed with steps to open the airway and perform rescue breathing.
• Refer to paragraph 2-7 for information on how to perform mouth-to-mouth resuscitation.
abdominal thrusts, choking rescue, unresponsive casualty, first aid
First Aid for Choking (Conscious Adult)
Critical Source: Family Emergency Handbook
Procedure:

1. Ask the victim: Can you speak?
2. If the victim can speak or cough:
- Reassure and encourage them to continue coughing.
- Do not hit them on the back.
3. If the victim cannot speak or cough but is conscious:
-...
Procedure:

1. Ask the victim: Can you speak?
2. If the victim can speak or cough:
- Reassure and encourage them to continue coughing.
- Do not hit them on the back.
3. If the victim cannot speak or cough but is conscious:
- From behind, wrap your arms around their waist.
- Make a fist with one hand and grasp it with the other hand.
- Place your hands above the navel to avoid the lower tip of the breastbone.
- Give one quick inward and upward thrust.
- Repeat the upward thrusts until the airway is clear or the victim becomes unconscious.
4. If the victim becomes unconscious, refer to resuscitation methods.
choking, first aid, adult
First Aid for Choking (Infant or Small Child)
Critical Source: Family Emergency Handbook
Procedure:

1. Support the infant's head and place it lower than the trunk.
2. Give four back blows to help dislodge the object.
3. Turn the infant face up, supporting their head.
4. Place two or three fingers on the breastbone between...
Procedure:

1. Support the infant's head and place it lower than the trunk.
2. Give four back blows to help dislodge the object.
3. Turn the infant face up, supporting their head.
4. Place two or three fingers on the breastbone between the nipples.
5. Give four chest thrusts.
6. Check the mouth for any visible objects and remove them if possible.
7. Give two quick puffs of breath to assist with airway clearance.
8. Repeat steps 2 through 7 until the object is dislodged or the infant becomes unconscious.
choking, infant, first aid

Cold Injuries Prevention (3) -

Prevent Minor Cold Injuries from Routine Duties
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

• Frost Burn Prevention: Avoid touching cold metal with bare hands.
• Fuel Handling Safety: When pouring fuel over the hands while filling stoves and lamps, use protective gloves to prevent second-degree frost-bite.
-...
Procedure:

• Frost Burn Prevention: Avoid touching cold metal with bare hands.
• Fuel Handling Safety: When pouring fuel over the hands while filling stoves and lamps, use protective gloves to prevent second-degree frost-bite.
• Contaminated Clothing Awareness: Be cautious of POL (petroleum oil lubricant) contaminated clothing, as it can cause frost-bite in contact areas.
• Dexterity Loss Mitigation: Take extra care with bulky clothing during routine duties to avoid cuts and bruises from reduced dexterity.
frost burn,fuel handling,contaminated clothing,dexterity loss,cold injury prevention
Identify Contributing Factors to Cold Injuries
Standard Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Contributing Factors to Cold Injuries:

Understanding the factors that contribute to cold injuries is essential for prevention and mitigation.

Key Contributing Factors Include:
• Moderate Cold with Wet or Windy Conditions: These...
Contributing Factors to Cold Injuries:

Understanding the factors that contribute to cold injuries is essential for prevention and mitigation.

Key Contributing Factors Include:
• Moderate Cold with Wet or Windy Conditions: These conditions increase the risk of cold injury significantly.
• Lack of Proper Training or Discipline: Individuals who are not well-trained or disciplined are more susceptible to cold injuries.
• Poor Personal Hygiene: Poor hygiene can lead to skin breakdown, increasing vulnerability to cold injury.
• Inadequate Exercise: Lack of regular exercise reduces circulation and increases the risk of cold injury.
• Neglect of Foot and Hand Care: Failure to properly care for feet and hands increases susceptibility to cold injuries.
• Failure to Use Protective Clothing: Not using appropriate protective clothing exposes individuals to greater risk from cold, wind, and moisture.
contributing factors, cold injuries, training, hygiene, exercise, foot care, hand care, protective clothing
Prevent Cold Injuries in Moderately Cold Conditions
Standard Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Prevention of Cold Injuries:

Cold injuries are most likely to occur when conditions are moderately cold, but accompanied by wet or windy conditions. Cold injuries can usually be prevented.

Key Preventive Measures:
1. **Maintain Discipline...
Prevention of Cold Injuries:

Cold injuries are most likely to occur when conditions are moderately cold, but accompanied by wet or windy conditions. Cold injuries can usually be prevented.

Key Preventive Measures:
1. Maintain Discipline and Training: Well-disciplined and well-trained service members can be protected even in the most adverse circumstances.
2. Understand Hazards of Exposure to the Cold: They and their leaders must know the hazards of exposure to the cold.
3. Personal Hygiene: Maintain good personal hygiene to reduce risk factors.
4. Exercise Regularly: Exercise helps maintain circulation and reduces the likelihood of cold injuries.
5. Care for Feet and Hands: Pay special attention to the care of the feet and hands, as these are particularly vulnerable to cold injury.
6. Use Protective Clothing: Use appropriate protective clothing to shield against cold, wind, and moisture.
cold injuries, prevention, personal hygiene, exercise, foot care, hand care, protective clothing

Chemical Burns And Poisoning (3) -

Protective Measures Against Blister Agents
Critical Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

1. If it is known or suspected that blister agents are being used:
a. STOP BREATHING immediately.
b. Put on your protective mask with hood.
c. Wear your protective overgarment.
2. CAUTION: Large drops of liquid...
Procedure:

1. If it is known or suspected that blister agents are being used:
a. STOP BREATHING immediately.
b. Put on your protective mask with hood.
c. Wear your protective overgarment.
2. CAUTION: Large drops of liquid vesicants on the protective overgarment ensemble may penetrate it if allowed to stand for an extended period.
- Remove large drops as soon as possible.
blister agents,protective mask,overgarment,vesicants,decontamination
Administering Additional Atropine Injections by Combat Lifesaver
High Priority Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

1. Verify if the individual has received three sets of MARK I or ATNAAs.
- If not, perform first aid as described for buddy aid above.
- If they have received the initial three sets of MARK I:
a. Administer additional...
Procedure:

1. Verify if the individual has received three sets of MARK I or ATNAAs.
- If not, perform first aid as described for buddy aid above.
- If they have received the initial three sets of MARK I:
a. Administer additional atropine injections at approximately 15-minute intervals until atropinization is achieved (defined as: heart rate above 90 beats per minute, reduced bronchial secretions, and reduced salivation).
b. Continue administering additional atropine at intervals of 30 minutes to 4 hours to maintain atropinization or until the casualty is placed under the care of medical personnel.
2. Check the heart rate by:
- Lifting the casualty’s mask hood.
- Feeling for a pulse at the carotid artery.
3. Request medical assistance as soon as the tactical situation permits.
atropine,combat lifesaver,MARK I,ATNAA,inject,heart rate,medical assistance
Administering Additional CANA Injections for Convulsions by Combat Lifesaver
High Priority Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

1. Administer a second and, if needed, a third CANA at 5 to 10-minute intervals for a maximum of three injections (30 milligrams diazepam).
2. Follow the steps and procedures described in buddy aid for administering the CANA.
3. **DO...
Procedure:

1. Administer a second and, if needed, a third CANA at 5 to 10-minute intervals for a maximum of three injections (30 milligrams diazepam).
2. Follow the steps and procedures described in buddy aid for administering the CANA.
3. DO NOT give more than two additional injections for a total of three (one self-aid plus two by the CLS).
CANA,convulsions,diazepam,combat lifesaver,injections

Sutures And Wound Closure (3) -

Proper Handling and Storage of MONOCRYL Sutures
Standard Source: Medical Survival Wound Closure Manual
MONOCRYL sutures are available as:

• Sterile monofilament strands: Dyed violet, sizes 6-0 through 2 (metric sizes 0.7–5), in various lengths.
• With or without needles: Available in sizes 3-0 through 1 (metric sizes 2–4) attached to...
MONOCRYL sutures are available as:

• Sterile monofilament strands: Dyed violet, sizes 6-0 through 2 (metric sizes 0.7–5), in various lengths.
• With or without needles: Available in sizes 3-0 through 1 (metric sizes 2–4) attached to CONTROL RELEASE* removable needles.

Packaging:
• Sold in one and three dozen boxes.

Important handling instructions:
• Do not resterilize opened packages or unused sutures.
• Discard any opened packages or unused material immediately.
• Avoid prolonged contact with salt solutions (e.g., urine, bile) to prevent calculus formation.
• As an absorbable suture, MONOCRYL may act transiently as a foreign body.
monocryl sutures,storage,handling,discard opened packages,salt solutions
Proper Handling of NUROLON* Nylon Sutures
Standard Source: Medical Survival Wound Closure Manual
Handling precautions for NUROLON suture:

• Avoid damage from surgical instruments: Do not crush or crimp the suture with forceps, needle holders, or other tools.
• Grasp the needle properly: Hold it one-third to one-half of the distance...
Handling precautions for NUROLON suture:

• Avoid damage from surgical instruments: Do not crush or crimp the suture with forceps, needle holders, or other tools.
• Grasp the needle properly: Hold it one-third to one-half of the distance from the swaged end to the point to prevent damage.
• Reshaping needles may reduce strength: Avoid reshaping to maintain structural integrity.
• Discard used needles safely: Place in designated "sharps" containers to prevent accidental needle sticks.

Description:
• NUROLON is a nonabsorbable, sterile braided suture made from Nylon 6 or Nylon 6,6.
• Available dyed black for enhanced visibility or undyed (clear).
• Meets U.S.P. standards for nonabsorbable surgical sutures.
nurolon suture,handling,needle safety,surgical instruments
Proper Knotting Technique for NUROLON* Sutures
Standard Source: Medical Survival Wound Closure Manual
To ensure adequate knot security:

• Use flat and square ties with additional throws as needed based on surgical circumstances.
• The number of throws should be determined by the surgeon’s experience and the specific situation.
• Ensure that...
To ensure adequate knot security:

• Use flat and square ties with additional throws as needed based on surgical circumstances.
• The number of throws should be determined by the surgeon’s experience and the specific situation.
• Ensure that the knots are tied tightly to prevent slippage or breakage, especially in high-tension areas.
nurolon suture,knotting technique,square ties

Respiratory Protection (3) -

Respiratory Protection for Operating Forces in TIC Hazard Areas
Critical Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. SCBA Use: For forces operating inside or near a TIC hazard area (e.g., reconnaissance or rescue personnel), use Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus (SCBA) as the primary protection.

2. Skin Protection Equipment: Wear...
Procedure:

1. SCBA Use: For forces operating inside or near a TIC hazard area (e.g., reconnaissance or rescue personnel), use Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus (SCBA) as the primary protection.

2. Skin Protection Equipment: Wear equipment certified for TIC use that prevents liquid or vapor exposure to skin.

Warnings:
• SCBA is essential in high-risk areas where evacuation is not possible.
• Ensure all protective gear is tested and certified for TIC use.
SCBA use, TIC hazard area, skin protection gear
Respiratory Protection for Evacuating Forces
Critical Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Military Filters: Standard issue NBC filters are not tested for TICs and should only be used in emergencies to escape the hazard zone.

2. Industrial Filters: If available, use industrial respirator filters specifically...
Procedure:

1. Military Filters: Standard issue NBC filters are not tested for TICs and should only be used in emergencies to escape the hazard zone.

2. Industrial Filters: If available, use industrial respirator filters specifically designed for identified TICs when the measured concentration is below the filter threshold.

3. Filter Limitations: Military filters may heat and burn when exposed to high concentrations of certain TICs.

Warnings:
• Do not rely on military filters as a long-term solution.
• Industrial respirators should only be used if appropriate for the specific TIC.
respirator use, TIC protection, military filters, industrial respirators
Use Respirator for Smoke Protection in Confined Spaces
Critical Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Wear a standard respirator whenever smokes are used in confined spaces.
2. The respirator provides adequate protection against all types of military smoke, including HC, CSA, FM, fog oil, and WP.
3. In open air, wear a...
Procedure:

1. Wear a standard respirator whenever smokes are used in confined spaces.
2. The respirator provides adequate protection against all types of military smoke, including HC, CSA, FM, fog oil, and WP.
3. In open air, wear a respirator if the smoke irritates the airway or is very thick.
4. Do not use a standard respirator for carbon monoxide exposure.

Warning: Failure to wear a respirator in confined spaces may result in severe respiratory damage or death.
respirator, smoke protection, confined space, chemical exposure

Skin Injuries (3) -

Treat Skin Injuries from Blister Agents
Critical Source: NATO Emergency War Surgery
Skin Treatment:

• Doctrinal treatment (FM 8-285) recommends:
- Opening and draining of blisters with removal of blister fluid.
- Syringe aspiration of bullous fluid from large blisters may be as effective.

**Supportive therapy for...
Skin Treatment:

• Doctrinal treatment (FM 8-285) recommends:
- Opening and draining of blisters with removal of blister fluid.
- Syringe aspiration of bullous fluid from large blisters may be as effective.

Supportive therapy for mustard burns:
• Similar to thermal burns: aggressive fluid replacement, pain relief, and vigilance against bacterial infection.

Forward medical resources:
• May be severely stressed in the event of widespread utilization of mustard gas by enemy forces.
mustard gas, blister treatment, skin injury, fluid replacement, infection prevention
Use Yarrow for Minor Burns and Rashes
Standard Source: USMC Summer Survival Course Handbook
Procedure:

1. Harvest fresh Yarrow leaves.
2. Crush the leaves into a compress or wrap them in cloth.
3. Apply the compress to the affected area (e.g., minor burns, poison oak/ivy rashes).
4. Leave the compress on for 10-15 minutes and reapply...
Procedure:

1. Harvest fresh Yarrow leaves.
2. Crush the leaves into a compress or wrap them in cloth.
3. Apply the compress to the affected area (e.g., minor burns, poison oak/ivy rashes).
4. Leave the compress on for 10-15 minutes and reapply as needed.

Notes:
• Do not confuse Yarrow with Poison Hemlock.
• This method is suitable for treating mild to moderate skin irritations.
yarrow, minor burns, rash treatment, first aid
Treating Sunburn
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Apply soothing agents: Use ordinary or carbolized vaseline to soothe the inflamed skin.
2. Prevent sunburn: Gradually harden the skin through exposure, and use toilet powder or boracic acid for protection.
3. **Avoid...
Procedure:

1. Apply soothing agents: Use ordinary or carbolized vaseline to soothe the inflamed skin.
2. Prevent sunburn: Gradually harden the skin through exposure, and use toilet powder or boracic acid for protection.
3. Avoid further irritation: Do not rub or scratch the affected area.

Warnings:
• Sunburn is an inflammation of the skin caused by prolonged sun exposure.
• Severe cases may require medical attention.
sunburn, treatment, vaseline, prevention, skin inflammation

Respirations (3) -

Treat a Sucking Chest Wound with a Vented Chest Seal
Critical Source: TCCC 17 13 Tactical Combat Casualty Care
Purpose: To manage a sucking chest wound in a tactical combat casualty care (TCCC) setting.

Procedure:
1. Place the vented chest seal directly over the wound, ensuring that it is centered and covers the entire opening.
2. Press firmly to...
Purpose: To manage a sucking chest wound in a tactical combat casualty care (TCCC) setting.

Procedure:
1. Place the vented chest seal directly over the wound, ensuring that it is centered and covers the entire opening.
2. Press firmly to secure the seal in place, making sure that there are no gaps or air leaks.
3. Monitor for signs of breathing and ensure that the seal remains in place.
4. If the seal becomes dislodged, reapply immediately.
vented chest seal, sucking chest wound, TCCC
Demonstrate Insertion of a Chest Tube (Medical Officers and Operating Room Special Operations Medics)
High Priority Source: TCCC 17 13 Army Tactical Combat Casualty Care
Procedure:

1. Ensure the casualty is in a safe location.
2. Identify the fifth intercostal space on the affected side of the chest.
3. Make an incision through the skin and pleura to insert the chest tube into the pleural cavity.
4. Secure the...
Procedure:

1. Ensure the casualty is in a safe location.
2. Identify the fifth intercostal space on the affected side of the chest.
3. Make an incision through the skin and pleura to insert the chest tube into the pleural cavity.
4. Secure the tube in place using sutures or other appropriate means.
5. Monitor for signs of complications such as bleeding or infection.
chest tube insertion, pneumothorax treatment
Demonstrate Oxygen Administration
High Priority Source: TCCC 17 13 Army Tactical Combat Casualty Care
Procedure:

1. Ensure the casualty is in a safe location.
2. Connect the oxygen source to the appropriate delivery device (nasal cannula, mask, etc.).
3. Adjust the flow rate according to the casualty's condition and medical guidelines.
4....
Procedure:

1. Ensure the casualty is in a safe location.
2. Connect the oxygen source to the appropriate delivery device (nasal cannula, mask, etc.).
3. Adjust the flow rate according to the casualty's condition and medical guidelines.
4. Monitor for signs of respiratory distress or improvement.
oxygen administration, respiratory support

Oral Hygiene Programs (3) -

Establish a Daily Oral Hygiene Routine at School
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Steps:
1. Encourage students to wash their hands before lunch and brush their teeth afterward.
2. Provide each student with a piece of soap and a toothbrush or brushstick (see page 4 for details).
3. Designate one day a week for the whole...
Steps:
1. Encourage students to wash their hands before lunch and brush their teeth afterward.
2. Provide each student with a piece of soap and a toothbrush or brushstick (see page 4 for details).
3. Designate one day a week for the whole class to treat their teeth with fluoride (see page 205 for instructions) to prevent cavities.

Details:
• This routine helps students develop lifelong habits of good oral hygiene.
• It also reinforces the importance of regular dental care.
daily hygiene, fluoride treatment, toothbrushing routine, school health program
Implement a Peer Assessment System for Oral Hygiene Progress
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Steps:
1. Have students score each other’s progress in maintaining good oral hygiene.
2. Ensure the scoring system is simple and easy to understand so that students are motivated to participate.
3. Use a visual chart or scale where each...
Steps:
1. Have students score each other’s progress in maintaining good oral hygiene.
2. Ensure the scoring system is simple and easy to understand so that students are motivated to participate.
3. Use a visual chart or scale where each tooth is either marked as clean or not clean.

Details:
• This peer assessment system encourages accountability and healthy competition among students.
• It also helps identify areas where additional education may be needed.
peer evaluation, oral hygiene scoring, student accountability, dental health tracking
Incorporate Daily Oral Hygiene into School Health Activities
Standard Source: Where There is No Dentist
Steps:
1. Make cleaning part of a daily health activity by assigning older students to look after younger ones.
2. Have the older students check the younger students’ hair for lice, sores for infection, and teeth for old food or germs.
3....
Steps:
1. Make cleaning part of a daily health activity by assigning older students to look after younger ones.
2. Have the older students check the younger students’ hair for lice, sores for infection, and teeth for old food or germs.
3. Encourage them to point out areas where washing and brushing can be improved.

Details:
• This activity promotes a culture of oral hygiene among students.
• It also helps identify potential dental issues early on.
daily cleaning, student health checks, oral hygiene routines, peer education

Dentistry (3) -

Checking for Blood Entry During Local Anesthetic Injection
High Priority Source: Where There is No Dentist
Before you inject the local anesthetic, wait a moment to see if any blood enters the syringe (Note: only an aspirating syringe will do this). Pull back on the plunger. If blood comes inside, it means you have poked a blood vessel. Pull the...
Before you inject the local anesthetic, wait a moment to see if any blood enters the syringe (Note: only an aspirating syringe will do this). Pull back on the plunger. If blood comes inside, it means you have poked a blood vessel. Pull the needle part way out and gently move it over to a different place.

If you inject local anesthetic into the blood vessel, there will be more swelling afterward, and the person may faint. If the person faints:
• Lie him on his back.
• Loosen his shirt collar.
• Lift his legs so they are higher than his head.
blood vessel detection, aspirating syringe, fainting response
Injecting Local Anesthetic for Upper Teeth Numbness
High Priority Source: Where There is No Dentist
1. First decide where to inject. Lift the lip or cheek. See the line that forms when it joins the gum.

The needle enters at the line where the lip or cheek meets the gum.
• 2. Push the needle in, aiming at the root of the tooth. Stop...
1. First decide where to inject. Lift the lip or cheek. See the line that forms when it joins the gum.

The needle enters at the line where the lip or cheek meets the gum.
• 2. Push the needle in, aiming at the root of the tooth. Stop when the needle hits bone. Inject about 1 ml of local anesthetic (½ of a cartridge). Pull the needle part way out and move it over to the next root. Inject again.

If the tooth is to be taken out, leave .25 ml for the next step.

3. If you are taking out a tooth, also inject the gums on the inside. Ask the person to open wide. Inject the remaining anesthetic (.25 ml) directly behind the back tooth that must come out.

One injection can numb the gum behind the 6 front teeth. Inject into the lump of gum behind the middle front teeth.

(Note: This injection hurts! It may help to use ‘pressure anesthesia.’ See page 141.)

4. Wait 5 minutes for the tooth to become numb.
upper teeth injection, local anesthetic dosage, pressure anesthesia
Proper Needle Orientation for Local Anesthetic Injection
High Priority Source: Where There is No Dentist
Before you push the needle under the skin, be sure its pointed end is facing in the correct direction. The local anesthetic must come out against the bone, where the nerve is.
needle orientation, dental injection, nerve location

Care Of Sick Person (3) -

Ensure Adequate Liquids for a Sick Person
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

1. Provide plenty of liquids to the sick person in nearly every sickness, especially when there is fever or diarrhea.
2. Offer water, tea, juices, broths, and other fluids to maintain hydration.
hydration, liquid intake, fever care, diarrhea management
Maintain Personal Cleanliness for a Sick Person
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

1. Bathe the sick person every day.
2. If they are too sick to get out of bed, wash them with a sponge or cloth and lukewarm water.
3. Keep their clothes, sheets, and covers clean.
4. **Avoid crumbs and bits of food in the...
Procedure:

1. Bathe the sick person every day.
2. If they are too sick to get out of bed, wash them with a sponge or cloth and lukewarm water.
3. Keep their clothes, sheets, and covers clean.
4. Avoid crumbs and bits of food in the bed to prevent infection.
personal hygiene, bathing sick person, infection prevention
Provide Comfort for a Sick Person
Reference Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

1. Ensure the sick person rests in a quiet, comfortable place.
2. Provide fresh air and light.
3. Keep the person from getting too hot or cold: If the air is cold or the person is chilled, cover them with a sheet or...
Procedure:

1. Ensure the sick person rests in a quiet, comfortable place.
2. Provide fresh air and light.
3. Keep the person from getting too hot or cold: If the air is cold or the person is chilled, cover them with a sheet or blanket. If the weather is hot or the person has a fever, do not cover them at all (see p. 75).
comfort care, rest, temperature regulation, fever management

Injections Medical Procedures (3) -

How to Inject Medicine
High Priority Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Before injecting, wash hands with soap and water.

1. Clean the skin with soap and water (or alcohol—but to prevent severe pain, be sure the alcohol is dry before injecting).
2. Put the needle straight in, all the way. (**If it is done with...
Before injecting, wash hands with soap and water.

1. Clean the skin with soap and water (or alcohol—but to prevent severe pain, be sure the alcohol is dry before injecting).
2. Put the needle straight in, all the way. (If it is done with one quick movement, it hurts less).
3. Before injecting, pull back on the plunger. (If blood enters the syringe, take the needle out and put it in somewhere else).
4. If no blood enters, inject the medicine.
5. Remove the needle and clean the skin slowly.
6. After injecting, rinse the syringe and needle at once. Squirt water through the needle and then take the syringe apart and wash it. Boil before using again.
administering injections, injection technique, post-injection care
Prepare a Syringe for Injection
High Priority Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Before preparing a syringe, wash hands with soap and water.

1. Take the syringe apart and boil it and the needle for 20 minutes.
2. Pour out the boiled water without touching the syringe or the needle.
3. Put the needle and the syringe...
Before preparing a syringe, wash hands with soap and water.

1. Take the syringe apart and boil it and the needle for 20 minutes.
2. Pour out the boiled water without touching the syringe or the needle.
3. Put the needle and the syringe together, touching only the base of the needle and the button of the plunger.
4. Clean the ampule of distilled water well, then break off the top.
5. Fill the syringe (Be careful that the needle does not touch the outside of the ampule).
6. Rub the rubber of the bottle with clean cloth, wet with alcohol or boiled water, and pour the powdered medicine into the bottle.
7. Shake until the medicine dissolves.
8. Fill the syringe again.
9. Remove all air from the syringe.

Be very careful not to touch the needle with anything—not even the cotton with alcohol. If by chance the needle touches your finger or something else, boil it again.
syringe preparation, injection procedure, boiling syringe, medical equipment sterilization
Where to Give an Injection
High Priority Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Before injecting, wash hands with soap and water.

• It is preferable to inject in the muscle of the buttocks, always in the upper outer quarter.
• _WARNING_: Do not inject into an area of skin that is infected or has a rash.
• Do not...
Before injecting, wash hands with soap and water.

• It is preferable to inject in the muscle of the buttocks, always in the upper outer quarter.
• _WARNING_: Do not inject into an area of skin that is infected or has a rash.
• Do not inject infants and small children in the buttock. Inject them in the upper outer part of the thigh.
injection site, safe injection practices, infant injection guidelines

Hygiene Practices (3) -

Maintain Hygiene for Children's Health
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Purpose: To reduce the risk of infection and disease in children.

Procedure:
1. Bathe children regularly.
2. Change their clothes frequently.
3. Cut their fingernails often to prevent germs and worm eggs from hiding beneath them.
4. Teach...
Purpose: To reduce the risk of infection and disease in children.

Procedure:
1. Bathe children regularly.
2. Change their clothes frequently.
3. Cut their fingernails often to prevent germs and worm eggs from hiding beneath them.
4. Teach children about personal hygiene and encourage them to help with projects that improve home or village cleanliness.

Notes: Good hygiene is essential for preventing infections and maintaining overall health.
child hygiene, personal cleanliness, infection prevention
Practice Good Hygiene to Prevent Disease Spread
Standard Source: FM 3 4 NBC Protection
Procedure:

1. Soldiers must practice good personal hygiene to prevent the spread of disease and protect against biological agents.
2. Clean all parts of the body, especially the face, hands, feet, and exposed skin.
3. Wash hands before meals or...
Procedure:

1. Soldiers must practice good personal hygiene to prevent the spread of disease and protect against biological agents.
2. Clean all parts of the body, especially the face, hands, feet, and exposed skin.
3. Wash hands before meals or anytime bare hands are used for eating, drinking, or smoking.
4. Brush teeth regularly and shave to ensure a proper seal of the mask.
5. Clean any small nicks, scratches, or cuts with soap and water followed by first-aid treatment.
hygiene,nbc conditions,biological agents,wound care,mask seal
Promote Regular Application of Safe Water Practices
Standard Source: Safe Water Manual
Objective: Ensure that students translate theory into practical application.

Procedure:
1. Establish a Safe Water Club composed of a teacher and motivated children to lead activities related to water treatment and hygiene practices.
2....
Objective: Ensure that students translate theory into practical application.

Procedure:
1. Establish a Safe Water Club composed of a teacher and motivated children to lead activities related to water treatment and hygiene practices.
2. Conduct regular, hands-on activities such as:
- Demonstrating proper hand washing techniques
- Practicing water filtration methods
- Cleaning and maintaining water storage facilities
3. Encourage students to apply these practices in their daily lives both at school and within their families.
4. Monitor the effectiveness of these activities through periodic assessments and feedback from participants.
safe water club, hygiene application, practical training, school activities

Parasitic Infections (3) -

Removing Nits from Hair Using Natural Methods
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

1. Apply olive oil: Rub olive oil into the hair to loosen nits for easier removal with a fine-tooth comb.
2. Use vinegar water soak:
- Mix 1 part vinegar to 1 part water and soak the hair for half an hour.
- Then...
Procedure:

1. Apply olive oil: Rub olive oil into the hair to loosen nits for easier removal with a fine-tooth comb.
2. Use vinegar water soak:
- Mix 1 part vinegar to 1 part water and soak the hair for half an hour.
- Then thoroughly comb the hair with a fine-tooth comb to remove all remaining nits.
3. Repeat daily: Continue this process every day for two weeks to ensure complete removal of lice and nits.
nits,removal,olive oil,vinegar water,comb
Treating Body Lice with Hot Water Baths and Hygiene Measures
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

1. Soak in hot water: Take a bath in hot water every day for 10 days.
2. Wash thoroughly with soap: After each bath, wash the body with soap and rinse well.
3. Use a fine-tooth comb: Comb any hairy areas to remove...
Procedure:

1. Soak in hot water: Take a bath in hot water every day for 10 days.
2. Wash thoroughly with soap: After each bath, wash the body with soap and rinse well.
3. Use a fine-tooth comb: Comb any hairy areas to remove lice.
4. Treat as for scabies if necessary: Follow the same procedures used for treating scabies if the infestation is severe.
5. Maintain cleanliness:
- Keep clothing and bedding clean at all times.
body lice,treatment,hot water,bathing,hygiene
Treating Head and Pubic Lice with Non-Medicated Methods
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

1. Scrub hair thoroughly: Use regular soap or shampoo for 10 minutes, then rinse well.
2. Use a fine-tooth comb: Carefully remove all lice and their eggs from the hair.
3. Repeat daily for two weeks: Continue this process...
Procedure:

1. Scrub hair thoroughly: Use regular soap or shampoo for 10 minutes, then rinse well.
2. Use a fine-tooth comb: Carefully remove all lice and their eggs from the hair.
3. Repeat daily for two weeks: Continue this process every day to ensure complete removal of lice and nits.
4. Avoid using lindane shampoos: Lindane is poison!
5. Use medicated shampoos if needed:
- Pyrethrins (RID) or permethrin (Nix) may be effective.
- Follow the instructions carefully, avoid contact with eyes, and watch for allergic reactions.
- Avoid use during pregnancy or in children under 2 years old.
lice,treatment,head lice,pubic lice,nits,shampoo,comb

Eye Infections (3) -

Treat Infection of the Tear Sac (Dacryocystitis)
High Priority Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Signs of Dacryocystitis:

• Redness, pain, and swelling beneath the eye near the nose.
• Excessive tearing from the affected eye.
• Pus may appear in the corner of the eye when the swollen area is gently pressed.

Treatment Steps:

1....
Signs of Dacryocystitis:

• Redness, pain, and swelling beneath the eye near the nose.
• Excessive tearing from the affected eye.
• Pus may appear in the corner of the eye when the swollen area is gently pressed.

Treatment Steps:

1. Apply hot compresses to the affected area regularly.
2. Use antibiotic eye drops or ointment as prescribed by a health worker.
3. Take penicillin (see page 350 of this book for dosage instructions).
dacryocystitis, tear sac infection, hot compresses, antibiotic drops, penicillin
Treat Pus Behind the Cornea (Hypopyon)
High Priority Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

1. Recognize the condition:
- Pus behind the cornea is a sign of severe inflammation and may be associated with corneal ulcers.
2. Apply antibiotic eye ointment as directed in the handbook (see page 378 for specific...
Procedure:

1. Recognize the condition:
- Pus behind the cornea is a sign of severe inflammation and may be associated with corneal ulcers.
2. Apply antibiotic eye ointment as directed in the handbook (see page 378 for specific instructions).
3. Seek immediate medical help from an eye doctor to prevent further complications.
4. Monitor progress:
- If the ulcer is treated correctly, the hypopyon may clear up on its own over time.

Note: This condition requires prompt treatment to avoid permanent damage to the eye.
hypopyon, corneal ulcer, antibiotic eye ointment, eye infection, medical help
Treat a Sty (Hordeolum)
Standard Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

1. Identify the sty: A red, swollen lump on the eyelid, usually near its edge.
2. Apply warm, moist compresses to the affected area:
- Use water with a small amount of salt added (e.g., 1 teaspoon salt per cup of warm...
Procedure:

1. Identify the sty: A red, swollen lump on the eyelid, usually near its edge.
2. Apply warm, moist compresses to the affected area:
- Use water with a small amount of salt added (e.g., 1 teaspoon salt per cup of warm water).
- Apply for 10–15 minutes several times a day until the sty begins to drain.
3. Apply an antibiotic eye ointment three times a day:
- This helps prevent further sties from forming and reduces infection risk.
4. Do not attempt to squeeze or pop the sty, as this may spread infection.
5. If symptoms persist or worsen after several days, seek medical help.
sty,hordeolum,warm compress,salt water,antibiotic ointment

Respiratory Care (3) -

Percussion and Postural Drainage for Mucus Clearance
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Objective: Loosen mucus and help the patient expel it.

Procedure:
1. Position the patient on their side on a pillow.
2. Tilt the head downward, ensuring the bottom is above the level of the head.
3. Use a cupped hand (not flat)...
Objective: Loosen mucus and help the patient expel it.

Procedure:
1. Position the patient on their side on a pillow.
2. Tilt the head downward, ensuring the bottom is above the level of the head.
3. Use a cupped hand (not flat) to gently tap the patient’s side, just under the armpit and across the back.
4. Repeat this process on both sides until the patient can expel mucus.
5. The position may be adjusted with the patient lying on their front or back, but the head-down position is essential.

Note: This technique is commonly used by caregivers for patients with chronic respiratory conditions such as cystic fibrosis.
mucus clearance, percussion, postural drainage, respiratory care
Breathing Exercise to Improve Lung Function and Mucus Clearance
Standard Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Objective: Help the patient improve lung function and clear mucus.

Procedure:
1. Blow up a balloon or perform a similar activity that requires deep breathing.
2. Alternatively, have the patient **suck in air through a straw for several...
Objective: Help the patient improve lung function and clear mucus.

Procedure:
1. Blow up a balloon or perform a similar activity that requires deep breathing.
2. Alternatively, have the patient suck in air through a straw for several minutes, repeating this 3-4 times per day.
3. This helps fully inflate the lungs and open smaller airways, promoting mucus clearance.
mucus clearance, breathing exercise, lung function, chest infection
Administer Decongestants Safely
Reference Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Decongestant is a medicine used to relieve swelling or stuffiness of the nose or sinuses. To use decongestants safely:

1. Follow the dosage instructions on the label.
2. Do not exceed the recommended dose.
3. Avoid using decongestants for more...
Decongestant is a medicine used to relieve swelling or stuffiness of the nose or sinuses. To use decongestants safely:

1. Follow the dosage instructions on the label.
2. Do not exceed the recommended dose.
3. Avoid using decongestants for more than 3-5 days without consulting a healthcare provider, as prolonged use can lead to rebound congestion.
4. Be cautious if you have high blood pressure or heart conditions, as decongestants may worsen these conditions.

Warning: Decongestants should not be used by individuals with certain medical conditions unless advised by a healthcare professional.
decongestant, nasal congestion, sinus relief

Digestive Health (3) -

Identify and Manage Hemorrhoids (Piles)
Reference Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Hemorrhoids, also known as piles, are small, painful bumps or lumps at the edge of the anus or inside it. They are swollen or varicose veins.

Management includes:
• Applying cold compresses to reduce swelling.
• Using over-the-counter creams or...
Hemorrhoids, also known as piles, are small, painful bumps or lumps at the edge of the anus or inside it. They are swollen or varicose veins.

Management includes:
• Applying cold compresses to reduce swelling.
• Using over-the-counter creams or ointments for relief.
• Increasing fiber intake and staying hydrated to prevent constipation, which can worsen hemorrhoids.
• Avoiding straining during bowel movements.
hemorrhoids, piles, anal pain
Manage Constipation Through Routine and Exercise
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Manage Constipation Through Routine and Exercise

To overcome constipation, follow this routine:

1. Drink a cool, copious draught of water upon arising. This helps to hydrate the body and stimulate digestion.
2. **Take some body-bending...
Manage Constipation Through Routine and Exercise

To overcome constipation, follow this routine:

1. Drink a cool, copious draught of water upon arising. This helps to hydrate the body and stimulate digestion.
2. Take some body-bending exercises. These help to promote bowel movement and improve overall physical health.
3. Follow with a sponge bath. A sponge bath can relax the body and prepare it for further activity.
4. If possible, take a walk around the block before breakfast. Physical activity is essential in promoting regular bowel movements.
5. After school, play some favorite game for at least an hour. Regular physical exercise helps maintain healthy digestion.
6. In the absence of this, take a good hike of three or four miles or a longer bicycle ride. If games are not available, long walks or bike rides can be effective substitutes.
7. At least twice a week, if possible, enter a gymnasium class and make special emphasis on body-bending exercises. Consistent physical activity is key to managing constipation.

Establishing a Regular Routine:
• Have a regular time for going to stool. A good plan is to go just before retiring and immediately upon arising.
• Go even if you feel no desire to do so. Establishing this habit can help regulate digestion.
• Always respond quickly to any call of nature. Delaying bowel movements can worsen constipation.

Dietary Recommendations:
• Toasted bread, graham bread, coarser foods, and fruits are helpful in managing constipation.
• These foods provide fiber and promote healthy digestion.
constipation,digestion,routine,exercise,fiber
Identify and Manage Heartburn Symptoms
Reference Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Heartburn is characterized by a burning sensation in the lower chest or upper part of the stomach.

This discomfort may be caused by acid reflux, overeating, or lying down after eating. It can often be relieved by sitting upright, avoiding spicy...
Heartburn is characterized by a burning sensation in the lower chest or upper part of the stomach.

This discomfort may be caused by acid reflux, overeating, or lying down after eating. It can often be relieved by sitting upright, avoiding spicy or fatty foods, and taking antacids if available.
heartburn, acid reflux, stomach pain

Cold Weather Injuries (3) -

Recognize and Treat Shock in Cold Weather Conditions
Critical Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Identify signs of shock: Look for the following symptoms:
- Apprehension or anxiety
- Sweating
- Pallor (pale skin)
- Rapid, faint pulse
- Cold, clammy skin
- Thirst
2. Reassure the casualty: Calm and...
Procedure:

1. Identify signs of shock: Look for the following symptoms:
- Apprehension or anxiety
- Sweating
- Pallor (pale skin)
- Rapid, faint pulse
- Cold, clammy skin
- Thirst
2. Reassure the casualty: Calm and comfort the individual to reduce pain and stress.
3. Reduce pain through proper positioning: Position the casualty so that their head and chest are slightly lower than their legs (about one foot difference) if it does not cause discomfort.
4. Keep the casualty warm: Maintain normal body temperature by covering them with blankets, clothing, or any available insulating material.
5. Minimize movement: Avoid unnecessary movement of the casualty to prevent further injury or shock progression. This includes moving from one stretcher to another or repositioning during treatment.
6. Loosen tight clothing: If weather conditions permit, loosen clothing around the neck, chest, and waist to improve circulation.
7. Provide warm fluids (if conscious): Offer small sips of warm soup, tea, cocoa, coffee, or any non-alcoholic liquid to help maintain body temperature and hydration.
8. Seek medical attention immediately: Ensure the casualty is evacuated to an aid station or medical facility as soon as possible for further treatment.

Important Notes:
• Shock can develop more rapidly in cold weather due to reduced blood flow and increased risk of hypothermia.
• Alcohol should never be given to a casualty, as it can worsen shock and lower body temperature.
shock treatment,cold weather,first aid,warm fluids,body temperature
Administer First Aid in Cold Weather Conditions
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Stop the bleeding: Apply direct pressure to the wound using a clean dressing until the bleeding stops.
2. Apply a dressing: Once bleeding is controlled, cover the wound with a sterile dressing and secure it in place.
3....
Procedure:

1. Stop the bleeding: Apply direct pressure to the wound using a clean dressing until the bleeding stops.
2. Apply a dressing: Once bleeding is controlled, cover the wound with a sterile dressing and secure it in place.
3. Use a splint if necessary: If there is a fracture or suspected fracture, apply a splint to immobilize the injured area.
4. Cover the wound to protect from cold: Quickly wrap the wounded area with a bandage or cloth to prevent exposure to cold air and further heat loss.
5. Begin anti-shock treatment immediately: Keep the casualty warm and in a stable position to prevent shock.
6. Do not leave the casualty unattended: Ensure continuous monitoring and provide reassurance until professional medical help arrives.

Important Notes:
• In cold weather, wounds bleed more easily due to reduced blood clotting.
• Wounds exposed to the cold can freeze quickly, increasing the risk of shock and infection.
• Early first aid is critical in cold environments to prevent complications from hypothermia or frostbite.
first aid,cold weather,wound treatment,shock prevention,blood clotting
Provide First Aid for Immersion Foot and Trench Foot
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1 + STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks + Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

1. Assess the casualty:
- Look for signs such as cold, painless feet; weak pulse; numbness; hot feeling; burning or shooting pains; pale skin with a bluish cast; swelling; blistering; redness; heat; hemorrhaging; and...
Procedure:

1. Assess the casualty:
- Look for signs such as cold, painless feet; weak pulse; numbness; hot feeling; burning or shooting pains; pale skin with a bluish cast; swelling; blistering; redness; heat; hemorrhaging; and gangrene.
- Consider exposure history (e.g., long exposure of feet to wet conditions at temperatures from about 50° F to 32° F).

2. Remove the affected individual from wet conditions immediately.

3. Dry the feet thoroughly:
- Remove wet socks and boots.
- Dry the feet completely using clean, dry materials.

4. Gradually rewarm the affected area:
- Use warm air (not direct heat) to rewarm the feet slowly.
- Avoid exposing the injured part to open fire or extreme heat.

5. Protect from trauma and infection:
- Cover the feet with dry, loose clothing or several layers of warm coverings.

6. Elevate the affected area:
- Elevate the feet above heart level to reduce swelling.

7. Transport to medical treatment facility (MTF):
- Move the casualty to an MTF as quickly and safely as possible for further evaluation and treatment.

8. Monitor symptoms:
- The casualty may experience burning sensations, progression of blisters, or other severe signs (e.g., gangrene).
- Seek professional medical evaluation if symptoms progress.

Warnings:
• Do NOT expose the injured part to an open fire.
• Do not attempt to rewarm or treat immersion foot/trench foot without proper guidance.
immersion foot, trench foot, first aid, cold weather injury, rewarming, infection prevention

Intravenous Care (3) -

Start an Intravenous Infusion for Hypovolemic Shock
Critical Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Overview: This procedure outlines how to start an intravenous (IV) infusion in a casualty experiencing hypovolemic shock from combat injuries, with abnormal mental status or no palpable radial pulse. A saline lock must already be...
Overview: This procedure outlines how to start an intravenous (IV) infusion in a casualty experiencing hypovolemic shock from combat injuries, with abnormal mental status or no palpable radial pulse. A saline lock must already be established.

### Conditions:
• The casualty is in hypovolemic shock due to combat injuries.
• The casualty has an abnormal mental status (AVPU) or no palpable radial (wrist) pulse.
• Bleeding has been controlled, and a saline lock has been established.
• Medical personnel will provide any supplies not carried by the individual performing this task.

### Supplies Required:
• IV solution bag
• IV infusion set
• 18-gauge catheter/needle unit
• Tape
• Scissors

### Steps:
1. Initiate an IV fluid line: Connect the IV infusion set to the saline lock and ensure that it is properly secured.
2. Maintain the IV line without causing further injury: Ensure that the IV line is maintained in a way that does not cause additional harm or discomfort to the casualty.
3. Monitor the casualty continuously: Keep an eye on the casualty's condition throughout the infusion process, watching for any signs of improvement or deterioration.
4. Ensure proper flow rate: Adjust the IV solution bag and infusion set as needed to maintain a safe and effective flow rate of fluid into the casualty.
5. Secure the IV line: Use tape to secure the IV line in place, ensuring that it is not likely to become dislodged or kinked during movement or activity.
6. Monitor for complications: Watch for signs of infiltration, infection, or other complications at the insertion site and address them promptly if they occur.
7. Document all actions: Keep a record of the IV infusion process, including the time it was started, the type and volume of fluid administered, and any changes in the casualty's condition.

### Additional Considerations:
• Follow universal precautions for handling sharps and contaminated waste as outlined by CDC guidelines.
• If the casualty becomes unstable or shows signs of worsening condition during the infusion process, seek immediate medical assistance.
intravenous infusion, hypovolemic shock, first aid, medical procedures, combat injuries
Prepare IV Infusion Set
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

Step 1: Obtain needed supplies.

Step 2: Prepare the IV infusion.

a. Remove the infusion set from the package.

b. Loosen the clamp, move it along the tubing until it is 6 to 8 inches from the drip chamber,...
Procedure:

Step 1: Obtain needed supplies.

Step 2: Prepare the IV infusion.

a. Remove the infusion set from the package.

b. Loosen the clamp, move it along the tubing until it is 6 to 8 inches from the drip chamber, and tighten the clamp.

c. Remove the protective covering from the outlet port (long spout) on the IV bag. Do not let the tip of the outlet port touch anything until the spike has been inserted.

d. Remove the protective cap from the spike on the infusion set.

- Grasp the drip chamber with one hand and the spike cap with the other hand.
- Remove the cap with a twisting motion without touching the spike.

e. Insert the spike into the exposed IV outlet port with a twisting motion. The spike will penetrate the seal in the outlet port. Do not touch the end of the port or the spike during the procedure.

f. Hang the bag on an object above the level of the casualty's heart, if possible, or hold the bag up until you have completely removed the air from the tubing.

g. Squeeze the drip chamber until half of the chamber is filled with IV solution.
IV infusion setup, spike insertion, drip chamber, clamp positioning
Remove Air from IV Tubing
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

Step 1: Hold the end of the tubing above the bottom of the bag.

Step 2: Release or loosen the tubing clamp.

Step 3: Loosen the protective cap over the adapter.

Step 4: Gradually lower the tubing until the solution...
Procedure:

Step 1: Hold the end of the tubing above the bottom of the bag.

Step 2: Release or loosen the tubing clamp.

Step 3: Loosen the protective cap over the adapter.

Step 4: Gradually lower the tubing until the solution reaches the end of the adapter.

Step 5: Reclamp the tubing and retighten the cap over the adapter.

Note: This step ensures that air is removed from the IV tubing before administration.
air removal from IV tubing, clamp positioning, adapter cap

Combat Medical (3) -

Monitor an Unconscious Casualty During Evacuation
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Objective: Monitor an unconscious casualty's airway, breathing, and bleeding during evacuation.

Steps:
1. Continuously monitor the airway to ensure it remains open. If necessary, reposition the casualty or clear any obstructions.
2....
Objective: Monitor an unconscious casualty's airway, breathing, and bleeding during evacuation.

Steps:
1. Continuously monitor the airway to ensure it remains open. If necessary, reposition the casualty or clear any obstructions.
2. Check for breathing periodically by looking, listening, and feeling for airflow at the mouth and nose.
3. Monitor for signs of bleeding, such as visible wounds or blood loss, and apply pressure if needed.
4. Ensure the casualty is secured properly during transport to prevent further injury.
monitor unconscious casualty, airway monitoring, breathing check, bleeding control, evacuation transport
Provide Care Under Fire
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Objective: Provide care under fire to casualties.

Steps:
1. Suppress enemy fire to keep the casualty from sustaining additional wounds.
2. Encourage responsive casualties to protect themselves and perform self-aid, if able.
3....
Objective: Provide care under fire to casualties.

Steps:
1. Suppress enemy fire to keep the casualty from sustaining additional wounds.
2. Encourage responsive casualties to protect themselves and perform self-aid, if able.
3. Administer life-saving hemorrhage control for any visible bleeding.
4. Transport casualties, weapons, and mission-essential equipment when the tactical situation permits.
care under fire, suppress enemy fire, self-aid, hemorrhage control, transport casualties
Evaluate and Identify Wounds or Conditions in a Casualty
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Objective: Evaluate and identify all wounds or conditions in a casualty.

Steps:
1. Systematically inspect the casualty's body for visible injuries such as wounds, burns, fractures, or signs of internal bleeding.
2. **Ask responsive...
Objective: Evaluate and identify all wounds or conditions in a casualty.

Steps:
1. Systematically inspect the casualty's body for visible injuries such as wounds, burns, fractures, or signs of internal bleeding.
2. Ask responsive casualties about the location of pain or other symptoms they may be experiencing.
3. Use available tools or materials to assist in identifying and assessing injuries (e.g., field medical card, casualty simulation kit).
4. Document all identified wounds or conditions, including their severity and location, for proper treatment and evacuation.
evaluate casualty, identify wounds, document injuries, assess conditions

Tactical Casualty Care (3) -

Coordinate Treatment of Casualties Using Self-Aid/Buddy Aid
High Priority Source: STP 21 24 Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 4
Procedure:

Steps:
1. Determine the availability and capability of self-aid or buddy aid.
2. Assign roles based on the tactical situation and available resources (e.g., one Soldier may provide care while another coordinates evacuation).
3....
Procedure:

Steps:
1. Determine the availability and capability of self-aid or buddy aid.
2. Assign roles based on the tactical situation and available resources (e.g., one Soldier may provide care while another coordinates evacuation).
3. Ensure that all actions are performed in accordance with TCCC guidelines.
4. Provide immediate life-saving interventions such as controlling bleeding, splinting fractures, and applying tourniquets if necessary.
5. Monitor the casualty’s condition continuously during treatment and coordination.

Notes:
• Self-aid refers to a Soldier treating themselves for injuries.
• Buddy aid refers to one Soldier providing care to another.
• Coordination should be based on the number of casualties, available personnel, and tactical conditions.

Warnings:
• Do not attempt to treat injuries beyond your training or capability without assistance.
• Ensure that all actions are performed in a manner that minimizes risk to both the casualty and yourself.
coordinate treatment,self-aid,buddy aid,TCCC,tactical casualty care
Evaluate Casualties According to Tactical Situation
High Priority Source: STP 21 24 Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 4
Procedure:

Steps:
1. Assess the tactical environment and level of threat.
2. Determine if casualties are under effective hostile fire (Care Under Fire phase) or in a relatively safe area (Tactical Field Care phase).
3. If evacuation is...
Procedure:

Steps:
1. Assess the tactical environment and level of threat.
2. Determine if casualties are under effective hostile fire (Care Under Fire phase) or in a relatively safe area (Tactical Field Care phase).
3. If evacuation is required, transition to Combat Casualty Evacuation Care phase.
4. Use established guidelines for Tactical Combat Casualty Care (TCCC), as outlined in STP 21-1 SMCT, task 081-831-1001.

Notes:
• In the Care Under Fire phase, prioritize immediate life-saving actions and minimize exposure to enemy fire.
• In the Tactical Field Care phase, provide comprehensive care such as controlling bleeding, splinting fractures, and applying tourniquets.
• During Combat Casualty Evacuation Care, ensure the casualty is secured for transport without causing further injury.

Warnings:
• Do not delay life-saving actions to wait for evacuation.
• Ensure all actions are performed in coordination with available resources and tactical conditions.
evaluate casualties,tactical situation,TCCC,Care Under Fire,Tactical Field Care,Combat Casualty Evacuation Care
Move a Casualty Using a Four-Man Litter Squad
High Priority Source: STP 21 24 Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 4
Procedure:

Steps:
1. Assemble a four-man litter squad with designated roles (bearer #1 is the leader).
2. Ensure that the casualty is properly secured to the litter using straps or improvised materials.
3. Position the litter in a safe and...
Procedure:

Steps:
1. Assemble a four-man litter squad with designated roles (bearer #1 is the leader).
2. Ensure that the casualty is properly secured to the litter using straps or improvised materials.
3. Position the litter in a safe and stable manner for transport.
4. Coordinate movement of the litter as a team, ensuring smooth and controlled motion.
5. Load the litter onto a military vehicle (ground or rotary-wing aircraft) without causing further injury to the casualty.
6. Ensure that all personnel involved are aware of their roles and responsibilities during the evacuation process.

Notes:
• If military vehicles or litter materials are not available, improvise using available resources.
• The four-man litter squad should move in a coordinated manner to ensure the safety of both the casualty and the bearers.
• Ensure that the casualty is loaded onto the vehicle without dropping or causing additional harm.

Warnings:
• Do not attempt to move a casualty if it could cause further injury.
• Ensure that all personnel are trained in the proper use of litters and evacuation procedures.
move casualty,four-man litter squad,tactical evacuation

Respiratory Emergencies (3) -

Check for Breathing Using the Look, Listen, Feel Method
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid + Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

1. Look for the chest to rise and fall.
2. Listen for air escaping during exhalation by placing your ear near the casualty’s mouth.
3. Feel for the flow of air on your cheek.
4. If breathing is not detected within 3 to 5...
Procedure:

1. Look for the chest to rise and fall.
2. Listen for air escaping during exhalation by placing your ear near the casualty’s mouth.
3. Feel for the flow of air on your cheek.
4. If breathing is not detected within 3 to 5 seconds, perform rescue breathing.

Notes:
• Maintain an open airway while checking for breathing.
• If the casualty resumes breathing, continue to monitor and maintain the open airway.
• Transport the casualty to a medical treatment facility as soon as practical.
breathing check, look listen feel, rescue breathing, airway management
Rescue Breathing (Artificial Respiration)
Reference Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Procedure:

If the casualty does not promptly resume adequate spontaneous breathing after the airway is open:

1. Be calm and act quickly.
2. Ensure the scene is safe and check for responsiveness of the injured person.
3. Open the airway by...
Procedure:

If the casualty does not promptly resume adequate spontaneous breathing after the airway is open:

1. Be calm and act quickly.
2. Ensure the scene is safe and check for responsiveness of the injured person.
3. Open the airway by tilting the head back slightly and lifting the chin to clear any obstructions.
4. If in doubt whether the casualty is breathing, give artificial respiration since it can do no harm to a person who is breathing.
5. Confirm breathing by feeling and seeing chest movement or hearing air being expelled near the mouth and nose.
6. Begin rescue breathing immediately to increase the likelihood of restoring the casualty’s breathing.
7. Pinch the nose shut and place your mouth fully over the person’s mouth, creating a seal (use the mouth-to-mouth method if possible; otherwise, use the mouth-to-nose method in cases of severe jaw fracture, mouth wound, or tightly closed jaws).
8. Blow gently into the person’s mouth for about 1 second, ensuring that their chest rises visibly.
9. Remove your mouth and allow the person to exhale naturally.
10. Repeat this process at a rate of 10–12 breaths per minute until the person begins breathing on their own or professional help arrives.

Notes:
• The sooner you begin rescue breathing, the more effective it will be.
• Rescue breathing should be performed only if the person is not breathing and has no pulse.
• If you are unable to perform mouth-to-mouth, use mouth-to-nose instead.
• Continue rescue breathing until trained medical personnel take over.
rescue breathing, artificial respiration, CPR, first aid, airway management
Understanding the Breathing Process and Oxygen Dependency
Reference Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid + Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Breathing Process and Oxygen Dependency

All humans require oxygen to survive. Through the breathing process, the lungs draw oxygen from the air and transfer it into the blood. The heart then pumps this oxygenated blood throughout the body to...
Breathing Process and Oxygen Dependency

All humans require oxygen to survive. Through the breathing process, the lungs draw oxygen from the air and transfer it into the blood. The heart then pumps this oxygenated blood throughout the body to supply cells with a constant oxygen supply.

Some cells are more dependent on continuous oxygen than others (e.g., brain and heart muscle cells). A disruption in the breathing process can lead to serious injury or death. For example, brain cells may die within 4 to 6 minutes without oxygen, leading to permanent damage, paralysis, or death. This highlights the critical importance of maintaining proper breathing in first aid situations.
breathing process, oxygen dependency, first aid, respiration, brain cells, heart muscle cells

Spider Bite Treatment (2) -

Identify and Respond to Tarantula Bites
High Priority Source: Dangerous Animals
Procedure:

1. Recognize tarantulas (Theraphosidae and Lycosa species) by:
- Large, hairy bodies, often sold in pet stores.
- Found primarily in tropical America, with some species in Europe and other regions.
2. Some South...
Procedure:

1. Recognize tarantulas (Theraphosidae and Lycosa species) by:
- Large, hairy bodies, often sold in pet stores.
- Found primarily in tropical America, with some species in Europe and other regions.
2. Some South American species can inject a dangerous toxin, while most produce only a painful bite.
3. If bitten by a tarantula:
- Expect immediate pain and bleeding at the site of the bite.
- Infection is likely due to the sharp claws used in biting.
4. Take immediate action:
- Clean the wound thoroughly with soap and water.
- Apply a cold compress to reduce swelling and pain.
- Monitor for signs of infection (e.g., redness, warmth, pus).
5. Seek medical attention if symptoms worsen or if you suspect an allergic reaction.
tarantula bite, spider treatment, infection prevention
Identify and Respond to Widow Spider Bites
High Priority Source: Dangerous Animals
Procedure:

1. Recognize widow spiders (Latrodectus species) by:
- Small, dark bodies with an hourglass-shaped marking on their abdomen (often white, red, or orange).
- Found in warmer regions worldwide; the black widow is...
Procedure:

1. Recognize widow spiders (Latrodectus species) by:
- Small, dark bodies with an hourglass-shaped marking on their abdomen (often white, red, or orange).
- Found in warmer regions worldwide; the black widow is common in North America.
2. If bitten by a widow spider:
- Experience severe pain, often described as burning or throbbing, at the site of the bite.
- Symptoms may include sweating, shivering, weakness, and disabling episodes that can last up to a week.
3. Take immediate action:
- Clean the wound with soap and water.
- Apply a cold compress to reduce pain and swelling.
- Monitor for signs of systemic symptoms (e.g., muscle cramps, nausea, difficulty breathing).
4. Seek medical attention if symptoms persist or worsen, especially in children, the elderly, or those with weakened immune systems.
widow spider, black widow, spider bite treatment, systemic symptoms

Foot Injuries (2) -

Manage Corns on Feet
Standard Source: the ten bushcraft books
Procedure:
• Pare down corns using appropriate tools.

Prevention:
• Use a reputable make of corn plaster.
• Avoid tight-fitting shoes to prevent the formation of corns.
corn removal,corn plaster,prevent corns,bushcraft foot care
Relieve Ingrowing Toenails
Standard Source: the ten bushcraft books
Procedure:
1. Use a file, rasp, sharp edge of a knife, or broken glass to scrape the top of the toenail.
2. Continue scraping until the nail is sufficiently thin to be easily depressed with the tip of your finger.

Note: This provides...
Procedure:
1. Use a file, rasp, sharp edge of a knife, or broken glass to scrape the top of the toenail.
2. Continue scraping until the nail is sufficiently thin to be easily depressed with the tip of your finger.

Note: This provides immediate relief from ingrowing toenails.
ingrowing toenail,relief for ingrown nails,bushcraft foot care

Gastrointestinal Treatment (2) -

Treat Diarrhea and Constipation in the Wilderness
Standard Source: The Complete Outdoorsmans Handbook
Objective: Manage diarrhea or constipation caused by changes in diet or contaminated water.

Materials Needed:
• Lomotil tablets
• Sulfasuxidine (for sterilizing intestines)
• Anticonstipation tablets

Instructions:
1. For diarrhea,...
Objective: Manage diarrhea or constipation caused by changes in diet or contaminated water.

Materials Needed:
• Lomotil tablets
• Sulfasuxidine (for sterilizing intestines)
• Anticonstipation tablets

Instructions:
1. For diarrhea, take Lomotil tablets as needed. Eighteen tablets should be sufficient for most emergencies.
2. In cases of suspected infection, use sulfasuxidine to help sterilize the intestines and reduce the risk of further complications.
3. If constipation occurs due to a change in diet, take anticonstipation tablets as needed.

Notes: Diarrhea is common when traveling in areas with contaminated food or water, such as parts of Africa or South America. Always ensure that medications are taken only if necessary and under the guidance of a physician.
treat diarrhea in wilderness, first aid for constipation, outdoor gastrointestinal care
Treat Nausea and Intestinal Cramps
Standard Source: The Complete Outdoorsmans Handbook
Objective: Alleviate nausea and intestinal cramps in the wilderness.

Materials Needed:
• Tridol or Campazinc tablets

Instructions:
1. For nausea and intestinal cramps, take Tridol or Campazinc tablets as needed.
2. The dosage...
Objective: Alleviate nausea and intestinal cramps in the wilderness.

Materials Needed:
• Tridol or Campazinc tablets

Instructions:
1. For nausea and intestinal cramps, take Tridol or Campazinc tablets as needed.
2. The dosage depends on the weight of the individual, but a dozen tablets should be sufficient for most emergencies.
3. If continuous vomiting occurs, seek medical help immediately, as it could indicate a serious condition such as food poisoning or an intestinal blockage.

Notes: These medications are effective for treating gastrointestinal discomfort caused by changes in diet or environmental factors.
treat nausea outdoors, first aid for stomach cramps, wilderness gastrointestinal care

Infection Treatment (2) -

Drain Pus from Vaginal Infection
High Priority Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure: To drain pus:

1. Use warm, wet cloths to draw out pus.
2. If the tear is open:
- Hold sterile cloths dipped in boiled warm water on the infected area.
3. If you know plant medicines that will draw out pus:
- Wrap them in a...
Procedure: To drain pus:

1. Use warm, wet cloths to draw out pus.
2. If the tear is open:
- Hold sterile cloths dipped in boiled warm water on the infected area.
3. If you know plant medicines that will draw out pus:
- Wrap them in a sterile piece of cloth or gauze and tie the cloth so the plants cannot fall out.
- Boil the wrapped plants, let them cool slightly, and press on the infected area.
4. If you feel a hard lump under the skin:
- Watch the lump each day.
- If it is painful or growing larger, get medical help.
drain pus, vaginal infection treatment, plant medicine
Administer Antibiotics for Severe Infections or Pneumonia
High Priority Source: The Complete Outdoorsmans Handbook
Objective: Treat severe infections, blood poisoning, and pneumonia with antibiotics.

Materials Needed:
• Penicillin or Achromycin tablets (10 tablets)

Instructions:
1. For severe infections, blood poisoning, or pneumonia,...
Objective: Treat severe infections, blood poisoning, and pneumonia with antibiotics.

Materials Needed:
• Penicillin or Achromycin tablets (10 tablets)

Instructions:
1. For severe infections, blood poisoning, or pneumonia, administer penicillin or Achromycin tablets as needed.
2. Take only the number of tablets required, and ensure that you do not exceed the recommended dosage unless advised by a physician.
3. Be cautious with penicillin: Some individuals may be allergic to it, so always consider this risk before administration.

Notes: These antibiotics are essential for treating serious infections in remote areas where medical help is not immediately available. Always carry them as part of your first-aid kit and consult a physician about their use.
administer antibiotics in the wilderness, treat infections outdoors, first aid for blood poisoning

Burns Scalds (2) -

Treat Burns and Scalds
High Priority Source: Family Emergency Handbook
Procedure:

1. Ensure there is no further danger to you or the victim from fire, hot liquids, etc.
2. Cool the affected area with cold water and ice to relieve pain.
3. Remove rings and bracelets before swelling begins to prevent...
Procedure:

1. Ensure there is no further danger to you or the victim from fire, hot liquids, etc.
2. Cool the affected area with cold water and ice to relieve pain.
3. Remove rings and bracelets before swelling begins to prevent constriction.
4. Cover the wound with a clean cloth and secure it lightly with a bandage.
5. If the burn or scald is larger than a dollar coin, seek medical attention immediately.
6. Do not breathe on, cough on, or touch a burn.
7. Do not open blisters or apply medications, ointments, or greasy substances to the burned area.
8. For dry or liquid chemical burns:
- Brush off dry chemicals.
- Flood the affected area with running water.
- Cover with a clean dressing and bandage lightly.
9. For electrical burns:
- Ensure that electricity is turned off before touching the victim.
- Cover the burn with a clean dressing and bandage lightly.
burn treatment, scald, first aid, chemical burn, electrical burn
Treat Burns or Scalds in Horses
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
For Slight Burns:
• Apply cold lead water to the affected area.

For Extensive Burns:
1. Prepare a liniment by mixing equal parts of linseed oil and lime water.
2. Apply this liniment to the burn.

If There Is Much Fever:
-...
For Slight Burns:
• Apply cold lead water to the affected area.

For Extensive Burns:
1. Prepare a liniment by mixing equal parts of linseed oil and lime water.
2. Apply this liniment to the burn.

If There Is Much Fever:
• Consider bleeding as part of the treatment.
burns,scalds,cold lead water,linseed oil,lime water

Insect Sting Treatment (2) -

Avoid Injury from Bees and Wasps
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. If a wasp or bee is swallowed:
- Take a teaspoonful of common salt dissolved in water to kill the insect before it can cause harm.
2. This method also cures the sting.
3. For external stings, use one of the following remedies:
...
Procedure:
1. If a wasp or bee is swallowed:
- Take a teaspoonful of common salt dissolved in water to kill the insect before it can cause harm.
2. This method also cures the sting.
3. For external stings, use one of the following remedies:
- Common salt
- Sweet oil
- Pounded mallows
- Onions
- Powdered chalk mixed with water to make a paste
- Weak ammonia
4. If bees swarm upon the head:
- Smoke tobacco and hold an empty hive over the head.
- The bees will enter the hive and leave the person unharmed.
bee sting, wasp sting, salt remedy, sweet oil, powdered chalk paste, tobacco smoke, empty hive
Relieve Pain from Reptile and Insect Bites or Stings
Standard Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:
1. Prepare a lotion by mixing:
- 5 oz. of distilled water
- 1 oz. of tincture of opium
2. Apply this lotion immediately to the affected area to relieve local pain caused by reptile and insect bites or stings.

**Alternative...
Procedure:
1. Prepare a lotion by mixing:
- 5 oz. of distilled water
- 1 oz. of tincture of opium
2. Apply this lotion immediately to the affected area to relieve local pain caused by reptile and insect bites or stings.

Alternative Method:
1. Mix the following ingredients:
- 5 1/2 oz. of soft water
- 1/2 oz. of water of ammonia
2. Wash the affected part repeatedly with this lotion until the pain abates.
reptile bite, insect sting, tincture of opium, ammonia water, pain relief

Drowning Rescue (2) -

Assisting a Person in Danger of Drowning
Critical Source: The Household Cyclopedia
Procedure:

1. If the rescuer can swim and is able to communicate with the drowning person, instruct them to keep their hands and arms under water until help arrives.
2. Throw towards the person a rope, pole, or any object that may assist in...
Procedure:

1. If the rescuer can swim and is able to communicate with the drowning person, instruct them to keep their hands and arms under water until help arrives.
2. Throw towards the person a rope, pole, or any object that may assist in bringing them ashore or onto a boat.
3. The person will likely seize whatever is within reach to save themselves.

Using a hat and handkerchief as flotation devices:
1. Spread a handkerchief on the ground or deck.
2. Place a stiff hat with the brim facing downward in the center of the handkerchief.
3. Tie the handkerchief around the hat, forming a bundle, ensuring knots are as close to the center of the crown as possible.
4. Hold the knots in one hand and keep the opening of the hat upward.
5. A non-swimmer can use this device to safely enter the water and assist someone else.

Rescuing techniques for expert swimmers:
1. Grasp the drowning person's arm firmly between the shoulder and elbow.
2. This prevents them from grabbing the rescuer, which could pull both under water.
drowning rescue, flotation device, hat handkerchief, swimming techniques
Rescue of the Drowning
Critical Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Refer to pages 279 to 285 in the original text for detailed instructions on how to rescue a drowning person.
2. Ensure that all rescuers are trained and prepared before attempting a rescue.
