3. Use proper techniques such as...
Procedure:

1. Refer to pages 279 to 285 in the original text for detailed instructions on how to rescue a drowning person.
2. Ensure that all rescuers are trained and prepared before attempting a rescue.
3. Use proper techniques such as reaching, throwing, or rowing to assist the victim without endangering yourself.
4. If possible, use a life ring, rope, or other floating device to help pull the person to safety.
5. Once the victim is out of the water, provide immediate first aid and call for emergency assistance if needed.
drowning rescue, life ring, floating device, first aid, emergency assistance

Pregnancy Discomforts (2) -

Relieve Back Pain During Pregnancy
Reference Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

1. Identify causes: Back pain is often due to the weight of the baby and womb straining bones and muscles.
2. Provide relief:
- Encourage family members or friends to massage the woman’s back.
- Apply a warm cloth or...
Procedure:

1. Identify causes: Back pain is often due to the weight of the baby and womb straining bones and muscles.
2. Provide relief:
- Encourage family members or friends to massage the woman’s back.
- Apply a warm cloth or hot water bottle on her back for comfort.
3. Reduce physical strain:
- Avoid standing in one place for long periods or leaning forward excessively.
- Allow others to assist with heavy tasks (e.g., carrying children, washing clothes).
4. Perform the angry cat exercise:
- Start on hands and knees with the back flat.
- Push the lower back up, then return to a flat position.
- Repeat several times in a row, 2 times per day or whenever pain occurs.
back pain,pregnancy,angry cat exercise,warm compress
Relieve Leg Cramps During Pregnancy
Reference Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

1. Recognize symptoms: Sudden sharp pain and tightening of a muscle in the foot or leg, especially at night or during stretching.
2. Stop the cramp:
- Flex the foot upward (point it toward the body).
- Gently stroke the...
Procedure:

1. Recognize symptoms: Sudden sharp pain and tightening of a muscle in the foot or leg, especially at night or during stretching.
2. Stop the cramp:
- Flex the foot upward (point it toward the body).
- Gently stroke the affected leg to help relax the muscle (do not stroke hard).
3. Prevent future cramps:
- Avoid pointing toes, even during stretching.
- Increase intake of foods rich in calcium and potassium.
- Consider taking calcium, magnesium, and potassium supplements as recommended.
4. Seek medical help if: Leg pain is severe or persistent (see page 273 for further guidance).
leg cramps,pregnancy,calcium,potassium,relief

Pregnancy Monitoring (2) -

Keep a Record of What Happens During Labor
High Priority Source: A Book for Midwives
Purpose:
• To help monitor the progress of labor and assist medical professionals if transport to a hospital is needed.

What to Record:
• How often the mother eats, drinks, and urinates.
• Her pulse, temperature, and **blood...
Purpose:
• To help monitor the progress of labor and assist medical professionals if transport to a hospital is needed.

What to Record:
• How often the mother eats, drinks, and urinates.
• Her pulse, temperature, and blood pressure (whenever checked).

Benefits:
• Helps identify if labor is going normally.
• Provides valuable information for doctors at a medical center.
• Can be used to analyze patterns in future labors and early signs of problems.

Tools:
• Use charts to keep records for all women you assist.
labor record, pulse, temperature, blood pressure, monitoring, medical center
Use a Labor Chart to Track Progress During Labor
High Priority Source: A Book for Midwives
Purpose:
• To systematically track the mother’s and baby’s condition during labor.

Chart Details:
| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| Name of Mother | Full name of the mother |
| Due Date | Expected date of birth |
| Date Labor Started |...
Purpose:
• To systematically track the mother’s and baby’s condition during labor.

Chart Details:
| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| Name of Mother | Full name of the mother |
| Due Date | Expected date of birth |
| Date Labor Started | When contractions began |
| Time Waters Broke | If membranes ruptured |
| Blood Pressure | Measured every 4 hours |
| Pulse | Measured every 4 hours |
| Temperature | Measured every 4 hours |
| Baby’s Position | Recorded at least once |
| Baby’s Heartbeat | Measured every hour |
| Cervix Dilation | Progress of cervical opening |
| Contractions | Frequency and intensity |

Instructions:
• Fill out the chart as labor progresses.
• Use it to identify patterns in labor and early signs of complications.
labor chart, baby heartbeat, cervix dilation, contractions, blood pressure

Labor Complications (2) -

Actions to Take While Traveling to Medical Help
High Priority Source: A Book for Midwives
While traveling to a medical center, follow these steps:

1. Help the mother stop pushing (see page 207).
2. Put her in the knee-chest position or another position with her hips up.
3. This helps take some of the pressure off the baby’s...
While traveling to a medical center, follow these steps:

1. Help the mother stop pushing (see page 207).
2. Put her in the knee-chest position or another position with her hips up.
3. This helps take some of the pressure off the baby’s head.
knee-chest position, labor during travel, midwife procedures
Help Prevent Tears in the Vaginal Opening During Birth
High Priority Source: A Book for Midwives
To help prevent tears in the vaginal opening during birth, follow these steps:

• Some midwives do not touch the vagina or baby at all during the birth. This is a good practice because interference can lead to infection, injury, or bleeding.
-...
To help prevent tears in the vaginal opening during birth, follow these steps:

• Some midwives do not touch the vagina or baby at all during the birth. This is a good practice because interference can lead to infection, injury, or bleeding.
• However, you may be able to prevent tears by supporting the vagina during the birth.
• Note: Tears often happen whether you try to prevent them or not.
prevent vaginal tears, midwife procedures, labor complications

Pediatric Care (2) -

Avoid Salicylate-Containing Products in Children with Influenza
High Priority Source: HHSPandemicInfluenzaPlan
Procedure:
1. Do not administer aspirin or other salicylate-containing products to children under the age of 18 years who have suspected or confirmed pandemic influenza.
2. This is due to an increased risk of Reye syndrome, which can lead to...
Procedure:
1. Do not administer aspirin or other salicylate-containing products to children under the age of 18 years who have suspected or confirmed pandemic influenza.
2. This is due to an increased risk of Reye syndrome, which can lead to acute encephalopathy and liver failure in this age group.
aspirin, Reye syndrome, children, salicylates
Monitor and Care for Uncircumcised Baby Boys
Reference Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

1. Clean the baby’s penis as part of regular hygiene, just like the rest of the body.
2. Do not pull back the foreskin to clean underneath it until it naturally retracts, usually after a few years.
3. Avoid forcibly retracting...
Procedure:

1. Clean the baby’s penis as part of regular hygiene, just like the rest of the body.
2. Do not pull back the foreskin to clean underneath it until it naturally retracts, usually after a few years.
3. Avoid forcibly retracting the foreskin, as this can cause injury or infection.
4. If there are signs of infection (redness, swelling, discharge), seek medical advice immediately.
5. Ensure that the baby’s genital area is kept clean and dry to prevent complications.
uncircumcised care, baby hygiene, foreskin cleaning, pediatric care

Infant Health (2) -

Treat Cord Infection in a Baby
High Priority Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

• If the umbilical cord stump becomes infected, take immediate action to treat it and prevent complications.

Action Steps:
1. Look for signs of infection around the cord: redness, pus, heat, or foul smell.
2. Clean the cord with...
Procedure:

• If the umbilical cord stump becomes infected, take immediate action to treat it and prevent complications.

Action Steps:
1. Look for signs of infection around the cord: redness, pus, heat, or foul smell.
2. Clean the cord with soap and boiled (then cooled) water, medical alcohol, strong drinking alcohol, or gentian violet.
3. Apply gentian violet to the infected area twice a day if the infection is mild.
4. Monitor for improvement over 1-2 days.
5. If there is no improvement or the condition worsens, administer the medicines listed in the box on page 279 of *A Book for Midwives*.
6. If the infection does not improve with home treatment, seek medical help immediately.
cord infection, gentian violet, baby care, infection treatment
Care for the Baby's Umbilical Cord Stump
Standard Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

• Keep the umbilical cord stump clean and dry to prevent infection.
• Teach the family how to properly care for the cord stump.

Action Steps:
1. Always wash your hands before touching the cord.
2. If the cord becomes dirty or...
Procedure:

• Keep the umbilical cord stump clean and dry to prevent infection.
• Teach the family how to properly care for the cord stump.

Action Steps:
1. Always wash your hands before touching the cord.
2. If the cord becomes dirty or has a lot of dried blood, clean it with soap and boiled (then cooled) water, medical alcohol, strong drinking alcohol, or gentian violet.
3. Be gentle when cleaning the cord to avoid causing injury.
4. Do not apply any other substances on the cord — dirt and dung are especially dangerous.
5. If there are many flies in the area, cover the cord stump with a clean dry cloth temporarily. Otherwise, leave it uncovered.
6. Monitor for signs of infection such as redness, pus, heat, or foul smell around the cord.
7. The cord stump usually falls off 5 to 7 days after birth. A few drops of blood or smooth mucus may be normal when the cord falls off.
8. If there is a lot of blood or any pus, seek medical help immediately.
umbilical cord care, infection prevention, baby hygiene

Sexual Health (2) -

Avoiding Sexual Intercourse to Prevent Pregnancy and STIs
Reference Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

• Complete avoidance of sexual intercourse is the most effective way to prevent pregnancy and reduce the risk of HIV and other STIs.
• This method may be difficult for couples to maintain over long periods but can be a good...
Procedure:

• Complete avoidance of sexual intercourse is the most effective way to prevent pregnancy and reduce the risk of HIV and other STIs.
• This method may be difficult for couples to maintain over long periods but can be a good option in certain circumstances.
avoiding intercourse, pregnancy prevention, STI prevention
Understanding Safe Sexual Activities Without Pregnancy Risk
Reference Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

• The following sexual activities do not cause pregnancy:
- Oral sex (mouth on genitals)
- Sexual touch (touching the genitals or other body parts)
- These activities have a **low risk of transmitting HIV and other...
Procedure:

• The following sexual activities do not cause pregnancy:
- Oral sex (mouth on genitals)
- Sexual touch (touching the genitals or other body parts)
- These activities have a low risk of transmitting HIV and other STIs
• Anal sex does not cause pregnancy, but it carries a high risk of transmitting HIV and other STIs
safe sex, non-pregnancy activities, STI transmission

Medication Administration (2) -

Determine Medication Timing Without a Clock
Standard Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

1. Check the medication instructions for how often to take it (e.g., “every 6 hours” or “4 tablets a day”).
2. Use natural time markers to schedule doses:
- Every 8 hours: Take one dose at sunrise, one in the afternoon, and...
Procedure:

1. Check the medication instructions for how often to take it (e.g., “every 6 hours” or “4 tablets a day”).
2. Use natural time markers to schedule doses:
- Every 8 hours: Take one dose at sunrise, one in the afternoon, and one at night.
- Every 6 hours: Take one dose in the morning, one at midday, one in the late afternoon, and one at night.
- Every 4 hours: Take six doses a day, spacing them evenly throughout the day (e.g., every 4 hours).
3. Ensure that the timing is consistent to maintain effective levels of medicine in the body.

Important Notes:
• Taking too much medicine at once can cause poisoning.
• Regular dosing ensures continuous effectiveness and prevents underdosing.
• Use visual aids or drawings for people who cannot read (see below).
medicine timing, medication schedule, without clock, dosing intervals
Create Visual Reminder for Medication Timing
Standard Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

1. Draw a simple picture or diagram to show the number of doses and when they should be taken.
2. In the blanks at the bottom, draw the amount of medicine (e.g., 1 tablet) and explain what it means:
- Example 1: **One tablet 4...
Procedure:

1. Draw a simple picture or diagram to show the number of doses and when they should be taken.
2. In the blanks at the bottom, draw the amount of medicine (e.g., 1 tablet) and explain what it means:
- Example 1: One tablet 4 times a day
- Draw one tablet in each of four boxes with labels: sunrise, midday, late afternoon, night.
- Example 2: Two tablets 4 times a day
- Draw two tablets in each of four boxes with the same time markers.
- Example 3: Two spoons of syrup 2 times a day
- Draw two spoons in each of two boxes with labels: morning and night.
3. Carefully explain to the person what the drawing means, ensuring they understand the timing and dosage.

Important Notes:
• This method is especially useful for people who cannot read or are illiterate.
• Ensure that the visual aid is clear and easy to interpret.
visual reminder, medication schedule, non-literate patients, dosing intervals

Sterilization (2) -

Sterilizing Tools for Medical Procedures
Standard Source: A Book for Midwives
Procedure:

To properly sterilize tools used in medical procedures:

1. Use Appropriate Solutions:
- Soak instruments in hydrogen peroxide or isopropyl alcohol to kill germs.
- Use iodine for disinfecting surfaces and skin...
Procedure:

To properly sterilize tools used in medical procedures:

1. Use Appropriate Solutions:
- Soak instruments in hydrogen peroxide or isopropyl alcohol to kill germs.
- Use iodine for disinfecting surfaces and skin before procedures.

2. Follow Steps Carefully:
- Clean tools with soap and water first, then rinse thoroughly.
- Immerse the tools in a sterilizing solution for at least 10 minutes.
- Allow tools to air dry completely before use.

3. Check for Contamination:
- Inspect tools visually and by touch to ensure they are free from debris or damage.
- If using an IUD, ensure it is properly loaded and sterilized before insertion.

4. Store Properly:
- Keep sterilized tools in a clean, dry container away from contaminants.
- Label containers with the date of sterilization to track effectiveness.
tool sterilization,disinfecting,iodine,hydrogen peroxide
Use Alcohol Prep Pads for Sterilization
Reference Source: Personal Wilderness Medical Kit
Procedure:

1. Select an appropriate site on the patient's skin (e.g., for injection or wound care).
2. Wipe the area with alcohol prep pads to sterilize and clean the skin.
3. Allow the alcohol to dry completely before proceeding...
Procedure:

1. Select an appropriate site on the patient's skin (e.g., for injection or wound care).
2. Wipe the area with alcohol prep pads to sterilize and clean the skin.
3. Allow the alcohol to dry completely before proceeding with any procedure (e.g., injection, catheter insertion).
alcohol prep pads,sterilization,wound care

Medical Emergency (2) -

Basic Life Support in Bush First Aid
Critical Source: Aids to Survival
Basic Life Support in Bush First Aid

• Check for responsiveness: Gently tap the person and shout to see if they respond.
• Call for help immediately: Use a mobile phone or radio to call emergency services (e.g., 000 in Australia) if...
Basic Life Support in Bush First Aid

• Check for responsiveness: Gently tap the person and shout to see if they respond.
• Call for help immediately: Use a mobile phone or radio to call emergency services (e.g., 000 in Australia) if available.
• Perform CPR if necessary: If the person is not breathing, begin chest compressions at a rate of 100–120 per minute and give rescue breaths if trained.
• Ensure an open airway: Tilt the head back slightly and lift the chin to clear any obstructions in the airway.
• Keep the person warm and comfortable: Cover them with clothing or a blanket to prevent hypothermia.

Important Notes:
• Only perform CPR if you are trained and it is safe to do so.
• If untrained, provide basic care and wait for professional help to arrive.
basic life support, CPR, first aid techniques
Managing a Person Having a Fit
High Priority Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Identify the fit: A person in a fit will experience convulsive movements followed by unconsciousness.
2. Ensure safety: Place the sufferer on the floor or ground where they cannot hurt themselves by striking anything.
3....
Procedure:

1. Identify the fit: A person in a fit will experience convulsive movements followed by unconsciousness.
2. Ensure safety: Place the sufferer on the floor or ground where they cannot hurt themselves by striking anything.
3. Loosen clothing: Remove any tight clothing that may restrict breathing or circulation.
4. Do not restrain movements: Allow natural convulsions to occur without interference.
5. Protect the mouth: Insert a wad of cloth into the sufferer's mouth to prevent them from biting their tongue.
6. Do not disturb when quiet: Once the person becomes still, leave them undisturbed until medical help arrives.

Warnings:
• Do not attempt to hold down or restrain the person during convulsions.
• Avoid putting anything in the mouth that could cause choking.
fit, convulsion, first aid, unconsciousness, safety, mouth protection

Unconsciousness (2) -

Respond to Unconsciousness
Critical Source: Aids to Survival
Procedure:

1. Remove the patient from the cause or the cause from the patient.
2. Examine the patient quickly for any visible injuries, bleeding, or signs of distress.
3. Commence cardio pulmonary resuscitation (CPR) if the patient...
Procedure:

1. Remove the patient from the cause or the cause from the patient.
2. Examine the patient quickly for any visible injuries, bleeding, or signs of distress.
3. Commence cardio pulmonary resuscitation (CPR) if the patient is not breathing or has no pulse.
4. Stop bleeding if necessary, using direct pressure and clean bandages.
5. Turn the patient on their side in the recovery position to prevent choking on vomit or fluids.
unconsciousness,CPR,recovery position,bleeding,emergency
Manage Unconscious Casualty with Airway Obstruction Risk
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Position the casualty on his side to prevent choking on blood, vomitus, or other substances.
2. If breathing is compromised:
- Clear the airway immediately.
- Initiate rescue breathing if necessary.
3. Look for signs of...
Procedure:

1. Position the casualty on his side to prevent choking on blood, vomitus, or other substances.
2. If breathing is compromised:
- Clear the airway immediately.
- Initiate rescue breathing if necessary.
3. Look for signs of inadequate oxygen supply (bluish or grayish color around lips and nail beds) and act quickly.
4. Do not attempt to clear the airway manually unless trained to do so.
airway obstruction, unconscious casualty, rescue breathing

Sprains (2) -

Treat Sprains in the Field
Standard Source: Aids to Survival
Procedure:

Diagnosis of sprains:
• Pain and swelling at the joint.
• Tenderness to touch.
• Bruising may be present.
• The patient can still move or use the affected limb or joint, though it is painful.

Treatment for sprains:
1. **Do...
Procedure:

Diagnosis of sprains:
• Pain and swelling at the joint.
• Tenderness to touch.
• Bruising may be present.
• The patient can still move or use the affected limb or joint, though it is painful.

Treatment for sprains:
1. Do not remove the boot if it comes above the ankle, especially in bushwalking situations.
2. If possible, perform contrast bathing: alternate between warm and cold water (5 minutes each) to reduce swelling.
3. Alternatively, immerse the injured area in cold water for 15 minutes to reduce inflammation.
4. After treatment, bandage the affected joint or limb with clean material to provide support.
sprain,bushwalking,cold water,swelling,treatment
Treat a Sprain Using the RICE Method
Standard Source: Medical Emergencies
Overview:

Sprains occur from overstretching tendons or ligaments. Signs include pain, swelling, tenderness, and discoloration.

Treatment Steps (RICE):
1. Rest:
- Avoid using the injured area to prevent further damage.

2. Ice:
...
Overview:

Sprains occur from overstretching tendons or ligaments. Signs include pain, swelling, tenderness, and discoloration.

Treatment Steps (RICE):
1. Rest:
- Avoid using the injured area to prevent further damage.

2. Ice:
- Apply ice for 24 hours to reduce swelling.
- After 24 hours, switch to heat therapy to promote healing.

3. Compression:
- Wrap or splint the affected area to stabilize it.
- If possible, leave a boot on a sprained ankle unless circulation is compromised.

4. Elevation:
- Keep the injured area elevated above heart level to reduce swelling.
sprain,treatment,RICE method,rest,ice,compression,elevation

Bleeding (2) -

Use Tourniquets as a Last Resort for Severe Bleeding
Critical Source: Aids to Survival
Procedure:
1. Only use a tourniquet if all other methods of stopping the bleeding have failed.
2. Apply the tourniquet above the wound, not directly on the injury site.
3. Ensure it is tight enough to stop the bleeding but not so tight as to...
Procedure:
1. Only use a tourniquet if all other methods of stopping the bleeding have failed.
2. Apply the tourniquet above the wound, not directly on the injury site.
3. Ensure it is tight enough to stop the bleeding but not so tight as to cause tissue damage.
4. Note the time the tourniquet was applied and inform medical personnel immediately.

Note: Tourniquets can lead to permanent limb loss if used improperly. They should only be used in life-threatening situations where the limb is severed or bleeding cannot be controlled.
tourniquet use, severe bleeding, last resort
Assess and Treat Bleeding Injuries
High Priority Source: Aids to Survival
Procedure:
1. Wipe away any blood or remove clothing to see the source of bleeding.
2. Apply pressure with a pad and bandage directly over the wound.
3. Elevate the injured area if possible.
4. If there is significant bleeding, monitor for signs...
Procedure:
1. Wipe away any blood or remove clothing to see the source of bleeding.
2. Apply pressure with a pad and bandage directly over the wound.
3. Elevate the injured area if possible.
4. If there is significant bleeding, monitor for signs of shock (e.g., rapid pulse, pale skin, confusion).

Note: Bleeding is bleeding — do not worry about whether it is arterial or venous. Focus on stopping the bleeding and stabilising the patient.
bleeding treatment, pressure bandage, shock prevention

Blister Treatment (2) -

Prevent and Treat Blisters on the Feet
Reference Source: Ranger Handbook 2006
Blisters Prevention and Treatment Procedure

1. Use moleskin: Apply moleskin to prevent blisters prior to movement or foot march.
2. Drain large blisters:
- Clean the area thoroughly.
- Puncture the blister with a sterilized...
Blisters Prevention and Treatment Procedure

1. Use moleskin: Apply moleskin to prevent blisters prior to movement or foot march.
2. Drain large blisters:
- Clean the area thoroughly.
- Puncture the blister with a sterilized needle.
- Allow the fluid to drain.
3. Protect the area: Place moleskin over the drained blister.
4. Monitor for infection: Observe the area for signs of infection (e.g., redness, swelling, pus).
5. Maintain foot hygiene:
- Keep feet as clean and dry as possible.
- Use foot powder to absorb moisture.
- Change socks regularly.
6. Allow air drying: Let feet air dry whenever the mission allows.
blisters, foot care, prevention, treatment
Treat Blisters Properly
Reference Source: Aids to Survival
Procedure:

1. Do not pop or puncture blisters, as this increases the risk of infection.
2. To reduce pressure and friction:
- Use band-aids, felt strips, or a specially designed blister dressing to pad the area.
3. If using a **felt...
Procedure:

1. Do not pop or puncture blisters, as this increases the risk of infection.
2. To reduce pressure and friction:
- Use band-aids, felt strips, or a specially designed blister dressing to pad the area.
3. If using a felt pad with a hole cut in it, place it over the blister so that it does not directly touch the skin.

Note: Only use dressings specifically designed for blisters if applying them directly to the blister.
blister care, pressure relief, infection prevention

Equipment Preparation (2) -

Assemble and Use a Hiker's First Aid Pack
Standard Source: Aids to Survival
Title: Assemble and Use a Hiker's First Aid Pack

Contents of the St John Ambulance Hikers First Aid Pack:
• 50pkt adhesive shapes
• 1 Adhesive tape [zinc oxide] 2.5cm x 5m
• 1 Triangular bandage 110 x 110 x 155cm
• 1 Conforming bandage...
Title: Assemble and Use a Hiker's First Aid Pack

Contents of the St John Ambulance Hikers First Aid Pack:
• 50pkt adhesive shapes
• 1 Adhesive tape [zinc oxide] 2.5cm x 5m
• 1 Triangular bandage 110 x 110 x 155cm
• 1 Conforming bandage 5cm
• 2 Non-adherent dressing 10 x 10cm
• 1 Wound dressing, No.14
• 3 Gauze swabs 7.5 x 7.5cm
• 1 Scissors, stainless steel
• 1 Forceps, stainless steel
• 3 Towels, disposable
• 2 Gloves, disposable
• 3 Plastic bags, re-sealable
• 1 Notepad and pencil
• 1 First Aid Quick Reference Guide
• 5 Safety pins

Packaging:
• Packed in a soft, green vinyl pouch designed to be worn on a belt.

Usage Instructions:
1. Ensure the first aid pack is easily accessible at all times during outdoor activities.
2. Familiarize yourself with the contents and their uses before an emergency occurs.
3. In case of injury or illness, use appropriate items from the kit to provide immediate care until professional medical help arrives.
first aid pack,hiker's first aid,equipment preparation,outdoor survival
Assemble and Use a Red Cross Hiking First Aid Kit
Standard Source: Aids to Survival
Title: Assemble and Use a Red Cross Hiking First Aid Kit

Contents of the Red Cross Hiking First Aid Kit:
• 1 Contents list
• 1 Adhesive tape, 2.5cm
• 1 Scissors, blunt, sharp stainless steel
• 1 Combine dressing, 90 x 200mm
• 5 Adhesive...
Title: Assemble and Use a Red Cross Hiking First Aid Kit

Contents of the Red Cross Hiking First Aid Kit:
• 1 Contents list
• 1 Adhesive tape, 2.5cm
• 1 Scissors, blunt, sharp stainless steel
• 1 Combine dressing, 90 x 200mm
• 5 Adhesive dressing strips
• 1 Crepe bandage, 7.5cm
• 1 Disposable foil blanket
• 1 Eyewash, 30ml
• 1 Gauze bandage, 5cm
• 1 Gauze bandage, 7.5cm
• 3 Gauze swabs
• 2 Non-adhesive dressing 10 x 10cm
• 1 Gloves, disposable
• 1 Pkt tissues, 10
• 5 Safety pins, 40mm
• 1 Splinter forceps
• 1 Normal saline, 30ml
• 1 Triangular bandage not less than 900mm
• 1 Conforming bandage 5cm
• 1 Wound dressing, No.14
• 1 ARC First Aid Notes
• 1 Pencil and notepad
• 3 Individual plastic bags 150 x 200mm

Packaging:
• A red, water resistant 100% nylon fold out soft pack with zippered inside compartments designed for organisations and individuals involved in outdoor activities.

Usage Instructions:
1. Ensure the first aid kit is easily accessible at all times during outdoor activities.
2. Familiarize yourself with the contents and their uses before an emergency occurs.
3. In case of injury or illness, use appropriate items from the kit to provide immediate care until professional medical help arrives.
red cross first aid,hiking first aid,equipment preparation,outdoor survival

Splinting Techniques (2) -

Stabilize a Splinted Limb for Comfort and Evacuation
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- Arrange the casualty so that the splinted limb is protected from impact or movement.
• Ensure proper immobilization to prevent further injury during transport.
limb stabilization, splint protection, evacuation positioning
Use of Splints in Fracture Management
Standard Source: Emergency War Surgery
Indications for Splinting
• Stable fractures (hand, wrist, forearm, foot, ankle, lower leg)

Technique
1. Apply Webril or similar cotton batting over bony prominences and soft tissues.
2. Wrap six-inch casting material over the Webril from...
Indications for Splinting
• Stable fractures (hand, wrist, forearm, foot, ankle, lower leg)

Technique
1. Apply Webril or similar cotton batting over bony prominences and soft tissues.
2. Wrap six-inch casting material over the Webril from ASIS to the foot on the affected side and distal thigh on the unaffected side (Fig. 23-4).
3. Reinforce the groin area with splints (Fig. 23-5) for added stability.

Notes
• Slab splinting may not be adequate for severely unstable fractures.
• Splints are appropriate for stable fractures and can be used in conjunction with casting materials.
splinting technique, fracture management, immobilization, splints for fractures

Prisoner Of War Care (2) -

Initial Physical Examination for Internees Upon Arrival
High Priority Source: Emergency War Surgery
Title: Initial Physical Examination for Internees Upon Arrival

Procedure:
1. Conduct a complete physical examination of each internee upon arrival at the facility.
2. Include a dental examination as part of the initial assessment.
3. Record...
Title: Initial Physical Examination for Internees Upon Arrival

Procedure:
1. Conduct a complete physical examination of each internee upon arrival at the facility.
2. Include a dental examination as part of the initial assessment.
3. Record vital statistics and other essential health information for each internee treated.
4. Provide immediate care based on the findings from the examination.
initial medical screening, internee physical exam, vital statistics
Routine Sick Call and Medical Care During Internment
Standard Source: Emergency War Surgery
Title: Routine Sick Call and Medical Care During Internment

Procedure:
1. Provide routine sick call on a daily basis during internment.
2. Include medication dispensing, wound care, and other minor procedures as needed.
3. Ensure that...
Title: Routine Sick Call and Medical Care During Internment

Procedure:
1. Provide routine sick call on a daily basis during internment.
2. Include medication dispensing, wound care, and other minor procedures as needed.
3. Ensure that medical personnel are available to address the health needs of internees regularly.
4. Maintain records of all treatments administered and medications dispensed.
daily sick call, internee medical care, wound treatment

Wound Infections (2) -

Clean and Treat Animal or Human Bites
High Priority Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Procedure for Cleaning and Treating Bites:

1. Thoroughly clean the wound: Wash the affected area with soap and water to remove any debris.
2. Remove dead tissue: Use sterile scissors to snip away any dead tissue from the wound.
3....
Procedure for Cleaning and Treating Bites:

1. Thoroughly clean the wound: Wash the affected area with soap and water to remove any debris.
2. Remove dead tissue: Use sterile scissors to snip away any dead tissue from the wound.
3. Apply prophylactic antibiotics: Administer antibiotics such as coamoxiclav or Augmentin to prevent infection (see also Chapter 20 for further guidance).
animal bite, human bite, wound cleaning, antibiotics, coamoxiclav
Recognize and Manage Wound Infections
High Priority Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Procedure for Recognizing and Managing Wound Infections:

Signs of infection include: Pain, redness, heat, swelling, loss of function, or the appearance of red lines running from a limb wound toward the body. Lymph nodes in the armpit,...
Procedure for Recognizing and Managing Wound Infections:

Signs of infection include: Pain, redness, heat, swelling, loss of function, or the appearance of red lines running from a limb wound toward the body. Lymph nodes in the armpit, groin, or neck may become enlarged, and fever may develop.

1. Monitor for symptoms: Watch for any signs of infection after an injury or wound.
2. Seek medical attention if necessary: If symptoms worsen or persist, consult a healthcare professional.
wound infection, signs of infection, red lines, lymph node enlargement, fever

Skin Protection (2) -

Skin Protection for Evacuating Forces
Critical Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Procedure:

1. Clothing Coverage: When evacuating a TIC hazard area, individuals must wear clothing that minimizes injury to exposed skin.

2. Exposed Skin Coverage: Cover exposed skin as much as possible to prevent deposition of liquid...
Procedure:

1. Clothing Coverage: When evacuating a TIC hazard area, individuals must wear clothing that minimizes injury to exposed skin.

2. Exposed Skin Coverage: Cover exposed skin as much as possible to prevent deposition of liquid TICs.

3. NBC Equipment Use: Normal NBC individual protection equipment may be used for this purpose if available.

Warnings:
• Failure to cover skin can lead to chemical burns or absorption through the skin.
• Ensure protective clothing is accessible and worn at all times in hazard areas.
skin protection, TIC exposure, clothing coverage, NBC gear
Manage Sunburn on an Expedition
Reference Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Procedure:

1. Prevent sunburn by using high-factor sunscreen and gradually increasing sun exposure.
2. If sunburn occurs:
- Apply hydrocortisone cream or calamine lotion to relieve discomfort from mild conditions.
3. Avoid further sun...
Procedure:

1. Prevent sunburn by using high-factor sunscreen and gradually increasing sun exposure.
2. If sunburn occurs:
- Apply hydrocortisone cream or calamine lotion to relieve discomfort from mild conditions.
3. Avoid further sun exposure until the skin has healed.

Notes:
• Sunburn can lead to blistering and should be avoided, especially in young people who may overexpose themselves early on an expedition.
sunburn,treatment,sun protection,expedition skin care

Mass Casualty Triage (2) -

Manage Casualty Triage in Mass Casualty Situations
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Triage Procedure Steps:

1. Assess the Scene and Casualties:
- Quickly evaluate the number of casualties and the severity of injuries.
- Identify any immediate life-threatening conditions (e.g., uncontrolled bleeding, airway...
Triage Procedure Steps:

1. Assess the Scene and Casualties:
- Quickly evaluate the number of casualties and the severity of injuries.
- Identify any immediate life-threatening conditions (e.g., uncontrolled bleeding, airway obstruction).

2. Categorize Casualties by Priority:
- Immediate (Red): Casualties with life-threatening injuries requiring urgent care (e.g., cardiac arrest, severe bleeding).
- Delayed (Yellow): Casualties with serious but non-life-threatening injuries that can wait for treatment after immediate cases.
- Minor (Green): Casualties with minor injuries who can be treated last.
- Expectant (Black): Casualties with no chance of survival despite intervention.

3. Mark and Label Casualties:
- Use colored tags or markers to indicate the triage category for each casualty.
- Ensure all personnel involved in the care chain are aware of the triage system.

4. Prioritize Care and Transport:
- Provide immediate care to red-tagged casualties first.
- Transport casualties based on their priority, ensuring that those with life-threatening injuries receive attention before others.
triage, mass casualty, priority classification, emergency care
Determine the Number of Casualties and Manage the Situation
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- Quickly determine if there is one casualty, several or many.

• If there are more casualties than you or your team can handle, seek help and issue an alert.

• A mass casualty situation requires a first phase of sifting the casualties and then...
- Quickly determine if there is one casualty, several or many.

• If there are more casualties than you or your team can handle, seek help and issue an alert.

• A mass casualty situation requires a first phase of sifting the casualties and then sorting them for priority of treatment according to the severity of their injuries.
casualties,triage,mass casualty,situation management

Situational Guidelines (2) -

Special Characteristics of Situations in Armed Conflicts
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Overview:
Armed conflicts and other situations of violence have unique characteristics that First Aiders must be aware of:

1. Protection rules and laws apply to individuals in these situations.
2. Major hazards and risks are caused by...
Overview:
Armed conflicts and other situations of violence have unique characteristics that First Aiders must be aware of:

1. Protection rules and laws apply to individuals in these situations.
2. Major hazards and risks are caused by weapons and the use of force or violence by individuals.
3. Humanitarian consequences, including societal disorganization, disruption of healthcare systems, and reduced availability of basic public health requirements such as:
- Water
- Food
- Shelter

Note: These characteristics must be considered when providing first aid in the field.
special characteristics, armed conflicts, humanitarian consequences, hazards
Types of Situations Covered by the Manual
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Overview:
This manual addresses two main types of situations:

1. Armed conflicts (international or non-international in nature).
2. Other situations of violence, such as riots, isolated acts of violence, and widespread...
Overview:
This manual addresses two main types of situations:

1. Armed conflicts (international or non-international in nature).
2. Other situations of violence, such as riots, isolated acts of violence, and widespread banditry.

Note: The contents apply to both armed conflict and other situations of violence unless otherwise stated. Practical instructions may vary depending on local circumstances and the training and preparedness of First Aiders.
armed conflicts, situations of violence, types of situations

Humanitarian Principles (2) -

Respect and Promote the Fundamental Principles of the International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
In your village or city communities, at home and at work, you must:

• Understand and show respect for the Fundamental Principles of the International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement, the distinctive emblems, and essentials of international...
In your village or city communities, at home and at work, you must:

• Understand and show respect for the Fundamental Principles of the International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement, the distinctive emblems, and essentials of international humanitarian law.
• Report any misuse or usurpation of the distinctive emblems to your National Society, the ICRC, or the International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies.
• Demonstrate explicitly through your actions the humanity, neutrality and impartiality of the International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement.
international humanitarian law, red cross principles, emblem misuse reporting
Understand and Respect the Humanitarian Role of First Aiders
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
If you are a Red Cross or Red Crescent First Aider, it is not enough to be experienced in life-saving and health-protection measures; you must also help at all times to ensure that the whole population understands and supports the right of...
If you are a Red Cross or Red Crescent First Aider, it is not enough to be experienced in life-saving and health-protection measures; you must also help at all times to ensure that the whole population understands and supports the right of people to be protected and to receive care, and the need to respect the distinctive emblems so that humanitarian aid is delivered more efficiently, for the benefit of all.
humanitarian role, red cross, red crescent, first aider duties

Specific Injuries (2) -

Recognize and Prepare for Specific Health Problems in Armed Conflicts
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Identify and manage health risks unique to armed conflicts.

Procedure:
1. Be prepared to encounter penetrating injuries, such as those caused by bullets or shrapnel, which may require immediate wound care and infection...
Objective: Identify and manage health risks unique to armed conflicts.

Procedure:
1. Be prepared to encounter penetrating injuries, such as those caused by bullets or shrapnel, which may require immediate wound care and infection prevention.
2. Expect blast injuries, including traumatic brain injury, internal bleeding, and damage to the ears, lungs, and abdomen.
3. Be ready to treat burns of varying degrees (first, second, third), using appropriate first aid techniques such as cooling burns with water and covering them with sterile dressings.
4. Manage blunt trauma, including fractures, head injuries, and internal bruising, by immobilizing the casualty and providing pain relief where possible.
5. Be aware of silent emergencies that may arise due to deteriorating living conditions, such as:
- Diarrhea
- Malnutrition
- Epidemics (e.g., cholera, measles)
6. Prepare for common trauma from peacetime incidents, including:
- Road traffic crashes and falls
- Household accidents
- Work-related injuries
- Hunting accidents
- Fires and natural disasters
blast injuries, penetrating wounds, burns, blunt trauma, silent emergencies, epidemic prevention
First Aid for Specific Injuries - General First Aid Measures
High Priority Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. Assess the casualty: Check their condition and determine if they are conscious, breathing, or showing signs of shock.
2. Ensure safety: Make sure the area is safe for both you and the casualty.
3. **Provide immediate...
Procedure:

1. Assess the casualty: Check their condition and determine if they are conscious, breathing, or showing signs of shock.
2. Ensure safety: Make sure the area is safe for both you and the casualty.
3. Provide immediate care: Administer first aid based on the specific injury, such as controlling bleeding or immobilizing fractures.
4. Monitor the casualty: Keep an eye on their condition and watch for signs of shock or other complications.
5. Call for emergency assistance: If the injury is severe or if the casualty’s condition worsens, call for help immediately.
injury care, first aid, specific injuries

Casualty Documentation (2) -

Complete Medical Card for Casualty Documentation
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
### Complete Medical Card for Casualty Documentation

Use the Medical Card to document essential information about a casualty and their treatment:

1. Personal Information
- Fill in the Date, Family name, First name, **Other...
### Complete Medical Card for Casualty Documentation

Use the Medical Card to document essential information about a casualty and their treatment:

1. Personal Information
- Fill in the Date, Family name, First name, Other name (father), Sex, Age, and Home address.
2. Time of Injury and Evacuation
- Record the Time of injury and Evacuation to including:
- Date and time
- Mode of evacuation (e.g., on foot, taxi)
3. Medical Examination Details
- Fill in the following fields:
- Pulse, BP, Resp, APVU
- Medical exam by, Place, and Date/Time of examination
4. Injury Type
- Mark the type of injury from the options provided (e.g., Gunshot wound, Fragment, Mine/ERW, Blast, Burn, Blow, Road crash, Fall, Other)
5. Treatment Provided
- Document treatments administered:
- Compressive bandage with date and time
- Recovery position with date and time
- Artificial ventilation with date and time
- Tetanus administration
- Antibiotics dosage (e.g., - mg)
- Painkiller dosage (e.g., - mg)
- Other drugs administered (specify drug name and dosage)
6. Allergies and Medical Conditions
- Note any Allergies or Other medical problems.
7. Home Treatment and Additional Notes
- Record Home treatment details, including:
- Penetrating wound, Other wound or burn, Bleeding, Bone trauma, Disability, Motor Sensitive, Death Location, and Date/Time of death (if applicable).
8. IV Access and Fluids
- Document:
- IV access since with date and time
- IV fluids administered, including volume in Litres.
9. Other Notes
- Include any additional information such as Intubation, During evacuation, or other relevant details.

### Important:
• Ensure all fields are completed accurately for proper medical record-keeping and coordination with further medical care.
• Use the FRONT and BACK sections to document injuries on both sides of the body.
• Record any Right or Left side-specific injuries.
medical card,casualty documentation,first aid,armed conflicts,documentation,emergency medical record
Documentation of Casualty Information
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- As soon as possible, complete a 'medical card' for each casualty that includes at least:
- The place, date and time.
- Personal details.
- Initial assessment of vital signs (consciousness, pulse and respiration), injuries and other major...
- As soon as possible, complete a 'medical card' for each casualty that includes at least:
- The place, date and time.
- Personal details.
- Initial assessment of vital signs (consciousness, pulse and respiration), injuries and other major health problems.
- Actions taken.
- Health status just before end of care (e.g. before evacuation).
• Document the status and any change in the condition of the casualty, what you have done, and the handover you have made.
medical card,casualty documentation,vital signs

Safety In Armed Conflict (2) -

Assess and Prioritize Personal Safety in Armed Conflict Zones
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

1. Continuously monitor your surroundings for visible or hidden dangers.
2. Evaluate the security conditions of the area you are operating in, including potential threats such as armed groups, minefields, or unstable structures.
3....
Procedure:

1. Continuously monitor your surroundings for visible or hidden dangers.
2. Evaluate the security conditions of the area you are operating in, including potential threats such as armed groups, minefields, or unstable structures.
3. Ensure that access to casualties and humanitarian aid is secured according to local agreements and protocols.
4. If movement within an area becomes restricted or unsafe, immediately report this to your team leader and follow established security procedures.
5. Always prioritize your safety before taking any action, even if it means delaying or halting first aid efforts until the situation is secure.

Important Notes:
• In extreme situations where the safety of International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement staff is at risk, armed escorts may be considered; however, this must strictly follow local security guidelines to avoid compromising neutrality and independence.
• The use of armed protection can increase the risk of being targeted by hostile groups.
personal safety, armed conflict, security assessment, first aid, safety protocols
Follow the Golden Rule of First Aid in Armed Conflict Zones
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

1. Always remember the golden rule: make safe.
2. Prioritize your own safety above all else before taking any action.
3. Maintain self-control and avoid impulsive decisions, even under pressure.
4. Observe the situation carefully...
Procedure:

1. Always remember the golden rule: make safe.
2. Prioritize your own safety above all else before taking any action.
3. Maintain self-control and avoid impulsive decisions, even under pressure.
4. Observe the situation carefully before intervening or providing assistance to casualties.
5. Proceed with first aid only if it is clearly safe to do so, based on a thorough assessment of potential risks.

Important Notes:
• This rule applies in all situations involving armed conflict or other violent scenarios.
• It ensures that you are not putting yourself or others at unnecessary risk.
golden rule, first aid safety, armed conflict, self-control

Conflict Zones Preparation (2) -

Collect Security Information Before Deployment
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Before deployment, gather the following security-related information:

1. Current situation: Understand whether fighting is ongoing or likely to occur.
2. Safe and dangerous areas: Identify zones that are secure versus those at risk from...
Before deployment, gather the following security-related information:

1. Current situation: Understand whether fighting is ongoing or likely to occur.
2. Safe and dangerous areas: Identify zones that are secure versus those at risk from violence.
3. Potential threats: Inquire about aerial bombardments, ambushes, sniper-fire, objects being thrown from buildings, or people throwing stones.
4. Minefields: Determine if there are any unexploded ordnance or minefields in the area.
5. Guarantees of safety: Check whether local commanders or leaders can provide assurances for your safety and access to casualties.

Important note: Be cautious not to be mistaken for a spy when gathering information.
security,minefields,sniper-fire,aerial bombardments,safe areas
Gather Pre-Arrival Information for Conflict Zones
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Prior to arriving at a conflict zone, collect detailed information about:

1. Geography of the area: Understand the terrain, landmarks, and potential obstacles.
2. Communication and transportation routes: Identify safe and accessible...
Prior to arriving at a conflict zone, collect detailed information about:

1. Geography of the area: Understand the terrain, landmarks, and potential obstacles.
2. Communication and transportation routes: Identify safe and accessible paths for movement and evacuation.
3. Location of medical structures: Know where field hospitals, clinics, or other medical facilities are located.
4. Safe and dangerous areas: Determine zones that are secure versus those at risk from violence or hazards.

Sources of information include:
• Your team leader or colleagues.
• Dispatch or command centre.
• Local residents, taxi or lorry drivers, staff of NGOs, UN personnel, military or police.

This information is essential for ensuring your safety and the effective delivery of first aid in a conflict environment.
preparation,conflict zones,safety,medical structures,communication routes

Conflict Zones Response (2) -

Assess Combat Environment Upon Arrival
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Upon arrival at the conflict zone, observe and listen for signs of combat:

• Look for: Individuals preparing or using force (aggressive postures), smoke or tear gas, unexploded bombs, or suspicious objects (do not touch them).
• **Listen...
Upon arrival at the conflict zone, observe and listen for signs of combat:

• Look for: Individuals preparing or using force (aggressive postures), smoke or tear gas, unexploded bombs, or suspicious objects (do not touch them).
• Listen for: Screams, gunshots, explosions, or other sounds indicating violence.

What to do and avoid:
1. Avoid entering areas of active violence until the situation calms down.
2. Use only familiar or recently used paths/roads for movement.
3. Identify nearby shelter options in case of sudden danger.
4. Determine the safest route to reach casualties and transport them to shelter.
5. Maintain contact with your team leader, who is in communication with the command centre for further guidance.
combat assessment,safe routes,shelter identification,team coordination
Manage Dynamic Security Conditions
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Security conditions can change rapidly. Be prepared to adapt your actions and deployment accordingly:

• Stay alert for sudden changes in the environment or threat levels.
• Reassess risks regularly and modify your approach as needed.
• Maintain...
Security conditions can change rapidly. Be prepared to adapt your actions and deployment accordingly:

• Stay alert for sudden changes in the environment or threat levels.
• Reassess risks regularly and modify your approach as needed.
• Maintain communication with your team leader and command centre for real-time updates.
• Adjust your position, movement, and protection strategies based on new information.

Key principle: Flexibility and responsiveness are essential to ensuring safety in a volatile environment.
security changes,adaptability,response strategy

Stabilization (2) -

Perform Stabilization Techniques and Provide Psychological Support
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

1. Provide psychological support to the casualty.
2. Ensure rehydration as needed.
3. Put the casualty in a comfortable position.
4. Monitor the casualty’s condition and the efficiency of measures taken.
5. Perform...
Procedure:

1. Provide psychological support to the casualty.
2. Ensure rehydration as needed.
3. Put the casualty in a comfortable position.
4. Monitor the casualty’s condition and the efficiency of measures taken.
5. Perform stabilization techniques at the end of the complete examination.

Details:
• Stabilization should be done after a thorough head-to-toe palpation.
• Be aware of conditions that may deteriorate during the initial assessment, as they require attention during stabilization.
• A deteriorating condition may lead to life-threatening situations and must be managed accordingly.
stabilization,psychological support,rehydration,casualty management
Casualty Stabilization and Immobilization
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Prevent further injury and prepare for evacuation.

Steps:
1. Immobilize Injuries: Use splints or improvised materials to stabilize fractures or dislocations.
2. Dress Wounds: Apply sterile dressings and bandages to...
Objective: Prevent further injury and prepare for evacuation.

Steps:
1. Immobilize Injuries: Use splints or improvised materials to stabilize fractures or dislocations.
2. Dress Wounds: Apply sterile dressings and bandages to control bleeding and protect wounds from infection.
3. Maintain Body Temperature: Cover the casualty with blankets or clothing to prevent hypothermia, especially in cold environments.
4. Psychological Support: Reassure the casualty and provide comfort to reduce anxiety and stress.
5. Rehydration: Offer water if the casualty is conscious and able to drink; avoid giving fluids if there is a risk of aspiration (e.g., unconscious casualties).

Notes: Stabilization should be done carefully, especially in cases of suspected spinal injury.
immobilization, wound dressing, body temperature, psychological support, rehydration

Evacuation (2) -

Evacuation Preparation and Priority Assessment
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Determine the urgency of evacuation and prepare for transport.

Steps:
1. Assess Casualty Condition: Evaluate whether the casualty is stable enough for evacuation or requires immediate medical attention.
2. **Determine...
Objective: Determine the urgency of evacuation and prepare for transport.

Steps:
1. Assess Casualty Condition: Evaluate whether the casualty is stable enough for evacuation or requires immediate medical attention.
2. Determine Evacuation Priority: Use a triage system to categorize casualties based on severity (e.g., red, yellow, green tags).
3. Select Transport Means: Choose appropriate transport based on availability and the casualty’s condition (e.g., stretcher, vehicle, helicopter).
4. Prepare for Handover: Ensure all medical interventions are documented and communicated to the receiving team.
5. Monitor During Evacuation: Continuously monitor the casualty’s vital signs during transport until they reach a medical facility.

Notes: Evacuation should be planned carefully to ensure the safety of both the casualty and responders.
evacuation preparation, triage system, transport selection, handover, monitoring
Implement NBC Casualty Evacuation Procedures
High Priority Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Objective: Safely evacuate NBC casualties while minimizing the spread of contamination.

Steps:
1. Organize evacuation routes and vehicles to prevent cross-contamination between casualties and medical personnel.
2. Use **dedicated NBC...
Objective: Safely evacuate NBC casualties while minimizing the spread of contamination.

Steps:
1. Organize evacuation routes and vehicles to prevent cross-contamination between casualties and medical personnel.
2. Use dedicated NBC casualty transport vehicles equipped with decontamination capabilities if available.
3. Ensure that evacuation teams are trained in NBC procedures, including the use of personal protective equipment (PPE) and decontamination protocols.
4. If there is a shortage of medical vehicles, develop contingency plans to retain casualties within units for extended periods or use alternative transport methods.
5. Coordinate with medical treatment facilities to ensure they are prepared to receive and treat NBC casualties upon arrival.
NBC casualty evacuation, transport vehicles, decontamination during transport

Consciousness Assessment (2) -

Assess Level of Consciousness Using AVPU Scale
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Determine the casualty's level of consciousness using the AVPU scale.

Steps:
1. Alert: Check if the casualty is awake, lucid, and responsive to their environment (e.g., eyes open spontaneously as you approach).
2. **Voice...
Objective: Determine the casualty's level of consciousness using the AVPU scale.

Steps:
1. Alert: Check if the casualty is awake, lucid, and responsive to their environment (e.g., eyes open spontaneously as you approach).
2. Voice Response: If the casualty is not alert, ask them a question. If they respond in a meaningful way, note this as a voice response.
3. Pain Response: If the casualty does not respond to verbal stimuli, apply a painful stimulus (e.g., pinch neck muscles, earlobe, or nipple; rub the bone above the eye or angle of the jaw). If they move or cry out in response, note this as a pain response.
4. Unresponsive: If the casualty shows no response to any stimuli, mark them as unresponsive.

Notes:
• The level of consciousness can deteriorate quickly in casualties with head trauma.
• Use this assessment to determine if immediate intervention is needed.
AVPU scale, consciousness level, first aid, head trauma, assessment
Assessing Consciousness in a Casualty
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Determine if the casualty is conscious or not to guide further care.

Steps:
1. Observe the casualty’s behavior: Most casualties are conscious and may be able to communicate their injuries, pain, and how they were injured.
2....
Objective: Determine if the casualty is conscious or not to guide further care.

Steps:
1. Observe the casualty’s behavior: Most casualties are conscious and may be able to communicate their injuries, pain, and how they were injured.
2. Use the ABCDE sequence for assessment: Quickly go through each step (Airway, Breathing, Circulation, Disability, Exposure) even if the casualty appears conscious.
3. Identify “walking wounded”: Casualties who are conscious and able to move may be classified as “walking wounded” and can assist in their own care or help with administrative tasks and logistics.

Notes:
• Even if a casualty is conscious, it is essential to perform the ABCDE sequence systematically for each individual.
• Conscious casualties should not be overlooked; they may still require immediate life-saving interventions.
consciousness assessment, walking wounded, ABCDE sequence

Respiratory Assessment (2) -

Breathing Assessment and Management
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Assess breathing for signs of compromise:

• Breathing involves the chest and lungs; injuries to the chest, head, or abdomen can affect it.
• Aim of the first aider on-site:
- Identify breathing problems, especially respiratory distress.
-...
Assess breathing for signs of compromise:

• Breathing involves the chest and lungs; injuries to the chest, head, or abdomen can affect it.
• Aim of the first aider on-site:
- Identify breathing problems, especially respiratory distress.
- Re-establish and maintain efficient spontaneous ventilation.
- Assist ventilation if the casualty is unable to breathe independently.
- Organize regular relief for the person assisting with ventilation.
- Continuously monitor the casualty’s condition and effectiveness of interventions.
breathing assessment,ventilation management
Examination of Breathing Patterns
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Assess breathing by observing the following:

• Normal breathing is silent and effortless with a regular pattern of inspiration and expiration.
• Signs of respiratory distress:
- Absence of chest wall movement.
- Shallow, deep, or...
Assess breathing by observing the following:

• Normal breathing is silent and effortless with a regular pattern of inspiration and expiration.
• Signs of respiratory distress:
- Absence of chest wall movement.
- Shallow, deep, or uneven rise and fall of the chest.
- Abnormal movements such as paradoxical breathing (flail chest).
- Agitation or anxiety, laboured breathing, abnormally slow or rapid breathing rate.
- Nasal flaring or use of accessory muscles for breathing.
- Cyanosis (blue discoloration) of the lips and nail beds.
breathing examination,respiratory distress,cyanosis

Tourniquet Use (2) -

Tourniquet Application Guidelines
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
A tourniquet is useless if placed on the forearm or lower leg.

It is dangerous and strictly prohibited to place it on the arm for a wound of the forearm or on the thigh for a wound of the lower leg.

**Use a tourniquet only as a temporary...
A tourniquet is useless if placed on the forearm or lower leg.

It is dangerous and strictly prohibited to place it on the arm for a wound of the forearm or on the thigh for a wound of the lower leg.

Use a tourniquet only as a temporary measure, for a matter of minutes, in cases of immediate danger to life, such as:

• Severe bleeding from a traumatic amputation above the knee or above the elbow;

• Only if digital pressure on the arterial pressure point has not controlled the bleeding.

Once the compressive bandage is applied to the stump, remove the tourniquet.

This situation should never arise in practice, as a compressive bandage and digital pressure alone should be sufficient to control bleeding.
tourniquet use,temporary measure,amputation,arterial pressure point
Tourniquet Use in a Survival Situation
Critical Source: USMC Winter Survival Course Handbook
Tourniquet Use in a Survival Situation:

• Apply a tourniquet only when there is severe bleeding that cannot be controlled by direct pressure.
• When to loosen the tourniquet: If no rescue or medical aid is expected for over 2 hours, you may...
Tourniquet Use in a Survival Situation:

• Apply a tourniquet only when there is severe bleeding that cannot be controlled by direct pressure.
• When to loosen the tourniquet: If no rescue or medical aid is expected for over 2 hours, you may attempt to slowly loosen it 20 minutes after application.
• Steps to loosen a tourniquet:
- Ensure a pressure dressing is in place to maintain control of bleeding.
- Confirm that the bleeding has stopped before loosening.
- Slowly loosen the tourniquet to restore circulation, but leave it in position if bleeding resumes.
• Important considerations:
- Never remove the tourniquet completely unless medical help is available.
- Monitor for signs of tissue damage or loss of function below the tourniquet.
tourniquet use, survival medicine, bleeding control

Neck Injuries (2) -

Control Bleeding from Neck Injuries
Critical Source: Full First Aid Manual FM 2111
Objective: Control bleeding from neck injuries while avoiding interference with breathing.

Steps:
1. Apply pressure above and below the injury to control bleeding.
2. Do not interfere with the breathing process when applying...
Objective: Control bleeding from neck injuries while avoiding interference with breathing.

Steps:
1. Apply pressure above and below the injury to control bleeding.
2. Do not interfere with the breathing process when applying pressure or dressings.
3. Apply a dressing to the wound.
4. Immediately evaluate for possible neck fracture or spinal cord injury.
5. If suspected, seek medical treatment immediately.

Notes:
• Neck injuries can be life-threatening and may involve damage to the airway or spinal column.
• Always ensure that breathing is not obstructed during first aid measures.
neck injury first aid, bleeding control, spinal cord injury
Manage Bleeding from a Small Neck Wound
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

• Apply direct pressure to the bleeding site using your gloved fingers and a clean dressing.

• Secure the dressing in place with a roll of gauze, adding more dressing if needed.

• Wrap a bandage over the dressing, around the neck...
Procedure:

• Apply direct pressure to the bleeding site using your gloved fingers and a clean dressing.

• Secure the dressing in place with a roll of gauze, adding more dressing if needed.

• Wrap a bandage over the dressing, around the neck and under the shoulder opposite the wound; avoid excess pressure on the airway.
neck wound, direct pressure, first aid

Open Fractures (2) -

Manage Open Fractures with Wound Care and Splinting
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- Splint the injured limb immediately to prevent further damage.
• Clean and dress any open wounds carefully using available materials.
• Ensure that the wound is protected from contamination and further injury.
open fracture management, wound care, splinting
Treating an Open Fracture (Broken Bone That Breaks Through the Skin)
Critical Source: Where There is no Doctor a Village Health Care Handbook
Procedure:

1. Wear protective gloves or use plastic bags to avoid direct contact with the wound.
2. Clean the wound and exposed bone thoroughly using cool, boiled water.
3. Do not attempt to push the bone back into the wound, even...
Procedure:

1. Wear protective gloves or use plastic bags to avoid direct contact with the wound.
2. Clean the wound and exposed bone thoroughly using cool, boiled water.
3. Do not attempt to push the bone back into the wound, even if it is visible.
4. Cover the wound immediately with a clean cloth or sterile dressing.
5. Splint the affected limb to prevent further injury and movement of the broken bone.
6. Administer an antibiotic immediately: Use dicloxacillin, clindamycin, or ciprofloxacin to reduce the risk of infection.
7. Seek immediate medical help, as professional care is essential for open fractures.
open fracture,wound care,antibiotics,splinting

Fracture Reduction (2) -

Align a Fractured Limb Using Traction
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- Explain the procedure to the casualty and obtain cooperation.
• Firmly grasp the foot or hand of the injured limb.
• Pull gently along the long axis of the limb, using the least amount of force necessary.
• Do not stop pulling until...
- Explain the procedure to the casualty and obtain cooperation.
• Firmly grasp the foot or hand of the injured limb.
• Pull gently along the long axis of the limb, using the least amount of force necessary.
• Do not stop pulling until the limb is fully re-aligned and splinted.
• Reassess distal circulation, movement, and feeling after alignment.
traction for fracture reduction, limb realignment, splinting
Handle Resistance During Traction for Fracture Reduction
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- If the casualty strongly resists traction, continue with gentle pulling.
• Muscles will relax over time, allowing broken bones to realign.
• Once the fracture is reduced or corrected, pain should decrease significantly or even disappear.
resistance during traction, muscle relaxation, fracture correction

Chemical Biological Radiological (2) -

Administer First Aid to a Nerve Agent Casualty (Buddy-Aid)
Critical Source: Medical NBC Battlebook
Objective: Provide immediate first aid to a casualty exposed to nerve agents.

Steps:
1. Ensure your own safety by donning the protective mask and ensuring the area is secure from further exposure.
2. Assess the casualty’s condition,...
Objective: Provide immediate first aid to a casualty exposed to nerve agents.

Steps:
1. Ensure your own safety by donning the protective mask and ensuring the area is secure from further exposure.
2. Assess the casualty’s condition, looking for signs of nerve agent poisoning (e.g., difficulty breathing, convulsions, pinpoint pupils).
3. Administer atropine or pralidoxime chloride as directed by medical training protocols.
4. Provide supplemental oxygen if available and appropriate to assist with respiration.
5. Keep the casualty calm and in a safe position, avoiding any further exposure to the agent.
6. Initiate decontamination procedures immediately after administering first aid, following established protocols.
7. Call for medical assistance or evacuation as soon as possible.

Notes:
• This procedure is essential for increasing survival rates in chemical warfare scenarios.
• Proper training and practice are required to perform this task effectively under stress.
• Do not attempt to move the casualty unless absolutely necessary.
buddy aid,nerve agent first aid,atropine,pralidoxime chloride,decontamination,chemical warfare
Provide First Aid for Exposure to Chemical Agents
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Overview: Chemical agents can be toxic to the nervous system or cause blistering and inflammation of the skin, airway, and lungs.

Procedure:
1. Identify exposure: Look for signs such as difficulty breathing, burning sensations on the...
Overview: Chemical agents can be toxic to the nervous system or cause blistering and inflammation of the skin, airway, and lungs.

Procedure:
1. Identify exposure: Look for signs such as difficulty breathing, burning sensations on the skin, or visible blisters.
2. Remove contaminated clothing: Carefully remove any clothing that may have come into contact with the chemical agent.
3. Decontaminate the affected area:
- Rinse the exposed skin with large amounts of water for at least 15 minutes.
- Use soap if available, but avoid scrubbing to prevent further irritation.
4. Administer first aid based on symptoms:
- For nerve agents: Monitor for signs such as muscle twitching, convulsions, or loss of consciousness.
- For blistering agents: Apply a sterile dressing to the affected area and keep it dry.
5. Seek medical assistance immediately: Contact a healthcare provider or evacuate the individual to a facility capable of handling chemical agent exposure.

Warning: Do not attempt to treat severe cases without professional help.
chemical agents, nerve agents, blistering agents, first aid, decontamination

Trauma Injuries (2) -

Provide First Aid for Injuries in Road Traffic Crashes in Hostile Environments
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Overview: Military vehicles often drive at high speed in difficult terrain, and accidents may occur in hostile environments with the presence of enemy forces or minefields.

Procedure:
1. Ensure scene safety: Check for any immediate...
Overview: Military vehicles often drive at high speed in difficult terrain, and accidents may occur in hostile environments with the presence of enemy forces or minefields.

Procedure:
1. Ensure scene safety: Check for any immediate threats such as enemy forces or unexploded ordnance before approaching the crash site.
2. Assess casualties: Quickly evaluate the condition of all individuals involved in the accident, prioritizing those with life-threatening injuries.
3. Provide first aid:
- Stop any severe bleeding using direct pressure and dressings.
- Immobilize suspected spinal injuries with a splint or rigid object if available.
- Administer CPR if necessary.
4. Evacuate casualties: If the environment is unsafe, evacuate the injured to a secure location before providing further care.
5. Report the incident: Notify command or medical personnel immediately for additional assistance and evacuation.

Warning: Be cautious of potential secondary threats such as minefields or enemy activity.
road traffic crash, hostile environment, first aid, evacuation
Provide First Aid for Beatings and Physical Abuse
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Overview: Mistreatment of civilians or suspected sympathizers can result in serious injuries that require immediate medical attention.

Procedure:
1. Ensure scene safety: Check for any threats such as ongoing violence or hostile...
Overview: Mistreatment of civilians or suspected sympathizers can result in serious injuries that require immediate medical attention.

Procedure:
1. Ensure scene safety: Check for any threats such as ongoing violence or hostile individuals before approaching the victim.
2. Assess injuries: Look for signs of trauma, including bruising, fractures, internal bleeding, and head injuries.
3. Provide first aid based on symptoms:
- For external injuries: Clean wounds with water and apply a sterile dressing to prevent infection.
- For suspected fractures: Immobilize the affected limb using splints or rigid objects if available.
- For head injuries: Monitor for signs of concussion, such as confusion, vomiting, or loss of consciousness.
4. Administer pain relief: If possible, provide over-the-counter pain medication to reduce discomfort.
5. Seek medical assistance immediately: Contact a healthcare provider or evacuate the individual to a facility capable of handling serious injuries.

Warning: Do not attempt to treat severe cases without professional help.
beating, physical abuse, first aid, trauma injuries

Vital Signs Monitoring (2) -

Body Temperature Ranges for Casualties at Rest
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

Use the following temperature ranges to assess a casualty’s condition:

• Hypothermia: Below 35.5 °C (95.9 °F)
• Normal body temperature: 35.5 – 37 °C (95.9 – 98.6 °F)
• Fever: 37 – 39 °C (98.6 – 102.2 °F)
• **High...
Procedure:

Use the following temperature ranges to assess a casualty’s condition:

• Hypothermia: Below 35.5 °C (95.9 °F)
• Normal body temperature: 35.5 – 37 °C (95.9 – 98.6 °F)
• Fever: 37 – 39 °C (98.6 – 102.2 °F)
• High fever: 39 °C (102.2 °F) and above

Note: This information is crucial for determining the severity of a casualty’s condition and guiding further care.
body temperature, hypothermia, fever, casualty monitoring
Measuring Pulse and Respiratory Rate in a Casualty
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

1. Count the pulse: Use your fingers to feel the casualty’s pulse.
- Count the number of beats in a 30-second period, then multiply by 2 to get the rate per minute.
2. Count respiratory rate: Observe the casualty’s...
Procedure:

1. Count the pulse: Use your fingers to feel the casualty’s pulse.
- Count the number of beats in a 30-second period, then multiply by 2 to get the rate per minute.
2. Count respiratory rate: Observe the casualty’s breathing (inhalation + exhalation).
- Count the number of breaths in a 30-second period, then multiply by 2 to get the rate per minute.
3. Avoid alerting the casualty: Ensure they are not aware you are counting their pulse or respiratory rate.
4. Report regularly: Provide this information to the relevant supervision level in the chain of casualty care to determine necessary assistance and materials.
pulse, respiratory rate, casualty monitoring, first aid, vital signs

Documentation (2) -

Maintain Patient Documentation Using S.O.A.P Format
High Priority Source: Survival and Austere Medicine
Purpose: Keep detailed and organized medical records for each patient treated.

Steps:
1. Subjective (S): Record what the patient reports, including symptoms, history, and concerns. Example: *A headache for 2 days with associated fever,...
Purpose: Keep detailed and organized medical records for each patient treated.

Steps:
1. Subjective (S): Record what the patient reports, including symptoms, history, and concerns. Example: *A headache for 2 days with associated fever, nausea, and a stiff neck.*
2. Objective (O): Document findings from physical examination or investigations. Example: *Fever of 39 degrees, dehydrated appearance, purple rash.*
3. Assessment (A): Provide your diagnosis based on the subjective and objective information. Example: *Probably bacterial meningitis.*
4. Plan (P): Outline the management plan for the patient. Example: *IV antibiotics and fluids; isolate from others.*

Additional Notes:
• Keep a chronological record of ongoing conditions and treatments.
• Document pre-event medical information such as blood type, existing health problems, etc.
• Maintain records for legal and ethical accountability in case of future review.
medical documentation, soap format, patient records, survival medicine, emergency care
Recording Casualty Information in a First Aid Log
Reference Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

1. Fill out the first aid log: Use the provided template to record information.
- Include: Name of the person in charge, date, time, location of injury, casualty details (name, age, gender), care given, and evacuation...
Procedure:

1. Fill out the first aid log: Use the provided template to record information.
- Include: Name of the person in charge, date, time, location of injury, casualty details (name, age, gender), care given, and evacuation details.
2. Use consistent numbering: Ensure that reference numbers match those on the casualty’s medical card.
3. Indicate death if necessary: If the casualty has died, record the location and time of death.
4. Write clearly: Make sure all entries are legible and easy to read.
5. Number casualties sequentially: Number each casualty from 1 onwards as they are treated.
6. Number new tables properly: Continue numbering any additional tables without interruption.
casualty log, first aid documentation, medical record, injury reporting

Injury Immobilization (2) -

Immobilize Suspected Neck Injury
Critical Source: FM 4 25 11 First Aid
Procedure:

1. If a neck injury is suspected, immediately immobilize the head and neck.
2. Place a roll of cloth under the casualty’s neck.
3. Put weighted boots (filled with dirt or sand) or rocks on both sides of his head.

Warning: Do...
Procedure:

1. If a neck injury is suspected, immediately immobilize the head and neck.
2. Place a roll of cloth under the casualty’s neck.
3. Put weighted boots (filled with dirt or sand) or rocks on both sides of his head.

Warning: Do not move the casualty to place padding.
neck injury, immobilize, head and neck, first aid, trauma
Immobilize the Upper Extremity in Emergency Situations
High Priority Source: NATO Emergency War Surgery
Procedure:

1. Assess the injury and determine the appropriate method of immobilization based on the type and severity of the fracture or dislocation.
