4. Exceptions to the rule:
- Lightning strike victims: Resuscitation has been successful after 2 hours or more.
- Cold water immersion victims: Similar outcomes as lightning strikes.
5. Important note: If the pulse is absent (cardiac arrest), resuscitation alone may not be sufficient for recovery. The casualty is considered deceased if attempts are unsuccessful.
CPR outcomes, cardiac arrest, time limits, lightning strike, cold water immersion

Medical Assessment (1) -

Assessment of Disorders of Consciousness on Expeditions
High Priority Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Common causes of disorders of consciousness on expeditions:

• Head injuries
• Fainting
• Convulsions
• Death

It is very worrying if someone cannot respond normally on an expedition because of an accident or illness. There are...
Common causes of disorders of consciousness on expeditions:

• Head injuries
• Fainting
• Convulsions
• Death

It is very worrying if someone cannot respond normally on an expedition because of an accident or illness. There are many reasons why someone may not be fully conscious; the above list includes some of the more common causes.
disorders of consciousness,head injury,fainting,convulsions,expeditions

Seizures (1) -

Management of Convulsions (Seizures)
Critical Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Causes of Seizures:

• Epilepsy
• Brain infections (e.g., meningitis, encephalitis)
• Head injuries
• Low blood sugar in diabetics
• Alcohol or drug withdrawal

Key Observations During a Fit:

• Duration of the fit
• **Loss of...
Causes of Seizures:

• Epilepsy
• Brain infections (e.g., meningitis, encephalitis)
• Head injuries
• Low blood sugar in diabetics
• Alcohol or drug withdrawal

Key Observations During a Fit:

• Duration of the fit
• Loss of consciousness
• Involvement of all limbs in convulsions
• Eye rolling, salivation, and incontinence
• Period of sleepiness after the fit

First Aid Steps:

1. Do not restrain the person unless injury is likely.
2. Open the airway using a head tilt and chin lift.
3. Do NOT force anything between the teeth (risk of broken teeth or bites).
4. Place the casualty in the recovery position (see Figure 13.1) to protect the airway.
5. If the fit occurs following a head injury, evacuate immediately.
6. If meningitis is suspected (high fever, severe headache, vomiting, stiff neck, sensitivity to light, rash), treat with antibiotics and arrange for immediate evacuation.
convulsions, seizures, first aid, head injury evacuation, meningitis signs, recovery position

Wound Dressing (1) -

Dressing and Bandaging Wounds
Reference Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Purpose: To properly dress and bandage wounds to promote healing.

Procedure:
1. Apply a non-stick sterile dressing (such as Melolin or Jelonet) directly against the wound.
2. Place sterile gauze swabs over the dressing to absorb any...
Purpose: To properly dress and bandage wounds to promote healing.

Procedure:
1. Apply a non-stick sterile dressing (such as Melolin or Jelonet) directly against the wound.
2. Place sterile gauze swabs over the dressing to absorb any pus or exudate.
3. Use crepe bandage, Tubinet, or Tubigrip to hold the dressing in place securely.
4. Ensure that the bandage does not apply pressure or cause constriction to the wound area.
5. Proper bandaging techniques are taught on all first aid courses and should be practiced regularly.
dressing wounds, sterile gauze, crepe bandage, wound protection

Wound Healing (1) -

Promoting Healing and Restoration of Function in Wounds
Standard Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Purpose: To aid wound healing and restore function to the affected area.

Procedure:
1. Ensure a healthy diet rich in protein, vitamins, and minerals to support tissue repair.
2. Provide rest for the injured area initially, but avoid...
Purpose: To aid wound healing and restore function to the affected area.

Procedure:
1. Ensure a healthy diet rich in protein, vitamins, and minerals to support tissue repair.
2. Provide rest for the injured area initially, but avoid prolonged splinting which can lead to stiffness or muscle wasting.
3. Keep joints adjacent to wounds or burns mobile to maintain flexibility and prevent contractures.
4. At altitudes below 3,000 meters, increased oxygen levels may accelerate healing.
5. Monitor the wound regularly for signs of infection or delayed healing.
wound healing, rest, joint mobility, nutrition for recovery

Amputation Management (1) -

Managing Amputation Injuries
Critical Source: First Aid and Management of Minor Injuries
Amputation of a digit or limb may be replaced by microsurgery if the patient and the amputated part can be delivered to a surgeon in less than 6 hours. The amputated part should be kept cool, preferably in a container with ice, but not in direct...
Amputation of a digit or limb may be replaced by microsurgery if the patient and the amputated part can be delivered to a surgeon in less than 6 hours. The amputated part should be kept cool, preferably in a container with ice, but not in direct contact with the ice. In an expedition setting it is highly unlikely that such surgical facilities will be available; in this case, treat the bleeding with direct pressure and elevation. The stump should be cleaned gently and then covered with a non-adherent dressing such as paraffin gauze. People with these injuries need to be evacuated to allow surgical treatment to shorten any bone ends and cover the stump with a flap of skin so that healing can take place.
amputation,microsurgery,ice_storage,stump_care,evacuation

Armed Conflicts Preparedness (1) -

Prepare for First Aid in Armed Conflicts
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Preparation Steps:

1. Understand your humanitarian role:
- Know and respect the distinctive emblems (Red Cross/Red Crescent) and the basic rules protecting individuals.
- Strengthen your moral standing and the image of the Red Cross...
Preparation Steps:

1. Understand your humanitarian role:
- Know and respect the distinctive emblems (Red Cross/Red Crescent) and the basic rules protecting individuals.
- Strengthen your moral standing and the image of the Red Cross and Red Crescent.

2. Duties and rights of First Aiders:
- Understand your duties, including providing care to casualties in conflict zones.
- Be aware of your rights, such as protection from retaliation for providing aid.

3. Training Programs:
- Acquire technical skills like wound management, airway clearance, and triage.
- Develop personal capabilities such as stress management and decision-making under pressure.

4. Equipment Preparation:
- Ensure you have a well-stocked First Aid kit/bag with essential supplies (e.g., bandages, tourniquets, antiseptics).
- Include tools for stabilizing injuries (splints, gauze, etc.).

5. Preparatory Plans:
- As a rule: Prepare mentally and physically for emergencies.
- During the mobilization phase: Coordinate with other First Aiders and ensure supplies are ready.
- On site: Follow protocols for casualty care and safety.

6. Coping with Stress:
- Practice relaxation techniques to manage psychological stress after interventions.
- Participate in debriefing sessions to process traumatic experiences.
first aid, armed conflicts, preparedness, training, equipment, stress management

Life Saving Techniques (1) -

Provide Immediate Life-Saving Care to Casualties
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Steps for Immediate Life-Saving Care:

1. Assess the Scene for Safety:
- Ensure your own safety and that of others before approaching casualties.
- Conduct a security assessment of the scene (e.g., check for ongoing threats or...
Steps for Immediate Life-Saving Care:

1. Assess the Scene for Safety:
- Ensure your own safety and that of others before approaching casualties.
- Conduct a security assessment of the scene (e.g., check for ongoing threats or hazards).

2. Check Airway, Breathing, and Circulation (ABCs):
- Open the airway using the head-tilt/chin-lift maneuver if there is no suspected spinal injury.
- Check for breathing by looking, listening, and feeling for at least 10 seconds.
- If not breathing, begin rescue breaths immediately.

3. Control Visible Bleeding:
- Apply direct pressure to the wound with a clean dressing or cloth.
- Use a tourniquet if bleeding is severe and cannot be controlled by direct pressure (apply above the injury site).

4. Stabilize Injuries:
- Immobilize fractures using splints or available materials.
- Avoid moving casualties with suspected spinal injuries unless absolutely necessary for safety.

5. Monitor Vital Signs:
- Continuously check breathing, pulse, and level of consciousness.
- Document observations and report to medical personnel if possible.
airway management, breathing assessment, circulation control, bleeding control, injury stabilization

Specific Health Problems (1) -

Provide Care for Specific Health Problems in Conflict Zones
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Steps to Address Common Health Issues:

1. Tear Gas Exposure:
- Remove the casualty from the affected area immediately.
- Wash exposed skin with water and soap.
- Rinse eyes thoroughly with clean water if they are affected.
-...
Steps to Address Common Health Issues:

1. Tear Gas Exposure:
- Remove the casualty from the affected area immediately.
- Wash exposed skin with water and soap.
- Rinse eyes thoroughly with clean water if they are affected.
- Administer oxygen if available, and seek medical help for severe cases.

2. Injuries from Anti-Personnel Mines or Explosive Remnants of War:
- Do not attempt to remove any explosive devices yourself.
- Mark the location clearly with warning signs (e.g., red flags) and notify authorities immediately.
- Provide first aid for injuries such as burns, lacerations, or amputations if possible.

3. Cardiac Arrest:
- Check for responsiveness and breathing.
- If unresponsive and not breathing normally, begin CPR (cardiopulmonary resuscitation) immediately.
- Use an automated external defibrillator (AED) if available and trained to do so.

4. Manage the Dying and the Dead:
- Provide comfort care for those who are dying, including pain relief if possible.
- Ensure the dead are treated with dignity and respect.
- Follow local protocols for collecting and burying the deceased safely.
tear gas, mine injuries, cardiac arrest, dying care, dead management

Adaptations For Conflicts (1) -

Adapting First Aid Practices for Armed Conflicts
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Adapting First Aid Practices in Armed Conflicts

When providing first aid in armed conflicts or other violent situations, consider the following adaptations:

• Knowledge of international humanitarian law: Understand and respect the...
Adapting First Aid Practices in Armed Conflicts

When providing first aid in armed conflicts or other violent situations, consider the following adaptations:

• Knowledge of international humanitarian law: Understand and respect the principles of international humanitarian law relevant to your role as a First Aider.
• Security and protection awareness: Maintain constant vigilance for physical and mental safety risks, including threats from violence, weapons, or environmental hazards.
• Specific skills for weapon-related injuries: Acquire specialized training in managing injuries caused by firearms, explosives, and other weapons commonly used in conflicts.
• Triage procedures: Implement triage to prioritize actions and resources when dealing with mass casualties and limited medical supplies.
• Broad approach due to health-care system deficiencies: Be prepared for disorganized or under-resourced health systems and address challenges such as lack of access to clean water, food, shelter, etc.
international humanitarian law,security awareness,weapon injuries,triaige,healthcare system deficiencies

Manual Scope (1) -

Scope of the Manual: What is Not Covered
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
What This Manual Does NOT Cover

This manual assumes you are already trained in basic life-saving and stabilizing techniques used during peacetime. It does not provide detailed information on:

• International humanitarian law: Including the...
What This Manual Does NOT Cover

This manual assumes you are already trained in basic life-saving and stabilizing techniques used during peacetime. It does not provide detailed information on:

• International humanitarian law: Including the Geneva Conventions and their Additional Protocols.
• Distinctive emblems: Use of Red Cross/Red Crescent symbols.
• History and principles of the International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement.
• Disease prevention and control: Refer to your National Society, local health authorities, or the World Health Organization for these topics.
• Logistical and administrative activities: These are not explained in detail here.
• Non-conventional weapons (nuclear, radiological, biological, chemical): Specific knowledge and resources required for handling such threats are beyond the scope of this manual. Refer to specialized documents from national civil defense departments or military sources.
manual limitations,international humanitarian law,disease prevention,non-conventional weapons

Preparation For Hostile Environments (1) -

Prepare for First Aid in Armed Conflicts and Other Situations of Violence
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Preparation is essential to provide effective first aid during armed conflicts or other violent situations:

1. Training: Ensure all personnel are well-trained in medical procedures, trauma care, and emergency response.
2. **Equipment...
Preparation is essential to provide effective first aid during armed conflicts or other violent situations:

1. Training: Ensure all personnel are well-trained in medical procedures, trauma care, and emergency response.
2. Equipment readiness: Maintain a stock of medical supplies, protective gear, and tools for field use.
3. Security awareness: Understand the risks posed by weapons, explosive remnants, and hostile environments to protect yourself and others.
4. Psychological preparedness: Be ready to support individuals affected by trauma, loss, or psychological distress.
first aid training, hostile environment preparation, trauma support, medical readiness

Preparedness (1) -

Establish a First Aider Preparedness Program
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
A good preparedness programme allows you to respond automatically:

• Limiting the effects of your emotional shock.
• Contributing to your protection in armed conflicts and other situations of violence, regardless of fear and the hazardous...
A good preparedness programme allows you to respond automatically:

• Limiting the effects of your emotional shock.
• Contributing to your protection in armed conflicts and other situations of violence, regardless of fear and the hazardous environment, by preventing you from being injured or sick.
• Enhancing your skills and strengthening your flexibility, despite the highly specific nature of the situations, casualties and tasks.

It is important to explain matters to your relatives and friends so that they are comfortable with and supportive of your duties, rights and tasks in such exceptional and dangerous situations. This is similar to explanations you already give about your responsibilities and activities in peacetime.

In the midst of a violent situation, you will work efficiently thanks to routines and automatic reflexes that you learn and practise in peacetime.
first aider, preparedness program, emotional shock, protection in violence, skills development

Ethical Guidelines (1) -

Maintain Moral Integrity and Respect for International Humanitarian Law in Armed Conflicts
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

• Understand and uphold international humanitarian law as a foundational protective system during armed conflicts and other situations of violence.

• Earn respect through attitude and actions, ensuring that your behavior...
Procedure:

• Understand and uphold international humanitarian law as a foundational protective system during armed conflicts and other situations of violence.

• Earn respect through attitude and actions, ensuring that your behavior reflects the values of the Red Cross and Red Crescent at all times.

• Demonstrate moral integrity in everyday interactions and during emergencies, especially when wearing the distinctive emblem of the National Society.

• Act without discrimination, providing assistance based solely on need, regardless of race, color, sex, language, religion, political opinion, national origin, or other status.

• Be aware of your role as a representative of the National Society and the Red Cross or Red Crescent. Your behavior can significantly influence public perception and the effectiveness of aid programs.

• Avoid accepting money or gifts from casualties or their families, friends, or colleagues during armed conflicts.

• Respect the dignity of casualties, ensuring that all actions are conducted with compassion and professionalism.

• Contribute to health education and preventive programs, helping to reduce injuries and the spread of disease in communities.

• Adhere to the Fundamental Principles of the International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement: humanity, impartiality, neutrality, independence, voluntary service, unity, and universality.

• Retain trust through consistent humanitarian commitment and demonstrated skills in all situations.
moral integrity,international humanitarian law,red cross,red crescent,discrimination,health education,ethical guidelines

Duties Of First Aiders (1) -

Uphold the Duties of a First Aider in Armed Conflicts
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

• Protect and save lives, assisting others in doing so during armed conflicts or other situations of violence.

• Do no harm to casualties or individuals under your care.

• Respect and preserve the dignity of all...
Procedure:

• Protect and save lives, assisting others in doing so during armed conflicts or other situations of violence.

• Do no harm to casualties or individuals under your care.

• Respect and preserve the dignity of all casualties, ensuring that they are treated with compassion and professionalism.

• Participate in disease control, contributing to efforts aimed at preventing illness and the spread of infectious diseases.

• Contribute to public health education, promoting preventive measures that reduce injuries and disease transmission.

• Be flexible and versatile, taking on diverse roles beyond direct casualty care, such as logistics or administration when needed.

• Provide assistance based solely on need, without discrimination based on race, color, sex, language, religion, political opinion, national origin, or other status.

• Follow the rules and procedures of your National Society and international humanitarian law in all actions.

• Do not refuse services required by medical ethics, even under difficult circumstances.

• Avoid accepting money or gifts from casualties or their families, friends, or colleagues as compensation for assistance provided.
duties of first aiders,international humanitarian law,medical ethics,disease control,public health education

Legal Rights And Protection (1) -

Understand the Legal Rights of First Aiders in Armed Conflicts
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Legal Protection for First Aiders:

During an armed conflict, first aiders who are providing care to the wounded and sick have the same legal protection under international humanitarian law as those they are assisting. This includes:

1. **Right...
Legal Protection for First Aiders:

During an armed conflict, first aiders who are providing care to the wounded and sick have the same legal protection under international humanitarian law as those they are assisting. This includes:

1. Right to be respected.
2. Right not to be attacked.
3. Access to areas where services are needed, subject to limitations such as ongoing fighting or minefields.
4. Right to care for the sick and wounded, regardless of whether they are civilians or military personnel, and to remove them from the field for treatment.
5. Right to provide assistance in accordance with your training and available resources.
6. Right not to be compelled to perform services that violate medical ethics.
7. Right not to be prevented from performing duties required by medical ethics.
8. Right to repatriation, if captured, provided your care is not essential for other prisoners.

These rights ensure the safety and effectiveness of first aiders in conflict zones.
first aider rights, legal protection, armed conflict, international humanitarian law

Technical Skills Training (1) -

Develop Technical Skills for First Aid in Armed Conflicts
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Training Objectives:

• Understand the Fundamental Principles of the International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement in practice.
• Know your duties and rights as a first aider under international humanitarian law during armed...
Training Objectives:

• Understand the Fundamental Principles of the International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement in practice.
• Know your duties and rights as a first aider under international humanitarian law during armed conflicts.
• Learn to adopt safe behavior when facing hazards such as armed conflict or violence, and encourage others to do the same.
• Use appropriate protective gear, such as gloves, and ensure others follow safe procedures.
• Master basic life-saving techniques, including:
- Transporting casualties safely using improvised stretchers.
- Adapting wound care for injuries caused by weapons.
- Improvising medical tools with available materials (e.g., splints from tree branches, bamboo strips, or carton; bandages from cloth; stretchers made from doors, blankets, and sticks).
• Conduct real-life simulations that include teamwork, natural obstacles, bystanders, public services, and telecommunications.

Training should be practical and action-oriented to ensure readiness in high-stress environments.
first aid training, technical skills, improvisation, armed conflict, wound care

Personal Development Skills (1) -

Enhance Personal Capabilities for First Aid in Armed Conflicts
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Personal Development Objectives:

• Improve emotional resilience and coping strategies to manage the psychological stress of working in conflict zones.
• Strengthen communication skills, especially when interacting with casualties,...
Personal Development Objectives:

• Improve emotional resilience and coping strategies to manage the psychological stress of working in conflict zones.
• Strengthen communication skills, especially when interacting with casualties, bystanders, and other humanitarian workers.
• Develop teamwork and leadership abilities for effective collaboration during emergencies.
• Learn how to manage administrative and logistical tasks, such as record-keeping, resource distribution, and coordination with other organizations.
• Participate in refresher training sessions to stay updated on best practices and share knowledge with colleagues from different parts of the National Society.

These capabilities are essential for maintaining both personal well-being and operational effectiveness in challenging environments.
personal capabilities, emotional resilience, communication skills, teamwork, administrative tasks

Risk Management In Conflict Zones (1) -

Assessing and Managing Danger in Armed Conflicts
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

1. Train yourself to quickly assess situations for danger:
- Practice by watching war movies or TV reports and identifying potential dangers.
- Ask yourself: *What are the dangers here? Where could I take cover if under...
Procedure:

1. Train yourself to quickly assess situations for danger:
- Practice by watching war movies or TV reports and identifying potential dangers.
- Ask yourself: *What are the dangers here? Where could I take cover if under threat?*
- Use real-life scenarios, such as walking to the market or driving to a healthcare center, and mentally simulate being fired upon. Identify safe places to go in these situations.

2. Develop situational awareness:
- Look around and evaluate your environment for potential threats regularly.
- Make this a routine exercise during daily activities until it becomes second nature.

3. Maintain self-control and calmness:
- Stay composed in high-stress situations to help others remain calm as well.
- Avoid impulsive actions; observe the situation before taking any steps.

4. Understand the environment of armed conflict:
- Recognize that society’s peacetime rules may not apply, and confusion or heightened emotions are common.
- Proceed only if it is safe and secure to do so.

5. Follow local security procedures:
- Participate in organized drills such as reaching a shelter, reacting to a rifle shot, or taking cover.

6. Know your limits:
- Understand when not to take action or when to stop.
- It is wise and courageous to refuse to put yourself in an unsafe situation.
- Recognizing that you may not yet have the required capabilities is a sign of strength, not weakness.
danger assessment,situational awareness,self-control,armed conflict,risk management,security procedures

Ethics And Guidelines (1) -

Maintain Neutrality and Impartiality in First Aid During Armed Conflict
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Ensure ethical conduct as a first aider during armed conflict.

Procedure:
1. Understand and commit to the Fundamental Principles of the International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement, including neutrality,...
Objective: Ensure ethical conduct as a first aider during armed conflict.

Procedure:
1. Understand and commit to the Fundamental Principles of the International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement, including neutrality, impartiality, and respect for human dignity.
2. Recognize that personal emotions may challenge your ability to remain neutral or impartial, especially if family or friends are involved.
3. Undergo thorough training in ethical guidelines and medical ethics to prepare for difficult situations.
4. Treat all casualties with dignity and without discrimination, regardless of their background or the circumstances under which they were injured.
5. Maintain confidentiality regarding any information shared by injured persons.
6. Avoid actions that may be perceived as taking sides in a conflict, even if it is difficult due to personal feelings.
7. In situations where triage is necessary (e.g., mass casualties), prioritize saving the greatest number of lives with available resources and time.
neutrality, impartiality, ethics, triage, armed conflict, first aid

Triage And Mass Casualties (1) -

Set Priorities in Triage During Mass Casualties
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Make ethical and effective decisions during mass casualty incidents.

Procedure:
1. Recognize that in situations with a large number of casualties, it may be impossible to save everyone or provide the same level of care to all.
2....
Objective: Make ethical and effective decisions during mass casualty incidents.

Procedure:
1. Recognize that in situations with a large number of casualties, it may be impossible to save everyone or provide the same level of care to all.
2. Use triage principles to prioritize treatment based on urgency and potential for survival (e.g., treat those with life-threatening injuries first).
3. Make decisions based on available resources and time: ask yourself, *"What is most urgent? What can I realistically achieve with the time and resources available to me?"*
4. Accept that some decisions may go against personal convictions or usual practices (e.g., not starting treatment for a severely injured casualty if resources are limited).
5. Understand that prioritizing care for the greatest number of people is a major achievement in such situations.
6. Ensure all actions align with medical ethics and international humanitarian law.
triage, mass casualties, prioritization, first aid, ethics

Communication Skills (1) -

Develop Communication Skills for Effective First Aid Delivery
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Improve communication skills to build trust and ensure effective care delivery in high-stress environments.

Procedure:
1. Practice active listening, verbal clarity, and non-verbal communication (e.g., body language,...
Objective: Improve communication skills to build trust and ensure effective care delivery in high-stress environments.

Procedure:
1. Practice active listening, verbal clarity, and non-verbal communication (e.g., body language, eye contact) when interacting with casualties, team members, or the public.
2. Adapt your behavior to local customs, beliefs, and rules, especially in unfamiliar environments.
3. Be respectful of cultural norms, such as restrictions on physical or verbal contact between unrelated individuals of different genders.
4. In situations where direct interaction is restricted by local rules, find alternative solutions within those boundaries (e.g., guide an authorized individual to assist a casualty).
5. Use common sense and flexibility in communication strategies when dealing with angry, frightened, or violent individuals.
6. Learn about the local community structure, traditional support networks, and how people interact in your area of operation.
7. Communicate clearly and calmly during emergencies to gain cooperation and support from others.
communication skills, cultural sensitivity, first aid, community relations

Teamwork And Conduct (1) -

Maintain Teamwork and Professional Conduct in Armed Conflicts
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
To ensure effective collaboration and adherence to humanitarian principles during interventions:

1. Respect the Fundamental Principles of the International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement:
- Explicitly refer to these principles.
-...
To ensure effective collaboration and adherence to humanitarian principles during interventions:

1. Respect the Fundamental Principles of the International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement:
- Explicitly refer to these principles.
- Encourage others to do so as well.

2. Stay within your humanitarian mission:
- Do not engage in activities outside of your assigned role.

3. Promote safe behavior among team members:
- Encourage secure practices and behaviors during missions.

4. Participate in security information sharing sessions:
- Attend briefings, discussions, and incident reporting meetings with your team.

5. Warn colleagues of dangerous situations:
- Use simple words or a pre-established code (e.g., an emergency radio frequency) to alert others if you are aware of danger.

6. Support and respect colleagues:
- Offer assistance when needed and show solidarity with your team members.

7. Share feelings with trusted individuals:
- Discuss emotional or psychological stress with people you feel comfortable with.

8. Relax after the mission:
- Take time to decompress and recover mentally and physically.
teamwork, humanitarian principles, safety, communication, support

Equipment Maintenance (1) -

Maintain and Use a First Aid Kit/Bag Properly
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
To ensure the kit is always ready for use in emergencies:

1. Keep the contents clean and organized:
- Ensure all items are in good working condition.

2. Replenish supplies after each use:
- Replace used or depleted items...
To ensure the kit is always ready for use in emergencies:

1. Keep the contents clean and organized:
- Ensure all items are in good working condition.

2. Replenish supplies after each use:
- Replace used or depleted items promptly.

3. Be prepared to improvise if necessary:
- Use alternative materials when kit contents are unavailable.

4. Display the distinctive emblem on the kit/bag:
- This identifies it as a First Aid item.

5. Do not use the kit for purposes other than First Aid:
- Ensure it is used only in emergency medical situations.

6. Never leave the kit unattended:
- Prevent theft or misuse by keeping it secure at all times.
first_aid_kit, maintenance, security, improvisation

Protective Emblems (1) -

Proper Use of Distinctive Emblems as Protective Devices
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Ensure the correct use and display of distinctive emblems for protection during armed conflicts.

Steps:
1. When used as a protective device, the distinctive emblem must be displayed prominently and in large dimensions...
Objective: Ensure the correct use and display of distinctive emblems for protection during armed conflicts.

Steps:
1. When used as a protective device, the distinctive emblem must be displayed prominently and in large dimensions (e.g., one on the chest and another on the back).
2. In armed conflict situations, according to international humanitarian law, the following apply:
- Medical personnel of armed forces and National Society staff/volunteers performing similar duties are entitled to wear white armbands with the distinctive emblem.
3. Conditions for wearing emblems:
- The National Society must be recognized and authorized by the government to assist the armed forces’ medical services.
- National Society members must be subject to military laws and regulations.
4. Armbands must be issued and authenticated by an official military authority.
distinctive emblem,protective device,international humanitarian law,white armbands,National Society,military authorization

Legal Compliance (1) -

Respect National Laws and Authorities in Armed Conflicts
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Ensure compliance with legal obligations during armed conflicts.

Procedure:
1. Always adhere to national laws, particularly those governing healthcare and aid activities.
2. Respect the decisions of local authorities at all...
Objective: Ensure compliance with legal obligations during armed conflicts.

Procedure:
1. Always adhere to national laws, particularly those governing healthcare and aid activities.
2. Respect the decisions of local authorities at all times.
3. Familiarize yourself with international humanitarian law and strictly obey its principles.
4. Use the distinctive emblems (e.g., Red Cross/Red Crescent) appropriately to identify your role as a First Aider.
5. Ensure that all actions are conducted in safe and secure conditions.
6. Avoid any behavior or action that could be interpreted as violating international humanitarian law.
international humanitarian law, legal compliance, armed conflicts, first aid, authority respect

Casualty Care (1) -

Responsibilities of a First Aider in Armed Conflicts
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Provide effective and ethical care to casualties during armed conflicts.

Procedure:
1. Do no harm: Ensure that your actions do not cause further injury or complications to the casualty.
2. **Provide assistance for the...
Objective: Provide effective and ethical care to casualties during armed conflicts.

Procedure:
1. Do no harm: Ensure that your actions do not cause further injury or complications to the casualty.
2. Provide assistance for the greatest number of people: Prioritize care based on severity of injuries, ensuring maximum impact with limited resources.
3. Preserve life by supporting vital functions: Focus on maintaining breathing, circulation, and consciousness in casualties.
4. Limit injury effects: Take steps to prevent worsening of conditions or complications from injuries.
5. Alleviate suffering: Provide pain relief and psychological support where possible.
6. Monitor and record vital signs: Regularly check and document the casualty’s breathing rate, pulse, blood pressure, temperature, and level of consciousness.
7. Effectiveness of measures taken: Keep detailed records of interventions performed to ensure continuity of care.
8. Transport casualties if needed: Coordinate with other medical personnel or evacuation teams for safe transfer.
9. Hand over casualties to the next link in the chain: Ensure a smooth transition by providing all relevant information about the casualty’s condition and treatment received.
10. Take care of yourself: Maintain your own physical and mental health to ensure you can continue assisting others.
first aid responsibilities, casualty care, armed conflicts, vital signs monitoring, psychological support

Safety Preparedness (1) -

Ensure Personal and Team Safety in Armed Conflicts
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Maintain the safety of First Aiders to ensure continued assistance to casualties.

Procedure:
1. Prioritize your own safety at all times: You cannot help others if you are injured or dead.
2. Be aware of emotional and...
Objective: Maintain the safety of First Aiders to ensure continued assistance to casualties.

Procedure:
1. Prioritize your own safety at all times: You cannot help others if you are injured or dead.
2. Be aware of emotional and psychological stress, including:
- Risk of personal injury
- Impact on family, friends, or colleagues (e.g., loss of contact, theft of belongings)
- Exposure to traumatic scenes and sounds
3. Manage bystander interference:
- Bystanders may become aggressive or obstruct care efforts.
- Use calm communication and assertive body language to de-escalate situations.
4. Work in safe environments: Ensure that you are not exposed to unnecessary danger while providing aid.
5. Maintain mental resilience:
- Recognize the emotional toll of working in violent environments.
- Seek support from colleagues or supervisors when needed.
6. Use common sense and skills effectively:
- Stay focused on your mission to help casualties, even under extreme pressure.
7. Stay informed about humanitarian law:
- Understand the legal protections for medical personnel in conflict zones.
8. Prepare mentally and physically for long hours of work:
- Ensure you have adequate rest, water, and food during extended operations.
personal safety, bystander management, emotional stress, mental resilience, humanitarian law

Communication With Casualties (1) -

Communicate with Bystanders and Casualty Relatives/Friends
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- Reassure bystanders and the casualty’s relatives and friends through your calm and self-control.
• Maintain good contact with them to gather information about the security situation and sometimes about the casualty (identity, health background,...
- Reassure bystanders and the casualty’s relatives and friends through your calm and self-control.
• Maintain good contact with them to gather information about the security situation and sometimes about the casualty (identity, health background, etc.).
• Enlist their help if needed to move or care for casualties.
communication,bystanders,casualty relatives,security information

Team Communication (1) -

Communicate with Colleagues
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- Share security-related information with colleagues.
• Discuss your feelings about yourself and others with people you feel comfortable with.
colleagues,security information,emotional support

Communication With Authorities (1) -

Communicate with Local Authorities and Forces Involved in Fighting
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- If you come into contact with local authorities or forces involved in fighting:
- Explain your aims.
- Mention the relevant basic rules protecting individuals in situations of violence.
- Reference humanitarian principles.
• Collect...
- If you come into contact with local authorities or forces involved in fighting:
- Explain your aims.
- Mention the relevant basic rules protecting individuals in situations of violence.
- Reference humanitarian principles.
• Collect important information for your safety and that of your colleagues, being careful not to be mistaken for a spy.
local authorities,humanitarian principles,safety information

Media Communication (1) -

Communicate with the Media
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- If approached by the media or if they start to film you:
- Ask them to stop.
- Direct them to your team leader or other on-site persons designated to deal with journalists.
media,team leader,journalists

Self Communication (1) -

Communicate with Yourself
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- Practice self-care and maintain a humane and humanitarian attitude toward yourself.
self-care,self-awareness

Communication Guidelines (1) -

General Communication Guidelines
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- Send as much information as possible in a timely manner to your team leader or dispatch/command centre, including:
- What you are doing and have already done.
- What happened and is happening in your area.
• Receive accurate security-related...
- Send as much information as possible in a timely manner to your team leader or dispatch/command centre, including:
- What you are doing and have already done.
- What happened and is happening in your area.
• Receive accurate security-related information as often as possible.
• In communications:
- Be factual (not subjective).
- Be brief.
- Go straight to the point, giving clear and concise information.
- Limit conversations to the minimum needed for essential information exchange.
• Never give out names of casualties or police/military information.
communication guidelines,factual reporting,brevity

Radio Communication (1) -

Use Common Language for Radio Communications
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- For radio communications, use a common language.
• Depending on the means available and instructions received:
- Try to have several means of communication (VHF and HF radio, mobile telephone, messengers, etc.).
- Test your communication...
- For radio communications, use a common language.
• Depending on the means available and instructions received:
- Try to have several means of communication (VHF and HF radio, mobile telephone, messengers, etc.).
- Test your communication channels.
- Inform your team leader (or dispatch/command centre) of all your movements (departure and return) and any changes in itinerary.
radio communication,common language,multiple channels

Incident Reporting (1) -

Incident Reporting Procedure
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- In case of an incident:
- Convey information quickly to your team leader or dispatch/command centre.
- Provide descriptive but not extensive details about:
- What happened (type of incident, any injuries, etc.).
- Your future...
- In case of an incident:
- Convey information quickly to your team leader or dispatch/command centre.
- Provide descriptive but not extensive details about:
- What happened (type of incident, any injuries, etc.).
- Your future intentions and needs or requests.
- Wait for instructions.
• Remember that any transmitted or shared information can be intercepted and have political, strategic, or security implications. Any information that _can_ be misunderstood _will_ be misunderstood.
incident reporting,safety information,security implications

On Site Management (1) -

On-Site Approach and Management Phases
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- You are prepared and equipped with two main successive phases to manage:
- Situation management.
- Casualty management.
• Finally, you must think about managing yourself.
situation management,casualty management,self-management

Behavior In Armed Conflict (1) -

Maintain a Calm and Respectful Attitude in Violent Situations
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Procedure:

1. Approach all individuals, including those involved in violence or under the influence of alcohol/drugs, with calmness and respect.
2. Avoid confrontational behavior; if possible, use humor or small gestures (e.g., offering a...
Procedure:

1. Approach all individuals, including those involved in violence or under the influence of alcohol/drugs, with calmness and respect.
2. Avoid confrontational behavior; if possible, use humor or small gestures (e.g., offering a cigarette) to de-escalate tensions.
3. If an individual is out of control, leave the situation tactfully without escalating further conflict.
4. Always listen to others and explain your actions clearly when providing first aid or assistance.
5. Obey all orders from your team leader and follow established rules and procedures.
6. Foster a sense of teamwork and maintain good relations with local communities by respecting their culture, traditions, taboos, and dress codes.
7. Avoid showing off or being overly confident in personal matters such as sex-related topics.

Important Notes:
• Your behavior is judged based on your attitude and respect for basic security rules.
• Others will trust you more if you act with discipline and maintain a respectful demeanor.
calm behavior, respect, de-escalation, cultural sensitivity, first aid

Conflict Zones Hazards (1) -

Identify and Avoid Additional Hazards
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Be aware of other hazards that may be present during conflicts or emergencies:

• Structural hazards: Collapsed buildings, falling debris.
• Fire hazards: Burning or smoke-filled buildings.
• Environmental hazards: Confined spaces,...
Be aware of other hazards that may be present during conflicts or emergencies:

• Structural hazards: Collapsed buildings, falling debris.
• Fire hazards: Burning or smoke-filled buildings.
• Environmental hazards: Confined spaces, downed electrical wires, road traffic crashes, and secondary crash risks.
• Chemical hazards: Dangerous gases released from destroyed facilities.

Environmental conditions to be mindful of:
• Extreme temperatures (heat or cold).
• Wind, rain, snow, or sandstorms.
• Uneven terrain that may impede movement or increase risk.

Note: In addition to violence-related risks, you must also consider the possibility of road traffic accidents or diseases.
structural hazards,fire hazards,environmental hazards,road safety

Casualty Protection (1) -

Protect Casualties from Further Harm
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Casualty protection is a critical priority in conflict zones:

• Emergency removal: Move casualties to safety if they are unable to do so themselves (e.g., due to injury or exposure to violence).
• Shelter provision: Provide shelter that...
Casualty protection is a critical priority in conflict zones:

• Emergency removal: Move casualties to safety if they are unable to do so themselves (e.g., due to injury or exposure to violence).
• Shelter provision: Provide shelter that protects against further violence and environmental elements such as extreme temperatures, sun, rain, or wind.
• Disease prevention: Use professional practices to prevent the spread of communicable diseases.

Legal protection: International humanitarian law provides specific legal protections for wounded and sick individuals in armed conflict situations.
casualty removal,shelter,disease prevention,international law

Minefield Rescue (1) -

Rescue a Casualty in a Minefield Safely
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- Do not rush to the wounded person. This is a minefield: you could become the next casualty.

• Stop other people from approaching the casualty.

• Get help from mine-clearance or military personnel.

• If the casualty is next to a road or a safe...
- Do not rush to the wounded person. This is a minefield: you could become the next casualty.

• Stop other people from approaching the casualty.

• Get help from mine-clearance or military personnel.

• If the casualty is next to a road or a safe path and within reach:
- Do not attempt to "prod" a path to the person unless you have been trained to do so;
- First make sure that you have the necessary physical resources to remove the casualty (or can obtain them);
- Throw the casualty a rope or branch to latch on to;
- Drag him out.

• Speed is a top priority, as is preventing further ESSENTIALS injury to the casualty.

• If possible, drag the casualty along the head-to-toe axis, avoiding any unnecessary movement in other directions.
minefield,casualty rescue,safety,dragging,rope

Team Assistance (1) -

Mobilize Available People to Assist with Casualty Management
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- Mobilize any available person (for example, bystanders or casualties slightly injured and able to walk) to assist you in:
- Getting information about security conditions (take care that they are not mistaken for spies);
- Sending out the alert...
- Mobilize any available person (for example, bystanders or casualties slightly injured and able to walk) to assist you in:
- Getting information about security conditions (take care that they are not mistaken for spies);
- Sending out the alert and calling in more advanced help;
- Finding other help;
- Building a safe shelter;
- Bringing materials with which to make improvised devices (e.g. tree branches for making splints);
- Offering some comfort (physical or psychological) to casualties;
- Preparing food;
- Quickly removing casualties from danger;
- Performing life-saving tasks (if those assisting you have the necessary training);
- Carrying a casualty on a stretcher.

• Encourage bystanders to become involved.

• Make sure they have a proper concern for safety and security.

• Explain to them what you want and also perhaps how to do it, and make sure they understand and are willing to follow your instructions.

• Obtain their commitment.
bystander assistance,casualty management,safety,communication

Alert Systems (1) -

Issue an Effective Alert for Additional Help
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- Alerts should be sent out as soon as possible, but only when manageable, taking into account the circumstances: the procedure in place, assessment results and the communication means available.

• Issuing a successful alert depends on:
- You,...
- Alerts should be sent out as soon as possible, but only when manageable, taking into account the circumstances: the procedure in place, assessment results and the communication means available.

• Issuing a successful alert depends on:
- You, the sender (what information you give, to whom, and what response you expect or require);
- The communication system (what means are available – the more varied they are, the better – and how reliable and sustainable they are);
- The receiver (how your message is understood, processed and followed up).

• Communication should be two-way.

• Unless you are very near your team leader, among the communication means available choose the one that guarantees the alert will be conveyed rapidly and reliably (e.g. by sending a messenger to the nearest radio communication station). If possible, use a communication system that permits dialogue.
emergency alert,communication,team coordination,message delivery

Alert Checklist (1) -

Construct an Effective Alert Message
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- After collecting the necessary information, you should include in your alert message the items listed in the checklist below.

• ALERT MESSAGE (be precise and brief)

• CHECKLIST
alert message,checklist,communication,effective alert

Casualty Examination (1) -

Conducting a Casualty Examination in the Field
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Perform a thorough examination of a casualty while respecting privacy, cultural factors, and minimizing movement.

Steps:
1. Undress the casualty as necessary, depending on the situation and injuries.
2. **Respect the...
Objective: Perform a thorough examination of a casualty while respecting privacy, cultural factors, and minimizing movement.

Steps:
1. Undress the casualty as necessary, depending on the situation and injuries.
2. Respect the casualty’s privacy and modesty, ensuring that only essential parts are exposed for examination.
3. Consider local religious and cultural practices when undressing or examining the casualty.
4. Minimize movement of the casualty to prevent further injury or discomfort.
5. Avoid removing clothing stuck to wounds or burns; do not forcibly remove such items.
6. Ensure the casualty stays warm, using blankets or other available materials to prevent hypothermia.
7. Protect personal belongings of the casualty and avoid mixing clothes between casualties.
8. Turn the casualty to one side at some point during the examination to inspect the back for injuries.

Notes:
• Ensure that the examination is conducted in a safe, sheltered area whenever possible.
• If there are multiple casualties, ensure that each casualty’s clothing remains separate and identifiable.
casualty examination, privacy, cultural considerations, wound care, hypothermia prevention

Immediate Life Saving Measures (1) -

Initial Examination and Immediate Life-Saving Measures
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Quickly assess and provide life-saving interventions to a casualty.

Steps:
1. Assess the casualty’s status using the ABCDE approach:
- A (Airway): Check if the airway is clear. If compromised, perform airway control...
Objective: Quickly assess and provide life-saving interventions to a casualty.

Steps:
1. Assess the casualty’s status using the ABCDE approach:
- A (Airway): Check if the airway is clear. If compromised, perform airway control (e.g., opening the airway).
- B (Breathing): Assess breathing. If compromised, provide ventilation as necessary.
- C (Circulation): Check for signs of bleeding or circulation problems. Apply compressive bandages to major chest wall injuries and control bleeding.
- D (Disability): Evaluate for neurological deficits. Place the casualty in the recovery position if unconscious and not breathing.
- E (Exposure): Cover the casualty with a blanket or other available material to keep them warm.
2. Check for consciousness: Determine if the casualty is conscious, alert, and able to communicate.
3. Identify the type of injury: Determine whether the injury is penetrating or blunt.
4. Handle the “walking wounded”: For casualties who are conscious and able to move, mobilize them as needed and allow them to assist in their own care if possible.
5. Immobilize the cervical spine if there is a suspicion of spinal injury based on the mechanism of injury.
6. Splint major fractures and apply compressive bandages for traumatic amputations.
7. Prevent shock by ensuring adequate circulation and covering the casualty to maintain body temperature.

Notes:
• These steps should be performed quickly and systematically, prioritizing life-saving interventions over other tasks.
• In situations involving mass casualties, triage may dictate that certain casualties receive less immediate care or that care is terminated in specific cases.
ABCDE examination, airway control, breathing support, circulation management, shock prevention, spinal immobilization

Determination Of Death (1) -

Determining if a Casualty is Dead or Alive
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Accurately determine the status of a casualty as dead or alive in armed conflict situations.

Steps:
1. Assess for signs of life using the ABCDE criteria:
- A (Air intake): No air intake (A = 0).
- **B (Lung...
Objective: Accurately determine the status of a casualty as dead or alive in armed conflict situations.

Steps:
1. Assess for signs of life using the ABCDE criteria:
- A (Air intake): No air intake (A = 0).
- B (Lung ventilation): No lung ventilation (B = 0).
- C (Pulse): No pulse (C = 0).
- D (Movement): No movement (D = 0).
- E (Body temperature): The body feels cold (E = 0).
2. Check for dilated pupils: Pupils are dilated and do not react to light.
3. If in doubt, assume the casualty is alive until a qualified health professional confirms death or until all ABCDE criteria are met.
4. In cases of doubt or according to local procedure, continue resuscitation measures until death is confirmed by a medical professional.

Notes:
• In armed conflicts, casualties may suffer severe injuries (e.g., decapitation) that make their death obvious.
• If the casualty is determined to be dead, refer to the appropriate section for handling deceased individuals.
death determination, ABCDE criteria, resuscitation, trauma assessment

End Of Life Care (1) -

Provide Comfort to Dying Persons in Armed Conflicts
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Provide comfort and support to dying persons during armed conflicts.

Steps:
1. Seek help from your team leader or a healthcare professional if available.
2. Respect the need for privacy and any rituals that apply to the...
Objective: Provide comfort and support to dying persons during armed conflicts.

Steps:
1. Seek help from your team leader or a healthcare professional if available.
2. Respect the need for privacy and any rituals that apply to the individual.
3. Ask if there is anything you can do to provide comfort, such as offering drinks, sweets, or cigarettes.
4. Listen and take any messages the dying person might have.
5. Talk to the person, even if you think they are not hearing you.
6. Check for relatives or friends nearby and, with the individual's agreement, call them and provide honest and accurate information at all times.

Notes:
• A human presence is crucial in such situations.
• Bringing comfort to the dying is a humane act that helps caregivers continue their work.
• In cases of severe injury or sickness, death can occur suddenly and unexpectedly.
dying persons, comfort, armed conflicts, end of life care, first aid

Deceased Management (1) -

Manage the Dead in Armed Conflicts
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Ensure dignified and respectful management of deceased persons during armed conflicts.

Steps:
1. Respect the deceased and their bereaved families at all times.
2. Approach the bereaved with sympathy and care.
3. **Observe...
Objective: Ensure dignified and respectful management of deceased persons during armed conflicts.

Steps:
1. Respect the deceased and their bereaved families at all times.
2. Approach the bereaved with sympathy and care.
3. Observe and respect cultural and religious beliefs.
4. Ensure that the family of the deceased has the following rights:
- To receive accurate information at every stage, including official recognition and certification of death.
- To see the corpse.
- To recover and mourn their dead and perform funerary rituals in accordance with their customs.
5. Maintain the dignity of the corpse by covering it completely and keeping bystanders away.
6. Protect the body from unnecessary public viewing or disturbance.
7. Avoid moving the body if at all possible.
8. Place personal belongings in a plastic bag, clearly marked with the deceased's name, date, and place of death, then hand them over to the appropriate authorities.
9. Report the death or discovery of the body to your team leader or the relevant authorities.
10. Record all necessary information, including time and place of death/finding, witnesses, personal details of the deceased, and circumstances of death/finding.

Notes:
• The family has the right to an investigation into the cause and manner of death when required.
• It is the responsibility of the authorities to ensure proper management of human remains, identification, and return to families.
• In some contexts, dead bodies may be booby-trapped. Do not touch or move them without confirmation from mine-clearance operators.
dead persons, management of dead, armed conflicts, dignity, first aid

Transport Of Casualties (1) -

Selecting Appropriate Transport Means for Casualties in Armed Conflicts
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
When selecting transport means for casualties, ensure the following criteria are met:

1. Permit emergency and stabilization measures to continue: The transport method should allow ongoing medical care during transit.
2. Be safe: Prioritize...
When selecting transport means for casualties, ensure the following criteria are met:

1. Permit emergency and stabilization measures to continue: The transport method should allow ongoing medical care during transit.
2. Be safe: Prioritize methods that minimize risk of further injury or harm to the casualty.
3. Minimize trauma: Choose a method that is not overly traumatic for the casualty.
4. Accommodate different positions: Select a transport means that can accommodate casualties in various lying or sitting positions.
5. Allow accompaniment by First Aiders: Ensure the transport allows a First Aider or other care provider to accompany the casualty during transit.
6. Provide protection against environmental elements: The transport should protect the casualty from extreme temperatures, sun, rain, wind, and other environmental hazards.

Transport Recommendations Based on Casualty Condition:

• Most casualties: Transport using a stretcher or manual-carrying techniques.
• Casualties with chest wounds who are conscious: Use a chair (or stretcher or any object that allows the casualty to maintain a sitting position).
• Casualties over long distances: Use ambulance, ground vehicle, helicopter, aircraft, boat, or other vessel for transport.

Important Considerations:

• Manual-carrying techniques are physically demanding and may increase injury severity. Prefer two-person carrying techniques if possible.
• Avoid driving too fast during evacuation to prevent road traffic crashes, pain, increased bleeding, or displacement of injured limbs due to bumps and potholes.
• Transport by air requires special considerations (e.g., effects of acceleration/deceleration, atmospheric pressure changes, oxygen levels) that are beyond the scope of this manual.
transport,casualty,armed conflicts,first aid,manual carrying,ambulance,stretcher,air transport

Humanitarian Tasks (1) -

Understand Additional Tasks Beyond First Aid
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
You may be required to contribute to tasks other than that of providing care. These tasks depend on:

• Local circumstances
• The scope of the particular humanitarian mission and the means available for accomplishing it
• Your training and...
You may be required to contribute to tasks other than that of providing care. These tasks depend on:

• Local circumstances
• The scope of the particular humanitarian mission and the means available for accomplishing it
• Your training and preparedness

These other tasks may involve:

• Administration (casualty registration, follow-up of evacuations, radio communications, etc.)
• Logistics (physical protection of the healthcare facility, stock management, equipment maintenance, etc.)
• Support for communities (disease-prevention programmes, efforts to maintain and restore family links, distribution of relief items, etc.)
• Collection and burial of the dead
additional tasks,humanitarian work,logistics,administration,community support,burying the dead

Team Management (1) -

Request or Accept Changes in Work Assignment
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
You can ask your team leader about changing your activity. Such a request may be granted if there are other needs and you have the skills required to meet them. You should also be prepared to accept changes in your work assignment that you have not...
You can ask your team leader about changing your activity. Such a request may be granted if there are other needs and you have the skills required to meet them. You should also be prepared to accept changes in your work assignment that you have not requested. You may decline the changes if you do not feel comfortable with the proposed new assignment.
work assignment,change request,team leader,skills,comfort level

Post Mission Procedures (1) -

Attend Debriefing Sessions After a Mission
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Title: Attend Debriefing Sessions After a Mission

Procedure:
1. Attend debriefing sessions led by your team leader and/or the supervisor in charge of the location you were assigned to.
2. Share security information, report on what...
Title: Attend Debriefing Sessions After a Mission

Procedure:
1. Attend debriefing sessions led by your team leader and/or the supervisor in charge of the location you were assigned to.
2. Share security information, report on what you did, results and problems, and make suggestions during the session.
3. Participate in collective debriefings if required, which are held at the end of the mission (e.g., end of the day) in a group meeting in a relaxed atmosphere.
4. Undergo individual debriefings if needed, led by your team leader in a face-to-face discussion in a relaxed atmosphere.
5. During debriefing sessions, provide a detailed account of the mission and follow-up, share feelings, reactions, painful emotions, etc., and seek advice on how to cope with them.
6. Do not include judgements of one’s actions and words, settling of scores, or criticisms during debriefings.
7. Debriefing sessions may result in reinforcement of the team and its management, adjustment of your work plan, change in your work assignment, further development of coping mechanisms, or advice and support for personal development.

Notes:
• Individual debriefings must remain confidential.
• Collective debriefings are for all involved in the same mission.
debriefing, post-mission, team leader, first aid, armed conflicts

Community First Aid (1) -

Establish Community Emergency Response for Explosive Remnants of War Incidents
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: To ensure communities are prepared to respond effectively to incidents involving explosive remnants of war (ERW).

Steps:
1. Ensure First Aid Coverage: There should be at least one trained First Aider equipped with a First Aid...
Objective: To ensure communities are prepared to respond effectively to incidents involving explosive remnants of war (ERW).

Steps:
1. Ensure First Aid Coverage: There should be at least one trained First Aider equipped with a First Aid kit per family.
2. Develop an Alert System: Create a system for quickly alerting First Aiders and healthcare professionals in the event of an ERW incident.
3. Stockpile Rescue Materials: Keep supplies such as blankets, stretchers (improvised if necessary), and ideally a vehicle for evacuation ready at all times.
4. Establish Communication Channels: Ensure there is a means to communicate with the nearest health-care centre in case of emergencies.
5. Conduct Annual Refresher Sessions: Organize an annual training session that includes field exercises to reinforce preparedness.
6. Train Families on Basic First Aid: Training should cover at least four key areas:
- Airway and breathing management
- Bleeding control
- Wound and burn dressing
- Transportation of injured individuals

Additional Information:
• These measures are crucial for communities affected by armed conflict as they return to peace.
• Training should be sufficient to handle common injuries resulting from ERW incidents.
ERW emergency response, community first aid training, mine incident preparedness, first aider training

Community Preparedness (1) -

Implementing First Aid Training for Community Awareness and Education
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
First Aid training is a primary vehicle for community awareness and education, with the following objectives:

1. Reduce vulnerability to hazards: Develop risk awareness within the community.
2. **Promote self-reliance in emergency...
First Aid training is a primary vehicle for community awareness and education, with the following objectives:

1. Reduce vulnerability to hazards: Develop risk awareness within the community.
2. Promote self-reliance in emergency preparedness and response.
3. Disseminate health-education messages, including:
- Environmental sanitation
- Hygiene promotion
- Vaccination campaigns
4. Promote social tolerance and humanitarian understanding: Highlight that everyone has the potential to help protect and save a life, which could be one’s own.

After First Aid training is provided, communities can:
• Reduce the incidence of illness and injury
• Assist in psychological recovery after an armed conflict or other violent situation
• Establish a new norm for community resilience
first aid training, community awareness, emergency preparedness, health education, social tolerance

Post Conflict Continuity (1) -

Continuing First Aider Efforts Post-Conflict
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
As a National Society First Aider, your role continues even after an armed conflict or other situation of violence ends. You should:

Prevention programmes: Promote:
• Use of clean water for drinking and preparing food
• Individual and...
As a National Society First Aider, your role continues even after an armed conflict or other situation of violence ends. You should:

Prevention programmes: Promote:
• Use of clean water for drinking and preparing food
• Individual and environmental hygiene (waste disposal, latrines)
• Safe and healthy lifestyle (good nutrition, breast-feeding, road safety)
• Vaccination campaigns

Emergency preparedness and response programmes: Include:
• Assessment and mapping of community vulnerability
• Planning of local action
• Surveillance of risks of epidemics

You should participate in any refresher courses offered and encourage others to do the same.
post conflict, emergency preparedness, prevention programs, refresher courses

Breathing Assessment (1) -

Assess Breathing in a Casualty
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Listen

• Casualty complains of difficulty in breathing.

• Normal breathing is soundless. Loud breathing indicates an effort to breathe.

• A sucking sound indicates a large chest wound.

Talk

• If the patient can reply normally then there...
Listen

• Casualty complains of difficulty in breathing.

• Normal breathing is soundless. Loud breathing indicates an effort to breathe.

• A sucking sound indicates a large chest wound.

Talk

• If the patient can reply normally then there is no problem with the airway or breathing.

Touch

• Feel chest movements by placing the hands flat on both sides of the chest; note uneven rise and fall of the chest.

• Press on both sides: abnormal movement and

• “click” indicate broken ribs.

Suspect

• Breathing can be compromised many hours after

• a blast injury, exposure to smoke or chemical inhalation because of production of fluid in the lungs (pulmonary oedema).
breathing assessment, casualty, airway, chest wound, broken ribs, pulmonary oedema

Cpr Procedures (1) -

Perform CPR for Non-Traumatic Causes of No Breathing
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
If a casualty is not breathing

• Check C for circulation.

• If there is no breathing and no pulse, and:

• cause is non traumatic: do standard CPR for five minutes; or

• cause is trauma with massive visible or hidden bleeding (in chest or...
If a casualty is not breathing

• Check C for circulation.

• If there is no breathing and no pulse, and:

• cause is non traumatic: do standard CPR for five minutes; or

• cause is trauma with massive visible or hidden bleeding (in chest or abdomen):

• most cases will show obvious signs of death: no use in doing CPR. Casualty is dead from shock: please refer to Section 6.1;

• if death is not obvious: stop visible bleeding and perform standard CPR for 5 minutes.

FIRST AID

Standard CPR using the mouth-to-mask technique is recommended: the mask protects against contamination, does not require supplemental oxygen and reduces gastric dilatation.
CPR, non-traumatic, no breathing, mouth-to-mask technique

Positioning For Breathing (1) -

Position a Conscious Casualty with Breathing Difficulties
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
If a casualty is conscious and has breathing difficulties only

• Help the casualty to sit in a comfortable position conducive to easy breathing.

• Make sure that clothes do not restrict chest and abdominal movements.
conscious casualty, breathing difficulties, positioning

Evacuation Positioning (1) -

Position a Casualty for Evacuation with Breathing Difficulties
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
EVACUATION POSITION

• Place the casualty in the position most comfortable for breathing: sitting, semi-sitting, lying on the back or the side.

• Casualty with assisted ventilation must be constantly monitored and accompanied by a trained...
EVACUATION POSITION

• Place the casualty in the position most comfortable for breathing: sitting, semi-sitting, lying on the back or the side.

• Casualty with assisted ventilation must be constantly monitored and accompanied by a trained person.
evacuation position, breathing difficulties, assisted ventilation

Ventilation Monitoring (1) -

Monitor Casualty with Assisted Ventilation
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- Casualty with assisted ventilation must be constantly monitored and accompanied by a trained person.

• Breathing involves the chest and the lungs.
assisted ventilation, monitoring, trained person

Breathing Compromise (1) -

Recognize and Respond to Breathing Compromises from Non-Airway Causes
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
- Some injuries compromise breathing in spite of the airway being open.

• CPR is useless if breathing and pulse stop because of massive bleeding.

• Blast injury and inhalation of smoke or chemicals can create breathing problems hours after the...
- Some injuries compromise breathing in spite of the airway being open.

• CPR is useless if breathing and pulse stop because of massive bleeding.

• Blast injury and inhalation of smoke or chemicals can create breathing problems hours after the injury.
non-airway breathing compromise, massive bleeding, blast injury, chemical inhalation

Fluid Management (1) -

Providing Drinks to a Conscious Casualty
Standard Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
You can give drinks if the casualty is conscious and not suffering from head trauma.

• Provide sips of clean water or rehydration fluids (ORS) up to a maximum of about two litres;

• Stop immediately if the casualty’s...
You can give drinks if the casualty is conscious and not suffering from head trauma.

• Provide sips of clean water or rehydration fluids (ORS) up to a maximum of about two litres;

• Stop immediately if the casualty’s consciousness deteriorates or they feel like vomiting.
hydration,casualty care,rehydration fluids,consciousness monitoring

Bleeding Management (1) -

Managing Bleeding Wounds and Shock
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Any visible bleeding from a wound must be stopped immediately.

• Almost all visible haemorrhaging can be controlled on the spot;

• Penetrating wounds often have an entry and exit point, so check the back and sides for additional...
Any visible bleeding from a wound must be stopped immediately.

• Almost all visible haemorrhaging can be controlled on the spot;

• Penetrating wounds often have an entry and exit point, so check the back and sides for additional injuries;

• Internal bleeding is difficult to manage in the field, so prioritize evacuation based on common sense;

• Peripheral haemorrhage from a limb can be managed effectively with proper techniques;

• A casualty is considered to be in shock due to haemorrhage unless proven otherwise;

• Bleeding wounds are often complex, involving foreign bodies, broken bones, or dirt, which increases the risk of infection;

• All bleeding casualties lose body heat, so keep them warm to support blood clotting.

Pneumatic anti-shock garment: used in advanced management.

Wide-bore intravenous lines: used for fluid resuscitation and medication administration.

Fluid resuscitation: to replace lost blood volume.

Analgesia: best administered intravenously.

Antibiotic: best administered intravenously.

Urinary catheter placement: to monitor urinary output as an indicator of shock and treatment effectiveness.

Surgical control of injured blood vessels: definitive management for severe haemorrhage.

Chest tube drainage: used for haemothorax.

Laparotomy: performed for intra-abdominal haemorrhage.
hemorrhage control,wound management,shock,fluid resuscitation,advanced care

Oxygen Management (1) -

Oxygen Therapy in Dangerous Areas
High Priority Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Supplemental oxygen therapy is not recommended in dangerous areas due to the risk of oxygen cylinders being equivalent to a bomb if hit by a bullet or shrapnel.

• In secure environments, **oxygen concentrators (requiring an electrical...
Supplemental oxygen therapy is not recommended in dangerous areas due to the risk of oxygen cylinders being equivalent to a bomb if hit by a bullet or shrapnel.

• In secure environments, oxygen concentrators (requiring an electrical supply) are preferred over compressed oxygen cylinders because they:

- Are lighter;

- Last longer at high flows;

- Pose less risk than compressed cylinders.
oxygen therapy,safety,dangerous areas,cylinder risks

Neurological Injuries (1) -

Assessment and Management of Disability (Brain/Spinal Cord Injury)
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Disability represents injury to the brain or spinal cord, resulting in unconsciousness and paralysis.

• This requires a thorough assessment and management plan;

• Immediate attention is needed for neurological injuries;

• Ensure proper...
Disability represents injury to the brain or spinal cord, resulting in unconsciousness and paralysis.

• This requires a thorough assessment and management plan;

• Immediate attention is needed for neurological injuries;

• Ensure proper immobilization of the casualty to prevent further damage;

• Monitor for signs of deterioration and provide appropriate care based on severity.
brain injury,spinal cord injury,unconsciousness,paralysis

Recovery Position (1) -

Place Casualty in Lateral Recovery Position with Spinal Alignment
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Safely position an unconscious casualty to maintain airway patency and spinal alignment.

Steps:
1. Clear Airway: Ensure the casualty’s airway is clear of any obstructions (e.g., vomit, mucus).
2. Position Casualty: Place...
Objective: Safely position an unconscious casualty to maintain airway patency and spinal alignment.

Steps:
1. Clear Airway: Ensure the casualty’s airway is clear of any obstructions (e.g., vomit, mucus).
2. Position Casualty: Place the casualty in the lateral recovery position (on their side) with their head, neck, and back aligned.
3. Support Head and Neck: Keep the head and neck in a neutral position to prevent further spinal injury.
4. Maintain Stability: Ensure that the body is positioned so that the airway remains open and the casualty does not slip into a dangerous posture.

Notes:
• This technique is used if consciousness is deteriorating or likely to do so.
• It helps reduce the risk of aspiration and maintains spinal alignment.
lateral recovery position, airway management, unconscious casualty, first aid

Spinal Injury Assessment (1) -

Examine for Signs of Spinal Cord Injury
Critical Source: First Aid in Armed Conflicts
Objective: Identify signs that may indicate a spinal cord injury.

Steps:
1. Look: Observe if there is absence of movement in one or more limbs compared to the opposite side. Note any difficulty in breathing.
2. Listen: Check for...
Objective: Identify signs that may indicate a spinal cord injury.

Steps:
